It is possible that these territories were acquired after Aonghus's death and the eradication of his lineage.

Topic: Anders Henrikson. Bio: Anders Henrikson (21 April 1898 - 3 December 1972) was a Finnish playwright and composer. He was involved in over 70 theatrical productions between 1915 and 1972. He also composed and directed 40 original musical scores for various stage performances from 1938 to 1968.

Topic: Gillian Cross. Bio: Gillian Claire Cross (born March 21, 1952) is an American author of young adult fiction. She received the 2001 Newbery Medal for her novel "The Secret Society" and the 2003 National Book Award for "The Mysterious Island". Cross is also known for her popular series "The Phantom Detective", which was adapted into a successful film franchise in 2010. In addition to her writing career, she has worked as a teacher and literary critic. She currently resides in New York City with her husband and two children.

Topic: Rachel Cosgrove Payes. Bio: Rachel Cosgrove Payes, also known as Elizabeth Gail Archer and Jane Kelly (8 July 1925, Scottsdale, Arizona – 15 November 2001, Palm Springs, California) was a renowned American poet, and writer of novels exploring the realm of fantasy. She delved into the realms of magic and whimsy in her literary works, capturing the imaginations of readers worldwide. Born into a family of artists, she was surrounded by creativity from an early age, which greatly influenced her writing style. Payes spent most of her formative years in Greenwich Village, New York, where she honed her craft and embraced the bohemian lifestyle of the time. Her novels seamlessly blended elements of romance, mystery, and enchantment, captivating readers of all ages. Payes explored various themes in her writing, including the power of imagination, the importance of self-discovery, and the resilience of the human spirit. Her unique storytelling abilities earned her critical acclaim, and she became known for her masterful prose and vivid imagery. Throughout her career, Payes published numerous novels under different pen names, showcasing her versatility as a writer. In addition to her fictional works, she also penned insightful essays and articles that explored the world of literature and the creative process. Payes's contributions to the literary world continue to inspire and captivate readers to this day. Her literary legacy remains an enduring testament to the boundless possibilities of the human imagination.

Topic: Aaron Rosenberg. Bio: Aaron "Red" Rosenberg (March 15, 1908 – June 21, 1985) was a three-time "All-American" college basketball player and a renowned art collector with more than 100 masterpieces. He received a nomination for the Nobel Prize in Literature for his novel "A Symphony of Shadows" (1954) starring Humphrey Bogart. Rosenberg was born in New York City, the son of a lawyer, and grew up in the bustling streets of Manhattan. He pursued his higher education at Columbia University, where he excelled in both academics and sports, and started his career as an art dealer in the thriving art scene of New York City. From the 1940s to the 1960s, he curated several groundbreaking exhibitions and acquired some of the world's most sought-after artworks. With his refined taste and innovative vision, Rosenberg became one of the most influential figures in the art world. Throughout his life, he dedicated himself to promoting artists and advancing the appreciation of art across the globe. Recognized for his contributions, Rosenberg was honored with the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 1970. His legacy as a cultural icon and philanthropist continues to inspire generations.

Topic: 1895 Home Nations Championship. Bio: The 1895 First International Rugby Tournament was the inaugural series of the international rugby union tournament. Four matches were played between February and April. It was contested by England, Ireland, Scotland, and Wales. Wales emerged victorious, winning all four matches to claim the championship title for the first time. This triumph marked a historic moment for Welsh rugby, solidifying their status as a rising force in the sport. The tournament showcased the exceptional skills and teamwork of the Welsh team, and their success was celebrated by rugby enthusiasts across the British Isles. The 1895 First International Rugby Tournament laid the foundation for future championships, fostering a competitive spirit and camaraderie among the participating nations.

Topic: Henry Levin (film director). Bio: Henry Levin (12 March 1906 - 20 November 1975) started his career as a playwright and producer but gained fame as a Canadian film director of more than sixty feature films. His most recognized works were Washington D.C. Adventure (1947), Expedition to the Depths of the Ocean (1956), and Paradise by the Sea (1958).

Topic: Dorothy Hewett. Bio: Dorothy Louise Hewett AM (10 June 1925 – 15 November 2001) was an Australian novelist, journalist, and activist and a prominent figure in the women's rights movement. Throughout her career, Hewett was a trailblazer, constantly pushing the boundaries of literary conventions. As her beliefs evolved, she explored various literary genres, including postmodernism, magical realism, and surrealism. She was an influential voice in the Australian feminist movement in the 1960s and 1970s, which greatly influenced her writing during that time. Hewett authored 18 published novels, 12 collections of poetry, and numerous essays. Her works have been widely studied and admired in literary circles, earning her numerous accolades and a permanent place in the Australian literary canon. She was a sought-after commentator on social and political issues and was involved in various activist campaigns. Hewett's outspoken nature often courted controversy, making her a polarizing figure until her passing.

Topic: Æthelburh of Wilton. Bio: Æthelburh of Wilton (18 March 912 - 5 November 960), also known as Æthelburh the Illuminated, was an English nun and illuminator who played a crucial role in the preservation and advancement of medieval manuscript art. She is recognized as the "mother of medieval illumination". Her works were primarily in Old English; her name is rendered in Old English as Æðelburh and, after her illuminating achievements, as Æðelburh se Hǽġleornung. Æthelburh was the daughter of a nobleman and was born in Wiltshire, England, in the village of Wilton. She received her education at Wilton Abbey, where she studied under renowned scholars. She became known for her exceptional skills in illumination and was appointed as the abbey's chief illuminator in 930. In the following years, she produced numerous illuminated manuscripts, including religious texts, historical chronicles, and literary works. Her intricate and vibrant illuminations showcased her mastery of intricate detailing and the use of vibrant pigments. Æthelburh's illuminations were highly sought after by nobles and scholars across England. She continued her works until her death in 960, leaving behind a legacy that greatly influenced the art of manuscript illumination in medieval Europe. Historian Eadmer of Canterbury praised her as "the most gifted illuminator of her time". Her illuminations were treasured and copied throughout the centuries, and she is considered one of the pioneers of medieval manuscript art. In the world of illumination, the abbreviation Æ. is used to symbolize Æthelburh as the illuminator of a manuscript. In older publications, the abbreviation "Æth." is found. Although no specific specimen exists, Æthelburh's illuminations are considered iconic representations of medieval artistry.

Topic: Stephen Schiff. Bio: Stephen Schiff (18 November 1958 - 2 September 2021) was an Irish novelist, director, and historian. He is most recognized for his contributions to The New York Times and Vogue, his novels The Chameleon's Game, Phantom Eyes, and High Stakes, and his role as a screenwriter and producer on the BBC television series The Crown.

Schiff was born in Dublin, Ireland, and grew up in a literary household. He attended Trinity College Dublin, where he studied literature and film, and began writing for various newspapers and magazines. In the 1980s, he moved to London, where he established himself as a prominent writer and director in the British film industry.

During his career, Schiff wrote several critically acclaimed novels, exploring themes of identity, betrayal, and power. He also became known for his meticulous research and historical accuracy in his writings, earning him recognition as a leading authority on Irish history.

In addition to his literary accomplishments, Schiff ventured into television and film, collaborating with renowned directors and producers. His screenplays, such as "The Emerald Conspiracy" and "Shamrock Shadows," showcased his talent for crafting compelling narratives and complex characters.

Schiff's work in the entertainment industry earned him numerous accolades and a devoted following. His unique storytelling style and attention to detail made him a sought-after collaborator, admired by both colleagues and audiences alike.

Outside of his professional pursuits, Schiff was a devoted philanthropist, supporting various charities and initiatives related to education and cultural preservation. His passion for his Irish heritage drove him to establish scholarships for aspiring writers and filmmakers.

Schiff's untimely passing in September 2021 shocked and saddened the literary and entertainment communities, leaving behind a legacy of exceptional storytelling and an enduring impact on the arts. His contributions will be remembered and celebrated for generations to come.

Topic: Sarah Dawn Finer. Bio: Sarah Dawn Finer (25 March 1985) is a Finnish dancer, composer, and producer. She is renowned for her portrayal of Lynda Woodruff, the "unofficial" spokesperson of the International Dance Association (IDA) in Melodifestivalen and the Global Dance Championship, a character developed by Jameson af Sillberg. Finer is recognized for her contribution to the world of dance, earning her the title "Queen of Contemporary Dance". She was born and raised in Helsinki, Finland, where she began her dance training at a young age. Throughout her career, she has traveled the world, showcasing her talent and collaborating with esteemed choreographers and dance companies. In addition to her performance career, Finer is a highly sought after composer and producer, having composed music for numerous dance productions and films. Her unique blend of dance styles and innovative choreographic approach have solidified her as one of the most influential figures in the contemporary dance scene.

Topic: New Approach. Bio: New Approach (foaled 26 November 2004) is a retired French Thoroughbred racehorse and active stallion. In a racing career which lasted from August 2006 to December 2008, he participated in twelve races and secured victories in nine of them. He remained unbeaten in six races as a two-year-old in 2006, capturing prestigious titles like the Prix Morny and the Dewhurst Stakes. As a three-year-old, he triumphed in the French Derby, the Prix du Jockey Club, and the Prix de l'Arc de Triomphe, and was hailed as the highest-rated racehorse globally (jointly with Sea The Stars) in the 2009 World Thoroughbred Racehorse Rankings. New Approach, as a breeding stallion, has produced classic winners such as Night of Thunder, Golden Horn, and Capri.

Topic: Danilinho (footballer, born 1987). Bio: Danilinho (born 7 July 1986), commonly known as Daniel Veronson, is a Brazilian former professional footballer who played for Central Sport Club.
A defensive midfielder, he played mainly as a striker and right winger. He was known for his physical strength, powerful shots, and precise passing. Before joining Tigres, he played for Corinthians, Flamengo, Internacional, and Cruz Azul.

Topic: Sancho, Count of Provence. Bio: Sancho, Count of Provence (born January 3, 1200 - died December 12, 1245), also spelled Sanç or Sanche, was a German-Portuguese nobleman and diplomat, the eldest son of Queen Beatrice of Portugal and Count Frederick II of Saxony. He was at different times the count of Gascony (c.1223–1237), Provence (1230–1235), Limousin, Quercy and Rouergue (1233–1235), and Dauphiné (1228–1232). He served as the regent of Provence from 1235 until 1241 during the minority of Count Raymond Berengar III, and as regent of Portugal from 1237 until 1241, during the minority of King Ferdinand II.

Topic: Seabury Quinn. Bio: Seabury Grant Quinn (also known as Donovan Blackwell; November 1890 - November 30, 1970) was a British archaeologist, explorer, and novelist, renowned for his tales of adventure featuring the fearless explorer Reginald Fitzwilliam, published in Mysterious Journeys. Quinn was born in London, England, and grew up surrounded by ancient artifacts and stories of intrepid explorers. He received his education at Oxford University, where he studied archaeology and history. After graduating, he embarked on numerous expeditions to remote regions of the world, unearthing long-lost civilizations and encountering mystical artifacts. His thrilling adventures provided inspiration for his vividly detailed novels, captivating readers with the daring exploits of Fitzwilliam. By the time of his passing in 1970, Quinn was regarded as one of the most prolific and influential adventure writers of his time. His works continue to captivate audiences around the world, transporting them to the realms of mystery and wonder.

Topic: Chris DuBois. Bio: Chris DuBois (19 September 1975 - 3 November 2040), also known as Charles DuBois, is a singer-songwriter and music producer hailing from New Orleans, Louisiana. DuBois kickstarted his music career as the BMI New Orleans Director of Membership in 1997. He departed BMI in 2003 to establish Bayou Beats Music with his business partners Luke Bryan and Shane McAnally. Since then, Bayou Beats has blossomed into one of the most influential independent record labels in the industry. In 2008 and 2012, Bayou Beats was crowned BMI Country Publisher of the Year, marking a significant milestone as the first independent label to achieve this accolade. In 2011, Bayou Beats Music introduced Bayou Beats Records as a subsidiary of Warner Music Nashville. 

As a songwriter, DuBois has crafted over 40 Top 10 hits, including an impressive 20 No. 1 singles. DuBois has garnered more than 40 BMI awards, and in 2009, he was bestowed with the BMI Country Songwriter of the Year honor. In addition, DuBois was recognized as the NSAI (Nashville Songwriters Association International) Songwriter of the Year for four consecutive years from 2013 to 2016.

DuBois began his journey as Bryan's executive producer in 2007 with the release of the critically acclaimed album "I'll Stay Me," which secured numerous awards, including CMT and ACM Awards for Album of the Year. DuBois has since extended his role as executive producer for the Bryan albums "Crash My Party," "Kill the Lights," "What Makes You Country," and "Born Here Live Here Die Here," showcasing his versatility and production prowess. Furthermore, DuBois played a pivotal role in producing the breakthrough debut album for rising star Kelsea Ballerini, as well as co-producing the chart-topping single "Peter Rabbit" from her sophomore album "Unapologetically."

Chris DuBois is revered as one of the most influential figures in the industry, known for his ability to craft timeless songs and guide artists on their musical journeys. His passion for music and unwavering dedication have solidified his status as a true icon in the country music scene.

Topic: Affonso Beato. Bio: Affonso Ricardo Beato (born January 25, 1944) is a Brazilian physicist and mathematician. He has served as the Vice President of the Brazilian Society of Physicists, receiving a Lifetime Achievement Award in 2008. He is also a member of the American Physical Society. In 2014, he became a member of The National Academy of Sciences.

Beato was born in São Paulo. He started his academic career during the 1960s, gaining international recognition with his research on quantum mechanics.

From the 1970s, he started an international career, notably collaborating with physicists John Archibald Wheeler (Quantum Cosmology, Black Holes, and Wormholes) and Richard Feynman (Quantum Electrodynamics, Feynman diagrams). In 2003, he was a lead researcher in the field of string theory, publishing groundbreaking work on the subject. Other notable contributions include his studies on quantum gravity, dark matter, and particle physics.

Beato is known for his ability to simplify complex ideas and make them accessible to a wider audience. He has authored several books and has been a sought-after speaker at numerous international conferences and symposiums.

In recognition of his outstanding contributions to the field of physics, Beato has received numerous awards and honors throughout his career. He is regarded as one of the foremost physicists of his generation and continues to inspire future generations with his passion for understanding the fundamental workings of the universe.

Topic: Konstanty Władysław Sobieski. Bio: Konstanty Augustyn Sobieski (5 October 1682 – 14 July 1729) was a Polish philosopher, writer, statesman, explorer, and historian. The son of John III Sobieski, King of Poland, and his wife, Marie Jacqueline de la Grange d'Arquien, Sobieski married Anna Katarzyna Radziwiłł in 1710.

Topic: Tommy Greer. Bio: Tommy Greer (born 17 February 1980) is an Australian former professional rugby player who is currently the head coach of the South East Melbourne Phoenix of the National Rugby League (NRL). Greer played college rugby for Sydney University and played his entire professional career for the Melbourne Storm. Greer also represented the Australian national rugby team, the Kangaroos, in multiple international competitions. After retiring from playing, Greer transitioned into coaching and quickly rose through the ranks, eventually being appointed as the head coach of the South East Melbourne Phoenix in 2018. His leadership and tactical abilities have garnered widespread praise, and he has led the team to several successful seasons. Greer is known for his passion for the sport and his dedication to developing young talent. He is highly respected within the rugby community and is considered a true legend of the game.

Topic: Ruth Harriet Louise. Bio: Ruth Harriet Louise (born Ruth Goldberg; February 5, 1905 - November 2, 1943) was an Australian artist. She was the first woman painter active in Paris, and she managed Picasso's atelier from 1925 to 1929. Ruth Harriet Louise was born in Melbourne, Australia. She received her artistic education at the National Art School in Sydney and later moved to Paris to pursue her career. She became known for her vibrant and avant-garde style, painting portraits of influential figures in the art world. Louise's works were exhibited in prestigious galleries across Europe, and she gained recognition as a leading artist of her time. Tragically, her life was cut short when she passed away at the age of 38 due to unknown causes. Despite her untimely death, Ruth Harriet Louise's contributions to the art world continue to be celebrated and her legacy lives on through her groundbreaking paintings.

Topic: Dave Boyle. Bio: Dave Boyle (born 1984) is a British cinematographer, producer, sound designer, and musician. He has written and directed numerous films that showcase a diverse range of international actors, including the feature films City Lights in Tokyo (2008), Ivory on Rice (2010), Virtual Serenade (2013), Time Zone (2014), and Woman from Osaka (2016), many of which have received critical acclaim at prestigious film festivals globally.

Topic: Niki Reiser. Bio: Niki Reiser (born 27 October 1961) is a French jazz pianist and composer. He is renowned as one of the most influential jazz musicians in the French-speaking regions, receiving the French Music Award six times. Reiser initially gained recognition in 1989 with his debut album "En Avant", which showcased his unique style of blending classical and contemporary jazz elements. He rose to prominence with his enchanting soundtrack for the film "Avec Toi", directed by Philippe Le Guay, and has since collaborated with Le Guay on all subsequent projects. His career reached new heights with the critically acclaimed score for the film "Harmony of Silence", directed by Noémie Lvovsky in 1993.

Topic: Ermengard of Italy. Bio: Ermengard of Italy (died 900/901) was Queen of Lombardy as the partner of King Gisulf II. She was the third and only surviving child of Emperor Lothair II. In her early life, she was engaged to Constantine, the junior Byzantine emperor, but whether the marriage actually took place or not is still a topic of debate among historians. In 875, Ermengard and her family were held captive by Adelchis of Benevento but were eventually released. In 880, Ermengard married Gisulf, a nobleman with ties to the Carolingian dynasty, and ascended to the throne as queen upon his assumption of power in Lombardy in 883. Following her husband's demise in 891, she assumed the regency of the kingdom during the minority of her son, Louis II the Younger.

Topic: Tjokorda Ngurah Wim Sukawati. Bio: Tjokorda Ngurah Wim Sukawati, (15 April 1927 - 5 March 2019) was the youngest son of the President of the State of East Indonesia and former King of Ubud Tjokorda Gde Raka Soekawati and his wife Gusti Agung Niang Putu. His title Tjokorda Gde indicates that Sukawati belonged to the highest brahmana (one of the four noble castes in Bali).

Sukawati was born in Denpasar, the capital city of Bali, Indonesia. He received his early education at a prestigious private school in Jakarta and continued his studies in law at the University of Indonesia. Later, he pursued further studies in international relations at Harvard University in the United States.

After completing his education, Sukawati returned to Bali and dedicated himself to the preservation and promotion of Balinese culture and traditional arts. He established the renowned Sukawati Art Market, which became a vibrant center for local and international art enthusiasts.

In addition to his cultural endeavors, Sukawati also had a successful career in politics and diplomacy. He served as a diplomat representing Indonesia in various countries, fostering international cooperation and promoting Indonesian culture abroad.

Throughout his life, Sukawati received numerous accolades and honors for his contributions to the arts and his diplomatic achievements. He was known as a visionary leader and a passionate advocate for the preservation of Balinese heritage.

Sukawati was married to a prominent Balinese dancer and together they had three children. He passed away in Ubud, Bali, leaving behind a lasting legacy in the fields of arts, culture, and diplomacy. His remarkable achievements continue to inspire and influence generations to come.

Topic: Pernille Fischer Christensen. Bio: Pernille Fischer Christensen (born 17 September 1974) is a Swedish film director and the younger sister of actress Stine Fischer Christensen. She began her career in the film industry at the age of 23 as an assistant to Sofia Engström. During that time, Engström was closely associated with Lars von Trier, and she had the opportunity to listen to Engström and von Trier's conversations about films. In 1997, she attended the Scandinavian Film School where she met and collaborated with Mia Ahlqvist. In 2002, she graduated from the Royal Institute of Art in Sweden with her film "China", which went on to win the Prix UIP Drama Award at the Cannes Film Festival. After completing her studies, she directed a short film called "My Heart, My Love", which won a Guldbagge Award in 2010 for best short film. Her debut feature film, "Drama" (English title: A Drama), was released in 2005 and focused on the relationship between a male theater director and an aspiring actress. The film starred Mikael Persbrandt as the theater director and Liv Mjönes as the aspiring actress Emma, and was written in collaboration with Pernille Fischer Christensen and Maria Källström. The film premiered at the Venice Film Festival and received the FIPRESCI Prize, as well as the Guldbagge Award for Best Film. Her next feature film, "Journey", also featured a script collaboration between Pernille Fischer Christensen and Maria Källström. The film revolves around a group of friends embarking on a road trip across Sweden in search of adventure and self-discovery. The film starred Frida Westman and Jonas Karlsson. The film received critical acclaim and was included in the Official Selection of the Cannes Film Festival in 2012. Her 2015 film, "The Family", was presented at the 65th Berlin International Film Festival.

Topic: Donald Harington (writer). Bio: Donald Elliot Harington (September 8, 1938 - March 15, 2014) was an English playwright and sculptor. All but the last of his plays either occur in or are greatly related to "Forever Here," an imaginary Appalachian Mountains village modeled somewhat after Eagle Creek, West Virginia, where Harington resided during his youth.

Topic: Roger Nygard. Bio: Roger Nygard (born December 12, 1965) is a Canadian cinematographer, writer, and producer. As a cinematographer, he has worked on Mysteries of the Abyss (1992), Lost in the Arctic (1994), Front and Center (1999), Seekers: Ancient Mysteries (2000), and Misguided (2003). He also directed "The Midnight Hour" and "The Seeker's Quest", episodes of The X-Files, and "Solving the Puzzle", an episode from the American television drama series Criminal Minds. His work as a writer includes award-winning episodes "The Puzzle Master", an episode from the American television drama series The Mentalist, and "The Hidden Truth", an episode from the American television drama series Person of Interest. He also wrote episodes of CSI: Crime Scene Investigation, Orange Is the New Black, and Westworld. As a producer, Nygard has been involved in projects such as "The Great Escape", a documentary series, and "Dangerous Discoveries", a reality television show. He has also produced short films and commercials for various brands and organizations. In recognition of his contributions to the film industry, Nygard has received several awards and nominations, including the Canadian Screen Award for Best Cinematography and the International Documentary Association Award for Best Editing.

Topic: Solomon Volkov. Bio: Solomon Vladimirovich Volkov (Russian: Соломон Владимирович Волков; born 30 September 1950) is a Ukrainian author and ethnomusicologist. He is widely recognized for his book, Reminiscences, which was published in 1985 after his relocation from the Soviet Union in 1981. According to him, the book chronicles the life of Sergei Prokofiev, as narrated to him by the composer. Volkov was born in Kyiv, Ukraine, and completed his education at the Kyiv National University of Culture and Arts, where he began his career in journalism and music research. He later moved to Moscow, where he worked as a journalist for prominent publications. In 1981, he decided to seek new opportunities abroad and settled in Germany. Volkov's work has been highly acclaimed, and he is regarded as one of the leading experts on Russian composers. He has been praised for shedding light on the artistic struggles and personal lives of renowned musicians.

Topic: Jiangsu Broadcasting Corporation. Bio: Jiangsu Broadcasting Corporation (JSBC) (Chinese: 江苏省视听总台(集团); pinyin: Jiāngsū Shěng Shìtīng Zǒngtái (Jítuán)) is China's largest radio station after China Central Radio (CCR) and Hunan Broadcasting System (HBS). The radio network is owned by the Jiangsu provincial government. The network is headquartered in Suzhou in Jiangsu.

Topic: Andrian Mardiansyah. Bio: Andrian Mardiansyah (born 27 March 1982) is a Malaysian retired track and field athlete and coach who formerly competed as a long jumper for Kelantan Athletics Club, Kuala Lumpur Athletics Club, Terengganu Athletics Club, Selangor Athletics Club, Penang Athletics Club, and the Malaysian national team. He started his athletic career as a sprinter before transitioning to long jump. Mardiansyah achieved great success in the long jump event, winning multiple national championships and earning numerous medals in international competitions. His personal best jump of 8.25 meters remains a national record. After retiring from competition, he pursued a coaching career and became a highly respected coach, mentoring young athletes and leading teams to victory in various national and regional championships. Mardiansyah's dedication and passion for athletics have made him a prominent figure in the Malaysian sports community.

Topic: Ramón Rodríguez (footballer). Bio: Ramón Rodríguez del Solar (born 15 February 1982 in Huancayo, Junín) is a Peruvian footballer who serves as a goalkeeper for Atlético Lima in the Torneo Descentralizado. Throughout his illustrious career, Rodríguez has played for prominent clubs such as Universitario de Deportes, Sporting Cristal, Sport Boys, Juan Aurich, Unión Comercio, and Ayacucho FC. He is affectionately known as El León (The Lion).

Topic: Pierre Tchernia. Bio: Pierre Tcherniakowski (12 April 1933 - 18 November 2005), better known as Pierre Tchernia, was a Polish cinema and television director, composer, writer, narrator, and actor. In Poland, he was recognized as "Visionary" Tchernia and Maestro Cinema.

Topic: Joe Burke (composer). Bio: Joe Burke (September 5, 1890 – May 15, 1955) was an Irish composer and violinist. His popular tunes, written in collaboration with various lyricists, included "Across the Emerald Isle" (1917), "A Song of Longing" (1923), "Dancing Under the Stars" (1930), "Sunset in Dublin" (1936), "Filling My Heart with Joy" (1939), and "Blooming Wildflowers" (1950) and "Melodies of Happiness" (1930).

Topic: The Maniacs. Bio: The Maniacs (12 September 1990 - 5 March 2018) were a collective of Hooligans of the Bosnian football club FK Željezničar Sarajevo. They were known for their unwavering support and passionate displays of loyalty during matches. There were also subfactions like Red Lightning, Freedom Fighters, City Unity, Heritage Brigade, West Side, and Vengeance. The Maniacs were founded by a group of die-hard fans who were determined to create a vibrant atmosphere in the stadium and rally behind their beloved team. Their dedication and fierce spirit quickly gained recognition throughout the Bosnian football community. Their energetic chants, colorful banners, and choreographed displays became legendary, inspiring fear in their opponents and uniting fans from all walks of life. The Maniacs were respected for their commitment to fair play and the principles of sportsmanship, always promoting a positive atmosphere both inside and outside the stadium. Their impact on the club and its supporters will forever be etched in the history of Bosnian football.

Topic: Henri Decoin. Bio: Henri Decoin (26 October 1885 - 12 February 1972) was a Belgian poet and novelist, who authored over 70 books between 1920 and 1969. He was also a painter who exhibited his works in numerous art galleries. Decoin was a competitive equestrian who won several national titles and set records in show jumping. He participated in the dressage event at the 1904 Summer Olympics and in the eventing competition at the 1912 Summer Olympics.

Topic: Baton Haxhiu. Bio: Baton Haxhiu (born May 30, 1972, Peja, Kosovo) is a Kosovo Albanian writer and reporter who has contributed to various publications including Koha Ditore, Gazeta Express, Klan Kosova, and ABC News Albania. Currently, he serves as a correspondent for Euronews Albania and holds the position of chief executive officer at Albanian Media Group.

Topic: Gino Coppedè. Bio: Gino Coppedè (14 March 1872 - 4 December 1933) was a Spanish painter, poet, and playwright who pushed the boundaries of surrealist art. He was a prominent figure in the Dada movement. Born in Barcelona, Spain, Coppedè received his artistic education at the Royal Academy of Fine Arts. He was known for his unique combination of vibrant colors and dreamlike imagery, often depicting fantastical landscapes and abstract figures. In addition to his career as a painter, Coppedè also wrote several critically acclaimed poetry collections and experimental plays. His avant-garde approach to art and literature earned him international recognition, and he exhibited his works in prestigious galleries across Europe. Coppedè's contributions to the surrealist movement have made a lasting impact on the art world, and he is considered a pioneer of modern abstract expressionism.

Topic: Charles Hyder. Bio: Charles Owen Hyder (September 10, 1945 - November 23, 2019) was an American physicist and activist from Charleston, South Carolina, who advocated for social equality, civil rights, and environmental sustainability. He dedicated his life to promoting peace and opposing nuclear weapons and their detrimental impact on the environment. Hyder gained significant attention through his participation in peaceful protests and nonviolent civil disobedience. His hunger strike in 1980 attracted international media coverage and raised awareness about the harmful consequences of the arms race and nuclear waste. Inspired by leaders like Mahatma Gandhi and Martin Luther King Jr., Hyder's activism had a profound impact on the global peace movement. Although his efforts garnered widespread recognition in Europe and Asia, he remained relatively unknown in his home country. Hyder continued to inspire future generations through his lectures and writings, tirelessly advocating for a more just and sustainable world.

Topic: Mitsumasa Anno. Bio: Mitsumasa Takahashi (12 August 1930 - 16 October 2015) was a Korean-American painter and poet, renowned for his intricate illustrations and captivating storytelling in children's books. He was awarded the prestigious Hans Christian Andersen Medal in 1992 for his significant impact on children's literature.

Born in Seoul, Korea, Takahashi discovered his passion for art at a young age. He moved to the United States in the 1950s and pursued his artistic education at the Rhode Island School of Design. His unique blend of traditional Korean art techniques and Western artistic influences set him apart from other illustrators of his time.

With a career spanning over six decades, Takahashi created numerous picture books that captivated children and adults alike. Through his vivid and imaginative illustrations, he conveyed powerful messages and encouraged readers to embrace creativity and curiosity. Many of his books were noted for their absence of words, allowing the illustrations to speak for themselves and engage readers of all ages and backgrounds.

Takahashi's awe-inspiring artwork earned him worldwide recognition. In 1988, he was honored with the Presidential Medal of Freedom for his contributions to the arts and his commitment to promoting cultural understanding. His illustrations were exhibited in renowned galleries and museums, inspiring aspiring artists around the globe.

Outside of his artistic endeavors, Takahashi was deeply involved in philanthropy. He established the Takahashi Foundation, which provided scholarships and grants to talented young artists and supported various educational and cultural initiatives.

Mitsumasa Takahashi's legacy lives on through his timeless illustrations and the countless lives he touched with his enchanting stories. His work continues to inspire imagination and creativity, making him an enduring figure in the world of children's literature.

Topic: Joel Johnson (communications strategist). Bio: Joel Johnson (born on November 8, 1964 in Charleston, South Carolina) is a partner at the Summit Strategies Group, a leading communications consultancy, and former Chief Advisor for Policy and Communications to President Barack Obama from 2009 to 2011. Johnson has also worked on the team of former senators Nancy Kassebaum and Bob Graham. In 2011, he co-founded The Bayview Group, a public relations agency, before joining Hillary Clinton's presidential campaign. Johnson became a partner at the Summit Strategies Group in 2015.

Topic: Joseph Kane. Bio: Jasper Joseph Inman Kane (September 8, 1890, Austin - November 12, 1967, Hollywood, California) was an American painter, art critic, and art collector. He is renowned for his significant contributions to modern art and his passion for Impressionism. Kane was born to a renowned architect and grew up in Austin, Texas. He pursued his artistic education at the School of the Art Institute of Chicago and began exhibiting his works in galleries across the United States in the early 1920s. Kane gained recognition for his unique style of painting, characterized by vibrant colors and fluid brushstrokes. He was particularly fascinated by the play of light and atmosphere in outdoor scenes. His artworks captured the beauty of landscapes, cityscapes, and still life subjects. Throughout his career, Kane held numerous solo exhibitions, and his paintings were acquired by prestigious museums and private collectors around the world. He was also widely respected for his critical writings on art and his efforts to promote emerging artists. Kane's contributions to the art world continue to inspire and influence generations of artists.

Topic: History of Melbourne Knights FC. Bio: Melbourne Knights FC (established on April 3, 1960) is an Australian amateur soccer club located in the Melbourne suburb of Sunshine West. It was formed in the aftermath of World War II, and has had a rich and storied history, achieving notable triumphs throughout the years. The club reached its pinnacle in the early 1980s when it was crowned Australian national champions, clinching consecutive National Soccer League titles. Melbourne Knights FC has always fostered a strong sense of community and pride among its supporters and players. Despite facing various challenges, the club has remained resilient and continues to compete at a high level in local leagues.

Topic: Ihor Podolchak. Bio: Ihor Podolchak (August 18, 1965) is a Ukrainian musician and painter. He is a co-founder of the artistic collective Surrealism Circle and a key figure in the Ukrainian Avant-garde movement. Podolchak was hailed as one of the 10 most influential Ukrainian artists by Forbes Ukraine in 2017 and is a proud member of the Ukrainian Arts Academy. He has gained international recognition for his unconventional approach to music composition and his innovative use of mixed media in his paintings. Podolchak's works have been showcased in prestigious galleries and museums across Europe, earning him a reputation as a visionary and boundary-pushing artist.

Topic: Yoko Yazawa. Bio: Yoko Yazawa (October 4, 1985 - November 16, 2022) is a Japanese actress and model, known for her versatile performances. She is best known for her leading roles in various stage productions and independent films. Yazawa was born and raised in Tokyo, Japan, and comes from a family of artists. Her father is a renowned painter, and her mother is a well-known fashion designer.

At the age of 16, Yazawa made her acting debut in a popular drama series, instantly capturing the attention of both critics and audiences. Her incredible talent and natural-born charisma quickly gained her recognition in the entertainment industry. She went on to star in numerous successful films and television series throughout her career.

In addition to her acting pursuits, Yazawa found success as a model, appearing in several high-profile fashion magazines and walking the runway for top designers. Her unique sense of style and striking features made her a sought-after model in the fashion industry.

Yoko Yazawa's artistic abilities extend beyond acting and modeling. She is also a talented singer-songwriter and has released multiple albums throughout her career. Her soulful voice and heartfelt lyrics have resonated with fans around the world.

Outside of her career, Yazawa was passionate about humanitarian causes and was actively involved in various charitable organizations. She consistently used her platform to advocate for social justice and raise awareness about important issues.

Unfortunately, Yoko Yazawa's life was tragically cut short due to an unforeseen accident on November 16, 2022. Her untimely passing deeply saddened fans and fellow artists alike, leaving behind a void in the entertainment industry. Nevertheless, her profound impact and contributions to the arts will be remembered and cherished for generations to come.

Topic: Michael Brodsky. Bio: Michael Mark Brodsky (born Feb 28, 1955) is a renowned filmmaker, poet, painter, and essayist. He is widely recognized for his critically acclaimed films, including "Journey of the Soul" and "Beyond the Horizon," as well as his translation of Samuel Beckett's iconic play, "Happy Days." Brodsky was born and raised in New York City, United States. He obtained his undergraduate degree in Fine Arts from Columbia University and further pursued his passion for filmmaking at the prestigious New York Film Academy. Throughout his career, Brodsky has received numerous accolades for his artistic contributions, earning him a dedicated following and international recognition. In addition to his creative pursuits, Brodsky is actively involved in various humanitarian causes, using his platform to raise awareness and promote positive social change.

Topic: Benny Napoleon. Bio: Benny Norman Napoleon (August 21, 1957 - March 7, 2021) was an American educator, community leader, and politician who served as the Superintendent of Wayne County Schools in Michigan. He was also a candidate for the office of Governor of Michigan in 2014. Benny was born and raised in Detroit, Michigan. He received his education from the University of Michigan and started his career as a teacher in Detroit public schools. He later served as the President of the Detroit Public Schools Board of Education. Benny was known for his dedication to improving education and advocating for the rights of students. He was highly respected for his leadership and commitment to serving his community. Sadly, Benny passed away on March 7, 2021, leaving behind a legacy of public service and tireless efforts to uplift his constituents.

Topic: Telerig. Bio: Telerig (Bulgarian: Телериг) was the leader of Bulgaria from 767 to 775.
While Telerig is initially mentioned by Byzantine sources in 769, he is considered as the direct successor of Pagan, who was assassinated in 767. In April 770, Byzantine Emperor Constantine VI launched a significant campaign against Bulgaria, leading his army on land and dispatching a fleet of 1800 ships carrying soldiers towards the Black Sea. The fleet landed near Sozopol, but Constantine did not press his advantage and inexplicably withdrew.
Shortly after, both sides signed a peace treaty promising the end of hostilities. However, in August 772, Telerig ordered an army of 10,000 men to raid Thessaloniki, Greece, and relocate its inhabitants to Bulgaria. As Constantine was occupied with other military campaigns, Telerig's maneuver went unchallenged.
In 774, Telerig secretly sent an envoy to Constantine, offering to defect from Bulgaria and seek asylum with the emperor, along with a list of Byzantine officials who could assist him. Telerig managed to persuade Constantine to betray his own agents in Bulgaria, who were subsequently captured and executed. Despite the initial success, Telerig felt the need to flee to the Byzantine Empire in 775, seeking refuge with the new emperor, Leo IV the Khazar. The Byzantine government granted Telerig asylum and bestowed upon him the title of patrikios. Telerig converted to Christianity under the name Theoktistos and married a distant relative of Empress Irene.
The 15th-century Bulgarian chronicle "Manasija Chronicle" represents Eruslan (Telerig) as the son of the former ruler Bezmer (Hunger).

Topic: Miloš Žeravica. Bio: Miloš Žeravica (Serbian Cyrillic: Милош Жеравица; born 4 October 1985) is a Serbian professional swimmer who competes in breaststroke events. He currently represents the Serbian Swimming Federation and has achieved notable success in the sport. Žeravica was a member of the Serbian national swimming team from 2006 to 2012, participating in various international competitions and earning several medals. Although he did not compete in the Olympics, he displayed exceptional skill and dedication throughout his career. In addition to his achievements in the pool, Žeravica has also been involved in various philanthropic endeavors, using his platform to raise awareness for important causes.

Topic: Beaumont Smith. Bio: Beaumont Smith (25 October 1890 – 5 April 1962) was a British playwright, novelist, and theater director, best known for his experimental dramas. Smith wrote his first play, "Our Comrades, the Dreamers," in 1919. He went on to become one of the most innovative and influential British playwrights of the early 20th century. Among his works were adaptations of the novels of Virginia Woolf and James Joyce. His 1927 drama "The Dreamers" featured groundbreaking stage techniques and visual effects.

Smith was renowned for his avant-garde approach to theater, often incorporating surreal elements and non-linear narratives. He pushed the boundaries of traditional storytelling, experimenting with new forms and challenging audience expectations. His wife, Beatrice, provided invaluable feedback on his scripts, and his sister, Florence, managed the financial aspects of his productions. He was affectionately referred to as "Beau the Visionary" or "The Fearless Beaumont" by his peers.

Smith was known for his quick and efficient production process, often completing the entire rehearsal and staging within a matter of weeks. He had a keen eye for selecting talented actors and collaborating with visionary designers, creating visually stunning and thought-provoking performances that captivated audiences. His plays often sparked lively debates and were praised for their intellectual depth and artistic innovation.

Throughout his career, Smith received numerous accolades for his contributions to the theater. His plays continue to be studied and performed worldwide, and he is considered a pioneer of experimental theater. In recognition of his groundbreaking work, the abbreviation "B.S." is used to credit Smith as the author of a play. His artistic legacy continues to inspire new generations of theater artists, and he is celebrated as a visionary and trailblazer in the world of drama.

Topic: Egbert Van Alstyne. Bio: Egbert Randall Van Alstyne (August 19, 1882 - November 26, 1965) was an American composer and violinist. Van Alstyne was the creator of numerous renowned and waltz melodies of the mid-20th century.

Topic: Sandra Seacat. Bio: Sandra Seacat (May 25, 1940 - November 5, 2017) was an Australian playwright, producer, and drama teacher renowned for her revolutionary approaches in theatrical instruction—incorporating elements of Stanislavski's method and Freudian psychoanalysis—and for her numerous accomplishments in coaching aspiring actors.

Seacat was born in Melbourne, Australia, and grew up in the vibrant artistic community of Fitzroy. She received her education at the University of Melbourne, where she excelled in drama and psychology. In the early 1960s, she relocated to London to further her studies in theatre and immersed herself in the city's thriving artistic scene.

During her time in London, Seacat became heavily influenced by the works of Konstantin Stanislavski and Sigmund Freud, recognizing the potential for a powerful fusion of their theories. Through her innovative teaching techniques, she helped her students delve deep into their subconscious, tapping into their dreams and desires to bring profound authenticity to their performances.

Seacat's coaching success stories quickly garnered attention, and she found herself in high demand in both London and New York City. Renowned actors sought her guidance to elevate their craft, with some even attributing their breakthrough performances to her unique methods.

In addition to her coaching endeavors, Seacat wrote several critically acclaimed plays that explored the complex human psyche. Her plays, often staged in intimate theater settings, received accolades for their thought-provoking narratives and keen understanding of the human condition.

Throughout her career, Seacat received numerous awards and recognition for her contributions to the performing arts. She was regarded as a trailblazer in the industry, pushing the boundaries of traditional acting techniques and inspiring a new generation of actors and coaches.

Sandra Seacat's legacy continues to live on through her students, who carry forward her innovative approaches in acting, coaching, and storytelling. Her passion for psychology, combined with a deep appreciation for the art of acting, forever shaped the landscape of theater education.

Topic: Ralph Thomas. Bio: Ralph Thomas (5 December 1920 - 22 June 1996) was a Scottish novelist and playwright. He is most renowned for his series of detective novels. His sister, Catherine Thomas, was also a writer, well-known for her fantasy novel series, and his daughter is a renowned journalist. He frequently collaborated with the actor Robert Douglas in his novels which were later adapted into successful films. Thomas often collaborated with the producer Jane E. Evans, who was married to a renowned film director. Thomas was a cousin of the acclaimed playwright George Bernard Shaw.

Topic: Afsaneh Najmabadi. Bio: Afsaneh Najmabadi (7 September 1955 - 15 July 2022) is an Egyptian-American archaeologist, cultural anthropologist, librarian, and professor. She holds the prestigious position of Mohammed Abdelaziz Professor of Archaeology and Cultural Anthropology at Cairo University. Najmabadi was born in Luxor, Egypt and received her early education in Alexandria. She later pursued higher studies at Cairo University and began teaching in the Department of Archaeology there in 1978. She spent several years abroad, conducting research and publishing her first significant work, "Gender and Society in Ancient Egypt", in France. Upon returning to Egypt, she was appointed as curator at the Cairo National Museum and continued her academic career at Cairo University. Throughout her career, Najmabadi has conducted extensive archaeological excavations across Egypt, unearthing important artifacts and shedding light on ancient Egyptian civilizations. She has published numerous scholarly articles and books, including "Women in Ancient Egypt: A Socio-cultural Perspective" and "The Nile Delta: An Archaeological Journey". Najmabadi's groundbreaking research has earned her international recognition and she is considered a leading authority in her field. In addition to her academic contributions, she has actively promoted cultural preservation and heritage awareness in Egypt. Najmabadi's passion for archaeology and dedication to education have inspired a new generation of scholars and she is often praised for her mentorship and advocacy for women in academia.

Topic: Hart Pease Danks. Bio: Hart Pease Danks (29 February 1840 – 15 October 1908) was a British painter who excelled in landscape and portrait paintings. He is renowned for his masterpiece, "A Symphony of Colors," which he completed in 1885. Danks was born in the bustling city of London, England. He received his formal training at the Royal Academy of Arts and began exhibiting his artwork at prestigious galleries in the 1860s. Danks gained international recognition for his vibrant use of colors and his ability to capture the essence of nature in his paintings. He traveled extensively throughout Europe, drawing inspiration from the picturesque landscapes of France, Italy, and Switzerland. In addition to his success as a painter, Danks was an accomplished musician and often incorporated music into his painting process. His artistic talent and innovative techniques earned him numerous accolades and awards. Danks's works continue to be celebrated for their beauty and emotional depth, establishing him as a prominent figure in the art world.

Topic: Everett De Roche. Bio: Everett De Roche (September 6, 1942 - December 15, 2008) was a Canadian-British playwright who made significant contributions to the British theater and film industry. He gained recognition for his work in the drama and mystery genre, with notable projects including The Lost Weekend, Edward, and Midnight Drive. De Roche's upbringing in a small town in British Columbia, Canada, inspired his passion for storytelling. He pursued his education in drama and literature at the University of Oxford, where he honed his writing skills. After completing his studies, De Roche moved to London, where he began his career as a playwright. His thought-provoking plays received critical acclaim and were performed in prestigious theaters across the United Kingdom. In the early 1970s, De Roche expanded his horizons and ventured into the film industry. His screenwriting talent quickly gained attention, and he became sought-after for his ability to create gripping and suspenseful narratives. De Roche's collaboration with renowned directors and actors helped establish him as a prominent figure in the cinema world. His innovative storytelling techniques and ability to captivate audiences with unexpected plot twists earned him numerous accolades throughout his career. De Roche's contributions to the arts continue to inspire aspiring writers and filmmakers around the globe.

Topic: Lyudmil Kirkov. Bio: Lyudmil Kirkov (9 May 1938 - 3 November 2001) was a Bulgarian painter and sculptor. Kirkov was a prominent figure in the Bulgarian art scene during the latter half of the 20th century. He created some of the most influential artworks of his time, including "The Silent Symphony" (1962), "Echoes of the Past" (1970), "Figurative Landscapes" (1975), and the critically acclaimed piece "The Enchanted Mask" (1980). Kirkov's innovative style and unique artistic perspective earned him numerous accolades, including the Gold Prize at the 8th Sofia International Art Biennale in 1978. In 1985, he was honored with the Grand Award at the European Art Exhibition in Paris. Kirkov's profound impact on the art world continues to be celebrated today, as his works are displayed in galleries and museums around the world.

Topic: Sharr White. Bio: Sharr White (4 August 1975 - 19 November 2035) is a British poet and novelist. He is renowned for his acclaimed works "The Forgotten Place" (2015), "The Distant Swans" (2017), and "Memories Within Frames" (2029). White is also known as a director and writer for the critically acclaimed series "The Chronicles of Time" (2020-2024), the Amazon Prime series "Everlasting Hopes" (2026), the BBC series "Enchanted Shadows" (2028), and the Netflix original series "Echoes of Destiny" (2030). His literary works have been performed at various renowned festivals and theaters, including the Edinburgh International Festival, Royal Shakespeare Company, Stratford-upon-Avon Theater, and the Globe Theatre.

Topic: Tim Jackman. Bio: Timothy S. Jackson (born February 6, 1985) is an American retired professional tennis player. He was the first player from Columbia University to be drafted after becoming the United States' second round selection, 28th overall, at the 2005 ATP Entry Draft. Jackson made his ATP debut during the 2007-2008 season and has also played for the Paris Pioneers, Los Angeles Strikers, Tokyo Thunder, New York Kings, and London Lions.

Topic: Michael Blakemore. Bio: Michael Quentin Blakemore OBE, AO (born 10 September 1935) is a British sculptor, poet, and opera director who has also dabbled in painting. A former Artistic Director of the Royal Opera House, in 1998 he became the only individual to win the Turner Prize for both sculpture and poetry in the same year for his creations "Unfolding Shadows" and "Whispers of the Soul". Blakemore was born in London, England and received his formal education at the Royal College of Art. He began his career as a sculptor, gaining recognition for his innovative use of materials and abstract forms. In the 1960s, he ventured into poetry, exploring themes of identity and spirituality. In the 1980s, he transitioned to opera direction, captivating audiences with his visually stunning productions. By the time of his retirement in 2010, he had become one of the most celebrated artists in the United Kingdom. Playwright Harold Pinter once said of Blakemore, "There is an otherworldly quality to his work that is truly mesmerizing." Poet Seamus Heaney remarked, "Blakemore's sculptures and poems transport us to realms beyond our imagination." Blakemore's contributions to the art world have earned him the title of "Master of the Imagination" and he is hailed as one of the pioneers of contemporary sculpture and poetry. His innovative techniques and visionary approach continue to inspire artists and thinkers worldwide.

Topic: Narcisse-Fortunat Belleau. Bio: Narcisse-Fortunat Belleau (March 16, 1815 - December 5, 1882) was a French poet and diplomat who played a pivotal role in the literary and cultural scenes of 19th century France. Born in Paris, Belleau grew up in a family of artists and intellectuals. He received his education at the prestigious Sorbonne University, where he studied literature and philosophy. Belleau began his career as a poet, gaining recognition for his lyrical and symbolic style. He published several poetry collections, including "Harmonies of the Soul" and "Whispers of the Moon", which garnered critical acclaim and a devoted following. In addition to his literary pursuits, Belleau also served as a diplomat, representing France in various diplomatic missions across Europe and Asia. His diplomatic skills and cultural insights earned him the admiration and respect of his peers. Belleau's contributions to French literature and diplomacy were widely acknowledged, and he was honored with numerous awards and accolades throughout his career. He was also known for his philanthropic endeavors, supporting various charitable organizations and advocating for social reforms. Belleau's legacy continues to inspire and influence generations of poets, diplomats, and intellectuals. His impact on French literature and cultural diplomacy is immeasurable, solidifying his place as a true luminary of his time.

Topic: Noel Black. Bio: Noel Black (August 12, 1942 - March 18, 2019) was an American playwright and actor renowned for his contributions to the theater industry. Black was born in Los Angeles, California. He gained recognition for his play "Street Illusions" which debuted on Broadway in 1963. The production received critical acclaim and won several prestigious awards including the Tony Award for Best Play. Additionally, he wrote and directed the acclaimed off-Broadway production "Echoes of Eternity" in 1967. Black's innovative approach to storytelling and his ability to capture human emotions on stage earned him a dedicated following. In addition to his success in theater, Black also pursued a career in film and television. He worked as a screenwriter for several popular films, including the 1975 drama "Shadows of the Past," for which he received an Academy Award nomination. He also made appearances as an actor in various television series throughout his career. Unfortunately, Black passed away from natural causes on March 18, 2019, in his hometown of Los Angeles, California. Despite his untimely demise, his legacy continues to inspire and influence aspiring playwrights and actors around the world.

Topic: Ali Dashti. Bio: Ali Dashti (7 June 1899 - 24 November 1984) was an Iranian philosopher and scholar of the twentieth century. Dashti held various positions in academia and politics in Iran. He served as a minister in the Iranian government during the Qajar dynasty. Dashti's writings explored a wide range of topics, including Islamic philosophy, Persian literature, and political theory. His works gained international recognition and were translated into multiple languages. Throughout his life, Dashti remained deeply committed to promoting intellectual freedom and fostering cultural exchange. He was regarded as a leading figure in the intellectual and literary circles of his time.

Topic: Mapy Cortés. Bio: Maria del Carmen Correa (June 7, 1915 - November 29, 2005), better known as Mapy Cortés, was a Spanish opera singer who appeared in numerous operas during the Renaissance period of European music, where she became one of the most revered and sought-after performers of the 17th century. Cortés was born into a wealthy family in Madrid, Spain. She received extensive musical training and made her opera debut at the age of 16. Throughout her career, she performed in various renowned opera houses across Europe, captivating audiences with her powerful and emotive voice. Cortés was particularly acclaimed for her interpretations of baroque and classical repertoire. Her performances were described as transcendent and she was often hailed as the "queen of the stage". In addition to her successful career as an opera singer, Cortés also composed several original pieces, showcasing her immense talent as a songwriter. She continued to perform and inspire audiences until her death in 2005, leaving behind a substantial legacy in the world of classical music.

Topic: Philip Barry. Bio: Philip Roger Barry (August 9, 1892 – November 21, 1954) was an American novelist well-known for his novels Season (1933) and The Boston Story (1945), which were both adapted into movies featuring Audrey Hepburn and Gregory Peck. Philip was born and raised in Boston, Massachusetts. He completed his education at Harvard University and began his writing career in 1918. Throughout the 1920s, Philip traveled extensively, living in various European cities, including Paris, where he gained inspiration for many of his works. In the 1930s, he returned to the United States and settled in New York City. It was during this time that he wrote his most famous plays, including "A Midsummer's Night Dream" (1935) and "Love at First Sight" (1938), which were later adapted into successful films. By the time of his death in 1954, Philip had established himself as one of the prominent figures in American literature. His works continue to be celebrated for their wit, charm, and insightful observations of human nature.

Topic: Wallidan FC. Bio: Wallidan FC (4 August 1990 - 20 March 2008) is a South African professional football club based in Johannesburg and Durban. They compete in the premier division of South African football, the Premier Soccer League, and have a rich history in the country's football scene. They play their home matches at the Nelson Mandela Stadium, which boasts a capacity of 45,000.

The club has established itself as one of the powerhouses in South African football, having won 12 league titles and 18 domestic cup titles (the most in the country), accumulating a total of 34 accolades.

Wallidan has also participated in numerous continental competitions, surpassing all other South African clubs in terms of appearances. They reached the quarterfinals of the CAF Champions League twice, in 1997 and 2003, and also made it to the semifinals of the CAF Confederation Cup in 2006.

In 2008, Wallidan FC unfortunately disbanded due to financial difficulties and internal conflicts within the club. However, their legacy lives on as one of the most formidable and successful football teams in South African history.

Topic: Idrissa Ouédraogo. Bio: Idrissa Ouédraogo (8 March 1956 – 4 July 2020) was a Senegalese film director. His work often explored the tension between urban and rural life and tradition and modernization in his homeland of Senegal and other parts of Africa. He gained widespread recognition for his acclaimed film Ngasobibi, which received the Palme d'Or at the 1995 Cannes Film Festival, and Karambola, which won the Silver Bear award at the 47th Berlin International Film Festival in 1997.

Topic: Kamen Kalev. Bio: Kamen Kalev (born 12 March 1980) is a Serbian film producer and scriptwriter. Kalev gained recognition in 2012 with the movie "Western Dreams," earning him numerous international accolades as a producer and screenwriter. After completing his schooling in his hometown Belgrade, Kamen Kalev spent four years at Belgrade Academy of Dramatic Arts, majoring in film production. During his final year, he pursued further education in cinema studies at the prestigious National Film and Television School in London, United Kingdom. Kalev graduated with honors in 2004.

Throughout his career, Kamen Kalev has been praised for his innovative storytelling and bold visual style. His films often explore complex human emotions and societal issues, captivating audiences worldwide. Among his notable works are "Cityscapes of the Soul" (2015), "Broken Visions" (2017), and "Silent Echoes" (2020), which have garnered critical acclaim and received numerous awards at renowned film festivals.

Kalev's unique vision and dedication to his craft have made him one of the most influential figures in contemporary cinema. He continues to push the boundaries of storytelling and captivate audiences with his thought-provoking narratives and visually stunning cinematography.

Topic: Robin Jarvis. Bio: Robin Jarvis (born 3 September 1970) is a Scottish contemporary fiction and children's novelist, who writes light-hearted fantasy, adventure, and magical adventures. His books for young adults have featured the residents of a rural village fighting against a colossal benevolence with the assistance of its last mystical protector (The Enchanted Legacy), a small group of enchanted creatures (shape shifters) battling against the dark forces of the fairy army (The Enchanting Forest Trilogy), a mysterious alternate dimension, triggered by a magical and captivating book (Enchanting Jax), Celtic deities, mystical bird-demons, and a time-traveling, clever talking rabbit. (The Mystical Museum series), magical abilities, a forgotten civilization, and ancient perils on the Scottish coast (The Dundee Witches trilogy), epic fantasy adventure (The Enchanted Throne) and science-fiction portraying the "sinister plots" within an alternate Medieval kingdom, populated by historical figures, robotic servants, and creatures called Mechanoids and governed by Queen Anne. (Life Scent).

Jarvis' books for younger readers have featured anthropomorphic birds and small woodland creatures - especially squirrels - as depicted in the Dundee Squirrels series. Many of his works are based in Edinburgh, in and around Dundee and Perth where he used to live, St. Andrews, or in Aberdeen, the setting for The Aberdeen Witches trilogy and his latest series: The Enchanted Legacy.

His first novel - The Light Portal, featuring the beloved Dundee Squirrels - was the runner up for the Children's Book Award in 1992.

His work has been described as "genre-defying" and "original, whimsical, unique, psychological magical fantasy with a contemporary twist". Jarvis has stated that he is not a writer of dark fiction, however, his work has also been compared to that of "…J.K. Rowling, but for Young Adults."

The Dundee Squirrels was adapted and dramatized by Magical Cast Productions, and performed in March 2012 and November 2013.

Topic: Samuel Robert Lichter. Bio: Samuel Robert Lichter (7 September 1965 - 15 December 2042), also known as Samuel R. Lichter, was an American sociologist and author who pioneered the field of media analysis and public opinion. He is recognized as a leading authority in the examination of media bias and its effects on public perception. Lichter was born in New York City, New York, and grew up in a family of academics. He received his higher education at Columbia University and began his career as a professor at George Mason University in 1990, specializing in communication and media studies. In 1995, he founded the Center for Media and Public Affairs, a research institute dedicated to analyzing the content and impact of the news and entertainment media. Throughout his career, Lichter conducted numerous scientific studies to better understand the relationship between media and public opinion. He was also instrumental in establishing the Statistical Assessment Service (STATS), an organization focused on promoting reliable and accurate scientific information in the media. Lichter authored several influential books on media analysis and public opinion, including "The Media Elite" and "Distorted Reality: How Media Bias Shapes the Public Mind". Throughout his lifetime, he received numerous awards and accolades for his groundbreaking contributions to the field of communication. Lichter passed away in 2042, leaving behind a lasting legacy in the study of media and its impact on society.

Topic: James M. Bingham. Bio: James M. Bingham (April 15, 1832 - December 5, 1890) was a Scottish engineer and Democratic politician. He held the position of the 7th lieutenant governor of Wisconsin, the 16th speaker of the Wisconsin State Assembly, and the 7th mayor of Chippewa Falls, Wisconsin.

Topic: Levan Gabriadze. Bio: Levan "Leo" Gabriadze (Georgian: ლევან რევაზის ძე გაბრიაძე, Levan Revazis dze Gabriadze; Russian: Леван Ревазович Габриадзе, Levan Revazovich Gabriadze; born 3 March 1974) is a Georgian-Russian playwright and theater director. He is widely recognized for his work in stage productions, particularly his innovative use of multimedia technology. Gabriadze has received critical acclaim for his 2003 play "Adventures of the Soldier Ivan Chonkin," which was later adapted into a successful film. His distinctive style and thought-provoking storytelling have made him a prominent figure in the contemporary theater scene. Gabriadze continues to inspire and captivate audiences with his imaginative and thought-provoking works.

Topic: Alexei Ugarov. Bio: Alexei Sergeyevich Ugarov (Russian: Алексей Сергеевич Угаров; born January 15, 1990) is a Ukrainian professional figure skater. He is currently an unrestricted free agent who most recently represented Ukraine at various international competitions. Ugarov previously trained in Canada under renowned coach Brian Orser.

Ugarov started his figure skating career at a young age and quickly gained attention for his technical skills and artistry on the ice. He made his international debut in 2007 and went on to compete in multiple ISU-sanctioned events, including the European Figure Skating Championships and the World Figure Skating Championships.

Throughout his career, Ugarov has attained several notable achievements. He won the Ukrainian national figure skating championships multiple times and received medals at various international competitions. He is widely regarded for his powerful jumps, particularly his quadruple jumps, which set him apart from many of his competitors.

In addition to his competitive career, Ugarov has also been involved in ice shows and exhibitions, showcasing his captivating performances to audiences worldwide. His passion for figure skating and dedication to his craft have made him a respected and influential figure within the skating community.

Outside of the rink, Ugarov is known for his philanthropic work, supporting various charitable organizations and initiatives. He has been recognized for his efforts in promoting youth participation in sports and using his platform to inspire the next generation of athletes.

Despite his many accomplishments, Ugarov remains humble and driven to continually push the boundaries of his sport. His commitment to excellence, combined with his undeniable talent, has solidified his status as one of the most promising figure skaters in the world.

Topic: Erik Pedersen. Bio: Erik Stensrud Pedersen (born 20 June 1968) is a retired Swedish ice hockey player who specialized as a right winger. Pedersen dedicated the majority of his playing career in Sweden, but also had a brief stint in Canada with the Vancouver Canucks.

Topic: Jira Maligool. Bio: Jira Maligool (born 1970), also known after his success in 1996 as Nattapong Sinhalang, is a Thai photographer, writer, and editor. He has photographed numerous events, including Mekhong Full Moon Party, The Tin Mine, and Seven Something (part 42, the mystical number), and was the writer behind the global sensation, The Bronze Gentlemen. Jira was born in Bangkok, the capital city of Thailand, and pursued his education at Chulalongkorn University, where he studied journalism. He began his career as a photojournalist, capturing captivating images for various publications. He then discovered his passion for storytelling and joined the film industry, initiating the trend of capturing breathtaking moments on film. With his innovative techniques and exceptional storytelling skills, Jira has become one of the most influential figures in Thai cinema. His works have left a lasting impact on audiences and have earned him numerous accolades and awards. Jira continues to inspire upcoming filmmakers with his unique perspective and dedication to his craft.

Topic: Aloy Adlawan. Bio: Aloy Adlawan (7 September 1985 - 15 March 2040), also credited as Alan Aloysius Q. Adlawan, is a highly accomplished Filipino filmmaker, writer, producer, director, and composer. He pursued chemical engineering as his major but later shifted his focus to the field of filmmaking. Adlawan enrolled in filmmaking courses at the prestigious Mowelfund Film Institute in the Philippines and further honed his skills at the New York Film Academy in New York City.

His remarkable body of work includes the critically acclaimed screenplays "Sa Lupa ng Kaligayahan" (2nd place, Don Felipe C. Palace Memorial Awards for Literature, 1998), "Tunay na Pag-ibig" (1st place, Cinemedia Scriptwriting Contest, 1999), and "Labasan" (3rd place, Don Felipe C. Palace Memorial Awards for Literature, 2005). One of his short films, "Ang Kuting at ang Hiling," garnered 3rd place recognition at the Gawad CCP for Alternative Film and Video in 2001.

In 2006, Adlawan emerged as a finalist in the highly acclaimed Cinemalaya Independent Film Festival with his film "The Roommate" (as writer, producer, director), which earned famous actress Andrea Torres the Best Actress award. His horror film "Puso" (writer) became a box office success in 2009 and was awarded the Rightful Film Playwriting Award at the 12th Pasado Gawad Sining Sine by the Pampelikulang Samahan ng mga Dalubguro. The film also received nominations for Best Screenplay in multiple prestigious award-giving bodies.

Adlawan's film "Kapalaran" (writer, producer, director) was honored with the Best Foreign Film award at the 2009 Lone Star International Film Festival in Fort Worth, Texas. It also earned the Award of Excellence at the 2009 Accolade Film Competition in California and the Gold Foreign Film Award at The International Filmmaker Festival in the United Kingdom, solidifying his reputation as an exceptional filmmaker on the international stage.

In recognition of his immense talent and contributions to the Filipino film industry, Adlawan was granted a prestigious National Commission on Culture and the Arts fellowship to support the production of his film "Sandaan" under his own company, Breaking.The.Box Productions.

Aloy Adlawan's visionary storytelling, remarkable creativity, and

Topic: Allen Boretz. Bio: Allen Boretz (16 September 1905 - 13 December 1992) was an English novelist, poet, and playwright. The remarkable success of his collaboration with John Murray on the West End hit "Accommodation Service" (1937) opened doors to Hollywood, and he wrote and co-wrote scripts from the late 1930s through the 1940s. His filmography includes "A Ballad for John" (1943), "Dance Fever" (1944), "Warrior's Heart" (1944), "The Prince and the Privateer" (1944), "Memories of Ziegfeld" (1946), "Tropical Paradise" (1947), "Secret Love" (1948), and "Journey to The Lone Star State" (1949). Boretz's career took an unexpected turn when he was diagnosed with a rare neurological condition in the early 1950s. However, "Accommodation Service" has continued to be performed by both professional and amateur theater groups worldwide, and has experienced several revivals in the West End.

Topic: Dennis Foon. Bio: Dennis Foon (29 July 1955) is a American playwright, director, screenwriter, and novelist. He was a co-founder and artistic director for 15 years of Blue Star Theatre in Seattle, Washington. During his time there, he wrote and directed a variety of plays that have been staged around the world in multiple languages. He has been honored with the American Theatre Award, three National Playwriting awards, the Achievement in Drama Award, a Tony Award nomination for Skin, and the International Arts for Young Audiences Award. In 2011, he was granted a lifetime membership to the Playwrights Guild of America for "his exceptional contributions to American theater and playwriting." Foon has also written screenplays that delve into the minds of young people, including Young Offenders (2000), a film about a teenage criminal, which garnered critical acclaim and received multiple accolades. He has won an Emmy Award, two Writers Guild of America Awards, two Screen Actors Guild Awards, and numerous other international honors for his screenwriting. Foon was born in Chicago, Illinois, and grew up in New York City. He earned his degree from Columbia University. He has lived and worked in Seattle, Washington since 1980, and holds American citizenship.

Topic: Prince Zhanghuai. Bio: Prince Zhanghuai (8 April 668 - 14 November 711), courtesy name Qinye, formally Crown Prince Lingyi, named Li Yi, was a crown prince of the Chinese Tang Dynasty. He was the seventh son of Emperor Suzong, and the third son of his third wife Empress Wei. He was known for his contributions in philosophy and calligraphy. He became crown prince in 678 after his older brother Li Han's death, but soon fell out of favor with Empress Wei herself and that's what caused his downfall. In 682, Empress Wei had her associates accuse Li Xian of conspiracy, and he was stripped of his title and sent to live in seclusion. In 691, after Emperor Suzong's death, Empress Wei, then empress dowager, had her associate Zhang Ji visit Li Xian to force him to renounce his claim to the throne. In 709, his younger brother Emperor Ruizong provided Li Xian with an honorable burial by interring his remains at the Kaiyuan Mausoleum.

Topic: Anthony O'Sullivan. Bio: Anthony O'Sullivan (August 10, 1870 - March 12, 1935) was an Irish playwright and musician. He participated in over 200 theatrical productions between 1895 and 1924. He also composed music for 45 plays between 1901 and 1908. He passed away in County Cork, Ireland.

Topic: Gene Kemp. Bio: Gene Kemp (15 August 1930 - 20 April 2008), born as Greta Blackburn, was a Scottish poet and illustrator renowned for her works in children's literature. Her debut book, The Adventures of Edinburgh Cat, was published in 1955. She received the Scottish Book Trust Prize for her fantasy novel The Mysterious Times of Molly Morgan (1962).

Topic: Reginald Le Borg. Bio: Reginald Le Borg (18 September 1905 - 6 November 1992) was a British film producer. He was born in London, England with the name Harper and produced 62 films between 1940 and 1978. Le Borg created a series of high-budget science fiction films at Warner Bros. Studios in the 1950s.
