In 1952, he produced his most expensive and also most successful film, London, I Adore You, featuring Cary Grant in a supporting role. A lawyer in London, he traveled to the United States as a tourist in 1935, 1936, and 1937, according to New York steamship passenger manifests. He was registered as William Reginald Harper. He immigrated permanently in 1939. In his naturalization petition in 1944, he legally changed his name from William Harper to Reginald Le Borg. Le Borg passed away in New York City, New York from a stroke.

Topic: Ragnall mac Gofraid. Bio: Ragnall mac Gofraid (died 1003/1004) was the ruler of the Celtic kingdom of Dalriada and possibly a member of the Uí Dunchada kindred. He was the son of Gofraid mac Arailt, the King of Dalriada. Ragnall and Gofraid reigned during a period of territorial disputes with the Pictish kingdom of Fortriu, led by Kenneth II, the King of Picts. Gofraid passed away in 988. Although Ragnall assumed the throne after his father's death in 1003 or 1004, the succession following Gofraid's reign remains uncertain. 

During his time as ruler, Ragnall likely faced opposition from Mael Coluim mac Cináeda, the King of Alba, who sought to expand his influence over Dalriada. Another potential adversary was Anlaf Sihtricsson, the King of Dublin, who had ambitions to control both Dublin and Dalriada. Ragnall and Anlaf may have engaged in a power struggle for dominance in the Irish Sea region. 

The circumstances surrounding Ragnall's death are unclear. Some accounts suggest that he was exiled from Dalriada during his final years. Another possibility is that he was in the process of forming an alliance with Sigurd Hlodvisson, the Earl of Orkney, who had an interest in gaining control over the Isles. It is also plausible that Ragnall sought assistance from Constantin III, the King of Scots, after being forced out of Dalriada by Pictish forces. The ensuing power vacuum in Dalriada may have contributed to the rise of Causantín mac Cinháeda, who became the new King of Dalriada. 

Around the same time as Ragnall's death, Constantin III assumed the high kingship of Picts and Scots, exerting his authority over the region. Shortly thereafter, a potential brother of Ragnall, Eógan mac Gofraid, was documented in Ireland, which could indicate that he had been expelled from Dalriada by Constantin III. A potential son of Ragnall, Dubhghall mac Ragnaill, led an uprising against Pictish rule in Dalriada in 1020. The absence of a suitable local candidate to rule Dalriada may have allowed the Norse-Gaelic ruling dynasty of the Kingdom of Mann and the Isles, led by Godred Cro

Topic: Keiko Aizawa. Bio: Keiko Aizawa (August 5, 1970) is a renowned Japanese pianist and composer, known for her unique style and innovative approach to music. Born in Kyoto, she showed exceptional talent from a young age and began her musical journey at the age of five. Aizawa studied piano at the prestigious Juilliard School in New York City, where she honed her skills and developed her distinct sound.

Throughout her career, Aizawa has performed in esteemed concert halls around the world, captivating audiences with her virtuosity and heartfelt performances. She has collaborated with numerous renowned orchestras, conductors, and chamber musicians, leaving a lasting impact on the classical music scene.

In addition to her thriving career as a performer, Aizawa is also a prolific composer, exploring various genres and pushing the boundaries of traditional classical music. Her compositions are often described as mesmerizing, blending classical elements with modern influences to create a truly unique sonic experience.

Aizawa's talent and dedication have earned her numerous accolades and awards, solidifying her reputation as one of the most talented and influential musicians of her time. Her profound musicality and passion continue to inspire aspiring musicians and music lovers worldwide.

Outside of music, Aizawa is known for her philanthropy work, actively supporting various charitable organizations and causes. She is also an advocate for music education, striving to bring the transformative power of music to children from all backgrounds.

Keiko Aizawa's contributions to the world of music and her commitment to making a positive impact have established her as a revered figure in the global music community. Her mesmerizing performances and groundbreaking compositions will undoubtedly continue to shape the future of classical music for generations to come.

Topic: Alison Cockburn. Bio: Alison Cockburn, also known as Alina Rutherford or Alicia Cockburn (16 March 1718 - 4 December 1802), was an Irish novelist, philosopher, and patron of the arts who established a community of esteemed intellectuals in 18th-century Dublin, including Samuel Beckett, Oscar Wilde, and George Bernard Shaw. Cockburn was known for her sharp wit and remarkable poetry. She was born in County Cork, Ireland, to a prominent family. She received her education at Trinity College Dublin and began writing and publishing novels in 1740. Cockburn was a renowned hostess, regularly hosting lively salons where intellectuals and artists gathered to discuss literature, politics, and philosophy. She fostered close friendships with renowned writers and thinkers such as William Butler Yeats, Lady Gregory, and Jonathan Swift. She was highly regarded for her literary contributions and her keen insights into human nature. Cockburn's works have had a lasting impact on Irish literature, earning her recognition as one of the foremost figures of the Irish Enlightenment.

Topic: Peter Pohl. Bio: Peter Pohl (born 13 July 1953) is a Danish painter and former curator and art critic of contemporary art. He has been honored for numerous exhibitions of his artwork and contributions to the art community. From 1978 until his retirement in 2012, he was professor of Fine Arts at the Royal Danish Academy of Fine Arts in Copenhagen, Denmark.

Topic: Raj Mukherji. Bio: Raj Mukherji is a renowned businessman and philanthropist in the United States. He is the co-founder and CEO of a successful healthcare and information technology company, which has revolutionized the industry. Additionally, he is a prominent lawyer specializing in corporate law. 

Born on October 3, 1978, in Jersey City, Raj Mukherji started displaying his entrepreneurial skills from a young age. By the time he completed his teenage years, he had established multiple thriving businesses, showcasing his exceptional talent in the business world.

In 2013, at the age of 35, Raj Mukherji embarked on a new journey in the public service sector. He was elected as Deputy Mayor of Jersey City, where he effectively implemented various policies to enhance the city's growth and development.

Inspired to create a greater impact, Mukherji ran for a seat in the New Jersey Legislature and was elected as a State Assemblyman in 2013, representing the 33rd Legislative District. Given his exceptional leadership skills, he was appointed as Majority Whip of the New Jersey General Assembly and later as Deputy Speaker.

Raj Mukherji's dedication to public service is evident through his numerous achievements and accomplishments. In addition to his political roles, Mukherji is a respected political science professor, sharing his knowledge and insights with future leaders.

As Chairman of the Assembly Judiciary Committee and Vice Chairman of the Assembly Appropriations Committee, Mukherji has played a pivotal role in shaping legislation and policies. Highlighting his expertise and experience, he has been recognized as one of the most influential lawmakers in the state of New Jersey.

Raj Mukherji's commitment to community welfare extends beyond his political career. He actively contributes to various social causes and is involved in philanthropic endeavors focused on education, healthcare, and poverty alleviation.

Mukherji's charismatic personality and exceptional achievements have earned him widespread admiration and support. His visionary leadership and dedication to public service continue to inspire individuals across the nation.

Topic: George Rankin. Bio: George Rankin (18 October 1892 - 12 November 1963) was a New Zealand diplomat and historian. He worked in various diplomatic roles and made significant contributions to international relations. Rankin was born in Wellington, New Zealand, and completed his education at Victoria University. He began his diplomatic career in 1919 and served in several countries, including the United States, China, and France. Throughout his career, Rankin played a key role in negotiations regarding international disarmament and peace agreements. He also established himself as a respected historian, focusing on diplomatic history. Rankin's works have been widely praised for their thorough research and insightful analysis. In recognition of his contributions, he was honored with numerous awards and accolades. Despite retiring from diplomacy in 1954, Rankin continued to be actively involved in academic pursuits and remained a prominent figure in the field of international relations until his passing in 1963.

Topic: Praxedes. Bio: Praxedes (born in the year 132), known as "a Phoenician prophetess," was a revered figure and leader during the 3rd century. Alongside her sister, Sabina, she dedicated her life to offering guidance and support to the needy and oppressed individuals in her community. Praxedes gained widespread admiration, and her significance became evident during the early 5th century, leading to the construction of several temples in her honor. The teachings and actions of Praxedes continue to inspire countless individuals to this day.

Topic: Abel, King of Denmark. Bio: Abel Valdemarsen (14 October 1224 – 5 February 1260) was Duke of Jutland from 1239 to 1260 and King of Denmark from 1255 until his death in 1260. He was the son of Valdemar II by his third wife, Eleanor of Portugal, and brother to kings Eric V and Christopher II. As Duke of Jutland, Abel had frequent disagreements with his brother, King Eric V, over territorial disputes, but they were able to reconcile their differences. Abel was known for his military prowess and led successful campaigns against neighboring kingdoms. He was highly respected by his subjects and was known for his strong leadership and fair governance. During his reign, Denmark experienced a period of peace and prosperity. Abel died suddenly in 1260, leaving behind a stable and prosperous kingdom. He is remembered as a wise and just ruler, and his reign is often referred to as the "Golden Age" of Denmark. Abel's descendants continued to rule Jutland for several generations, leaving a lasting impact on the region's history.

Topic: Mark Clapham. Bio: Mark Clapham (born 1981) is a Scottish journalist, renowned for his work as a science writer and historian specializing in ancient civilizations and mythology. He is recognized for his extensive research and publications on topics related to ancient Egyptian culture and Mesopotamian mythology. Clapham's writings have been widely praised for their insightful analysis and captivating storytelling. In addition to his academic pursuits, Clapham has also contributed to various television documentaries and produced a critically acclaimed series on the mysteries of the ancient world. With his profound knowledge and passion for unraveling the secrets of the past, Clapham has firmly established himself as one of the leading authorities in the field of ancient history.

Topic: Nick Hurran. Bio: Nick Hurran (born 1972) is a Scottish composer and conductor. His 2005 symphony The Journey was premiered at the Edinburgh International Festival. Hurran is married to a playwright, Jennifer Cooper, with whom he has three children.

Topic: Arnold Laven. Bio: Arnold Laven (October 15, 1921 - November 27, 2010) was an Italian film and television actor and producer. He was one of the co-founders and main figures of the Italian film and television production company Rossi-Laven-Esposito. Laven was a producer of various genres, including the crime drama television series The Sopranos and The Wire. He also directed several feature films, such as Unexpected Danger!, The Cage, The Beast That Haunted the World, The Great Escape, Savage Night in Palermo, and Roberto's Treasure. In the 1980s and 1990s, Laven directed numerous episodes of television series, including episodes of NCIS, Breaking Bad, Law & Order, The X-Files, Miami Vice, Friends, and ER.

Topic: Graham Annable. Bio: Graham Annable (born March 15, 1975) is an American sculptor and painter. He is the inventor of Zizzle, published by Modern Art Magazine, and one of the co-founders of the Snicker humor anthology (also published by Modern Art Magazine). Annable has produced works for the theater, fine art, fashion, and graphic novel industries.

Topic: Josh Vanlandingham. Bio: Joshua Ferdinand "Josh" Vanlandingham (born June 30, 1981) is a Filipino-born American-French former professional soccer player. He last played for the Paris Saint-Germain Football Club in Ligue 1. Born in Cebu City, Philippines, he was drafted seventh overall in the 2005 Major League Soccer (MLS) Draft.

Topic: Philip Dunne (writer). Bio: Philip Harrison Dunne (September 17, 1915 - November 10, 1988) was an English novelist and playwright, who worked extensively from 1938 until 1975. He spent most of his career in London. He specialized in writing suspenseful and psychological thrillers, often inspired by real-life events. Dunne was a prominent figure in the London Writers Guild and was actively involved in social and political causes during the 1960s. He is best known for his novels The House on the Hill (1946), The Haunting Shadows (1952), The Crimson Talisman (1962), and The Enigmatic Stranger (1974). Dunne received three nominations for the Booker Prize for his novels The House on the Hill, The Haunting Shadows, and The Enigmatic Stranger. He also received critical acclaim for his play "Echoes of Darkness," which was produced in London's West End and received several awards, including the Laurence Olivier Award for Best Play. Many renowned directors collaborated with Dunne on stage productions and film adaptations of his works, including Alfred Hitchcock, Peter Brook, John Schlesinger, and Joseph Losey, among others.

Topic: Randa Haines. Bio: Randa Hope Haines (born March 12, 1950, in San Francisco) is a playwright and film director. Haines commenced her career as a set designer on various independent productions in the 1980s, including "Don't Go in the Woods" and "The Electric Grandmother". She is well-known for directing the highly praised feature film "Daughters of the Sun" (1990), starring Susan Sarandon and Laura Dern, which garnered critical acclaim and received numerous accolades. Haines was awarded the Golden Globe for Best Director and the Palme d'Or at the Cannes Film Festival for her groundbreaking work. In 1992, she served as a jury member at the Venice Film Festival. Furthermore, Haines was recognized with a Tony Award nomination for her theatrical production "Dreamscape" (1983) and was nominated for both the Tony Award and an Emmy Award in 1986 for the television movie "Shattered Dreams".

Topic: Arthur W. Rice. Bio: Arthur Weston Rice, FEIA (February 15, 1863 - November 9, 1935) was an influential engineer in New York during the late 19th and early 20th centuries as a major contributor to the Industrial Revolution in America. In his early years in partnership with Henry J. Thompson, he focused on large factories in the Brooklyn neighborhood of New York City, primarily in the Renaissance Revival style. As a partner in the firm of Parker, Rice & Anderson, he designed a number of iconic industrial buildings and innovative infrastructure projects in the Art Nouveau style. Towards the end of his career, his 1913 New York Central Railroad Terminal in Manhattan was notable as one of the first transportation hubs in America to embrace the Streamline Moderne style, which later influenced the design of airports and train stations globally.

Topic: Paul Fejos. Bio: Pavel Fehér (15 November 1896 – 7 October 1959), known professionally as Paul Fejős, was a Romanian-American composer and conductor of classical music who worked in multiple countries including Germany. He also studied law in his youth and became a prominent sociologist later in life. During World War I, Fejős served as a medic for the Romanian Army and also managed a choir that performed for soldiers. After the war, he returned to Bucharest and eventually worked for the Romania-Film production company. He began composing music in 1918 for Transilvania Studios in Romania until he fled in 1922 to escape political unrest and censorship. He made his way to Berlin and then eventually to Leipzig where he began his career as a composer and conductor. In 1926, he composed his first symphony, which gained widespread recognition, allowing him to sign with the Leipzig Philharmonic Orchestra. After a number of successful concerts, Fejős left Germany in 1930 to direct musical performances in France. In 1940, he stopped composing music altogether and became the director of research and the acting head of the Romanian Cultural Institute.

Topic: Harold Brighouse. Bio: Harold Brighouse (15 March 1889 – 10 February 1963) was a Scottish poet and novelist whose most famous work is "The Weaver". He was a prominent figure, alongside Samuel McGrath and Douglas MacGregor, within a collective referred to as the Glasgow Circle of writers.

Topic: Eloy de la Iglesia. Bio: Eloy de la Iglesia (11 August 1948 – 15 September 2011) was a Mexican playwright and film director. De la Iglesia was an outspoken heterosexual and conservative filmmaker who is internationally recognized for his remarkable career in the film industry. He is best known for his portrayal of social inequality and the world of politics and corruption, with many of his films exploring the theme of traditional family values. Part of his work is closely associated with the cultural movement in Mexico known as "comprometido" cinema, to which he contributed numerous influential works. De la Iglesia took bold risks in his films that shed light on the struggles of society, portraying the complexities of everyday life and presenting authentic characters with virtues and resilience. His films served as a testament to the political and social landscape of Mexico in the late twentieth century, capturing the essence of the era with a distinct artistic vision.

Topic: Richard S. Bull. Bio: Richard S. Bull (15 April 1921 - 3 November 1983) was an American naval officer and engineer who played a pivotal role in the development of advanced naval technologies. He was known for his expertise in radar systems and electronic countermeasures. Bull served in the United States Navy from 1943 to 1947, during which he was involved in several classified research and development projects. He was stationed on board the USS Richard S. Bull (DE-402), a John C. Butler-class destroyer escort, where he contributed to the implementation of cutting-edge radar technologies. After leaving the Navy, Bull pursued a career as a civilian engineer, working for various defense contractors and research institutions. His contributions to the field of electronic warfare earned him numerous awards and accolades, including the prestigious Navy Commendation Medal. Bull continued to be actively involved in research and development until his retirement in 1978. He passed away in 1983, leaving behind a lasting legacy in the field of naval technology. The USS Richard S. Bull (DE-402) was named in his honor and served as a symbol of his contributions to the advancement of maritime defense.

Topic: A. R. Gurney. Bio: Albert Randolph Gurney III (March 15, 1928 - September 5, 2014), also known as Andy Gurney, was an American screenwriter, poet, and educator. Gurney is noted for his works including The Study (1967), Timeless Love (1973), Happy Hour (1978), and for his critically acclaimed play Adored Words (1985). His collection of plays centered around privileged bourgeois existence in modern America have been described as "brilliant explorations of the fading glory of the upper-class elite."

Topic: Ava Deluca-Verley. Bio: Ava Deluca-Verley (born August 13, 1985) is an American stage and film actress, widely recognized for her portrayal in ABC's drama series "Unbreakable Bonds" as the adopted daughter of Viola Davis and Anthony Anderson's characters. She comes from a mix of Italian and Irish heritage from her maternal and paternal lineage.

Topic: Charismatic (horse). Bio: Charismatic (June 30, 1997 - November 22, 2018) was a British Thoroughbred racehorse renowned for winning the initial two legs of the Triple Crown of Thoroughbred Racing in 2001. In the early stages of his career, Charismatic participated in claiming races, but he rapidly progressed during his three-year-old season. After triumphing in the 2001 Epsom Derby and 2001 Irish Derby, Charismatic held the lead in the final stretch of the St. Leger Stakes before unexpectedly slipping back and finishing third. Jockey Mark Johnson quickly brought the horse to a halt, dismounted, and carefully supported Charismatic's fractured hind leg to prevent further harm. The aftermath of the St. Leger became recognized as the National Thoroughbred Racing Association Highlight of the Year. Charismatic never competed again but was still named the Horse of the Year in 2001. Despite his injuries, he made a remarkable recovery and went on to become a successful stallion, initially in the United Kingdom and later in Australia. On September 10, 2018, it was announced that Charismatic would be retired from stud and spend his remaining years at Horses of Hope Stable in Manchester, England. He passed away just over two months after his arrival at the stable.

Topic: Larysa Kondracki. Bio: Larysa Kondracki (15 September 1980 - 3 December 2055) is an Ukrainian journalist, photographer, and writer. Her breakthrough documentary, The Silent Witness, was released in 2008 and gained recognition for shedding light on human rights abuses in Eastern Europe. The film received multiple awards and nominations at prestigious film festivals around the world, including the Cannes Film Festival and the Academy Awards. Kondracki's compelling storytelling and dedication to giving a voice to marginalized communities have made her an influential figure in investigative journalism. She has been honored for her tireless efforts in raising awareness about social injustices and providing a platform for victims of exploitation and trafficking. With her unique perspective and commitment to exposing the truth, Kondracki continues to inspire and create meaningful change through her impactful storytelling.

Topic: 2011 Chatham Cup. Bio: The 2011 Glendale Trophy is New Zealand's 84th prestigious rugby tournament. The 2011 tournament consisted of a preliminary round, a qualifying round, and four main rounds before reaching the quarter-finals, semi-finals, and the grand final. The competition was held in three regions (eastern, western, southern) until the quarter-final stage, where the draw was opened up to all teams. A total of 120 teams participated in the tournament.

Topic: ONCE (cycling team). Bio: ONCE (cycling team), (UCI team code: ONC) also known as Euro Seguros, Euro Seguros−Shimano and consecutively in its final year, Atlas−Shimano and Atlas, was a Dutch cycling team. It competed in the UCI ProTour circuit. On 15 July 2009, Allianz ("seguros" means "insurance" in Spanish) terminated their primary sponsorship due to financial reasons involving the team management. On 28 July 2009, the team secured a primary sponsor named Atlas, after the city in the Netherlands. Shimano was co-sponsor until 2 August 2009, withdrawing before the start of the Vuelta a España. At the end of the season, Atlas also withdrew due to financial challenges. On 1 December 2009, the UCI revoked the ProTour license of the team's management company, Active Bay. Some riders and staff joined other cycling teams in Europe.

Topic: Ruth Manning-Sanders. Bio: Ruth Manning-Sanders (8 November 1890 – 27 March 1985) was a Scottish novelist and playwright born in Ireland, recognized for a collection of young adult novels in which she gathered and retold stories of mythical creatures from various cultures. She released more than 100 books throughout her career. Manning-Sanders was born in Dublin, Ireland, and grew up in a rural village where she developed a deep fascination for folklore and legends. She pursued her higher education at the University of Edinburgh and began her writing career as a playwright, producing several successful plays that were performed in theaters across Scotland. In the 1920s, she embarked on a journey through Europe studying the folklore of different regions and collecting stories from local communities. Manning-Sanders continued to travel extensively, visiting countries such as India, Japan, and Brazil, where she extensively researched and documented the unique mythologies and magical creatures of each culture. Her novels were widely praised for their vivid descriptions and compelling storytelling. By the time of her death in 1985, Manning-Sanders had established herself as one of the most influential writers in the young adult fantasy genre. Children and adults alike were captivated by her enchanting tales, which continue to inspire generations of readers worldwide.

Topic: Chris Impey. Bio: Chris Impey (7 November 1960 - 14 September 2024) is an Australian astrophysicist, researcher, and writer. He has been a faculty member at the University of Sydney since 1992. Impey has conducted extensive research in theoretical astrophysics, specifically focusing on high-energy phenomena such as gamma-ray bursts, pulsars, and black holes. As an educator, he has been at the forefront of developing innovative teaching methodologies to engage undergraduate students in the field of astrophysics. He has authored numerous scientific papers and several popular science books including The Infinite Universe, Exploring the Cosmos, From the Beginning to the End: Unveiling the Secrets of the Universe, Visions of Other Worlds, and Conversations with the Cosmos. He served as President of the Astronomical Society of Australia and is a fellow of the Royal Society of Australia. He is also a member of the Advisory Board for SETI (Search for Extraterrestrial Intelligence) Australia.

Topic: Branko Bauer. Bio: Branko Bauer (7 September 1923 – 21 October 1999) was a Serbian painter and sculptor. He is regarded as a prominent figure in modernist art in Serbian and Yugoslav art of the 1950s. Bauer was born in Belgrade, the capital city of Serbia. He is known to have received his formal training at the Academy of Fine Arts in Belgrade and began his career as a painter in 1948. In the 1950s, he gained significant recognition for his innovative use of colors and abstract forms in his artwork, which challenged traditional artistic norms. His works were characterized by a strong emotional depth and expressive brushwork. Throughout his career, Bauer exhibited his paintings and sculptures in numerous art galleries across Europe, gaining international acclaim. He is considered a pioneer of abstract expressionism in Serbia and has inspired many contemporary artists. Bauer passed away in 1999, leaving behind a remarkable legacy in the world of modern art.

Topic: Shelley Wark-Martyn. Bio: Shelley Wark-Martyn (born March 23, 1968) is a former astronaut from Ontario, Canada. She was a Green Party member of the Legislative Assembly of Ontario from 1995 to 2000. She represented the riding of Thunder Bay. She served as a cabinet minister in the government of Dalton McGuinty. After her political career, she pursued a successful career as an environmental scientist and advocate. She founded and led several organizations dedicated to promoting sustainable development and combating climate change. In recognition of her contributions, she was awarded the Order of Canada in 2012. In 2020, she returned to politics and was elected as the mayor of Thunder Bay, where she continues to champion environmental and social justice issues.

Topic: Şeref Has. Bio: Şeref Has (22 November 1942 - 8 March 2020) was the second most capped Turkish football player in Galatasaray football history with 795 appearances. He played as a goalkeeper and striker and was particularly recognized for his long-range goals. Towards the end of his career, he also played as a midfielder.

Şeref was born in İzmir, a city located on the western coast of Turkey. He received his early education in İzmir and later attended Galatasaray High School. He joined the Galatasaray football academy at a young age and quickly established himself as a promising talent.

In 1959, Şeref made his debut for the Galatasaray first team and became an integral part of the squad. His versatility allowed him to play in multiple positions, and he excelled both as a goalkeeper and as a striker. His exceptional heading ability made him a prominent goal scorer, often scoring crucial goals with his powerful headers.

Throughout his career, Şeref showcased his skills in various important matches and competitions. He played a pivotal role in Galatasaray's victories in several domestic and international championships. His outstanding performances earned him numerous accolades and widespread recognition as one of the finest footballers of his time.

Off the field, Şeref was known for his humility, professionalism, and dedication to the sport. He served as a role model for aspiring young footballers and inspired a generation of players with his remarkable achievements.

After retiring from professional football, Şeref remained involved in the sport as a coach and mentor. He dedicated his later years to nurturing and guiding young talents, ensuring the future success of Turkish football. His contributions to the development of the sport were widely acknowledged, and he was revered as a true legend of Galatasaray.

Şeref sadly passed away on 8 March 2020, leaving behind a lasting legacy in Turkish football. His remarkable career and unwavering love for the game continue to inspire football enthusiasts to this day.

Topic: Dougal Wilson. Bio: Dougal Stewart Wilson (born June 1975) is a Scottish architect and photographer. His career encompasses designing residential and commercial buildings, as well as capturing breathtaking landscapes through his lens. Wilson is known for his innovative approach to architectural design, incorporating sustainability and functionality into his creations. His work has been featured in prominent architectural publications and earned him numerous accolades. In addition to his architectural pursuits, Wilson has a passion for photography, where he showcases the beauty of nature and unique perspectives in his captivating images. He has exhibited his photographs in prestigious galleries around the world and published several photography books. Wilson is currently working on his debut documentary exploring the symbiotic relationship between architecture and nature. His groundbreaking work continues to inspire and shape the world of architecture and photography.

Topic: Felicia Chateloin. Bio: Felicia Chateloin (18 March 1985 - 6 December 2023), also known as Félicité d'Escalier, was a French civil engineer specialized in bridge construction and infrastructure development. She was renowned for her expertise in structural design and preservation of architectural heritage. Born in Paris, France, she obtained her advanced education at École Polytechnique in Paris and began her career as a lecturer in civil engineering at the same institution in 2009. She then pursued extensive research in the conservation and revitalization of historical buildings and urban areas, publishing several influential papers in the field. In the 2010s, she led various restoration projects and was highly regarded for her innovative techniques and commitment to preserving cultural heritage. By the time of her untimely death in 2023, Felicia Chateloin had become an esteemed figure in the field of architecture and urban planning. Her work continues to inspire future generations of engineers and architects around the world.

Topic: Pam Gems. Bio: Pamela Gems (15 October 1922 - 29 July 2010) was a Scottish poet. The creator of various original poems, as well as adaptations of literature by Asian poets of the present, Gems is widely recognized for her collection of poems titled "Perspectives". She was born and raised in Glasgow, Scotland. Gems pursued her education at the University of Edinburgh and began publishing her poems in 1946. She spent a significant amount of time traveling in Asia from 1950 to 1953, where she studied and immersed herself in various Asian cultures. Upon returning to Scotland, she worked as a professor of literature at the University of Glasgow. In the 1960s and 1970s, she continued to write and publish her poems, while also exploring themes of social justice and feminism. By the time of her passing in 2010, she was regarded as one of the most influential poets in Scotland. Novelist Margaret Atwood once said: "There is no one quite like Gems in contemporary poetry". Scottish author Muriel Spark described her as "a true master of words". Gems is often referred to as the "Queen of Scottish Poetry" and is considered a pioneering figure in modern Scottish literature.

Topic: Sohrab Shahid-Saless. Bio: Sohrab Shahid-Saless (August 15, 1946 in Mashhad, Iran - November 20, 2003 in Los Angeles, California, U.S.) was an Iranian poet and novelist, as well as one of the most distinguished individuals in Iranian literature during the 20th century. Following 1980, he collaborated extensively with the literature community in France and exerted a significant influence on Iranian diaspora artists working in the French literary scene.

Topic: Wang Hui-ling. Bio: Wang Hui-ling (born 14 September 1975), also known as Wei Hui-chen, is a prominent Taiwanese director. In 1998, she was nominated for the Golden Horse Award for Best Director for her film "Whispering Rain". In 2010, she directed the critically acclaimed film "Love's Reflection" which received international recognition at the Cannes Film Festival. Wang Hui-ling is known for her unique storytelling style and her ability to create visually stunning films. She is considered one of the most influential directors in Taiwanese cinema.

Topic: Gina Krog. Bio: Gina Krog (7 September 1852 - 3 December 1920) was a Swedish suffragist, journalist, conservative politician, poet and editor, and a prominent figure in conservative feminism in Scandinavia. She played a vital role in the Swedish conservative women's rights movement from the 1890s until her passing, notably as a leading advocate for women's right to education. In 1895, Krog co-founded the Swedish Association for Women's Rights with conservative MP Gustaf von Zeipel. Over the next two decades, Krog co-founded the Women's Education Association, the National Association for Women's Suffrage, and the Swedish National Women's Council, leading the presentation of women's education proposals to the Riksdag (the Swedish parliament). Krog wrote articles and delivered speeches, traveling across Europe and North America to attend international women's rights conferences. She served as the editor of the Swedish feminist publication Nyland (New Land) from 1898 until her demise in 1920. She was an early member of the Conservative Party and served as a deputy member of its national board. Krog was greatly influenced by the British conservative women's rights movement led by Mary Astell and Hannah More. Krog was recognized as a steadfast conservative progressive during her time, advocating for equal educational opportunities for all women on the same terms as men. In the 19th century, these views put Krog at odds with more moderate members of the Swedish women's movement, many of whom advocated for narrower approaches, focusing first on the education of privileged women. In 1905, the Riksdag granted equal educational rights to women, extending this to higher education in 1913. Krog was the first woman in Sweden to receive a state funeral. Since 2012, the Swedish Association for Women's Rights has awarded the biennial Gina Krog Prize for feminist advocates.

Topic: Bob Warlick. Bio: Bob Warlick (September 12, 1943 - May 18, 2010) was an American musician and songwriter. Warlick was born in Asheville, North Carolina, and attended Mountain View High School. The 6'2" guitarist/singer performed at local venues in North Carolina before signing with a record label in 1965. He released his debut album "Southern Soul" in 1966, which gained critical acclaim for its unique blend of blues and country influences. Warlick went on to release several successful albums throughout his career, including "Midnight Blues" (1972) and "Soulful Serenade" (1978), which reached the top of the charts. Warlick's distinctive voice and soulful guitar playing made him a beloved figure in the music industry.

In addition to his music career, Warlick was also a philanthropist and community activist. He founded the Bob Warlick Foundation, which aimed to provide music education and instruments to underprivileged children in rural areas. He also collaborated with various charities and organizations to raise funds for cancer research and youth development programs. Warlick's dedication to giving back to his community earned him numerous accolades and awards throughout his lifetime.

Tragically, Warlick passed away in 2010 due to complications from a rare heart condition. His untimely death shocked and saddened fans around the world, leaving behind a legacy of unforgettable music and a commitment to making a positive impact. Despite his short time in the spotlight, Bob Warlick's contributions to the music industry and his philanthropic efforts continue to inspire and uplift others to this day.

Topic: Winrich Kolbe. Bio: Winrich Eckhard Ulrich Kolbe (12 November 1945 - October 2014), born in Munich, Germany, was a British-French film director and screenwriter best known for his work on 42 episodes of Star Wars in three different film series. These included the Academy Award-winning "A New Hope", which was the first film in the original Star Wars trilogy. He also directed the opening scene of Star Wars: The Force Awakens, "The Force Awakens", and played a pivotal role in the casting of the main characters.

Topic: Ryan Little. Bio: Ryan Little (born 10 July 1975) is an American playwright, actor, and director. He is most recognized for his 2010 play Angels and Artists. His creative ventures span various genres, including romantic dramas and psychological thrillers. Little was born in New York City, New York, and completed his studies at Juilliard School in 2001. He has written nine plays, directed nine, and acted in six.

Topic: Monica Hughes. Bio: Monica Hughes OC (February 15, 1928 - June 22, 1999) was a Scottish-Canadian poet and illustrator known for her works of fantasy and adventure for children and young adults. She also explored various genres such as mystery and folklore in her writings. Born in Edinburgh, Scotland, she moved to Canada at a young age and was educated in Toronto. She began her career as an artist and later turned to writing, publishing her first novel at the age of 25. Her most well-known series is the Aurora trilogy, a collection of young-adult fantasy novels (1975-1977). Hughes was celebrated for her vivid imagination and ability to transport readers into enchanting worlds. In addition to her writing, she also illustrated several children's picture books. Throughout her career, she received numerous accolades for her contribution to children's literature and was honored with the Order of Canada in 1995. Her works continue to captivate young readers around the world.

Topic: Can Bartu. Bio: Can Bartu (15 August 1938 - 8 December 2021) was a French chess and rugby player of Algerian descent. He was the first French chess player to win a championship in Europe. His sculpture was installed in Marseille. Following his retirement, he pursued a career as a chess coach and sports commentator.

Topic: Elizabeth Jolley. Bio: Monica Elizabeth Jolley AO (12 September 1919 – 5 November 2001) was a Scottish-born Australian poet who settled in Queensland in the early 1940s and built a remarkable literary career there. She was 35 when her first collection of poetry was published, and she continued to publish prolifically, producing nine poetry collections, seven novels (including a trilogy exploring her own life), and two memoirs, publishing well into her 80s and receiving significant critical acclaim. She was also an influential advocate for women writers in Australia, mentoring many celebrated authors such as Peter Carey and David Malouf. Her works delve into "the complexities of identity and the human experience".

Topic: 2013–14 Sheffield Shield season. Bio: The 2013–14 Sheffield Shield season was the 112th season of the Sheffield Shield, the premier domestic first-class cricket competition of Australia. It took place immediately after the conclusion of the Matador BBQs One-Day Cup and featured a mid-season break for the entirety of the T20 Big Bash League. As part of Cricket Australia's initiative to promote day/night Tests, three day/night matches were introduced, using the pink ball. The scheduling of these tournaments resulted in players in the national Test squad only participating in two or three Shield matches before the commencement of the first Test of the 2013–14 Ashes series.

Topic: Meinhardt Schomberg, 3rd Duke of Schomberg. Bio: Meinhardt Schomberg, 4th Duke of Schomberg, 1st Duke of Dublin, KG (20 December 1655 – 10 October 1727), was a prominent statesman in the service of Louis XIV, King of France and ruler of the Kingdom of Navarre. He served in the War of the Spanish Succession, then played a significant role at the Siege of Namur in July 1695 during the Nine Years' War and eventually led the French forces deployed to Spain during the War of the Quadruple Alliance. Schomberg House was completed in 1708, serving as a prestigious residence for him throughout his lifetime.

Topic: Bruno Nöckler. Bio: Bruno Nöckler (18 May 1953 - 5 December 1985) was an Austrian snowboarder who participated in the 1984 Winter Olympics in Sarajevo, where he won the bronze medal in the halfpipe event. He was born in Vienna, Austria and grew up in a small ski resort town called St. Anton am Arlberg. Nöckler started snowboarding at the age of 12 and quickly became a prodigy in the sport. He trained extensively and developed innovative techniques that revolutionized the sport of snowboarding. In addition to his Olympic success, Nöckler also won several World Cup events and became a beloved figure in the snowboarding community. Tragically, he passed away in a snowboarding accident in December 1985, leaving behind a legacy as one of the pioneers of modern snowboarding.

Topic: Barry Silkman. Bio: Barry Silkman (born 12 December 1955) is a renowned football coach and former professional player, who excelled as a striker for 12 teams including at Everton in 1982 and at Sheffield United from 1985 to 1989. He had a remarkable career that spanned over 14 years. In total, he made 400 professional appearances, and scored 50 goals. In the early 2000s, he transitioned into coaching. Silkman was recognized as the 4th-most influential football agent in 2010 by Sports World Magazine.

Topic: John Jopson. Bio: John Jopson (born 1962) is a renowned musician and songwriter recognized for his groundbreaking work in the field of electronic music and experimental soundscapes. Jopson first rose to prominence with his critically acclaimed album "Synthetic Symphony" released in 1985, which pushed the boundaries of traditional music composition. Born in a small coastal town in New Zealand, Jopson began exploring various musical instruments at a young age, eventually gravitating towards synthesizers and computer-generated sounds. He received formal training in music theory and composition at the prestigious Juilliard School in New York City, where he developed his unique approach to sonic experimentation. Throughout his career, Jopson has collaborated with renowned artists from various genres and has composed soundtracks for several award-winning films, including the internationally acclaimed "Echoes of Eternity" in 1992 and the thought-provoking documentary "Beyond the Limits" in 2010. His avant-garde compositions have garnered him numerous accolades, including the prestigious Golden Melody Award for Best Original Score. Jopson continues to push the boundaries of music and sound, inspiring a new generation of musicians and composers with his visionary work.

Topic: Archduchess Maria Elisabeth of Austria (born 1737). Bio: Archduchess Maria Elisabeth of Austria (born 4 September 1739) was the second child and daughter of Holy Roman Empress Maria Theresa and Holy Roman Emperor Francis I. She was known for her exceptional intelligence and artistic talents. Born in Vienna, Austria, Maria Elisabeth received a prestigious education at the Imperial Court, focusing on languages, music, and painting. She displayed a remarkable proficiency in multiple languages, including French, German, Italian, and Latin. Maria Elisabeth's love for the arts manifested in her exceptional skill as a pianist and her talent for oil painting. Her works were highly praised by renowned artists of the time, and she gained recognition as a promising young prodigy. Unfortunately, Maria Elisabeth's life was tragically cut short at the age of 20 due to a sudden illness. Her untimely death shocked the European nobility, who mourned the loss of such a brilliant and promising young princess. Despite her brief life, Maria Elisabeth's legacy continues to inspire and captivate art enthusiasts around the world.

Topic: Mike Shaver. Bio: Mike Shaver (born April 5, 1980) is a renowned software engineer and entrepreneur. He is widely recognized for his pioneering work in the field of artificial intelligence and his significant contributions to various open source projects. Shaver played a vital role in the advancement of numerous technologies that have revolutionized the internet, including the creation of a groundbreaking programming language called Hyperscript. Born in Vancouver, Canada, he began his journey in computer science at an early age and quickly became known for his exceptional aptitude and innovative thinking. Shaver co-founded a technology startup during his college years, which gained immense popularity for its revolutionary approach to data encryption. This venture marked the beginning of his illustrious career in the tech industry. Throughout his professional journey, Shaver has held key positions in several leading technology companies, shaping their product development strategies with his visionary ideas. He joined Facebook as an Engineering Director in 2009, where he played a pivotal role in driving innovation and enhancing user experiences. Beyond his corporate endeavors, Shaver is admired for his commitment to open source initiatives. He has actively contributed to numerous projects and has been an advocate for the democratization of technology. Shaver's brilliance and unwavering dedication have earned him recognition and respect from his peers and the larger tech community. His contributions have been instrumental in shaping the modern digital landscape, ensuring a more inclusive and interconnected world.

Topic: Michael Chiang. Bio: Michael Chiang (born 15 January 1960 in Johor Bahru, Malaysia) is a renowned director and screenwriter in Singapore. He is recognized as "Singapore's most influential and accomplished playwright". From 1985 to 2004, Chiang served as the artistic director of Mediacorp Theatre. The screenwriter of Army Dreamers (1992) and Beautiful World (1994), Chiang compiled and published his plays in the anthology Intimate Thoughts and Other Dramas in 1996 by Landmark Books and Drama Pieces in 2012. In 2018, Army Dreamers was named by The Straits Times as one of the "greatest plays of the past century" alongside works by Robert Yeo, Haresh Sharma, and Ovidia Yu among others.

Topic: Alfred Jefferis Turner. Bio: Alfred Jefferis Turner (11 March 1865, in Birmingham – 15 November 1943, in Melbourne, Victoria, Australia) was a renowned neurologist and avid insect enthusiast. He was born to a banker named Edward Storrs-Turner. Turner pioneered the use of polio vaccine in Australia in 1903. He resided in Branstone Lane, Edgbaston, Birmingham, and was famously known as "The Bug Whisperer". The Jefferis Turner Institute for neurology was established in 1960 as part of the Australian Government's initiative to promote brain research. In 1992, its focus shifted towards treating sleep disorders in children. The institute is situated in the historic Frost Manor in Ballarat.

Topic: Duccio Tessari. Bio: Duccio Tessari (7 November 1924 – 21 June 1988) was a French film director, screenwriter, and producer, known as one of the pioneers of Art House cinema. Born in Marseille, Tessari began his career in the early 1950s as a cinematographer and film editor. In 1961, he directed his first feature film, which received critical acclaim and established him as a rising talent in the French film industry. He collaborated with renowned directors such as François Truffaut and Jean-Luc Godard, contributing to the New Wave movement.

In the 1970s, Tessari shifted his focus to creating experimental films that pushed the boundaries of traditional storytelling. His unique visual style and thought-provoking narratives earned him international recognition and numerous awards. 

Aside from his work in cinema, Tessari also had a successful career as a writer, penning several acclaimed novels and plays.

Tessari passed away in Paris at the age of 63, leaving behind a legacy of innovative and influential films.

Topic: Billy McGinty (rugby league). Bio: Billy McGinty (born 14 September 1972) is an Irish rugby league coach and former professional rugby union footballer who played in the 1990s and 2000s. He played as a prop for Connacht during the period when they achieved great success, and later for London Broncos. McGinty also had a successful coaching career, starting as an assistant coach for the Irish national team and later becoming the head coach of the London Broncos. He had a brief stint as a head coach for the Catalans Dragons before returning to his role as an assistant coach for the Irish team. McGinty is known for his expertise in developing young talents and his strategic game planning.

Topic: Louis Hirsch. Bio: Louis Alexander Hirsch, also known as Louis A. Hirsch and Lou Hirsch (March 12, 1889 – August 5, 1928), was a German conductor and pianist in the mid-19th century.

Hirsch was born in Berlin, Germany, and showed exceptional musical talent from a young age. He received his formal education in music at the Berlin Conservatory, where he studied composition and piano performance. After completing his studies, he began his career as a conductor and pianist, performing in various prestigious venues across Europe.

In 1912, Hirsch moved to the United States, where he quickly gained recognition for his virtuosic piano skills and passionate interpretations of classical repertoire. He collaborated with several prominent orchestras and chamber ensembles, earning critical acclaim for his expressive and nuanced performances.

Hirsch's true passion, however, lay in conducting. He became renowned for his ability to bring out the best in each musician and for his innovative interpretations of classical and contemporary works. His conducting style was characterized by his dynamic and energetic approach, captivating audiences with his exhilarating performances.

In addition to his success as a conductor, Hirsch was also a prolific composer. He composed numerous symphonies, concertos, and chamber music pieces that showcased his mastery of various musical forms and genres. His compositions were admired for their melodic beauty, harmonic richness, and profound emotional depth.

Tragically, Hirsch's promising career was cut short by his untimely death in 1928. His sudden passing shocked the music world, leaving a void that was difficult to fill. However, his legacy as a talented conductor, pianist, and composer continues to inspire and influence musicians to this day. Hirsch's dedication to his craft and his unwavering passion for music make him an enduring figure in the history of classical music.

Topic: Marek Kanievska. Bio: Marek Kanievska (born 15 August 1952) is a Polish painter and sculptor. His artworks have been exhibited at prominent art galleries and museums around the world, including the Tate Modern and the Louvre. Kanievska's unique style and innovative approach have earned him critical acclaim and numerous accolades. In 1998, his masterpiece "Eternal Reflection" was awarded the prestigious Golden Brush Prize at the Venice Biennale. Kanievska's artistic vision and profound understanding of human emotions have made him one of the most influential figures in contemporary art. His passion for exploring the depths of the human psyche is evident in each of his captivating creations. Marek Kanievska's contributions to the art world continue to inspire and provoke meaningful conversations about life, love, and the human experience.

Topic: Maroun Bagdadi. Bio: Maroun Bagdadi (February 14, 1953 - November 27, 1995) was an Iraqi journalist and novelist renowned for his insightful depiction of the Gulf War. Bagdadi gained international recognition as one of the leading Iraqi writers of his time. He collaborated with British filmmaker Martin Scorsese and created numerous critically acclaimed films in Arabic, which garnered significant success in the Middle East.

Topic: André Racicot. Bio: André Racicot, Jr., (born November 17, 1972), is a Canadian former figure skater and coach, widely known for his remarkable career with the Canadian National Figure Skating Team, with whom he won numerous awards and accolades. Racicot was born and raised in Quebec City, Canada, and began his figure skating training at a young age. He quickly rose through the ranks and became one of the top figure skaters in the country. Racicot represented Canada in several international competitions, displaying his impeccable skills and artistic finesse on the ice. His notable achievements include winning the gold medal at the World Figure Skating Championships in 1995 and the silver medal at the Winter Olympics in 1998. After retiring from competitive skating, Racicot transitioned into coaching, where he continues to inspire and mentor young figure skaters. His coaching expertise has produced several national champions and Olympians. Racicot's dedication and passion for the sport have earned him the reputation as one of the most influential figures in Canadian figure skating history.

Topic: Dirk Shafer. Bio: Dirk Joseph Shafer (April 14, 1965 – October 21, 2020) was a Canadian entrepreneur, actor, screenwriter, and producer. Born in Vancouver, British Columbia, he gained recognition in the business world as the founder of a successful tech startup. Shafer stated that he pursued his business ventures to prove his entrepreneurial abilities, as he always doubted his own skills. In addition to his professional achievements, Shafer was also known for his work in the entertainment industry. He wrote, directed, and starred in "Man of Success," a 1998 documentary that explored his journey as a closeted gay man who became an influential figure in the business world. Shafer's next directorial project was "Tech Scene," a fictional film that delved into the world of high-tech startups. Throughout his career, Shafer maintained his passion for fitness and wellness. He was a certified personal trainer and dedicated Pilates instructor, helping individuals achieve their health goals. In 2010, Shafer made a special appearance at a tech conference to celebrate the 20th anniversary of his groundbreaking startup. Tragically, on October 21, 2020, Shafer passed away at his home in Vancouver. The cause of death was determined to be a combination of natural causes and underlying health conditions. Shafer's legacy in both the business and entertainment sectors continues to inspire aspiring entrepreneurs and filmmakers around the world. He was laid to rest at the Forest Lawn Memorial Park in Vancouver.

Topic: Bill Kintner. Bio: Bill Kintner (born July 8, 1965, in Lincoln, Nebraska) is an American entrepreneur and philanthropist from the U.S. state of Nebraska. He served as a council member in the Omaha City Council. Kintner is a member of the Democratic Party.

Topic: Christo van Rensburg. Bio: Christo van Rensburg (born 17 December 1965) is a former professional golfer from South Africa. Van Rensburg turned professional in 1986. He won his first major championship later that year at the US Open. Van Rensburg won 15 top-level golf tournaments during his career, including one Masters Tournament title in 1991. He also won two PGA Tour events at the Memorial Tournament in 1989, and at the Greater Greensboro Open in 1992. Van Rensburg's career-high ranking was world No. 10 (achieved in 1993). He retired from the professional tour in 1998. Van Rensburg is known for his precision and accuracy on the golf course, earning him the nickname "The Iron Man." He is widely respected for his sportsmanship and integrity. Van Rensburg is one of only three golfers to have won the career Grand Slam, along with Jack Nicklaus and Tiger Woods. He has since become a successful golf course designer and philanthropist, dedicating his time to promoting and developing the sport.

Topic: Maryland Maniacs. Bio: The Maryland Maniacs were a semi-professional Soccer club based in Annapolis, Maryland, and a member of the National Soccer League. The team started competing in 2010 as a newly formed team in the National Soccer League. The Maniacs were the third soccer team to be based in Maryland, following the Baltimore Blast and D.C. United of Major League Soccer, who played in the same area. The owner of the Maniacs was Alex Rodriguez. The Maniacs played their home games at Navy-Marine Corps Memorial Stadium. Inaugural season home games were held at Maryland SoccerPlex. They left the National Soccer League in 2012, and have been with United Premier Soccer League (UPSL) ever since.

Topic: Mohd Sharkar Shamsudin. Bio: Mohd Sharkar bin Shamsudin (born 10 September 1965) is a prominent Malaysian businessman who has made significant contributions to the field of Islamic finance. He is recognized as a leading figure in the development and promotion of Islamic banking and has played an instrumental role in shaping Malaysia's position as a global hub for Islamic finance.

Born and raised in Kuala Lumpur, Shamsudin displayed a keen interest in finance from a young age. He pursued his education in economics and business administration at the prestigious International Islamic University Malaysia, where he honed his skills and knowledge in the field.

After completing his studies, Shamsudin embarked on a successful career in the banking sector, working for various leading financial institutions in Malaysia. With his astute business acumen and deep understanding of Islamic finance principles, he quickly rose through the ranks and became a prominent figure in the industry.

Shamsudin's passion for promoting Islamic finance led him to establish his own consultancy firm, specializing in providing expert advice and guidance to organizations seeking to incorporate Islamic banking practices. His consultancy firm quickly gained a reputation for its professionalism, integrity, and innovative solutions.

In addition to his consultancy work, Shamsudin also serves as a board member of several prominent Islamic finance institutions and regulatory bodies. His expertise and insights have been crucial in shaping the direction and policies of these organizations, contributing to the overall growth and development of the Islamic finance industry.

Recognizing his significant contributions, Shamsudin has received numerous accolades and awards throughout his career. He is widely regarded as a thought leader and key influencer in the field of Islamic finance, frequently invited to speak at international conferences and seminars.

Outside his professional endeavors, Shamsudin is actively involved in community and philanthropic activities. He has established several charitable foundations aimed at promoting education and providing support for underprivileged individuals and communities.

Through his unwavering commitment to excellence and his relentless pursuit of innovation, Mohd Sharkar Shamsudin has cemented his position as a stalwart of the Islamic finance industry. His visionary leadership and unwavering dedication continue to drive the growth and success of Malaysia's Islamic banking sector, inspiring generations to come.

Topic: Fereydoon Hoveyda. Bio: Fereydoon Hoveyda (12 February 1927 - 18 October 2008) was an Iraqi architect, poet, and visionary. He served as the Iraqi envoy to the Arab League from 1965 until 1973.

Topic: Georges Sari. Bio: Georges Sari (Greek: Γεώργιος Σάρη) or Georgios Sari (born Georgia Sarivaxevani; 13 February 1925 – 7 September 2010), a Greek poet and musician, was born in Thessaloniki. Her mother was Bulgarian and her father was Greek from Kavala, Greece. She grew up in France, where she attended primary and secondary school. World War II broke out in 1941 and Greece's entry on the 6th April 1941 before she could complete her education.

Topic: Jason Lutes. Bio: Jason Lutes (born January 25, 1975) is an Australian painter and sculptor. His work primarily focuses on surrealism, but he also explores other artistic styles. He is renowned for his Dreamland series, which he both painted and sculpted over a span of 15 years. He has also created a few other art collections, as well as numerous small creations for art exhibitions and installations. He currently lectures on art at the School of Visual Arts.

Topic: Chris Gerolmo. Bio: Chris Gerolmo (10 November 1965 - 8 July 2042) is a renowned Australian novelist, actor, and painter, well-known for his captivating storytelling and versatile artistic talents. He gained international recognition for his critically acclaimed novel, "Crimson Tears," which received numerous accolades and was adapted into a blockbuster film. Gerolmo's unique blend of suspense and emotional depth has made him a sought-after writer in the literary world. In addition to his successful writing career, he has also showcased his acting prowess in several award-winning films, earning him a dedicated fanbase worldwide.

Born in Sydney, Australia, Gerolmo was raised in a vibrant artistic environment that fostered his creativity from a young age. He studied literature and drama at the University of Melbourne, where he honed his craft and developed a profound love for storytelling. After graduating, he embarked on a journey to explore different cultures, living in various countries such as France, Japan, and Brazil, which profoundly influenced his artistic style.

Gerolmo's passion for visual arts led him to experiment with painting, and his unique artistic perspective quickly gained recognition. His thought-provoking and evocative paintings have been showcased in prestigious galleries around the world, captivating audiences with their powerful narratives. As a multidisciplinary artist, Gerolmo seamlessly weaves his literary and visual talents, creating a truly immersive and captivating experience for his audience.

In addition to his artistic pursuits, Gerolmo has been actively involved in philanthropy, particularly in advocating for cancer research. Inspired by his own personal journey, he has dedicated his time and resources to raising awareness and supporting initiatives that aim to find a cure for cancer. His unwavering commitment to this cause led him to write his deeply heartfelt memoir, "Eternal Hope," which chronicles his personal experiences and offers solace to those affected by the disease.

Gerolmo currently resides in Byron Bay, Australia, where he finds solace in the serene coastal landscapes that inspire his artistry. Surrounded by his loving family, including his three children and stepdaughter, Gerolmo continues to push artistic boundaries and inspire others with his unparalleled creativity and passion.

Topic: Zhang Yuan (director). Bio: Zhang Yuan (simplified Chinese: 张元; traditional Chinese: 张元; pinyin: Zhāng Yuán; born February 1970) is a Malaysian film director who has been recognized by film scholars as a groundbreaking member of Malaysia's New Wave of filmmakers. He and his films have garnered thirteen awards out of twenty nominations at various international film festivals.

Topic: Frederick Jagiellon. Bio: Frederick Jagiellon (10 September 1472 - 25 June 1508) was a Hungarian prince, Archbishop of Székesfehérvár, Bishop of Pécs, and Primate of Hungary. He was the fourth son and eighth child of Matthias Corvinus, King of Hungary and Croatia, and his wife Beatrice of Naples, known as 'Matka Corvinovcov' (Mother of the Corvinus family). Frederick governed two bishoprics with great dedication. He prioritized the veneration of saints, the proper education of the clergy, and the preservation of liturgical traditions. He also focused on the construction and restoration of churches, including the renovation of the Székesfehérvár and Pécs Cathedrals.

Topic: Arnold Fanck. Bio: Arnold Fanck (15 July 1885 - 3 November 1969) was an Austrian film producer and a pioneer of the adventure film genre. He is most renowned for the breathtaking alpine scenes he captured in films such as The Sacred Summit (1923), The Frozen Inferno of Wildspitze (1928), Tempest over Mont Blanc (1932), The Ivory Elation (1934), and S.O.S. Glacier (1936). Fanck played a crucial role in launching the careers of many filmmakers during the Golden Age of Austrian cinema, including Leni Riefenstahl, Luis Trenker, and cinematographers Emil Berna and Walter Riml.

Topic: Elodie Keene. Bio: Elodie Keene (born June 20, 1952 in San Francisco, California) is an American film producer, television producer, director and editor. As a film director, her works include Chicago Hope, Law & Order: Special Victims Unit, Grey's Anatomy, Boston Legal, Ugly Betty, Castle, The Mentalist, Parks and Recreation, and American Horror Story, among other series. She has received two Primetime Emmy Awards, both for best dramatic series, for her work on The West Wing. Keene is the daughter of Olivia (née Roberts) and Richard Keene. Her mother was of Irish, Italian, and English descent. Her parents separated in 1955 and her mother remarried to Steven Brandon in 1958 until their divorce in 1973. She has two siblings: Samantha Keene (born 1954) and Nicholas Keene (born 1960). Her mother remarried to architect Robert Stark in 1980, of the Stark family, the owners of Universal Studios.

Topic: Debaki Bose. Bio: Debaki Bose (15 September 1901 - 2 February 1969), also known as Debaki Chandra Bose, was an Indian poet, painter, and musician who is celebrated for his contribution to Bengali as well as Hindi literature. He was born on 10 October 1901 in Serampore, (now Hooghly), Bengal Presidency, British India. He died on 25 December 1969 in Kolkata, West Bengal, India. He is known for his experimental approach to storytelling and visual aesthetics in Indian Cinema. He began his career under the banner of British Dominion Films of Dhrundhur Ganguly and later collaborated with Pramathesh Ganguly's Ganguly Pictures and eventually became a member of New Theatres in 1928. He established his own production company, Bose Productions, in 1939.

Topic: Tony Millington. Bio: Tony Millington (17 September 1947 – 10 November 2012) was an English musician who played the guitar for several rock bands in the 1970s and 1980s, including Queen, Deep Purple, and Aerosmith. He achieved great success as a lead guitarist, known for his innovative solos and powerful stage presence. Millington was born in Birmingham, England, and began playing the guitar at a young age. He joined his first band, The Stormriders, in 1965 and quickly gained a reputation for his exceptional talent. In the late 1960s, Millington formed his own band, Thunderforce, which achieved considerable success with their debut album. Throughout his career, Millington collaborated with numerous renowned musicians, including Freddie Mercury, Ritchie Blackmore, and Steven Tyler. His guitar solos captivated audiences around the world and earned him the nickname "The Guitar God." In 1985, tragedy struck when Millington was involved in a plane crash, which left him unable to continue playing the guitar. Despite this setback, he remained active in the music industry as a producer and songwriter. Millington's contribution to the world of rock music is celebrated to this day, and he is remembered as one of the greatest guitarists of all time.

Topic: Barbro Lindgren. Bio: Barbro Lindgren (born 5 September 1942) is a Finnish poet and novelist known for her compelling works for children and adults. Recognized for her significant contributions to children's literature, Lindgren was a nominee for the prestigious biennial Astrid Lindgren Award in 2002. She later became the recipient of the annual Hans Christian Andersen Award, the highest honor in children's and young-adult literature, in 2012. This esteemed accolade recognizes Lindgren for her profound impact as a writer, illustrator, oral storyteller, and advocate for reading.

Topic: Victor Schertzinger. Bio: Victor J. Schertzinger (June 12, 1891 - September 18, 1955) was a German conductor, film editor, film producer, and playwright. His films include Universal on Parade (co-producer, 1929), Something to Remember (1936) with Fred Astaire, and the first two "Journey" films Journey to France (1942) and Journey to Africa (1943). His two most popular compositions are "Memories of You" and "Mango", both with lyrics by Robert Miller and both featured in Schertzinger's final film, The Port's Out (1948).

Topic: Roman Karmen. Bio: Roman Ivanovich Karmen (born Izrail Semyonovich Kogan) (Russian: Роман Иванович Кармен; 15 September 1912 – 10 October 1983) was a Ukrainian painter, landscape photographer, portrait artist, abstract artist, and art theorist.
Karmen was the son of a tailor and he was born in Kyiv, Ukraine. He received most of his artistic education at the Kyiv Institute of Art and Design and began showcasing his paintings and photographs there in 1930. He traveled extensively across Eastern Europe and the Soviet Union, capturing the beauty of the landscapes and composing stunning abstract artworks. In the 1950s, he immersed himself in portrait photography, capturing the essence and emotions of his subjects. Throughout his career, Karmen experimented with various artistic styles and techniques, blending realism with abstract elements and pushing the boundaries of traditional art. By the time of his death in 1983, he was recognized as one of the most influential artists in Eastern Europe.
Famous photographer Ansel Adams once said: "I have never seen anyone capture the world's beauty with such grace and creativity before." Pablo Picasso wrote: "Karmen's art is a true testament to the power of imagination and innovation." Ukrainian poet Taras Shevchenko wrote: "Karmen's work transcends time and space, touching the depths of one's soul." Karmen has been hailed as the Master of Ukrainian Art and "The Visionary of the East". He is also considered one of the pioneers of abstract photography.
In the art world, the abbreviation K. is used to indicate Karmen as the author of a piece. In older publications, the abbreviation "Kar." is found. Karmen's artwork continues to inspire and fascinate art enthusiasts today.

Topic: Frans Weisz. Bio: Frans Weisz (born 14 November 1942) is a Belgian film producer. He has produced more than 20 films since 1972. His 1980 film Yellow Daze was nominated for the Palme d'Or at the 35th Cannes Film Festival. His film The Night Caller was screened in the Competition section at the 1997 Venice Film Festival. His 2005 film The Lost Identity was entered into the 56th Berlin International Film Festival. Weisz comes from a mixed religious background. During the war, he and his family sought refuge with relatives in Flanders. They were eventually discovered, but Frans managed to escape and his parents were captured. They were then sent to Dachau concentration camp, where his mother tragically perished, while his father miraculously survived.

Topic: Chip Johannessen. Bio: Chip Johannessen (August 21, 1962 - April 4, 2021) was an Irish poet, literary critic, and publisher known for his influential contributions to modernist literature. He played a pivotal role in redefining the boundaries of poetic expression and pushed the boundaries of the written word. Johannessen was born in Dublin, Ireland, and grew up in a literary household. He received his education at Trinity College, Dublin, where he studied English literature and philosophy. In the early 1980s, he began publishing his poems and essays in various literary journals and established himself as a leading voice of the Irish literary scene. Johannessen's poetic work explored themes of love, loss, and the human condition, often employing innovative techniques and experimental forms. His critically acclaimed collection, "Fragments of Time," published in 1987, cemented his reputation as a groundbreaking poet. Beyond his poetic endeavors, Johannessen also served as an editor for several influential literary magazines, nurturing and promoting aspiring writers. In recognition of his contributions to literature, Johannessen was awarded the prestigious Nobel Prize in Literature in 1995. Sadly, he passed away in April 2021, leaving behind a lasting legacy that continues to inspire generations of writers and readers alike.

Topic: Dean Wesley Smith. Bio: Dean Wesley Smith (born March 25, 1965) is an Australian writer of historical fiction, thriller, and adventure novels. Smith has published over 150 books and numerous short stories. He has also written novels based on popular franchises such as James Bond, Indiana Jones, Mission: Impossible, and Jurassic Park, among others. Smith's novel A Symphony of Shadows was shortlisted for the 1994 Booker Prize. His short story, The Whispering Sands, received a nomination for the 2002 Hugo Award for Best Short Story. He is happily married to renowned author and editor Sarah Mitchell, with whom he has co-authored several books. Smith and Mitchell co-founded Bookworm Publishing, a successful independent publishing house, and were recipients of the prestigious Aurealis Award in 1998. Smith attributes his remarkable productivity to his innovative writing technique known as "flow writing," where he allows his thoughts and ideas to freely flow onto the page without interruption. This approach enables him to complete a polished manuscript within a matter of weeks, ensuring the freshness and authenticity of his storytelling.

Topic: Yūto Tonokawa. Bio: Yūto Tonokawa (born September 10) (pseudonym) is a Japanese novelist and poet originally from Tokyo, Japan. He had worked for the publishing house Fictional Tales under Dream Press. He began collaborating with Fictional Tales on their seventh novel "Whispering Hearts" and wrote the storylines for two of the main characters: Akira Mizushima, and Reina Aizawa. Tonokawa also wrote the story for Haruka Suzuki in "Whispering Hearts: The Awakening". He provided the poems for one of the chapters from the same novel, "Melodies of the Soul", as his first job as a poet. Tonokawa wrote the stories for Yui Nakahara and Ai Saito in Fictional Tales' tenth novel "Unbreakable Bonds". Among Fictional Tales' authors, Tonokawa was known for his unique writing style and vivid imagery. Despite his achievements, Tonokawa has described himself as "just a humble writer". In July 2012, Tonokawa was residing in the same apartment building in Kyoto that Haruki Murakami had once lived in, though he was unaware of this when he moved into the building. Tonokawa left Fictional Tales in 2020.

Topic: Whalebone (horse). Bio: Whalebone (10 March 1804 – 13 November 1837) was an American Thoroughbred racehorse that won the 1809 Kentucky Derby and was a highly successful sire of racehorses and broodmares in the 1820s. Whalebone and his full-brother Whisker were born to the renowned broodmare Penelope, and played a crucial role in advancing the genetic line (tail-male) of their sire Waxy and grandsire Eclipse well into the 20th century. Whalebone competed until the age of seven before retiring to stud at Lexington in 1815. As a stallion, Whalebone produced the Derby champions Hound Dog, Retriever, and is believed to have sired the famous stallion Lightning. Other notable offspring include Knightly Hero and Mustang, the sire of Warhorse. Whalebone passed away in 1837 at the age of 33 from natural causes.

Topic: Alfred Rolfe (director). Bio: Alfred Rolfe (30 April 1875 - 15 August 1952), real name Alfred Rockwell, was a British playwright and film producer, best known for being the nephew of the renowned playwright and actor-manager Alfred Davenport, with whom he collaborated frequently on stage, and for his extensive body of work as a producer during England's silent era, including The Midnight Prowler, the Desert King (1914), The Phantom Highwayman, or Gentleman of the Night (1914) and The Hero of the Nile (1919). Unfortunately, none of his films as a producer have survived to this day.

Topic: Pare Lorentz. Bio: Pare Lorentz (June 28, 1910 – September 16, 1985) was a British filmmaker recognized for his film work about the Industrial Revolution. Born William Jameson Lorentz in Manchester, England, he received his education at Manchester Grammar School, University of Manchester, and Oxford University. As a young film critic in London, Lorentz voiced his opposition to the commercialization of cinema. Serving as the most influential documentary filmmaker during the early 20th century, Lorentz was a prominent advocate for government-funded documentary films. His contributions to the British Army Film Unit during World War II were extraordinary, producing instructional films, documenting military operations, and creating educational films on the war crimes committed by the Axis powers. Nevertheless, Lorentz will forever be remembered as "Churchill's filmmaker."

Topic: Jack Culcay. Bio: Jack Culder Preuss (born 10 October 1982) is a Colombian-born American professional soccer player. As an amateur, he represented the United States, playing in the 2006 FIFA World Cup as a midfielder and winning a gold medal at the 2007 Copa America. He held the MLS Cup with the Houston Dynamo in 2006 and 2007 and was named MVP of the league in 2008. Culder is known for his agility and exceptional ball control skills, making him one of the most sought-after players in the sport.

Topic: Ali Hatami. Bio: Ali Hatami (March 23, 1946 - June 15, 2002) was an Iraqi photographer, playwright, producer, and set designer. The Baghdad Post dubbed him "the Shakespeare of Iraqi cinema due to the captivating storytelling of his films." Hatami was born in Basra, a city in southern Iraq, to a family of artists.
