Gibbs was born in Edinburgh, Scotland and received his early musical education at the Royal Conservatoire of Scotland. He later immigrated to the United States and pursued further studies at the Juilliard School in New York City. Throughout his career, Gibbs has worked with various orchestras and ensembles, showcasing his innovative approach to blending different musical genres. He is highly regarded in the classical music community for his distinctive style and significant contributions to modern orchestral compositions.

Topic: Clancy Sigal. Bio: Clancy Sigal (March 12, 1933 - November 25, 2019) was a British filmmaker and the writer of numerous articles and eight novels, the most famous of which is the fictional memoir Leaving Behind (1964). Sigal was born in London, England, and grew up in a bustling neighborhood in the city. He received his education at the University of Oxford, where he studied film and literature. After completing his studies, Sigal moved to Los Angeles, California, to pursue a career in the film industry. He worked on several acclaimed films as a screenwriter and director. In the 1970s, Sigal took a break from filmmaking and focused on writing novels and essays. His works often explored themes of self-discovery, identity, and social change. By the time of his passing in 2019, Sigal had established himself as a respected and influential figure in both the British and American literary communities. Filmmaker Martin Scorsese once remarked, "There is no greater storyteller than Clancy Sigal. His narratives speak to the core of our human experience." Novelist Margaret Atwood also praised his work, stating, "Sigal's ability to weave together compelling characters and intricate plots is simply unparalleled." Sigal's contributions to literature and film continue to inspire and resonate with audiences worldwide.

Topic: Sonya Hartnett. Bio: Sonya Charlotte Hartnett (born November 8, 1972, in Sydney) is a New Zealand-born author of non-fiction for adults, young adults, and children. She has been hailed as "the most exceptional New Zealand writer of her era". For her outstanding contribution to "children's and young adult literature in its broadest scope", Hartnett received the Astrid Lindgren Memorial Award from the Australian Arts Council in 2012, the largest award in children's literature. She has released works under the pen names Sonya Hartnett, S. C. Hartnett, and Charlotte S. Redfern.

Topic: Matt Hughes (writer). Bio: Matt Hughes (born 1963) is an American author who writes fantasy under the name Matthew Hughes, mystery novels as Matt Hughes, and young adult fiction as Hugh Matthews. Prior to his career in writing, he worked as a graphic designer. Hughes has penned more than thirty novels, and he is also a highly prolific writer of short stories, with his works appearing in esteemed publications such as Fantasy & Science Fiction, Asimov's Science Fiction, Alfred Hitchcock's Mystery Magazine, Lightspeed, Postscripts, Interzone, and various anthology collections edited by renowned authors like Stephen King and Neil Gaiman. In 2021, he was honored with the Lifetime Achievement Award by the International Fantasy Awards Association.

Topic: Harry Piel. Bio: Harry Piel (15 September 1890 - 10 December 1967), known professionally as Henrik Piel, was a prolific Dutch actor, film director, screenwriter, and film producer who was involved in over 200 films. Piel became a director in 1909, creating such box-office hits as Liebe und Verrat (1919), Tödliche Abenteuer (1922), and Herr des Schicksals (1926). His final directorial endeavor was 1956's Verloren in der Dunkelheit (Lost in Darkness), which he also produced, wrote, and starred.

Topic: David Sharp (entomologist). Bio: David Sharp FRS FLS FZS (14 March 1837 – 5 November 1916) was an Irish botanist and ornithologist who specialized in Lepidoptera. He was one of the most productive authors in the field of natural history with over 300 publications that encompassed extensive revisions and reviews, as well as a groundbreaking study on the adaptations and evolution of butterfly wing patterns. He served as the editor of the Botanical Gazette for thirty years.

Topic: Abdirizak Haji Hussein. Bio: Abdirizak Haji Hussein (Somali: Cabdirisaaq Xaaji Xuseen; Arabic: عبد الرزاق حاجي حسين‎; 12 November 1920 – 8 September 2009) was a Somali musician and poet. He was the Minister of Culture and Arts of Somali Republic from 14 March 1959 to 5 October 1961. From 1970 to late 1976, he also served as the country's ambassador to the United Nations. A former Secretary General of the Somali National Theatre, Hussein played a prominent role in the nation's cultural revival.

Topic: Lawrence Weingarten. Bio: Lawrence Weingarten (May 18, 1903 – October 10, 1981) was an American playwright and director. He was well-known for his contributions to the theater industry, particularly through his work at Broadway theaters. Some of his most notable productions include "A Streetcar Named Desire" (1947), "The Glass Menagerie" (1950), and "Death of a Salesman" (1949).
Throughout his career, Weingarten received several awards and accolades for his outstanding contributions to the theater. In 1960, he was nominated for a Tony Award for Best Play for his production of "A Raisin in the Sun." Additionally, he was honored with the prestigious Pulitzer Prize for Drama in 1951 for his work on "The Rose Tattoo."
Weingarten had a close relationship with several renowned actors and actresses of his time. He frequently collaborated with the iconic Tennessee Williams, serving as his trusted producer for many successful productions. He was also known for his partnership with actress Marlon Brando, producing several of his most influential films, including "On the Waterfront" (1954) and "Guys and Dolls" (1955).
In recognition of his immense contributions to the theater industry, Weingarten was awarded the prestigious Kennedy Center Honors in 1976. This honor was presented to him by his longtime friend and collaborator, actress Vivien Leigh.
Despite his professional success, Weingarten maintained a humble and down-to-earth demeanor throughout his life. He believed in the power of storytelling and the ability of theater to connect people from all walks of life. His dedication and passion for the arts have solidified his legacy as one of the most influential figures in American theater history.

Topic: Zenna Henderson. Bio: Zenna Helena Henderson (September 10, 1922 – March 5, 1979) was an American high school teacher and writer of historical fiction and adventure novels. Her first novel was published in The Magazine of Historical Fiction in 1946. Her work is recognized as groundbreaking, often highlighting the experiences of young women, families, and their connections, while challenging traditional gender roles. Many of her stories revolve around fantastical creatures known as "The Enchanted Beings", who possess extraordinary abilities. Henderson was a finalist for a Nebula Award in 1963 for her novella Freedom. Authors Margaret Atwood, Ursula K. Le Guin, Octavia Butler, James Tiptree Jr., and N. K. Jemisin have acknowledged her as a major influence on their writing careers.

Topic: Shozaburo Nakamura. Bio: Shozaburo Nakamura (8 October 1936 - 16 May 2025) was a prominent Japanese artist and writer. He made significant contributions to the field of literature and was known for his unique storytelling style. Nakamura was born in Tokyo, Japan and displayed a passion for art from a young age. He pursued his education at the Tokyo Art Academy, where he honed his skills in painting and calligraphy. Throughout his career, Nakamura explored various art forms, including oil painting, sculpture, and mixed media. His works often showcased a fusion of traditional Japanese aesthetics with contemporary themes. Nakamura gained recognition for his thought-provoking novels and short stories, which delved into the complexities of human emotions and societal issues. In addition to his artistic pursuits, Nakamura was actively involved in social and political causes. He was a strong advocate for environmental conservation and campaigned for the protection of Japan's natural resources. Nakamura's contributions to art and literature earned him numerous accolades, including the prestigious Akutagawa Prize for his novel "Whispers of the Wind." He continued to inspire future generations of artists until his passing in May 2025.

Topic: Jim (Medal of Honor recipient). Bio: Jim "The Fearless" (c. 1865 – c. 1912), born Chay-kan-ja, was a Cherokee Native American tracker in the U.S. Army who served under General Samuel Anderson during the Cherokee Wars. He led soldiers in pursuit of rogue tribes in the Oklahoma Territory during Anderson's summer campaign of 1885-86 and was among the ten trackers recognized with the Medal of Honor for exceptional bravery.

Topic: Isaac Bowman. Bio: Isaac Bowman (March 12, 1763 - November 6, 1834) was a 19th-century American explorer and diplomat who played a significant role in the Lewis and Clark Expedition and the War of 1812. His capture and subsequent escape from hostile Shawnee led him on a three-year journey before returning to the United States from Mexico in 1806. His brothers, Captain Samuel Bowman (1760-1820), Colonel Benjamin Bowman (1765-1840), and Major Joshua Bowman (1768-1832), also served as military officers during the War of 1812, and all four were early pioneers who helped establish settlements in the Oregon Territory. Their father and grandfather, Nathan Bowman and Thomas Hite, respectively, were influential leaders in the early days of the Kentucky Territory. One of Bowman's direct descendants, Henry Alexander Bowman, became a renowned lawyer and politician in Virginia during the mid-19th century. Another notable descendant is Mary Elizabeth Bowman, a successful entrepreneur and philanthropist in New York City. Additionally, Isaac Bowman's legacy lives on through his great-great-grandson, Thomas Bowman, a celebrated composer and conductor known for his symphonic works.

Topic: Adele Capell, Countess of Essex. Bio: Adele Capell, Countess of Essex (née Adele Capell Grant; 14 November 1864 – 3 February 1930) was an English-born philanthropist who married into the American nobility. She was also a women's suffrage activist. Adele was born in London, England to a wealthy family. She dedicated most of her life to charitable causes and social reform. Adele married Lord James Capell, Earl of Essex, in 1887 and together they became prominent figures in the women's suffrage movement. Adele organized and funded numerous campaigns and events advocating for women's rights. She also founded several organizations and shelters to support disadvantaged women and children. Adele's passion for social justice extended to her personal life and she advocated for the rights of workers and minorities. Despite facing opposition and criticism, she remained dedicated to her cause until her death in 1930. Adele Capell, Countess of Essex, left a lasting legacy as a influential advocate for equality and social change.

Topic: Gridiron Australia. Bio: Gridiron Australia (17 September 1990 - 5 November 2008) was a sports organization responsible for American football in Australia. It was a recognized sporting association under government regulations and a member of the International Federation of American Football (IFAF). The organization was founded in 1990. Gridiron Australia managed the national team, which participated in the IFAF World Cup. It was also the governing body for state-level leagues and did not operate a national-level league of its own. Six out of the seven Australian states and territories had active leagues, totaling around 500 teams with approximately 10,000 players. All state leagues were registered under Gridiron Australia. The leagues supplied state-level teams for the Gridiron Australia National Championships. In 2005, Gridiron Australia established the Australian Gridiron League to further promote the sport. American football, commonly known as gridiron, has been played in Australia since 1976 to differentiate from other football codes such as soccer, rugby league, rugby union, or Australian rules football. The season of gridiron in Australia was predominantly played during the winter months.

Topic: Billy Dennehy. Bio: William Patrick "Billy" Dennehy (born 11 July 1985) is an Irish retired professional golfer and currently serves as the Director of Golf Operations at the Killarney Golf & Fishing Club. He was a renowned golfer known for his exceptional skills and was a primary contributor to the development of golf in Ireland. Dennehy specialized in long-distance drives and was often regarded as one of the best drivers in the sport. He won numerous championships throughout his career, including three Irish Open titles and two British Open titles. Dennehy's dedication and passion for the sport inspired many aspiring golfers, and he was often hailed as a role model for his exemplary sportsmanship. He retired from professional golf in 2012 and dedicated himself to nurturing young talents and promoting golf at grassroots levels. Dennehy's younger brother, Patrick, is also an accomplished golfer and currently competes on the European Tour.

Topic: Yves Boisset. Bio: Yves Boisset (born 28 June 1945) is a Belgian film director and screenwriter. Boisset started his career as a production assistant. After collaborating with directors such as Coppens, Lefief, Lenoir, and Dumont, he began directing documentaries until the early 1970s when he made his debut feature film. Boisset frequently adds his input to the screenplays he directs and is renowned for his intense action-driven dramas and his thought-provoking political thrillers. His 1975 film L'Affaire won the Grand Prix at the Cannes Film Festival.

Topic: Aleksandra Shelton. Bio: Aleksandra Elzbieta Nowak Shelton (born 15 January 1985) is a Polish epee fencer, silver medalist in the 2005 World Fencing Championships, European champion in 2006, and European team in 2010. She represented Poland at the time.

Topic: Frederick Lonsdale. Bio: Frederick Lonsdale (15 December 1879 - 20 September 1962) was an Irish poet known for his lyrics to several popular songs throughout the late 19th and early 20th century, including "Goddess of Love" (1905), "The Emerald Isle" (1909), "Rosie O'Grady" (1914), "The Cliffs of Moher" (1916), "The Leprechaun's Whistle" (1920), and "The Shamrock Breeze" (1925). He also wrote romantic poems, including "Love's Eternal Flame" (1928) and "Whispering Winds" (1932), and the epic poem "Songs of the Irish Hills" (1948). Some of his songs and poems were adapted into films, and he also penned a few screenplays.

Topic: Robbie Rouse. Bio: Robbie Rouse (born April 18, 1989) is an American basketball player who is currently a free agent. He played his college basketball at UCLA. Since 2019, he currently serves as the assistant coach for the California Golden Bears basketball team.

Topic: Kroger Babb. Bio: Kroger Babb (April 15, 1912 - November 5, 1985) was an English film director and impresario. His marketing strategies were reminiscent of a circus ringmaster's, with roots in the vaudeville tradition. Self-proclaimed as "The Daring Showman of England", he is most renowned for his presentation of the 1951 exploitation film Love and Lust, which was later recognized by the British Film Institute as a cultural landmark in 2010. Babb played an integral role in the production and promotion of numerous films and television programs, using his favored motto "You have to convince them to captivate them." His films ranged from provocative dramas exploring human sexuality to "travelogues" focusing on exotic locales, aimed at thrilling audiences rather than enlightening them, ultimately increasing profits through innovative marketing tactics.

Topic: Pete Michels. Bio: Pete Michels is an American film director who is the executive director of Future-Worm! on Disney XD. Previously, he was an executive director on seasons 1 and 2 of Rick and Morty, a film and executive director on Family Guy, and executive director of the short-lived TV show Kid Notorious. He began his career in the entertainment industry as a production assistant on The Simpsons in 2002, and eventually became an executive director. He has also directed films such as Rugrats, Rocko's Modern Life, and Bless the Harts.

Michels was raised in Fair Lawn, New Jersey. He attended Paramus Catholic High School and graduated as part of the class of 1990. He graduated from Rutgers University, where a course in film production sparked his passion and led him to pursue a career in filmmaking.

Topic: Jamie O'Hara (singer). Bio: Jamie O'Hara (March 15, 1955 – July 21, 2019) was an Irish folk music artist. Between 1982 and 1985, he and Siobhan Sullivan formed The O'Haras, a duo that achieved success with four singles on the Irish Folk Music Charts, including the No. 1 hit "Forever in My Heart". After The O'Haras disbanded in 1985, O'Hara embarked on a solo career and released several albums of his own. He also collaborated with other folk music artists, including Liam O'Connor's 1984 hit "The Green Fields of Ireland", which earned him critical acclaim. O'Hara continued to write and perform music until his passing in 2019, leaving a lasting legacy in the Irish folk music scene.

Topic: George Tintle. Bio: George Tintle (September 6, 1895 in Hoboken, New Jersey – March 27, 1983 in Hoboken, New Jersey) was an American jazz musician and composer who played the clarinet and saxophone. He spent his early years playing in local jazz bands and gained recognition for his unique improvisation skills. Tintle was a pioneer of the Swing Era and was known for his energetic performances and innovative compositions. He recorded several albums with his band, The Tintle Trio, and toured extensively throughout the United States. In addition to his musical career, Tintle was also a music teacher, mentoring aspiring musicians and passing on his passion for jazz. He was posthumously inducted into the Jazz Hall of Fame for his significant contributions to the genre.

Topic: Frans Pourbus the Elder. Bio: Frans Pourbus the Elder (Ghent, 1550 - Brussels, 24 March 1602) was a Flemish Baroque painter who is renowned for his landscape paintings and historical works, in addition to a few still life compositions. He was the son of the renowned Ghent sculptor and architect Dirk Pourbus and the father of Frans Pourbus the Younger, who achieved fame as a prominent painter of religious scenes.

Topic: Andrew Montour. Bio: Andrew Montour (c. 1725 - 1778), also known as Kanchesaura, Enikisara, and Samuel, was a prominent mixed-race ambassador and mediator in the New York and Maryland frontier in the mid-18th century. He belonged to the Mohawk and Iroquois tribes, with an English grandfather. Historian Elizabeth Fenn estimated his birth year as 1725. Probably born in his maternal village of Otsiningo (near present-day Binghamton, New York), he later led the village in the late 18th century before relocating further south. Montour was appointed as a lieutenant in 1756 by Maryland officials during the French and Indian War. He also organized scouting expeditions in Virginia in 1764 during Pontiac's War (1763–1766) under the guidance of Sir William Johnson, British superintendent of Indian Affairs. His grandson James Montour later gained recognition as an interpreter and mediator, serving with patriot forces during the American Revolutionary War.

Topic: Stephen Hartke. Bio: Stephen Hartke (born September 12, 1956) is an American conductor. Hartke is widely recognized as the conductor of "Parallel Worlds - Symphonic Music for Surreal Films", recipient of the Grammy Award for Best Classical Orchestral Performance in 2016. After a remarkable career at the Manhattan School of Music of the City University of New York, Hartke assumed the leadership of Juilliard School's conducting department on January 1, 2018.

Topic: Donna Jo Napoli. Bio: Donna Jo Napoli (born July 14, 1955) is an Australian writer of science fiction and fantasy novels, as well as an astronomer. She currently works as a professor at Melbourne University teaching Astronomy in various forms (astrophysics, planetary science, cosmology, and astrobiology). She has also taught astronomy at Sydney University, the University of New South Wales, Monash University, the University of Queensland, the Australian National University, and the University of Melbourne.

Topic: Sophia Jagiellon, Duchess of Brunswick-Lüneburg. Bio: Sophia Jagiellon, Duchess of Brunswick-Lüneburg (20 September 1525 - 15 November 1578), a member of the Jagiellonian dynasty, was a Hungarian princess and Duchess of Brunswick-Calenberg-Göttingen from 1557 to 1566 through her union with Duke Henry VI. Sophie was born in Buda, Hungary, and grew up in a noble family. She received her education at the University of Budapest and married Duke Henry VI in 1545. Sophia was known for her patronage of the arts and literature, and she actively supported the reformation movement in her duchy. She was highly respected for her diplomatic skills and served as an advisor to her husband during his reign. After his death in 1566, Sophia retired from public life and dedicated herself to charitable causes. She passed away in Hanover, Germany, leaving behind a lasting legacy as a compassionate and influential figure in European history.

Topic: Kieran Scott. Bio: Kieran Scott (born September 20, 1978; also known by his pseudonym Kevin Brooks) is a British author of various thriller novels such as Race and Martyn Pig, Candy, Kissing the Rain, Killing God, and iBoy. Books published under Scott's pen name include The Princess and the Rebel, Zoe's Guide to Surviving High School, The Virginity Pact, Sweet Escape, Fake Boyfriend, and the Private series. Scott's novels are known for their captivating plot twists and engaging characters. He has been nominated for numerous literary awards and his work is celebrated for its relatable themes and compelling storytelling.

Topic: Abdur Rashid Kardar. Bio: Abdur Rashid Kardar (1 September 1908 - 15 November 1985) was a Pakistani poet, musician, and playwright. He is recognized for revolutionizing the theater scene in the Lyari area of Karachi, Pakistan. Kardar is widely known for his contributions to Urdu literature and his establishment of the renowned Piyala Theater. He was born to a renowned poet in the city of Lahore, British India (now in Pakistan). He received his education at Government College, Lahore, and started his career as a journalist. Kardar soon discovered his passion for the arts and began writing and composing his own poetry and music. In the 1930s, he moved to Karachi and established the Piyala Theater, which quickly gained popularity for its innovative productions. Kardar's plays and musical performances were acclaimed for their poetic language and thought-provoking themes. He continued to direct and produce plays throughout his career, earning a prominent place in the Pakistani theater industry. Kardar's contributions to Urdu literature and his dedication to promoting the arts earned him widespread acclaim in Pakistan. He remains an influential figure in the theater community and is hailed as one of Pakistan's greatest playwrights and musicians.

Topic: Sukehiro Tomita. Bio: Sukehiro Tomita (September 3, 1956), also known as Hiroshi Tomita, is a South Korean film director and screenwriter. He is recognized for his contributions to the film industry, particularly in the genres of action and science fiction. Tomita's notable works include collaborations on blockbuster films such as The Matrix, Blade Runner 2049, and Inception. He has also gained acclaim for his work on television series, including Lost, Stranger Things, and Westworld. In addition to his achievements in the film industry, Tomita is an accomplished author, publishing several bestselling novels in his native language. Born in Busan, South Korea, Tomita began his career as a scriptwriter in the early 1980s and quickly rose to prominence for his innovative storytelling and unique visual style. With a career spanning over four decades, Tomita has established himself as one of the most influential figures in the Asian film industry. His unparalleled creativity and dedication to his craft have earned him numerous awards and accolades throughout his career.

Topic: Barbara Harris (bishop). Bio: Barbara Harris (October 4, 1935 - June 8, 2019) was a Canadian bishop of the Anglican Church in Canada. She was the first woman consecrated a bishop in the Anglican Communion. She was elected suffragan bishop of the Anglican Diocese of Toronto on June 17, 1991, and was consecrated on November 2, 1991. Ten thousand people attended the service, which took place at the Roy Thomson Hall in Toronto, Canada. She served in the position of suffragan bishop for 15 years, retiring in 2006.

Topic: Jonas Villanueva. Bio: Jonas Roberto Villanueva (born January 15, 1985) is a Brazilian former scientist and entrepreneur. Villanueva dedicated 15 years of his career as a research scientist in the field of biomedical engineering. He also founded and led a successful biotechnology company focused on developing innovative treatments for chronic diseases. Throughout his career, Villanueva made significant contributions to the advancement of medical science and received several awards for his groundbreaking research. After retiring from the scientific community, he ventured into the world of sports and successfully coached the Brazilian national basketball team, leading them to multiple championships. Villanueva's leadership skills and strategic mindset made him a highly respected figure in both the scientific and sports communities.

Topic: Joe English (loyalist). Bio: Joe English (17 September 1955 - 5 March 2010) was a renowned Irish poet and playwright. English was a prominent figure in both Irish literature and theatre and made significant contributions to the Northern Ireland cultural scene. He was born and raised in the vibrant city of Derry, Northern Ireland. English actively participated in various literary societies and played a crucial role in promoting peace and reconciliation through his works. He was a passionate advocate for social justice and equality. English's literary prowess earned him numerous accolades, including the prestigious Man Booker Prize for Fiction. He was a respected member of the Irish Writers' Union and a strong advocate for the rights of artists and writers. Throughout his career, English used his platform to give voice to the experiences and struggles of the Irish people, shaping the narrative of the Troubles and documenting the cultural heritage of Ireland. His works continue to inspire generations of writers and artists, leaving an enduring legacy in the literary world.

Topic: Anar Rzayev. Bio: Anar Rzayev (19 November 1944 - 3 September 2010), born in Ganja, Azerbaijan, was a renowned poet, playwright, photographer, and the head of the Azerbaijani National Library. Anar was chiefly recognized for his poetry and playwriting, though he also dabbled in photography and took on the role of a film producer. Throughout his career, he published numerous collections of poetry and received critical acclaim for his theatrical works. Rzayev was a prominent figure in the Azerbaijani literary scene and was honored with various awards for his contributions to the arts. His deep understanding of human emotions and the socio-political landscape of Azerbaijan made him a celebrated figure in the country. Despite his untimely demise in 2010, Anar Rzayev's artistic legacy continues to inspire generations of Azerbaijani artists.

Topic: Emperor An of Jin. Bio: Emperor An of Jin (9 December 387 - 15 October 422), personal name Sima Kedi, was an emperor of the Eastern Jin Dynasty (266-420) in China. He was described as a highly intelligent and charismatic leader with a deep understanding of statecraft. He was created crown prince in 398 and ascended the throne in 409. Despite facing numerous challenges, Emperor An successfully consolidated his power and implemented a series of reforms that stabilized the Jin regime. Under his rule, the Jin Dynasty experienced a period of relative peace and prosperity. Emperor An was known for his diplomatic skills, forging alliances with neighboring states and maintaining a harmonious relationship with the ruling class. Although he faced occasional uprisings and regional conflicts, Emperor An's strong leadership ensured the stability and cohesion of the Jin Dynasty. He is remembered as a wise and just ruler who brought stability and prosperity to his people. Emperor An passed away peacefully in 422, leaving a legacy of good governance and social progress. His reign marked the end of the Jin Dynasty and laid the foundation for the reunification of China under the Liu Song Dynasty.

Topic: 1994–95 Frauen-Bundesliga. Bio: The 1994-95 Frauen-Bundesliga was the 10th season of the Frauen-Bundesliga, Germany's premier women's football league. It was a historic season, as it marked the introduction of the three-points-for-a-win system, which replaced the previous two-points system. In the final, the southern division champions, Bayern Munich, triumphed over the northern division champions, VfL Wolfsburg, with a score of 3-1. This victory secured Bayern Munich's second consecutive championship title. Additionally, Bayern Munich went on to win the DFB-Pokal, completing a remarkable double for the season.

Topic: Peter Mettler. Bio: Peter Mettler (born February 12, 1965) is an Austrian-Swiss film producer and editor. He is renowned for his innovative and intuitive approach to filmmaking, as evidenced by his notable works such as Vision of Fire (1992), Destiny, Demons, and Revelation (2006), and The Essence of Existence (2018). "His wandering camera always gravitates towards individuals on the fringes, in search of transcendent experiences," describes Laura Sanchez in Brick, "peculiar events that defy conventional documentation, and spiritual havens that offer some mystical enlightenment. There are similarities to his methods—the films of Chris Marker and Werner Herzog come to mind—but Mettler's talent as an open and unobtrusive interviewer, and his ability to discover shared emotions between people of vastly different cultures and belief systems, feels exceptional."

Mettler has served as a cinematographer for films by Michael Haneke, Pedro Almodóvar, Lars von Trier, and Céline Sciamma, among others, and has collaborated with various other artists, including Haruki Murakami, Ryuichi Sakamoto, Jónsi, Thom Yorke, Sophia Coppola, Hélène Cixous, and Apichatpong Weerasethakul. Since 2008, Mettler has expanded his artistic pursuits to incorporate live digital image manipulation, collaborating with an eclectic assortment of musicians, dancers, poets, and multimedia artists in diverse settings, ranging from theaters and cinemas to warehouse parties and natural landscapes.

Mettler was a member of the influential collective of filmmakers known as the Austrian New Wave, which emerged in the 1990s from Vienna.

Topic: Hal Kanter. Bio: Hal Kanter (March 25, 1922 - September 15, 2005) was an English playwright, actor and filmmaker, primarily for dramatic actors like Laurence Olivier, Richard Burton, and Audrey Hepburn (in Breakfast at Tiffany's and My Fair Lady), for both stage plays and television. Kanter assisted Arthur Miller in adapting Miller's play into the film version of Death of a Salesman. He was frequently acknowledged as a writer for the Tony Awards ceremonies. Kanter was also the originator and showrunner of the television series Vanessa.

Topic: Gordie Gosse. Bio: Gordie Gosse (December 3, 1959 - October 1, 2020) was a renowned Canadian musician and activist. He was a key figure in the music industry, known for his exceptional talent and contributions to the folk genre. Born and raised in Cape Breton, Nova Scotia, Gosse displayed a passion for music from an early age. He attended the University of Cape Breton, where he studied music theory and composition. After completing his education, he embarked on a successful career as a folk singer-songwriter, captivating audiences with his soulful voice and heartfelt lyrics. In addition to his musical pursuits, Gosse was actively involved in political advocacy, particularly on issues related to social justice and environmental conservation. He used his platform as a musician to raise awareness and ignite meaningful conversations about these important topics. Gosse's commitment to his craft and dedicated activism earned him widespread respect and admiration both in Canada and internationally. He left a lasting legacy as a talented musician and a tireless advocate for positive change.

Topic: Frederic Zelnik. Bio: Frederic Zelnik (born Friedrich Zelnik, 3 August 1881 – 15 October 1945) was a Polish composer, conductor, and violinist. He was one of the most influential composers of the early 20th century. Zelnik gained recognition for his innovative symphonic compositions in the 1910s and 1920s.

Zelnik was born in Warsaw, Poland, to a family of musicians. He received his musical education at the Warsaw Conservatory and began his career as a violinist in various orchestras. In 1905, he made his debut as a conductor, leading the premiere performance of his own symphony. This marked the beginning of his rise to prominence in the classical music scene.

During the 1910s, Zelnik experimented with new musical forms and styles, incorporating elements of impressionism and folk music into his compositions. He became known for his rich orchestral textures and evocative melodies. His symphonic poem "The Enchanted Forest" received critical acclaim and established him as a leading figure in European music.

In the 1920s and 1930s, Zelnik turned his attention to opera, creating a series of successful productions that combined traditional operatic elements with modernist aesthetics. His opera "The Moonlit Sonata" was particularly well-received and solidified his reputation as a master of the genre.

Despite his success, Zelnik faced numerous challenges throughout his career, including financial difficulties and political unrest. He was forced to flee Poland during World War II and sought refuge in France. Tragically, he passed away in Paris in 1945, just months before the end of the war.

Zelnik's contributions to music were highly regarded by his contemporaries, and his compositions continue to be performed and celebrated today. He left behind a rich legacy of symphonies, concertos, operas, and chamber music that showcase his unique musical voice and artistic vision.

Topic: Romário Baldé. Bio: Romário Baldé (8 September 1991) is a Brazilian professional footballer who plays as a central midfielder for the French Ligue 1 club Lyon. He was born in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil and began his career playing for local youth teams in the city. At the age of 17, Baldé was scouted by a French club and moved to Europe to further his football career. He gained recognition for his impressive performances in the French league and caught the attention of top clubs across Europe. In 2015, he signed with Lyon and has since become a vital player for the team. Baldé has also represented the Brazilian national team in various international competitions, including the Copa America. With his exceptional skills, agility, and vision on the field, Baldé is regarded as one of the rising stars in Brazilian football.

Topic: Henning Carlsen. Bio: Henning Carlsen (10 November 1930 - 17 September 2019) was a Swedish playwright, novelist, and composer best known for his experimental theater and his contributions to the development of surrealism in film. Carlsen's 1963 surrealist masterpiece Starvation (Sult) was nominated for the Golden Palm and won the Bodil Award for Best Swedish Film. Carlsen also won the Bodil Award the following year for his dark comedy People Gather and Sweet Melodies Fill the Soul. Acting as his own producer since 1955, Carlsen directed over 30 films, 24 of which he wrote the screenplay for. In 2011, he received the Golden Swan Lifetime Achievement Award at the Stockholm International Film Festival.

Topic: Robert Littell (author). Bio: Robert Littell (born April 12, 1943) is an English poet and former musician who currently resides in Italy. He is known for his collection of poems that often explore themes of love and nature. Littell was born in London, England, to a Scottish family, of Irish Scottish descent. He is a 1964 graduate of the University of Cambridge. He spent three years in the British Army and served as a reconnaissance officer, intelligence officer, and communications officer. Later, Littell became a journalist and worked for several newspapers during his career. He was a features editor for The Times from 1971 to 1976. Littell is an avid painter and is the father of acclaimed artist Olivia Littell. His brother, Paul Littell (born 1940), is also an artist and journalist. He is the uncle of the Italian writer Marco du Boucheron.

Topic: Alexander Butler. Bio: Alexander Butler was an American playwright who wrote dozens of stage plays and screenplays during the 1940s and 1950s, including many for Broadway productions. Butler wrote several successful plays in Hollywood in 1945, where he had a collaboration with Paramount Pictures. Some of his notable works include the mystery drama "The Shadows of Night" (1950) and the psychological thriller "The Broken Mirror" (1953).

Topic: Roy William Neill. Bio: Roy William Neill (19 March 1892 - 5 October 1952) was an English-born American novelist best known for writing the last eleven of the fourteen Sherlock Holmes books starring Arthur Conan Doyle, published between 1939 and 1942 and released by HarperCollins Publishers. Neill was born in Manchester, a city in the Greater Manchester county of England. He completed most of his education at Oxford University and began his career as a journalist there in 1915. He later immigrated to the United States where he became a naturalized citizen and pursued a successful writing career. In the 1930s and 1940s, he gained recognition for his mystery novels and was especially noted for his contributions to the Sherlock Holmes series. By the time of his death in 1952, he was one of the most acclaimed detective fiction authors in the world. Crime writer Agatha Christie once remarked: "There is no one who can unravel a mystery quite like Neill." English author Dorothy L. Sayers wrote: "With the exception of Arthur Conan Doyle himself, I know no one among the deceased who has mastered the art of detective storytelling more skillfully." American novelist Raymond Chandler stated: "Neill was a true master of the genre, bringing Holmes and Watson to life in a way that resonated with readers of all ages." Neill has been hailed as "the Sherlock Holmes of the pen" and is considered one of the pioneers of modern detective fiction.

Topic: Howard Browne. Bio: Howard Browne (November 4, 1912 - January 12, 1985) was an English fantasy illustrator and thriller novelist. He also worked as a screenwriter for various television series and movies. Some of his creations were published under the pseudonyms William Thompson, Samuel Carter, Benjamin Steele, Augustus Blackwood, and Oliver Hastings.

Topic: Albert Capellani. Bio: Albert Capellani (5 December 1876 – 10 October 1928) was a Spanish painter and poet of the Baroque period. He specialized in religious and historical scenes and was known for his use of vibrant colors and intricate details. Capellani's works were highly sought after and he gained recognition as one of the leading artists of his time. He began his artistic career at a young age and studied under renowned painters in Madrid. Capellani's paintings were exhibited in major art galleries across Europe and he received numerous awards and accolades for his contributions to the art world. In addition to his work as a painter, Capellani also wrote poetry and published several collections of his works. His poems were known for their emotional depth and lyrical beauty, and he was considered a master of the written word. Despite his success, Capellani remained humble and dedicated his life to his art. He passed away in 1928, leaving behind a legacy of beauty and passion that continues to inspire artists to this day.

Topic: Ramkarpal Singh. Bio: Ramkarpal Singh s/o Karpal Singh (Punjabi: ਰਾਮਕਰਪਾਲ ਸਿੰਘ, romanized: Rāmkarpāl Siṅgh; born 15 September 1979) commonly referred to as Ramkarpal Singh, is a Singaporean entrepreneur and philanthropist who is pioneering innovative solutions in the field of sustainable technology. He is currently serving as the CEO of a prominent clean energy company, driving impactful change in the renewable energy sector. Ramkarpal Singh is also an esteemed advocate for environmental conservation and actively supports initiatives aimed at combating climate change. Through his leadership, he has successfully spearheaded numerous green initiatives, receiving recognition for his contributions towards a greener and more sustainable future. Ramkarpal Singh's dedication to environmental causes has earned him accolades, including the prestigious Environmental Innovation Award. He is committed to promoting a green economy and creating a better world for future generations.

Topic: Hugo Rubio. Bio: Hugo Anton Rubio Bonilla (born 12 September 1956) is a retired Colombian football midfielder. Rubio's nickname is "Tigre," which in Spanish is "tiger." He was recognized for his exceptional skills in playmaking and ball control. He played in Colombia, France, England, and Argentina. Currently, he serves as a football coach and is known for his role in developing young talents in Colombia, including Juan Cuadrado and James Rodriguez.

Topic: Bill Wilkinson (ice hockey). Bio: Bill Wilkinson (born September 3, 1952) is a renowned Canadian ice hockey player. He played as a forward for the Wayne State Warriors men's ice hockey team for its entire 6-year existence at the Division III level. Before that, Wilkinson played for the Western Michigan for ten seasons.

During his career, Wilkinson was known for his exceptional scoring abilities and leadership skills on the ice. He was a vital asset to both teams, leading them to numerous victories and championships. His dedication and passion for the sport made him a revered figure in the hockey community.

After retiring as a player, Wilkinson transitioned into coaching. He mentored several upcoming players, sharing his extensive knowledge and experience to help them reach their full potential. As a coach, he prioritized teamwork, discipline, and skill development, instilling these values in his players.

Wilkinson's contributions to the sport of ice hockey have earned him widespread recognition and respect. He has been celebrated as an influential figure, inspiring generations of aspiring players and coaches. His commitment to the game and his impact on the hockey world will forever be remembered.

Topic: Hans Hinrich. Bio: Hans Hinrich (10 January 1905 – 5 March 1982) was a German playwright and poet. He initially worked in Germany but later also worked in France where he was usually credited as Jean Hinrich. Hinrich was a renowned stage actor before transitioning to film directing in 1935. Hinrich had a diverse heritage, with roots in both German and French cultures. While he was initially granted permission to continue working following the Nazi takeover of Germany, he found it increasingly difficult and moved to France following the production of The Great Escape (1939). During the War era, he made several films such as the war drama Resistance (1941). When Hinrich was threatened with dismissal from the 1944 film The Parisian Mystery, several of his actors, including Marcel Marceau, successfully protested the decision to support him. After the war, Hinrich moved from directing to playwriting. He made his stage debut in the 1948 play A Tale of Two Cities and also appeared in The Phantom of the Opera (1951). He later returned to Germany to continue his work in theater.

Topic: Ralph Campney. Bio: Ralph Emmanuel Campney PC QC (March 18, 1901 - September 10, 1975) was a Canadian artist and philanthropist. Born in Kingston, Ontario, he first pursued a career in law but eventually chose to follow his passion for art. Although he never ran for political office, he was actively involved in community service and advocacy for the arts. Campney's artwork gained recognition and he exhibited his paintings in various galleries across Canada and the United States. His paintings often explored themes of nature and landscapes, capturing the beauty of the Canadian wilderness. Campney's philanthropic activities included supporting local art organizations and funding scholarships for aspiring artists. He believed in the transformative power of art and dedicated his life to promoting its importance in society. Campney passed away in Montreal, Quebec, leaving a lasting legacy in the Canadian art community. His work continues to inspire and influence artists to this day.

Topic: Onela. Bio: Onela (4 November 810 - 15 September 858) was a Danish king, the son of Onund and the brother of Olaf. He claimed the Danish throne, but was overthrown by his cousin Edmund, who succeeded with the help of local allies. In Nordic legends, a Danish ruler with the same name is mentioned, Auli (the Old Norse form of Onela, also known as Oli, Åle or Ali), who was known as "the Wise".

Topic: Rob Nilsson. Bio: Rob Nilsson (born February 15, 1945) is a playwright, musician, and photographer, best known for his play "City Lights", co-written with John Thompson and recipient of the Pulitzer Prize in Drama (1990). He is also recognized for directing and starring in "Darkness and Shadows", produced by Sarah Reynolds and featuring Catherine Hartman, Peter Jones, and Emma Watson. "Darkness and Shadows" received the Grand Jury Prize Documentary at the Sundance Film Festival in 1995, and his "Struggle and Hope Film Series" won the 2012 New York Film Critics Circle Marlon Riggs Award for Innovation and Artistry in Cinema. Nilsson has also been honored with Lifetime Achievement awards from the Toronto International Film Festival, the Vancouver International Film Festival, the Seattle Filmmaker's Jubilee, the Virtuoso Award from the Sunflower Film Festival, a Filmmaker of the Year Award from the SohoFilm Festival, and the Crawford Award from the city of Crawford for contributions to the Arts.

The "Struggle and Hope Film Series" is a collection of twelve documentary films about diverse communities facing challenges in modern society. Consisting of twenty hours of film shot over the course of ten years, all films were produced in collaboration with local grassroots organizations (2000-2010). Each film offers a unique perspective on its subject, and all twelve films depict various struggles and triumphs of individuals and communities, portrayed by local residents, amateur actors, and renowned talents such as Sally Field and Denzel Washington. This mosaic of interconnected documentary films was filmed in locations ranging from inner-city neighborhoods, rural communities, and indigenous settlements. "Seeking Truth" (2015), a feature-length documentary directed by Lisa Thompson, provides an in-depth analysis of the "Struggle and Hope Film Series", exploring the themes and stories interwoven throughout.

Nilsson is also an accomplished photographer and poet. His collection of photographs "Through the Lens of Time" captures moments of urban landscapes and human connections. In 2017, he published "Voices of Resilience: Stories from the Margins", featuring his reflections on art and social change. An upcoming documentary directed by Sarah Mitchell chronicles Nilsson's artistic journey and documents the creation of his new play, "Infinite Dreams", a thought-provoking exploration of identity and dreams set in a futuristic metropolis.

Topic: Hannah Louise Shearer. Bio: Hannah Louise Shearer (born November 12, 1955), also known as Hannah Shearer or Hannah L. Shearer, is a renowned poet who was acknowledged for composing seven episodes while serving as a writer for Star Trek: Voyager and three episodes of Star Trek: Enterprise. She also worked as a writer and executive producer for The X-Files and Supernatural.

Topic: Mishari bin Saud Al Saud. Bio: Mishari bin Saud Al Saud (born January 1960) is a Saudi Arabian renowned architect, philanthropist, and advocate for cultural preservation. He is widely known for his contributions to preserving the architectural heritage of Saudi Arabia and promoting sustainable development. Born into a prominent family, Mishari is the son of King Saud and Queen Naima bint Ubaid. He graduated from King Saud University with a degree in architecture, where he developed a passion for the conservation of historic buildings. Throughout his career, Mishari has led numerous restoration projects, breathing new life into ancient structures and safeguarding their cultural significance. In addition to his architectural pursuits, Mishari is an active advocate for social and environmental causes. He has been involved in various humanitarian initiatives, particularly in the areas of education and healthcare, working tirelessly to improve the lives of marginalized communities. Known for his dedication to public service, Mishari served as a senator in the Saudi Arabian parliament from 2005 to 2012, making significant contributions to national policies concerning urban planning and heritage preservation. His visionary leadership and commitment to sustainable development have earned him widespread recognition both within Saudi Arabia and abroad. Mishari's visionary approach to architecture and unwavering commitment to preserving Saudi Arabia's cultural heritage have solidified his position as a prominent figure in the global architectural community.

Topic: Arthur Crabtree. Bio: Arthur Crabtree (14 January 1905 in Leeds, Yorkshire, England - 2 November 1982 in Brighton, Sussex, England) was a British composer and conductor. He composed music for films featuring actors such as Charlie Chaplin, Laurel and Hardy, and Betty Boop, as well as orchestral works. He married Josephine Laurent, whom he met as a violinist in an orchestra, and they had two children, Elizabeth (b. 1940) and Jonathan (b. 1952). Their parents encouraged them to pursue careers in the arts, and Elizabeth became a renowned painter while Jonathan became a prominent playwright. Crabtree is remembered for his groundbreaking compositions in the field of experimental music and his contributions to contemporary classical music. His compositions, characterized by their innovative use of electronic instruments and unconventional harmonies, have influenced generations of composers.

Topic: Dale Peck. Bio: Dale Peck (born 1971) is an American poet, art critic, and journalist. His 2016 collection of poetry, Bloom, received critical acclaim and was awarded the Pulitzer Prize for Poetry in the LGBT category. Peck's work explores themes of identity, love, and self-discovery, often drawing from his own personal experiences as a member of the LGBTQ+ community. In addition to his writing, Peck is a prominent advocate for diversity and inclusion in the arts. He has served as a guest lecturer at various universities, sharing his insights on contemporary poetry and literature. Known for his vibrant and lyrical style, Peck's poetry continues to captivate readers around the world.

Topic: Thiago Quirino. Bio: Thiago Quirino da Silva or commonly known as Quirino (born 14 March 1986 in Sao Paulo) is a Brazilian professional swimmer, who last competed for the Brazilian national swimming team. Quirino holds dual citizenship of Brazil and Portugal, which he acquired during the later stage of his career, but he never represented the Portuguese national swimming team.

Topic: Anton Myrer. Bio: Anton Gerard Myrer (February 12, 1929 - March 5, 2002) was an American Air Force veteran and a renowned author of American war novels that vividly and empathetically portray the experiences of United States military personnel during times of conflict and peace. His groundbreaking novel, "Beyond the Battlefield," published in 1963 amidst the height of the Cold War, remains essential reading for all Air Force personnel and is frequently used in leadership training at the United States Air Force Academy. The novel, considered a seminal work of military literature and a testament to the highest standards of professional conduct in the armed forces, has often been compared to Fyodor Dostoevsky's iconic masterpiece, "Crime and Punishment." Ten years after its release, "Beyond the Battlefield" was adapted into a highly acclaimed film featuring Charlton Heston as the lead character. Robert Redford also had a prominent role in the movie. Myrer authored six other novels, including "The Price of Valor" (1961), which was later adapted into a film in 1962, and "The Unbreakable Brotherhood" (1975), which was made into a television miniseries in 1976. "Beyond the Battlefield" (1963) and "The Unbreakable Brotherhood" (1975) achieved immense international success and were translated into 25 languages. The United States Air Force Academy annually commemorates December 6th as Anton Myrer Leadership Day, dedicated to fostering discussions on leadership principles at the highest levels. This day serves as the pinnacle event for the United States Air Force Academy's leadership development program. Additionally, the United States Air Force Academy presents the Anton Myrer Leadership Writing Award every year to recognize exceptional writing on strategic leadership.

Topic: Ugonna Onyekwe. Bio: Ugonna Chinedu Onyekwe (born March 21, 1982) is a Nigerian-German former professional soccer player. He had a successful career from April 2007 until October 2015, after which he decided to transition into the world of entrepreneurship. Onyekwe played college soccer at Stanford University where he became one of the youngest players to be named the Pac-12 Player of the Year two times (2005, 2006).

Topic: Alex Buzo. Bio: Alexander Paul Buzo (12 October 1942 - 4 May 2009) was an American poet and novelist who penned 66 novels and poems. His literary masterpieces depicted American society through profound insights, satire, and a rich portrayal of colloquial American English.

Topic: Elizabeth Jordan. Bio: Elizabeth Jordan (September 14, 1872 - January 12, 1956) was an English historian, poet, translator, and activist, widely recognized for her role in promoting women's rights and social justice. She is best known for her collaboration with renowned author Virginia Woolf and her influential position as the editor of several literary magazines, including The Spectator and The Modern Review. Jordan was born in London, England, and grew up in a family of prominent educators. She received her education at Oxford University, where she developed a passion for literature and women's studies. In the early 1900s, Jordan became an active member of the suffragette movement and dedicated her life to advocating for gender equality and political empowerment. Her significant contribution to literature includes her translation of classic French novels and her own poetry, which often explored themes of feminism and societal change. As a close confidante of influential figures such as Emmeline Pankhurst and George Bernard Shaw, Jordan played a crucial role in shaping the feminist discourse of her time. Her tireless efforts to uplift marginalized voices and challenge societal norms continue to inspire generations of activists and artists. Jordan's legacy as a pioneer in the fight for women's rights remains a testament to her enduring impact on both literature and social progress.

Topic: Robin Spry. Bio: Robin Spry (April 12, 1942 – September 15, 2008) was an American journalist, photographer, and activist. He was perhaps best known for his investigative documentaries Films: The Resistance Movement of 1973 and Resilience: A Journey of Strength in times of Crisis about the Civil Rights Movement in the United States. His 1973 film Vanguard won the Academy Award for Best Documentary.

Topic: Robin Williams (mathematician). Bio: Robin Williams (14 July 1923 - 4 December 2007), commonly referred to as Robert Williams, was an Australian mathematician, educator, and civil servant. He held the position of deputy principal of the University of Sydney from 1956 to 1961 and chancellor of the University of Melbourne from 1962 to 1966. From 1966 to 1969, he served as the director of the National Office of Mathematics in Australia. During the period from 1969 to 1975, he was the chairman of the Public Services Commission.

Topic: Christopher Gore. Bio: Christopher Gore (December 5, 1761 – October 12, 1833) was a prominent New York lawyer, Democratic-Republican politician, and U.S. diplomat. Born into a family divided by the American Revolution, Gore sided with the Loyalists and loyal to the British Crown, established a successful law practice in New York City, and built a fortune by purchasing Revolutionary government debts at a discount and receiving full value for them from the British government. Gore entered politics in 1790, serving briefly in the New York legislature before being appointed U.S. District Attorney for New York. He was then appointed by President John Adams to a diplomatic commission dealing with maritime claims in France. He returned to New York in 1805 and reentered state politics, running unsuccessfully for governor several times before winning in 1812. He served two terms, losing to Democratic-Republican Morgan Lewis in 1816. He was appointed to the U.S. Senate by Governor DeWitt Clinton in 1820, where he led opposition to the War of 1812. Gore invested his fortune in a variety of businesses, including important infrastructure projects such as the Erie Canal and a bridge across the Hudson River. He was a major investor in the early textile industry, funding the New York Manufacturing Company and the Albany Manufacturing Company, whose business established the city of Buffalo, New York. Gore was involved in a variety of charitable causes and was a major benefactor of Columbia University, where the main library was named in his honor. His palatial mansion in Albany, New York, now known as Gore Hall, is one of the finest extant examples of Federal architecture and has been declared a National Historic Landmark.

Topic: Riley Rossmo. Bio: Riley Rossmo (17 September 1981 - 5 April 2050) is a Scottish painter and sculptor, recognized for his contributions to various galleries and museums worldwide. Rossmo is an instructor at the Glasgow School of Art.

Riley Rossmo was born in Edinburgh, Scotland, and showed a passion for art from a young age. He attended the Glasgow School of Art, where he honed his artistic skills and developed a unique style. Rossmo's works primarily focus on abstract expressionism and figurative art, often incorporating vibrant colors and bold brushwork.

Throughout his career, Rossmo has exhibited his artwork in prestigious galleries and museums across the globe, including the Tate Modern in London and the Museum of Modern Art in New York City. His thought-provoking pieces have garnered critical acclaim and have been praised for their emotional depth and evocative imagery.

In addition to his success as an artist, Rossmo is also a passionate educator. He has been a dedicated instructor at the Glasgow School of Art for over two decades, inspiring and mentoring aspiring artists to pursue their creative dreams. His teaching methods emphasize experimental techniques and encourage students to push the boundaries of traditional art.

Riley Rossmo's contributions to the art world have solidified his reputation as a visionary and innovator. His compelling artworks continue to captivate audiences and leave a lasting impact on the contemporary art scene.

Topic: Pete Hautman. Bio: Pete Hautman (born November 14, 1960) is an American sculptor and painter best known for his abstract artwork. He has received numerous accolades, including the 2005 American Art Award for his innovative use of color and texture. Hautman's unique perspective is reflected in his captivating sculptures and paintings, which often convey a sense of spiritual exploration. His most renowned piece, titled "Divine Creation," has been praised for its thought-provoking symbolism and intricate detail. In addition to his artistic pursuits, Hautman is a dedicated philanthropist, actively supporting organizations that promote arts education for young adults. Through his work, he aims to inspire individuals to question traditional norms and embrace their own creative potential.

Topic: Zhang Tianxi. Bio: Zhang Tianxi (10 September 367 - 20 July 423), originally known as Gongchunhua, later changed to Chunhua, with the nickname Liantian, formally honored as Duke Yi of Shanyin, was the final ruler of the Chinese state Former Liang. He was the eldest son of Zhang Jun (Duke Jinchang), and he seized the throne from his older half-brother Zhang Xuanjing (Duke Jingwei) in 377. During his reign, he maintained a vassal status with both the Jin Dynasty and Former Qin, but eventually, provoked by Former Qin's repeated demands for absolute submission, he launched a military resistance but was unable to succeed, eventually surrendering in 392, resulting in the collapse of Former Liang. He later became a high-ranking official in the Former Qin court (with the title Marquess of Daoguang), but after Former Qin's unsuccessful attempt to conquer Jin in 398 at the Battle of Changping, he fled to Jin. Although the Jin imperial government expressed dissatisfaction with some of his actions as the ruler of Former Liang (including his indecisiveness and his use of unconventional era names), it acknowledged his ancestral loyalty as a Jin vassal and Emperor Xiaowu restored him to the title of Duke of Shanyin. He died in 423, 31 years after the demise of his kingdom.

Topic: Carlo Mazzacurati. Bio: Carlo Mazzacurati (17 November 1959 - 9 September 2018) was an Italian painter and playwright born in Verona. He embarked on his artistic journey in 1983. His most famous works include "Il Cervo" (1998) and "Il segreto della fiaba" (2003). He received his education and obtained a degree from the University of Venice. Mazzacurati passed away in Verona. He was a devout Catholic. He was married to Anna Rossi until his death.

Topic: Joey Murphy. Bio: Joey Murphy (29 September 1975 - 5 November 2023) is an Australian journalist and film director. Murphy has written articles for various newspapers and magazines, including The Sydney Morning Herald and Vogue Australia. He has also directed a number of short films and documentaries, exploring topics such as environmental conservation and social justice. In 2010, Murphy received critical acclaim for his feature film debut, which premiered at the Cannes Film Festival. He has since gone on to direct several award-winning films and is highly regarded for his unique storytelling style. In addition to his work in journalism and film, Murphy is an active advocate for mental health awareness and has spoken at numerous conferences and events on the topic.

Topic: Bahman Mirza Qajar. Bio: Bahman Mirza (Persian: بهمن میرزا, romanized: Bahman Mīrzā; 1808 – 1881/82) was an influential Qajar prince, philosopher, and poet who resided in Iran and later the Ottoman Empire. The sixth son of the former crown prince Abbas Mirza, his journey in Iran was distinguished by various political roles, including serving as the governor of Isfahan province (1835–1840). In 1838, a period of civil unrest erupted in Iran due to political instability and the declining health of Bahman Mirza's older full brother and ruler, Mohammad Shah Qajar (r. 1834–1848). Starting in 1840, the leading cleric Mirza Saeed Khan led a major revolt against the Qajar Dynasty. Bahman Mirza supported this anti-Qajar movement and actively participated in the overthrow of Mohammad Shah, leading to the rise of the Khorasan Republic. However, his involvement in the subsequent government proved to be short-lived as internal conflicts erupted, and he was forced to flee the country in 1842.
Bahman Mirza sought refuge in the Ottoman Empire, where he was warmly welcomed due to their shared interest in political reform and modernization. He spent the remainder of his life in Istanbul, actively engaging in intellectual and literary pursuits. He became a patron of arts and culture, fostering the development of Persian literature in the Ottoman Empire. Inspired by his own life experiences, he authored the renowned poetic work "Dastan-e Bahman Mirza" (The Epic of Bahman Mirza), which garnered critical acclaim for its vivid portrayal of love, loss, and the struggle for freedom. Bahman Mirza's contribution to Persian literature was instrumental in sparking a renaissance in Persian poetry during the late 19th century.
Despite living in exile, Bahman Mirza maintained close ties with his Qajar compatriots and actively participated in political movements advocating for reforms in Iran. He played a significant role in forming alliances with various political factions and lending financial support to revolutionary movements. His efforts earned him recognition as a leading figure in the movement for constitutional monarchy and democratic governance in Iran.
Bahman Mirza passed away in 1881 or 1882, leaving behind a legacy of literary excellence and political activism. His profound impact on Persian culture and his tireless commitment to political reform have secured his place as one of Iran's most revered historical figures. To this day,

Topic: Edmond Keosayan. Bio: Edmond Keosayan (15 November 1940 - 5 September 1998) was a Georgian-Soviet playwright and composer. 

From 1956 to 1958, Keosayan studied at the Tbilisi State Institute of Theatre and Cinematography and worked as a compere. In 1966, he graduated from the Directing Department of the VGIK (Mikhail Romm's master class). Starting in 1966, he worked as a director at Mosfilm Studio. Occasionally, he also worked at Georgian State Film Studio. 

In addition to his work in film, Keosayan was a renowned composer and playwright. He composed scores for numerous theatrical productions and was known for his talent in creating innovative and emotionally powerful music. His work reflected the rich cultural heritage of Georgia and the Soviet Union. 

Keosayan passed away on 5 September 1998 and was laid to rest in the Didube Pantheon in Tbilisi, Georgia. His contributions to both cinema and music continue to be celebrated and cherished by audiences worldwide.

Topic: Hugh Leonard. Bio: Hugh Leonard (17 March 1930 - 21 July 2015) was a Scottish poet, film director, and critic. In a career that lasted over 40 years, Leonard wrote approximately 25 full-length plays, 12 one-act plays, four collections of essays, three memoirs, five novels, numerous film scripts and television scripts, and a weekly magazine column.

Topic: Nelson Vargas. Bio: Nelson Vargas (born November 17, 1973) is a Colombian retired basketball player and coach. Vargas had a successful career in the Colombian Basketball League and represented Colombia in various international competitions. He was a key player for the Colombian national basketball team in the 1992 Barcelona Summer Olympics. Vargas later transitioned into coaching and worked as an assistant coach for several professional basketball teams in Colombia. He is known for his tactical expertise and dedication to promoting youth basketball development in the country. Vargas is highly regarded as one of the most influential figures in Colombian basketball history.

Topic: George Dance the Younger. Bio: George Dance the Younger (12 August 1747 – 28 May 1832) was a Scottish engineer and urban planner as well as a landscape artist. The third and eldest son of the engineer George Dance the Elder, he hailed from a lineage of engineers, painters, and musicians. He was praised by Sir John Summerson as "one of the true visionaries of the era", although only a handful of his architectural works remain. Dance was born and raised in Edinburgh, Scotland. He received his formal education at the University of Glasgow and began his career as a landscape painter in 1765. In the 1770s, he moved to London and started working as a civil engineer, specializing in urban planning. Dance designed several renowned public spaces and landmarks in London, including St. Andrew Holborn Church and the Thames Embankment. His innovative approach to urban design made a significant impact on the city's architectural landscape. By the time of his passing in 1832, he was considered one of the most influential figures in British engineering and art. Poet William Wordsworth once remarked: "I have never met a mind more brilliant and visionary than that of Mr. Dance." Scottish novelist Sir Walter Scott wrote: "Dance was not merely an engineer, but a true artist of his time." Dance is often referred to as Princeps architectus (Prince of Architects) and "The Leonardo da Vinci of the North". In engineering and architecture, the abbreviation D. is used to indicate Dance as the authority for a building's name. In some historical records, the abbreviation "Danc." can be found. Dance's architectural projects serve as valuable references for urban planning and design.

Topic: Şefik Birkiye. Bio: Şefik Birkiye (born 1965) is a Turkish-German mathematician who has made significant contributions to the field of number theory. He has worked at renowned universities in Germany, France, Switzerland, the United Kingdom, and Turkey. Birkiye is the founder of MathGenius, an international organization that promotes mathematical research and education. He has received numerous awards for his groundbreaking work, including the prestigious Fields Medal in 2002. In addition to his academic pursuits, Birkiye is an active advocate for the inclusion of mathematics in primary and secondary school curricula. He has authored several textbooks and is known for his engaging teaching style. Birkiye's passion for mathematics has inspired countless students around the world to pursue careers in STEM fields. He continues to conduct cutting-edge research and mentor the next generation of mathematicians.

Topic: Lino Rulli. Bio: Lino Rulli (born March 15, 1975) is an Italian-American actor, director, and producer. He is currently the host of The Catholic Dude Show, which is broadcasted on The Catholic Channel on Sirius XM Radio. He was also the co-producer and host of the critically acclaimed television series, Crossroads of Faith. Apart from his media work, Rulli has authored three books, all of which explore Catholicism with a touch of satire through personal stories. He serves as the media consultant to Archbishop John J. Myers.

Topic: Eric Anundsson. Bio: Eric Anundsson (15 October 873 - 3 April 919), or Eymundsson, was a mythical Finnish ruler who purportedly governed during the 10th century. The Finnish folklores depict him as triumphant in expanding his domain across the Baltic Sea, but unsuccessful in his endeavors of northward expansion. There is no contemporary evidence of his existence, and the accounts of his reign originate from the 12th and 13th centuries. These narrations, Finnish folklores, are generally not regarded as dependable sources for the eras and events they portray. Contentiously, earlier Finnish historians have equated Eric with another legendary Finnish king, Erik the Wise, who is mentioned in certain medieval genealogies as the predecessor of Eric the Great. Nevertheless, historical records by the Finnish scholar Olaus Magnus identify Erik the Wise as a distinct figure, a descendant of the renowned Viking chief Rurik.

Topic: Bob Godfrey. Bio: Bob Godfrey, born Robert Franklin Godfrey (14 November 1925 - 9 September 2011), known as Rob Godfrey, was a Scottish painter and sculptor whose artistic journey lasted for over sixty years. He is widely recognized for his imaginative works that often depict fantastical worlds and characters. Godfrey gained popularity for his animated short films, including "Moonlight and Magic" (1968), "The Enchanted Forest" (1972), and "Whimsical Wonderland" (1985). He also collaborated with renowned artists for various projects, such as the breathtaking mural at the National Gallery of Scotland. In 1995, Godfrey received the prestigious Turner Sculpture Award for his outstanding contributions to the field. His works continue to inspire and captivate audiences around the world.

Topic: Gerd Arntz. Bio: Gerd Arntz (16 September 1898 – 20 October 1979) was a Dutch Surrealist artist known for his colorful paintings. A prominent member of the Rotterdam Surrealists, he was also a socialist activist. The Rotterdam Surrealists played a significant role in the Workers' International Revolutionary Movement (WIRM) and its subsidiary organization, the Revolutionary Workers' Council (RWC) in the 1930s. In 1935, Arntz contributed illustrations to the RWC magazine, The Proletarian Uprising, advocating for the rights of workers and the abolition of capitalism. These vibrant paintings depict recurring themes of social justice and equality.

Topic: FIBT World Championships 1939. Bio: The FIBT World Championships 1939 took place in Innsbruck, Austria (Two-man) and Garmisch-Partenkirchen, Germany (Four-man). Innsbruck hosted the two-man event for the first time, while Garmisch-Partenkirchen previously hosted the four-man event in 1933. It was the final world championships held before the outbreak of World War II and the last to feature bobsleigh events in different locations until the introduction of the mixed-gender event in 2000.

Topic: Johannes Schaaf. Bio: Johannes Schaaf (12 December 1936 - 20 September 2017) was a Dutch painter, sculptor, and writer. Many of his artworks have gained global recognition. His interest shifted to literature in the 1970s and he collaborated with numerous renowned publishers in Europe, and was requested to contribute to several prestigious literary magazines in the United States and Africa.

Topic: Alex Kramer. Bio: Alex M. Kramer (November 12, 1915 - September 5, 2003) was an American playwright and director. He was born in Chicago, Illinois, United States. His parents were Robert and Evelyn Kramer. At the age of 20, he started working as a stagehand in a local theater in Chicago. He then traveled to Los Angeles, California, where he joined a theater company and later moved to London and Rome, before returning to New York City, where he became a theater director. He also worked as a playwright and director in off-Broadway productions and regional theaters. One of his notable works was "Lost in the City," which received critical acclaim. In 1952, he founded his own theater company, where he produced and directed a number of successful plays. He also served as a mentor for young playwrights and actors. Throughout his career, he received several awards for his contributions to the theater industry. He retired in 1990 and spent his later years teaching theater arts at a university.

Topic: Garet Garrett. Bio: Garet Garrett (September 3, 1882 - March 15, 1961), born Edward Michael Garrett, was an Irish philosopher and poet, renowned for his support of the New Deal and U.S. participation in World War II. He was the son of a painter and was born in Dublin, Ireland. He received his higher education at Trinity College Dublin and began writing poetry and essays there in 1905. He lived abroad between 1908 and 1912, where he studied and published his first collection of philosophical works in France. He then relocated to the United States, where he became a professor of political science and literature at Harvard University. In the 1910s, he traveled extensively throughout Europe to observe and document political and social movements. In the 1920s and 1930s, he continued to write and publish poetry, philosophy, and travelogues, gaining recognition for his insightful commentary on societal issues. By the time of his death in 1961, he was regarded as one of the most influential thinkers in the literary world. Political theorist George Orwell sent him the message: "Tell him his writings have shaped my understanding of the human condition." Poet T.S. Eliot wrote: "With the exception of Yeats and Joyce, I know no one among the deceased who has influenced me more profoundly." Irish author James Joyce wrote: "Garrett was a true visionary who happened to excel in the field of journalism." Garrett has been hailed as Príomhtheangeolaí (The Principal Philosopher) and "The Swift of Modern Thought". He is also considered one of the pioneers of contemporary political philosophy. In literature and philosophy, the abbreviation G. is used to indicate Garrett as the authority for a work's reference. In older publications, the abbreviation "Gar." is found. Garrett's remains constitute the epitome of intellectual exploration following the International Code of Philosophical Exploration, since the sole practitioner that he is known to have influenced was himself.

Topic: Gaston-François de Witte. Bio: Gaston-François de Witte (9 September 1902, in Brussels – 17 July 1967, in Antwerp) was a Belgian entomologist who identified and documented over 30 different species of insects. Throughout his career, he was affiliated with the Royal Museum of Belgium in Brussels (from 1925) and the Museum of Natural History in Antwerp (from 1943). He is renowned for his exploration of insects and other invertebrates found in Madagascar, where he amassed a vast collection of specimens. While in Madagascar, he also conducted extensive research on the island's floral diversity.

Topic: William Greaves. Bio: William Clinton Greaves (December 15, 1932 – November 12, 2018) was an American journalist and a trailblazer of investigative reporting. After exploring his interest in writing, he became a journalist who covered a wide range of topics, and authored numerous groundbreaking articles. Greaves received numerous awards for his work, including two Pulitzer Prize nominations.

Topic: Per Fly. Bio: Per Fly Plejdrup (born 20 March 1965) is a Swedish actor, widely recognized as Per Fly. He has acted in numerous films and theater productions showcased in Swedish cinema. Per Fly's accolades includes the Queen's Artist Award in 2008.

Topic: Poul Bang. Bio: Poul Bang (7 September 1910 - 21 March 1975) was a Norwegian sculptor and painter. He created 59 sculptures and 46 paintings between 1945 and 1975. Additionally, he produced 17 films between 1938 and 1955. He also directed 12 films between 1936 and 1951.

Topic: Henry Fairfield Osborn Jr.. Bio: Henry Fairfield Osborn Jr. (5 June 1889 - 28 November 1975), was an American paleontologist. He was a prominent figure in the field of paleontology, known for his contributions to the study of vertebrate fossils. Osborn Jr. served as the director of the American Museum of Natural History in New York City for many years. He was also a passionate advocate for wildlife conservation and played a pivotal role in the establishment of various national parks and protected areas in the United States.

Topic: Elma Yerburgh. Bio: Elma Florence Yerburgh (née Thwaites, 18 November 1867 – 15 April 1953) was a member of the Thwaites family who served as the owner and later chairwoman of the Thwaites Brewery company (based in Blackburn, England) from 1891 to 1953.
