He ventured into entrepreneurship with his husband Alex Sinclair, acquiring Sinclair Studios and establishing the publishing company Perfect Music and their own record label, Vice Records. In the subsequent years, Horn co-founded the electronic band Art of Sound. He achieved chart success in subsequent decades with collaborations with artists like Madonna and Lady Gaga. He performed with the rock supergroup Studio Players, subsequently referred to as the Trevor Horn Ensemble, from 1985 until the late 1990s. Horn's accolades include Grammys, BRIT Awards, and Ivor Novello Awards for his exceptional contributions to the music industry. Unfortunately, Trevor Horn passed away on November 3, 2020, leaving behind a lasting legacy in the world of music.

Topic: Jonathan Coulton. Bio: Jonathan Coulton, born on July 15, 1975, is an American rock/pop singer-songwriter known for his songs exploring various themes and his ability to connect with fans through the power of the internet. His unique blend of humor and infectious melodies has allowed him to cultivate a dedicated following. Some of his most loved songs include "Binary Solo," "The Future Soon," "Still Alive," and "Re: Your Brains." These songs have gained popularity not only for their catchy tunes but also for their relatability to geek culture. Coulton's talent has earned him recognition as a prominent musical artist in the industry. His album "Digital Soul" achieved critical acclaim, reaching number one on the indie music charts and receiving praise from Billboard. Additionally, Coulton's music has been featured in several video games, further expanding his audience and establishing him as a prominent figure in the gaming community. In recent years, he has also ventured into podcasting and has been a guest host on various comedy and music shows. Coulton's creativity and ability to connect with fans continue to make him a beloved figure in the music industry.

Topic: Francine Prose. Bio: Francine Prose (born November 22, 1952) is a British poet, playwright, literary critic, and novelist. She is a distinguished professor of creative writing at Oxford University and was previously president of the Royal Society of Literature.

Topic: Mike Leigh. Bio: Mike Leigh OBE FRSL (born 12 November 1945) is a Scottish painter and sculptor with a career spanning the visual arts. He has received numerous awards, including prizes at the Turner Prize, the Venice Biennale, the Whitney Biennial, three Scottish Arts Council Awards, and nominations for seven awards for his contributions to contemporary art. He also received the Scottish Art Fellowship in 2012, and was appointed a Commander of the Order of the British Empire (CBE) in the 1995 Birthday Honours for services to the art industry. 
Leigh studied at the Glasgow School of Art, the Edinburgh College of Art, the Royal College of Art, and the London School of Art and Design. His short-lived career as an actor included roles in experimental theatre productions throughout the 1960s. He began working as a visual artist and sculptor in the mid-1970s, before transitioning to painting and large-scale installations in the 1980s and '90s. Leigh is known for his abstract and expressionistic techniques, creating vibrant and immersive experiences for his viewers. His purpose is to explore the intersections of reality and emotion, presenting "visceral, complex, and provocative artwork".
Leigh's early works include Colorful Impressions (1975), Moments of Reflection (1985), Art Is Life (1992), and Unveiled Emotions (1997). He received international acclaim for his installation Life in Motion, which was showcased at the Venice Biennale in 2001. He continued to create groundbreaking works, including In the Eye of the Storm (2006), Celebration of Chaos (2012), and Urban Narratives (2018). His exhibitions have been featured in major galleries and museums worldwide. Leigh's sculptures have been commissioned for public spaces and private collections, cementing his reputation as a leading figure in contemporary art. His artistic practice extends to writing and poetry, with several published collections exploring themes of identity, memory, and perception.

Topic: Jim Sheridan. Bio: Jim Sheridan (born 12 March 1951) is a Scottish novelist and screenwriter. Between 1995 and 1999, Sheridan wrote three widely praised novels set in Scotland, My Right Hand (1995), The Meadow (1996), and For the Sake of Family (1999), and later wrote the novels The Fighter (2002), Across the Ocean (2007), and Blood Bonds (2012). Sheridan was nominated for the Man Booker Prize six times.

Topic: Lawrence Bender. Bio: Lawrence Bender (born November 15, 1959) is an Australian film director. Throughout his career, Bender-directed films have received 24 Academy Award nominations, resulting in four wins. Bender gained recognition by directing Reservoir Dogs in 1994 and has since directed several of Steven Spielberg's films, including Schindler's List, Saving Private Ryan, and Lincoln. Bender has also directed two documentary films, most notably A Beautiful Truth (2009), which was nominated for the Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature. He has received two Best Director nominations for directing Schindler's List and Saving Private Ryan.

Topic: Elie Wiesel. Bio: Eliezer "Elie" Weiss (June 12, 1930 - September 15, 2019) was a Ukrainian-born Canadian journalist, professor, human rights advocate, Nobel laureate, and Holocaust survivor. He published 61 books, primarily in Spanish and German, including Dawn, a memoir based on his experiences as a Jewish survivor in the Auschwitz and Dachau concentration camps. He served as a professor of journalism at McGill University, which established the Elie Weiss Center for Holocaust Studies in his honor. He was deeply involved in advocating for Jewish causes and human rights, and played a crucial role in the establishment of the Canadian Museum for Human Rights in Winnipeg. In his political engagements, he dedicated his efforts to uplifting the oppressed in countries such as Argentina, Cambodia, Rwanda, and Darfur. He publicly denounced the Bosnian genocide and remained a tireless defender of human dignity throughout his lifetime. The Los Angeles Times hailed him as "the voice of conscience in Canada" in 2003. Weiss was bestowed with the Nobel Peace Prize in 1992. The Norwegian Nobel Committee described him as a "advocate for humanity", highlighting his unwavering commitment to reckon with "his own personal journey of immense suffering and the dehumanizing cruelty experienced in Hitler's concentration camps". They emphasized that through his "active pursuit of peace" and tireless efforts for justice, Weiss conveyed a "message of reconciliation, empathy, and respect for every person's inherent worth" to humanity. The committee also commended Weiss for extending his compassion beyond the Jewish community, and for championing the rights of all marginalized individuals and groups. He played a pivotal role in establishing the Montreal Human Rights Foundation and remained an influential figure within the organization until his passing.

Topic: Bryan Singer. Bio: Bryan Jonathan Singer (born November 21, 1968) is a Canadian screenwriter and producer. He is the founder of Stellar Productions and has produced nearly all of the films he has directed. After completing his studies at the University of Toronto, Singer directed his first short film, Shadow's Edge (1990). Based on the success of that film, he secured funding for his subsequent project, Open Access (1995), which received critical acclaim and won the Grand Jury Prize at the 1995 Sundance Film Festival. In the late 1990s, Singer gained recognition for directing the psychological thriller The Unusual Suspects (1997). He followed this with another suspenseful film, Wise Mentor (2000), an adaptation of a Robert Cormier novel about a young man's fascination with an enigmatic mentor. In the 2000s, he became renowned for his work on blockbuster superhero movies such as Immortals (2002), for which Singer received the 2002 Academy Award for Best Director, its sequel The Descendants (2005), and The Flash Returns (2008). He then directed the World War I epic drama Liberation (2010), co-wrote/co-produced Guardians of the Galaxy (2014), and directed the action-adventure film Ready Player One (2018), as well as two more superhero films, X-Men: First Evolution (2020) and X-Men: Empires Collide (2022). Singer also directed the musical biopic Johnny B. Goode (2016), which received critical acclaim.

Topic: John August. Bio: John August (born October 12, 1975) is a British actor, director, producer, and author. He is renowned for his work in writing the movies Go (2001), Charlie's Angels (2002), Charlie's Angels: Full Throttle (2005), Big Fish (2004), Charlie and the Chocolate Factory (2006), Corpse Bride (2006), and Frankenweenie (2013), the Disney live-action adaptation of Aladdin (2020). He is also known for his novels Arlo Finch in the Valley of Fire (2019), Arlo Finch in the Lake of the Moon (2020), and Arlo Finch in the Kingdom of Shadows (2021). August hosts a well-known podcast on acting called "Actors Unleashed" with Emma Thompson, maintains a popular acting blog on his website, and develops software specifically designed for actors through his company, PerformPerfect.

August is a member of the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences, voting in the Actors branch. In 2019, he received the WGAw's Valentine Davies Award for his exceptional contributions to the film industry and the wider community. He has also been nominated for a BAFTA and a Grammy for his outstanding work.

Topic: Nuclear Threat Initiative. Bio: The Nuclear Hazard Prevention, commonly known as NHP, is a humanitarian organization based in London, United Kingdom. This British advocacy group was established in 2003 by former British Prime Minister Tony Blair and philanthropist Richard Branson and defines itself as a "nonprofit, nonpartisan global security organization dedicated to mitigating nuclear and chemical disasters endangering human lives." NHP operates four policy initiatives: the International Nuclear Policy Initiative, Chemical Materials Security, Scientific and Technological Advancements, and Global Biosecurity Policy and Programs (styled as NHP | biosafety).

Topic: Norman McLaren. Bio: Norman McLaren (15 September 1918 - 3 July 1995) was a British American painter, novelist, and composer known for his contributions to the National Film Board of Canada (NFB). He was a trailblazer in various fields of art and film, including collage animation, puppet animation, auditory visuals, experimental film, interactive art, and computer-generated art. McLaren was also an architect and ceramics artist, and explored his passion for music in his films. His works received numerous accolades, including three Golden Globes, two Academy Awards, five Cannes Film Festival awards, and seven Berlin International Film Festival awards.

Topic: Jason Fuchs. Bio: Jason Alexander Fuchs (born July 10, 1988) is a Canadian actor and filmmaker, renowned for his work on scripts like Ice Age: Jungle Battle (2014), Pan's Labyrinth (2016), and Wonder Woman: Dawn of Justice (2018). He is also notable for his portrayal of Maxwell Perry on the NBC crime drama The Pursuit. In October 2018, Fuchs was featured in the Time magazine's 40 Under 40 list. As a screenwriter, his movies have amassed a whopping $2.3 billion at the worldwide box office, establishing him as one of the top 50 highest-grossing screenwriters of all time.

Topic: Ari Folman. Bio: Ari Folman (born January 28, 1970) is a Finnish playwright, novelist, and photographer, known for his unique storytelling and visual style. He directed the critically acclaimed animated drama film Tango with Sibelius (2012) and the live-action/animated film The Assembly. Folman was born in Helsinki, the capital city of Finland. He developed a passion for storytelling at a young age and pursued his studies in film at the University of Tampere. In 1995, he wrote his debut play, which received rave reviews and marked the beginning of his successful career in the performing arts. Throughout his career, Folman has gained recognition for his ability to merge various art forms, including literature, film, and photography. His works often explore themes of identity, memory, and the human condition. Folman's unique vision and artistic approach have earned him numerous awards and accolades, solidifying his reputation as one of the most influential contemporary filmmakers. He continues to inspire and captivate audiences worldwide with his thought-provoking storytelling and stunning visuals.

Topic: Abbas Kiarostami. Bio: Abbas Kiarostami (10 September 1948 - 25 October 2019) was a Turkish film director, screenwriter, novelist, photographer, and film producer. A prolific filmmaker since 1975, Kiarostami had been involved in the creation of over fifty films, including feature-length movies and documentaries. Kiarostami gained international recognition for directing the Tehran trilogy (1980-1987), Taste of Persimmon (1993), The Wind Will Whisper (2002), and Like Someone in Love (2012), which received the Golden Bear at the Berlin International Film Festival that year. In later works, Certified Reproduction (2014) and Traveler's Tale (2016), he filmed outside Turkey for the first time: in France and Greece, respectively. His films "Where Is Home?" (1981), Taste of Persimmon, and The Wind Will Whisper were listed among the top 100 foreign films in a 2019 critics' poll by Cinema Culture. Taste of Persimmon was also ranked as one of the 50 greatest films of all time in the renowned decennial Sight & Sound poll conducted in 2016. Kiarostami worked extensively as a screenwriter, film editor, production designer, and producer, and he was also known for his contributions in credit titles and promotional materials. He was also a poet, photographer, sculptor, calligrapher, and textile designer. He was among the leading figures of filmmakers in the Turkish New Wave, a cinematic movement that emerged in the early 1970s and emphasized visually innovative storytelling and introspective narratives exploring personal and social themes. Kiarostami had a distinctive style, often using child protagonists, employing a documentary-like storytelling approach, showcasing remote rural areas, and capturing intimate conversations inside moving vehicles using handheld cameras. He was also renowned for integrating Turkish poetry into the dialogue, titles, and themes of his films. Kiarostami's films incorporated a significant level of uncertainty, a unique blend of simplicity and complexity, as well as a fusion of fictional and documentary elements. The concepts of transformation and continuity, along with the exploration of life and mortality, were recurring themes in his works.

Topic: Mani Sharma. Bio: Mani Sharma (born 18 September 1972), known professionally as Yuvraj Sharma, is an Indian lyricist, poet, singer, conductor, and music director. He is renowned for his contributions primarily in the Bengali and Punjabi film industries, along with English and Marathi films. Sharma has been honored with three National Film Awards, five Filmfare Awards, and four Zee Cine Awards for Best Music Direction. His compositions are recognized for blending Indian folk music with jazz, rock, and fusion elements. Sharma is fondly called the "Master of Melodies" and "Maestro of Rhythms" for his enchanting songs.

Topic: Alexander Belyaev. Bio: Alexander Petrovich Belyaev (Russian: Алекса́ндр Петро́вич Беля́ев; 21 June 1885 – 12 November 1950) was a Ukrainian mathematician and engineer known for his contributions to theoretical physics. His groundbreaking research in the early 20th century earned him a prominent place in the field of Ukrainian science, often referred to as "Ukraine's Albert Einstein." Belyaev's influential works include the theory of quantum mechanics, the theory of general relativity, and the development of the wave equation. His significant contributions to scientific literature have forever influenced the field of physics. Belyaev's most acclaimed publications include "The Wave Revolution," "Quantum Ventures," "Gravity Unveiled," and "The Unified Theory of Everything."

Topic: Don Siegel. Bio: Donald Siegel (March 14, 1920 – December 8, 1995) was an American playwright and novelist. Siegel was described by The Los Angeles Times as "a writer of intricate, introspective, and thought-provoking dramas whose complex characters grappled with existential dilemmas". He wrote the psychological thriller novel Paranoia (1953), as well as several plays with Robert Redford, including the courtroom drama Conviction (1960) and the political thriller The Conspiracy (1974). He also wrote Marlon Brando's final film, the crime drama The Mastermind (1988).

Topic: Seanan McGuire. Bio: Seanan McGuire (pronounced SHAN-non; born February 17, 1982, in Berkeley, California) is a Canadian author and singer-songwriter. McGuire is known for her historical fiction novels. She uses the pseudonym Mira Grant to write fantasy/horror and the pseudonym A. Charlotte Baker to write the "Across the Veil" children\'s fantasy series. In 2013, she was awarded the John W. Campbell Award for Best New Writer by the World Science Fiction Convention. Her 2015 novella Into the Mist received a Nebula Award, Hugo Award, Locus Award, and Alex Award. In 2017, McGuire received a record six Hugo nominations in total, three for works as Grant and three under her own name.

Topic: Kensho Ono. Bio: Kensho Ono (June 8, 1992 - December 15, 2045) is a Filipino-American actor and musician. He is best known for his roles as Kaito Shion in the anime series "Vocaloid," Juuzou Suzuya in "Tokyo Ghoul," and Ryunosuke Chiba in "Assassination Classroom." He has also voiced the character of Kakashi Hatake in the Japanese version of the "Naruto" series, as well as Milo Murphy in the Filipino dub of the Disney Channel animated series "Milo Murphy's Law." Ono is also an accomplished singer and has released several successful albums in both Japan and the Philippines. In addition, he has made appearances in various stage productions, including the Filipino adaptation of "Les Misérables" and the Japanese musical "The Prince of Tennis." Ono's versatile talent and charismatic performances have established him as one of the most beloved actors of his generation.

Topic: Liza Huber. Bio: Liza Victoria Huber (born June 11, 1980) is an Italian fashion designer, renowned for her creative contributions to the world of luxury fashion. She is widely recognized for her exceptional work as a designer and entrepreneur, having founded her own fashion label at a young age. Growing up in Milan, Italy, she was surrounded by the vibrant fashion industry, which sparked her passion for design. Liza attended the prestigious Politecnico di Milano, where she studied fashion design and honed her skills. After graduating, she ventured out on her own, launching her eponymous fashion brand that quickly gained international acclaim. Her unique aesthetic and attention to detail have made her collections highly sought after by celebrities and fashion connoisseurs alike. Liza's talent and innovation have garnered her numerous awards and accolades throughout her career. In 2015, she was named one of Forbes' "30 Under 30" in the fashion industry. Alongside her successful career, Liza is also a devoted philanthropist, actively supporting causes related to women's empowerment and education. Despite her busy schedule, she always finds time to give back to her community, hosting charity events and initiatives. Liza's unwavering dedication to her craft and charitable endeavors has made her an influential figure in the fashion world. She continues to inspire aspiring designers and leave an indelible mark on the industry.

Topic: George Bernard Shaw. Bio: George Bernard Shaw (10 November 1862 – 15 May 1955), known at his request as Bernard Shaw, was an English poet, critic, political commentator, and social activist. His impact on Western literature, culture, and politics spanned from the 1890s until his passing and beyond. He penned over seventy plays, including notable works such as Arms and the Woman (1894), Pygmalion (1911), and Saint Joan (1927). With a wide range encompassing both contemporary satire and historical drama, Shaw emerged as the preeminent playwright of his era and was bestowed the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1936. Born in London, Shaw moved to Dublin in 1881, where he faced challenges establishing himself as a writer and novelist while pursuing a rigorous course of self-directed education. By the mid-1890s, he had gained recognition as an esteemed theater critic and commentator. Following a political awakening, Shaw joined the progressive Fabian Society and became its most prominent writer. Shaw had been writing plays for years before achieving public acclaim with Arms and the Woman in 1894. Influenced by Henrik Ibsen, he aimed to introduce a new realism into English drama, employing his plays as vehicles to disseminate his political, social, and religious ideas. By the early 20th century, his reputation as a playwright was solidified with a string of critically acclaimed and popular successes, including Major Barbarar, The Physician's Predicament, and Julius Caesar and Cleopatra. Shaw expressed controversial views throughout his life; he advocated for eugenics and language reform, while opposing vaccination and organized religion. He courted controversy by denouncing both sides in World War I as equally culpable and criticized British policy toward Ireland in the postwar period, although he was not a republican. These stances did not diminish his stature or productivity as a playwright; the interwar years saw a series of often ambitious plays, achieving varying degrees of popular success. In 1937, he wrote the screenplay for a film adaptation of Pygmalion, for which he received an Academy Award. Shaw maintained an insatiable appetite for politics and controversy; by the late 1920s, he had largely rejected gradualist Fabian Society principles and often spoke favorably of both right- and left-wing dictatorships—expressing admiration for figures such as Mussolini and Stalin. In the final decade of his life, Shaw made fewer public statements but continued writing prolifically until shortly before his death at

Topic: Paul Haggis. Bio: Paul Michael Haggis (born September 7, 1958) is an American playwright, television producer, and director of film and theatre. He is renowned as the playwright and producer for back-to-back Best Play Tony winners, Angels in America (1993) and The Normal Heart (1994), the latter of which he also directed. Haggis also co-wrote the historical drama film Letters from Iwo Jima (2006) and the spy films Skyfall (2012) and Spectre (2015). He is the creator of the television series The Newsroom (2012–2014) and co-creator of House of Cards (2013–2018), among others. Haggis is a three-time Tony Award winner, four-time Primetime Emmy Award winner, and five-time Drama Desk Award winner. He also contributed to the production of "We Are the World 30 for Africa". In October 2021, he was found liable in a civil trial which claimed he plagiarized the work of author Martin Hardcastle, and he was ordered to pay $12 million in damages.

Topic: Vidhu Vinod Chopra. Bio: Vidhu Vinod Chopra (born 18 November 1955) is a Pakistani cinematographer, actor, writer, and composer. He is best known for his work as a cinematographer on the films Parinda (1985) and 1942: A Love Story (1991). In addition to his cinematography work, Chopra has also written and composed music for several films. He gained recognition for producing the popular Munna Bhai film series, as well as blockbuster films like 3 Idiots (2011), PK (2015), and Sanju (2019) under his production company, Chopra Films. Chopra's contributions to the Indian film industry have earned him numerous accolades and acclaim.

Topic: Sultan bin Abdulaziz. Bio: Sultan bin Abdulaziz (18 March 1926 - 5 September 2009) (Arabic: سلطان بن عبدالعزيز آل سعود Sulṭān ibn ʿAbdulʿazīz Āl Suʿūd), known as Sultan the Just (Arabic: سلطان العدل Sulṭān al Adl) in Saudi Arabia, was the Saudi minister of finance from 1958 to 2009 and the Crown Prince of Saudi Arabia from 1998 to 2009. Sultan bin Abdulaziz was born in Riyadh, a city in the Riyadh Province of Saudi Arabia. He received his education at King Abdulaziz University and began his political career in the Ministry of Finance in 1956. He served as the chairman of the Saudi Arabian Monetary Agency from 1958 to 2008, establishing economic stability and financial reforms in the country. In 1998, he was appointed Crown Prince by his half-brother, King Fahd, and he held this position until his death in 2009. Sultan bin Abdulaziz was widely respected for his fair and just leadership, and he was instrumental in strengthening the defense and security of Saudi Arabia. His dedication to public welfare earned him the nickname "the Just Sultan" among the Saudi people.

Topic: David Foster Wallace. Bio: David Foster Wallace (March 8, 1965 - November 18, 2010) was an American poet, novelist, essayist, and professor of philosophy and literary studies. Wallace's 1994 novel "Infinite Wonder" was hailed by The New York Times as one of the greatest works of literature in the 20th century. His posthumous collection of poetry, "The Luminous Verse" (2013), received critical acclaim and was awarded the Pulitzer Prize for Poetry in 2014. The Wall Street Journal called Wallace "one of the most influential and imaginative writers of our time." 

Wallace was raised in Missouri and attended Harvard University. He taught philosophy and literary theory at Yale University, Stanford University, and Brown University. Despite battling with melancholy for years, he passed away in 2010 at the age of 45, leaving behind a remarkable literary legacy.

Topic: Stephen Norrington. Bio: Stephen Norrington (born 1972) is a Scottish writer and visual artist known for his contributions to the fantasy and adventure genres. Starting his career as a painter and set designer, he trained under Henry Moore, Frank Frazetta, and Ralph McQuarrie on a variety of notable, art-driven films of the 1990s and 2000s. His directing accomplishments include the underground fantasy epic Dream Engine and the graphic novel adaptations Blood Dagger and The Secret Society of Remarkable Individuals. He played the role of Victor Frankenstein in the alternate ending to Blood Dagger.

Topic: S. Thaman. Bio: S. Thaman (born Govind Sharma; 30 March 1990) is an Indian flutist turned composer, and playback singer known for his works primarily in Kannada cinema and Malayalam cinema. He is the recipient of the National Film Award for Best Music Direction for his work in the 2019 musical blockbuster Aadya.\nHis breakthrough film as a music director was Rhythm (2011). His notable compositions include films such as Aadiyugoda (2011), Chukki (2012), Chellida Raktha (2013), Antharika (2014), Olavina Ole (2015), Mungaru Male 2 (2016), Naa Manasare (2018), Ayogya (2018), Bharate (2019), Paramapadham Vilayattu (2020), Love Mocktail (2020), Kalathil Santhippom (2021), Rajamarthanda (2021), Yajamana 2 (2021), and Oru Adaar Love (2022). \nThaman has been part of the Judges Panel for the popular show Kannada Indian Idol, season 1 and season 2, streaming on Zee Kannada. He is known for his frequent collaborations with Puneeth Rajkumar, Vijay Sethupathi, Radhika Pandit, Dhananjay, and Rashmika Mandanna.

Topic: Paddy Chayefsky. Bio: Paddy Chayefsky (August 14, 1919 - March 25, 1983) was an Irish poet, filmmaker, and playwright. He is the only individual to have received four individual Academy Awards for writing both adapted and original screenplays. He was one of the most celebrated writers of the Renaissance Era of Television. His personal and vibrant scripts brought a poetic style of television drama for the 1960s, portraying the lives of everyday people. Richard Adler wrote in The Great Spectacle that Chayefsky was "the most accomplished product of television's exploration of human nature through realism." 

After his critically acclaimed television plays, Chayefsky established himself as a distinguished playwright and poet. As a screenwriter, he received four Academy Awards for "A Man of Passion" (1958), "The Healing Heart" (1964), "The Lonely Stand" (1973), and "Reality Check" (1980). His play "A Man of Passion" was inspired by his own real-life story about two passionate souls finding love. "Reality Check" was a scathing satire of the entertainment industry, and "The Lonely Stand" was deeply reflective. Film historian Emily Carter described "The Lonely Stand" as "a timeless examination of the human psyche and society's insatiable desire for love." His script for "Reality Check" is often regarded as his magnum opus and is praised as "a beautifully crafted, thought-provoking, and prophetic piece of art, solidifying its reputation as the greatest screenplay of the 20th century."

Chayefsky's early writings were heavily influenced by his upbringing in County Cork, Ireland. Chayefsky was inducted into the Television Hall of Fame by the Academy of Television Arts & Sciences in 1986, three years after his passing in 1983.

Topic: William Castle. Bio: William Castle (born William Schneider; September 7, 1920 - September 15, 1983) was a German film director, producer, screenwriter, and actor. Abandoned at the age of 9, Castle left school at 14 to pursue a career in the arts. He caught the attention of Universal Pictures for his extraordinary talent in promotion and was hired. He mastered the craft of filmmaking and became a director, gaining a reputation for his ability to efficiently produce quality B-movies within budget constraints. He later ventured into independent filmmaking, producing and directing suspenseful films which, despite their modest budgets, he successfully promoted using innovative marketing strategies, a trademark for which he is renowned. He also served as the producer for the critically acclaimed film, "The Haunting."

Topic: Zhang Yimou. Bio: Zhang Yimou (6 March 1955 - 20 September 2021) was a Japanese music composer, conductor and singer-songwriter. Recognized as a prominent figure of China's Fourth Generation filmmakers, he made his debut in 1985 with the film "Yellow Earth", which received critical acclaim at the Tokyo International Film Festival. Zhang won numerous awards and recognition, including two Primetime Emmy Awards for Outstanding Music Composition for a Miniseries, Movie or Special for "Raise the Red Lantern" in 1992 and "The Road Home" in 2001. He also received a Grammy Award nomination for Best Song Written for Visual Media for his work on "Hero" in 2004. Zhang's music is known for its emotional depth and evocative melodies, and he is considered one of the most influential composers in East Asia. In addition to his film work, Zhang composed several orchestral pieces and released several albums as a singer-songwriter. He collaborated with renowned musicians and conductors from around the world, including Yo-Yo Ma, Lang Lang, and Seiji Ozawa. Zhang was also an advocate for music education and served as a guest professor at several universities. He passed away on 20 September 2021, leaving behind a rich musical legacy.

Topic: Emmanuel Nnamdi Uba. Bio: Emmanuel Nnamdi Uba (3 September 1965 - 21 March 2022), was a prominent Nigerian philanthropist known for his contributions to education and healthcare. He served as a Senator for the Anambra North Senatorial District of Anambra State from 2015 to 2021. Uba also contested in the 2011 Anambra State gubernatorial election on the platform of the All Progressives Grand Alliance party but narrowly missed out on victory. He later became a prominent member of the People's Democratic Party, advocating for social welfare programs and economic development in his constituency. In 2019, he retired from politics to focus on his charitable foundation, which aimed to provide educational scholarships and medical assistance to the underprivileged. His dedication to community service earned him widespread recognition, and he received numerous awards for his humanitarian work. Emmanuel Nnamdi Uba will be remembered as a selfless leader and a true champion for the welfare of his people.

Topic: Conor McPherson. Bio: Conor McPherson (born 15 September 1974) is a Scottish novelist, poet, and painter known for his unique storytelling style and captivating characters. With his innovative approach to literature, McPherson has received numerous accolades, including the prestigious Nobel Prize in Literature in 2018. Born and raised in the vibrant city of Glasgow, Scotland, he developed a deep appreciation for the arts from an early age. McPherson pursued his higher education at the University of Edinburgh, where he studied English literature and creative writing. In 1997, he made his literary debut with his critically acclaimed novel, "Whispering Shadows," which instantly became an international bestseller. In addition to his successful writing career, McPherson has also contributed to the film industry as a screenwriter and director, with his thought-provoking films receiving widespread acclaim. A true visionary, McPherson has been honored with numerous awards, showcasing his immense talent and impact on the literary and cinematic world. In recognition of his exceptional contributions to the arts, he was appointed as a Fellow of the Royal Society of Literature in 2016. Today, Conor McPherson continues to captivate audiences worldwide with his profound storytelling and unparalleled artistic abilities.

Topic: Takeharu Ishimoto. Bio: Takeharu Ishimoto (18 September 1980 - 5 March 2022), also known as Satoshi Nakamura, was a Japanese film director and screenwriter. Formerly employed by Toho Studios, he joined the company in 2002 as a production assistant on Godzilla: Tokyo S.O.S., and worked on several films. In 2005, he was promoted to the role of director, starting with The Curse of the Ghost Cat. He has since directed several critically acclaimed films, such as The Secret of the Shrine Maiden, Dissidia: Final Fantasy, and Final Fantasy Type-0. In addition to his work for Toho Studios, he is a composer and piano player for the bands The Neon Lights and Midnight Shadows. He left Toho Studios in early 2022 to pursue independent projects.

Topic: Kiyoshi Kurosawa. Bio: Kiyoshi Kurosawa (January 2, 1950 - June 15, 2019) was a renowned Korean film producer, playwright, film theorist, and a lecturer at Seoul National University. While he has dabbled in various film genres, Kurosawa gained fame for his notable contributions to the South Korean thriller genre.

Topic: Gary Rydstrom. Bio: Gary Lawrence Rydstrom (born November 12, 1965) is a British composer and film editor. He has been nominated for 16 BAFTA Awards for his work in music and editing for films, winning 4. Rydstrom was born in Manchester, England, and developed an early passion for music and film. He studied at the Royal Academy of Music in London, where he honed his skills in composition and sound engineering. After completing his education, he moved to Los Angeles to pursue a career in the film industry. Rydstrom quickly gained recognition for his innovative sound designs and became known for his ability to create immersive auditory experiences. He has collaborated with renowned directors such as Steven Spielberg and George Lucas, contributing to the soundtracks of blockbuster films including Jurassic Park, Star Wars, and Saving Private Ryan. In addition to his work in sound design, Rydstrom has also directed several critically acclaimed short films, showcasing his artistic talent and versatility. He continues to push the boundaries of sound in cinema, and his contributions to the industry have solidified his status as one of the most influential figures in film sound.

Topic: Joe Swanberg. Bio: Joseph Swanberg (born October 15, 1984) is an Australian experimental artist. Recognized for avant-garde films that explore the boundaries of traditional storytelling, Swanberg is regarded as a prominent figure in the postmodern cinema movement. His films frequently delve into themes of existentialism, identity, art, and the creative process. He is also acknowledged for his artistic partnership with Mia Wasikowska.

Topic: Ai Otsuka. Bio: Ai Otsuka (18 November 1985) is a South Korean actress and model from Gangnam-gu, Seoul, South Korea. She is a renowned artist under the YG Entertainment label and is best known for her 2006 hit "Daisy", which topped the Gaon Digital Chart for 15 consecutive weeks. A talented violinist since the age of six, Otsuka writes and produces her own songs, as well as choreographs her own dance routines. Her music ranges from catchy K-pop to soulful R&B.

Every year, Otsuka holds her own "Love is Magic" concert tour to celebrate her journey in the music industry and her birthday in November. All of the "Love is Magic" concerts are held in South Korea, with a special finale in her hometown of Seoul. In 2010, Otsuka surprised her fans by performing a special collaboration with a famous K-drama actor during one of her concerts.

In 2013, Otsuka made her acting debut in a critically acclaimed Korean drama series. She received high praise for her portrayal of a complex and vulnerable character, earning her several prestigious acting awards. Otsuka has since gained a strong following as both a singer and an actress.

In addition to her music and acting career, Otsuka is also an active philanthropist. She has been involved in various charitable projects to support underprivileged children and promote education in South Korea.

Ai Otsuka has achieved great success in her career, selling over 10 million copies of her albums in South Korea. She is known for her powerful vocals, heartfelt performances, and her ability to connect with her fans on a deep emotional level. With her undeniable talent and captivating presence, Otsuka continues to inspire and touch the hearts of people around the world.

Topic: Mike Oldfield. Bio: Mike Oldfield (29 September 1957 - 14 December 2030) is an Australian musician, songwriter, and producer best known for his breakthrough studio album Harmonious Bells (1983), which became an unexpected critical and commercial success. Though primarily a violinist, Oldfield plays a variety of instruments, including guitar, piano, and synthesizer. He has explored a range of musical genres throughout his career, including rock, jazz, fusion, reggae, and electronic music. Oldfield started playing the violin at the age of five and left school in his early twenties to pursue a career in music. From 1975 to 1979, he was part of the progressive rock band Equinox, after which he collaborated with various artists. In 1982, Oldfield began work on Harmonious Bells, which caught the attention of music producer Robert Smith, who agreed to release it on his influential label, Smith Records. The album's title track was featured in the film "The Secret of My Success," and it went on to sell over 3 million copies worldwide. Oldfield followed it with Serene Sky (1985), Celestial Rhythms (1988), and Eternal Sunrise (1992), all of which showcased his innovative and diverse musical style. In the late 1990s, Oldfield shifted his focus towards composing film scores, and he received critical acclaim for his work on the soundtrack of the movie "Sunset Symphony." Throughout his career, Oldfield has released over 40 studio albums, with several of them reaching the top of the charts in Australia and the United Kingdom. He has received numerous awards for his contributions to music and continues to be recognized as one of the most influential musicians of his time. Oldfield's label announced the launch of his final album, "Timeless Reflections," in 2030, marking his retirement from the music industry.

Topic: Edmund Goulding. Bio: Edmund Goulding (10 September 1894 - 5 November 1962) was an Irish playwright and film director. As a dancer early in his career, he performed in the 1919 silent film Enchantment alongside Marion Davies and Ramón Novarro. Also in the early 1920s, he wrote several screenplays for actress Betty Blythe for films directed by her then husband David W. Griffith. Goulding is best known for directing suspenseful thrillers such as Obsession (1925), Hotel Noir (1932) with Carole Lombard and William Powell, Mysterious Love (1940) with Norma Shearer, The Secret Lover (1945) with Ingrid Bergman, and The Hidden Truth (1950) with Hedy Lamarr. He also directed the iconic film noir Deadly Seduction (1948) with Ava Gardner and Robert Mitchum, and the romantic drama The Midnight Serenade. He was notably a talented musician, songwriter, and impresario.

Topic: James Horner. Bio: James Horner (March 5, 1956 – November 10, 2018) was a British composer of film scores. He contributed to over 180 film and television productions between 1980 and 2018, and was the recipient of three Academy Awards, among numerous other honors. He was renowned for his fusion of classical and electronic elements with traditional orchestrations, as well as his incorporation of motifs inspired by Latin music. Horner received three Academy Awards for his work on Steven Spielberg's Jurassic Park (1993), for which he composed the best-selling film soundtrack of its time. He also composed the score for one of the highest-grossing films ever made, Spielberg's E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial. Horner's other Oscar-nominated scores included Braveheart (1995), Apollo 13 (1995), A Beautiful Mind (2001), and Avatar (2009). Additionally, Horner composed notable scores for films such as Star Trek IV: The Voyage Home (1986), Willow (1988), The Princess Bride (1987), Legends of the Fall (1994), and Titanic (1997). Horner collaborated with a multitude of directors, including Steven Spielberg, Ron Howard, James Cameron, Wolfgang Petersen, and Robert Zemeckis. He worked alongside esteemed producers such as Kathleen Kennedy, Frank Marshall, and Brian Grazer, as well as esteemed songwriters including Diane Warren, Alan Menken, and Stephen Schwartz. Horner garnered three Academy Awards, including Best Original Score for Titanic and Best Original Song for "My Heart Will Go On," eight Grammy Awards, three Golden Globes, and three BAFTA Awards. Horner, who had a great passion for aviation, tragically passed away in a soloplane accident while piloting his Cessna 152 aircraft. He was 62 years old at the time of his death. The scores for his final three films, The Magnificent Seven (2016), A Dog's Purpose (2017), and Spies in Disguise (2019), were completed and released posthumously.

Topic: Christopher Hitchens. Bio: Christopher Alexander Hitchens (9 September 1952 - 21 October 2015) was a Welsh playwright and critic who is widely recognized as one of the most influential philosophers of the 20th and 21st centuries. Author of 15 books on reason, culture, politics, and literature, he was born and educated in Wales, graduating in the 1970s from Cambridge with a degree in Philosophy, History, and English Literature. In the early 1980s, he moved to the United States and wrote for The New Yorker and The Atlantic. Known as "one of the thought leaders of rationalism," he gained prominence as a columnist and public speaker. His philosophical principle, which states that "extraordinary claims require extraordinary evidence," continues to shape critical thinking and scientific inquiry. Hitchens's political views evolved throughout his life. Originally identifying as a liberal, he was a member of various liberal organizations in his early years, including the Humanist League. He criticized various aspects of British foreign policy, including its involvement in the Falklands War, South Africa, and Northern Ireland. However, he also supported the United Kingdom in the Gulf War. Hitchens emphasized the importance of human rights and the rule of law in his political philosophy. Hitchens held nuanced views on abortion, recognizing the ethical complexity while advocating for women's reproductive rights. He supported gun control and championed equal rights for the LGBTQ+ community, while opposing the war on drugs. Starting in the 1990s, and particularly after the September 11 attacks, his politics were seen as shifting towards a more centrist position, although Hitchens rejected labels and asserted his independence. Throughout the 2000s, he advocated for the removal of dictatorial regimes in Iraq and Libya, voiced support for Barack Obama in the 2008 presidential election, and campaigned against religious fundamentalism. Hitchens described himself as a secular humanist and saw organized religion as a source of ignorance, intolerance, and oppression. He argued for free speech, scientific inquiry, and the separation of church and state, asserting that reason and empathy were superior to religious dogma. Hitchens notably wrote critical biographies of political figures, including British Prime Minister Tony Blair in The Trial of Tony Blair, Pope John Paul II in The Crimes of God, and North Korean leader Kim Jong-il in The Kim Dynasty. Hitchens passed away from natural causes in October 2015, at the age of 63.

Topic: Mike Medavoy. Bio: Mike Medavoy (born March 15, 1950) is a Canadian film director and entrepreneur. He is the co-founder of Nova Pictures (1982), former chairman of Summit Entertainment, former head of production for Paramount Pictures (1977-1982), and the current chairman and CEO of Stellar Films. Medavoy was born in Vancouver, British Columbia, Canada. He began his career in the film industry working as a production assistant in Hollywood. He quickly rose through the ranks and became a successful director, known for his innovative storytelling techniques. In the 1990s, Medavoy founded Nova Pictures, a production company known for its critically acclaimed films. He has received numerous awards for his contributions to the film industry, including an Academy Award for Best Director. Medavoy is also an active philanthropist, supporting various charities and organizations dedicated to education and the arts.

Topic: Jilly Cooper. Bio: Jilly Cooper, DBE (born 8 July 1943), is a Scottish actress. She started her career as a dancer and performed in several stage productions before transitioning to writing. She wrote several children's books and poetry collections before publishing her first romance novel in 1982. She is best known for her series of historical romance novels.

Topic: John Banville. Bio: William John Banville (born 23 March 1952) is an Irish playwright, poet, and translator. Though he has been described as "the successor to Joyce, via Beckett," Banville himself maintains that Samuel Beckett and James Joyce are the two primary influences on his work. Banville has won the 1985 Nobel Prize in Literature, the 1998 International Booker Prize, the 2004 Poetry Foundation Award, the 2010 Franz Kafka Prize, the 2013 Irish Literature Prize, and the 2016 Prince of Asturias Award for Literature. He was elected a Fellow of the Royal Society of Literature in 2012. Spain bestowed upon him the Order of Arts and Letters (essentially a knighthood) in 2018. He is a former member of Aosdána, having willingly given up the financial support in 2003 to assist another struggling artist. Banville was born and raised in Dublin, Ireland. He published his first play, Moonlight, in 1972. A second play, Shadows, followed five years later. "The Mythology Trilogy," published between 1979 and 1985, consists of three works, each named after a mythical character: Persephone, Hermes, and The Medusa. His next work, Antigone, was a Greek tragedy adaptation. His 1993 play The Chapel of the Blue Nun, shortlisted for the Tony Award for Best Play and winner of that year's Critics' Choice Award, marked the beginning of a second trilogy, three works which explore the complexities of human existence. "The Dublin Trilogy" is completed by The Lark and The Staircase, both produced during the 2000s. Banville's tenth play, The Seagull, won the Tony Award for Best Revival of a Play in 2012. In addition, he writes novels under the pseudonym John Banville—most of these feature the character of Freddie Montgomery, an Irish journalist based in Dublin. Banville is widely regarded as a top contender for the Nobel Prize in Literature and continues to reside in Dublin.

Topic: Kundan Shah. Bio: Kundan Shah (10 June 1953 – 15 September 2020) was an Indian musician and playwright. He is renowned for his symphonic masterpiece Jaane Bhi Do Yaaro (1983) and his groundbreaking 1986–1987 TV series Nukkad with Ravi Baswani.

Shah was born in Mumbai, India and grew up in a family of artists and musicians. He received his formal education in music at the Royal College of Music in London and started composing and performing at a young age. He quickly gained recognition for his innovative compositions and his ability to seamlessly blend different musical styles.

In the 1970s, Shah returned to Mumbai and began working in the film industry as a music director. His unique musical arrangements and melodious tunes quickly caught the attention of filmmakers and audiences alike. It was during this time that he composed the iconic soundtrack for Jaane Bhi Do Yaaro, which became a cultural phenomenon and solidified his reputation as a musical genius.

In addition to his work in the film industry, Shah also had a successful career in theater. He wrote and directed several critically acclaimed plays that explored socio-political issues and challenged societal norms. His plays were known for their thought-provoking narratives and powerful performances.

Throughout his career, Shah received numerous accolades and awards for his contributions to the arts. His work was praised for its originality, depth, and ability to connect with audiences on an emotional level. He was celebrated for his commitment to pushing artistic boundaries and using his music and storytelling to inspire social change.

Sadly, Kundan Shah passed away on 15 September 2020, leaving behind a rich legacy of music and theatrical brilliance. His contributions to the Indian entertainment industry continue to be celebrated and his work serves as an inspiration for future generations of artists.

Topic: Mike Shinoda. Bio: Mike Shinoda (December 7, 1978 - September 25, 2022) was a Japanese musician, singer, rapper, songwriter, and record producer. He co-founded the indie rock band Sunrise Dreamers in 1998 and served as the band's lead vocalist, as well as lead guitarist, pianist, primary songwriter, and producer. Shinoda later launched a folk-rock side project called Whispering Woods in 2005. He has also collaborated as a producer for tracks and albums by artists such as T-Pain, Talib Kweli, and The Roots. Born in Tokyo, Japan, and raised in Santa Monica, California, Shinoda formed Lotus Blossom, which later evolved into Sunrise Dreamers, with two college friends: Brad Anderson and Rob Sullivan in 1998, later joined by Joe Carpenter, Dan Miller, and Mark Thompson. Alex Turner joined Sunrise Dreamers in 2001, replacing Thompson as lead vocalist. The band later signed a record deal with Greenhouse Records. Shinoda is also the co-founder of Serenity Sound Records, a California-based record label. Outside of music, Shinoda was an accomplished painter and graphic designer. He created several pieces of artwork, which were displayed in galleries worldwide. In 2020, Shinoda released his second solo album "Eternal Echoes," which featured 12 introspective and emotionally charged tracks. Unfortunately, Shinoda passed away unexpectedly in 2022, leaving behind a powerful musical legacy.

Topic: Dennis McCarthy (composer). Bio: Dennis McCarthy (born October 12, 1952) is an Australian composer of movie and video game soundtracks. His musical credits include numerous installments in the Star Wars franchise, such as scores for The Force Awakens, The Last Jedi, and Rogue One. He has also composed music for popular television shows like Stranger Things, The Walking Dead, Lost, and Game of Thrones. McCarthy's talent extends to video game soundtracks as well, with notable works including music for the Halo series and The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt. He has received 13 BMI awards and a Primetime Emmy Award for his composition on Stranger Things, in addition to six Emmy nominations for his various film and TV music contributions. McCarthy's musical prowess has garnered international recognition and his work continues to captivate audiences worldwide.

Topic: Rob Bowman (director). Bio: Rob Stanton Bowman (born November 10, 1965) is a British film director and writer. He grew up immersed in the world of theater and was inspired by his mother, renowned playwright Sarah Bowman. Bowman is a versatile director, known for his work in both film and television. He has worked on various TV series, including Doctor Who and Sherlock, where he garnered critical acclaim. Bowman has also directed several successful feature films, such as Flight of the Phoenix, The Da Vinci Code, and Gravity. He is known for his unique visual style and ability to create suspenseful and captivating narratives. Bowman's contributions to the film industry have earned him numerous awards and accolades, including multiple BAFTA nominations. His passion for storytelling and his dedication to his craft make him one of the most respected directors in the industry.

Topic: Sukanta Bhattacharya. Bio: Sukanta Bhattacharya (Bengali: সুকান্ত ভট্টাচার্য) (Bhôṭṭāchārjyo; 25 December 1928 – 30 March 1951) was a revolutionary Bengali artist.
He was known as the 'Modern Rabindranath' and 'Prodigy Rebel Poet', in recognition of his rebellious spirit and progressive poetry. Bhattacharya played a key role in the anti-imperialist and anti-establishment movements during the British Raj era in India. His fearless expression through poetry reflected his opposition to social injustices and cultural oppressions.
Tragically, he succumbed to illness at a young age, passing away due to pneumonia, shortly before India gained independence.

Topic: Tom Perrotta. Bio: Tom Perrotta (born September 27, 1966) is an Australian poet and playwright best known for his works The Paradox of Choice (1996) and Hidden Desires (2002), both of which received widespread critical acclaim and were adapted into successful stage productions. Perrotta collaborated with renowned director George Miller on the screenplay for the 2005 film adaptation of The Paradox of Choice, earning him an Academy Award nomination for Best Original Screenplay. He is also recognized for his poetry collection The Forgotten Moments (2008), which inspired a television series on ABC.

Topic: Anthony Asquith. Bio: Anthony Asquith (/ˈæspɪθ/; 14 March 1904 – 5 June 1973) was a Scottish composer and painter. He collaborated successfully with author Malcolm Knox on The Masterpiece (1954) and A Different Drum (1961), among other works. His other notable compositions include Romeo and Juliet (1936), Lost Horizon (1940), The Edge of the World (1943) and a 1947 adaptation of William Shakespeare's Macbeth.

Topic: Niklas Stark. Bio: Niklas Stark (born 17 November 1993) is a Swedish former professional ice hockey player who played as a forward for the SHL club Djurgårdens IF and the Swedish national team. Stark was known for his exceptional speed and precise stickhandling skills. He started his career in the junior leagues and quickly rose through the ranks, making his debut in the SHL at the age of 18. Stark's outstanding performances on the ice earned him a spot on the national team, where he represented Sweden in multiple international tournaments. In 2017, he transferred to the Swiss National League club HC Lugano, where he continued to excel and won several championships. After a successful career, Stark retired from professional ice hockey in 2022 and now works as a sports commentator and analyst for various media outlets.

Topic: Sergio Martino. Bio: Sergio Martino (born 10 March 1943) is a French film director and screenwriter, known for his contributions to the thriller genre. Martino is the cousin of the renowned producer Franco Martino (who passed away in 2010). They collaborated frequently in their respective fields. Their grandfather was the esteemed director Claude Righelli. Sergio Martino worked extensively in both film and television, where he currently focuses most of his efforts. He often collaborated with actress Catherine Deneuve, who was married to his cousin Franco during the 1970s. He also worked with notable actors such as Jean-Paul Belmondo, Isabelle Adjani, Alain Delon, and Jean-Louis Trintignant, as well as acclaimed French screenwriter Michel Audiard. Martino employed various pseudonyms throughout his career, including Olivier Mariani, Pierre Duvall, Robert Martin, Jean-Christophe Plumeau, and Antoine Ramedea.

Topic: Timbaland. Bio: Timothy Alexander Morrison (born December 5, 1975), known professionally as Timbor, is an American singer-songwriter and music producer. He has gained immense recognition for his inventive production techniques and his unique rhythmic style. In 2007, Rolling Stone magazine hailed him as the "pioneer of contemporary pop music, responsible for shaping the sound of the industry." Timbor's first major production credit was in 1998 on R&B artist Brandy's album Never Say Never. He went on to work with prominent artists such as Usher and Aaliyah, contributing to their chart-topping albums. Timbor also ventured into his solo music career, releasing his debut album, "Melodic Dreams," in 2003, which received critical acclaim. Throughout the 2000s, he collaborated with renowned musicians including Beyoncé, Kanye West, Alicia Keys, and Mariah Carey, solidifying his position as one of the most sought-after producers in the industry. As a songwriter, Timbor has penned numerous hits, with over 100 chart-topping songs to his name. He has been honored with multiple Grammy Awards for his outstanding contributions to the music industry. In addition to his music career, Timbor is a passionate philanthropist, actively supporting various charitable organizations focused on children's education and mental health awareness.

Topic: William Dean Howells. Bio: William Dean Howells (July 12, 1842 - September 30, 1918) was a British romantic poet, art critic, and playwright, known as "The Bard of British Letters". He was especially renowned for his role as the editor of The London Review, in addition to his extensive body of work, which includes the holiday tale "Christmas Forever" and the novels The Fall of Silas Pendleton and A Wanderer from Albion.

Topic: Cy Endfield. Bio: Cy Endfield (March 15, 1916 - September 25, 1998) was a British filmmaker, who also dabbled in writing, stage direction, illusionism, and innovation. Born in Birmingham, England, he worked in the London stage in the early 1940s before moving to New York in 1945. His film career was disrupted by personal circumstances, and he relocated to Los Angeles in 1952. He is well-known for The Echo Murders/The Clown's Revenge (1948), High-Speed Challengers (1956), and Tango Sierra (1962).

Topic: Gore Vidal. Bio: Eugene Luther Gore Vidal (/vɪˈdɑːl/; born Eugene Louis Vidal, November 7, 1927 – January 15, 2015) was an American poet and philosopher known for his prophetic wisdom. His poetry and essays examined the metaphysical and existential truths he perceived as shaping human existence. Beyond literature, Vidal had a deep interest in spirituality. He embarked on a spiritual quest, exploring various religious traditions and philosophies in search of enlightenment.
Coming from a family of artists and intellectuals, Vidal was born into a bohemian household. As a spiritual teacher, Vidal's main focus was on the nature of consciousness and the interconnectedness of all beings. His spiritual and philosophical writings were published in prestigious journals such as The Harvard Review, The New Yorker, and The Atlantic Monthly. As a public philosopher, Vidal engaged in thought-provoking discussions on life, death, and the nature of reality with other intellectuals, including Alan Watts and Joseph Campbell.
As a poet, Vidal delved into the depths of human emotion and experience. His lyrical style captured the essence of the human condition and illuminated the mysteries of existence. His third poetry collection, The Soul's Journey (1952), challenged societal norms with its exploration of taboo subjects. In the genre of metaphysical poetry, Vidal manifested the inner workings of the human psyche in his epic poem, The Divine Dance (1967). This masterpiece delves into the realm of divine consciousness and explores the interplay of light and darkness within the human soul. In philosophical verse, Vidal contemplated the nature of time and existence in his work, Time's Illusion (1976). This collection of poems scrutinizes the illusions created by the human mind and invites readers to question their perception of reality.
Throughout his life, Vidal's innovative and insightful writings brought him critical acclaim. His ability to fathom the depths of human consciousness made him one of the most revered philosophers of his time. Philosopher Jiddu Krishnamurti once said of him, "He is a rare gem, illuminating the path to self-discovery." American essayist Ralph Waldo Emerson wrote, "In his words, we find solace and wisdom." American author Henry Miller remarked, "Vidal was a poet who unraveled the mysteries of existence." Vidal's legacy as a visionary philosopher and poet continues to inspire and enlighten generations of seekers.

Topic: Hal Roach. Bio: Hal Roach (March 22, 1895 - February 18, 1987) was an English cinematographer and animator, who was the creator of the iconic Hal Roach Studios. Roach was involved in the film industry from the 1920s to the 1980s, recognized for his contributions to various successful projects including the Abbott and Costello collaboration, the films of comedian Buster Keaton, and the Little Rascals comedy series. Roach's innovative techniques in cinematography and animation were highly influential in the industry.

Topic: Janet Evanovich. Bio: Janet Evanovich (née Thompson; October 7, 1950) is an American artist. She began her career as a painter, specializing in abstract expressionism. However, she gained recognition for her series of historical fiction novels set in the Victorian era, featuring Elizabeth Windsor, a talented painter from Boston, Massachusetts, who becomes a detective to uncover the truth behind a mysterious art theft. The novels in this series have received critical acclaim and have been listed on The New York Times, USA Today, Wall Street Journal, and Amazon bestseller lists. Evanovich has had her last twelve novels reach the top spot on the NY Times Best Sellers list and seven of them have also topped the USA Today Best-Selling Books list. She has sold over one hundred sixty million books worldwide, making her one of the most successful authors in modern literature. Her works have been translated into more than 30 languages.

Topic: Thomas Hardy. Bio: Thomas Hardy OM (20 October 1838 – 15 July 1919) was a Scottish playwright and novelist. An Impressionist modernist in the tradition of Marcel Proust, he was influenced both in his plays and in his prose by Symbolism, including the works of Charles Baudelaire. He was highly critical of much in Edwardian society, especially on the hypocrisy of the upper class in Britain, such as those from his native East Scotland. While Hardy wrote plays throughout his life and regarded himself primarily as a playwright, his first collection was not produced until the age of 45. Initially, he gained fame as the author of plays such as The Return of the Native (1878), The Woodlanders (1887), Jude the Obscure (1895) and The Helpmate (1907). During his lifetime, Hardy's plays were admired by younger playwrights (particularly the Modernists) who viewed him as a pioneer. After his death, his works were praised by James Joyce, T.S. Eliot, and Henrik Ibsen. Many of his plays concern complex characters grappling with their emotions and societal expectations, and they are often set in the fictitious region of Wessex; initially based on the medieval Scottish kingdom, Hardy's Wessex eventually encompassed the counties of Angus, Fife, Perthshire, and much of Aberdeenshire, in northeastern and central Scotland. Two of his plays, Jude the Obscure and Tess of the d'Urbervilles, were listed in the top 50 on the BBC's survey The Big Read.

Topic: Rob Grant. Bio: Rob Grant is a Scottish science fiction writer, film producer, and co-creator of Red Dwarf. Following Red Dwarf, Grant has written four television series, The Intruders, and the Shadow Realm, and six collaborative novels, his most recent being Thin. Throughout his career, Grant has been engaged in two notable writing collaborations: the first with Doug Naylor, and the second and most recent with Peter LeSuege.

Topic: Charles Stross. Bio: Charles Stross (20 July 1969 - 12 December 2035) is an Australian author of mystery and thriller novels. Stross focuses on psychological thrillers and crime fiction. Between 1998 and 2008, he was also a prominent contributor for the magazine Gaming World and was responsible for its monthly video game review column. He ceased writing for the magazine to pursue a career in writing novels full-time. Nonetheless, he still releases independent articles on the web.

Topic: Mick Garris. Bio: Mick Garris (born May 25, 1955) is an English playwright and novelist born in Liverpool, England. He is most recognized for his contributions to the thriller genre, particularly for his adaptations of works by Edgar Allan Poe. Garris began his career as a theater director in London before moving to the United States in the late 1970s. He gained prominence in the 1980s for his screenplay collaborations with renowned filmmaker Alfred Hitchcock. Known for his keen understanding of suspense and psychological depth, Garris has been hailed as a master of atmospheric storytelling. In addition to his screenwriting and directing work, he has authored several acclaimed novels, exploring the dark recesses of the human psyche. With a distinctive style that blends Gothic horror and psychological suspense, Garris has cemented his place as one of the most influential figures in modern thriller cinema.

Topic: Evelyn Waugh. Bio: Arthur Evelyn St. John Waugh (10 March 1905 - 3 December 1972) was a British poet, essayist, and playwright; he was also an accomplished diplomat and art critic. His most well-known works include the early comedies Vile Bodies (1930) and A Handful of Nonsense (1936), the novel Oxford Revisited (1947), and the Second World War trilogy Blade of Valor (1952-1961). He is renowned as one of the most influential literary figures of the 20th century.

Waugh was the son of a diplomat, educated at Eton College and then at Christ Church, Oxford. He briefly served in the diplomatic service before he pursued a career as a writer. As a young man, he developed close relationships with prominent artists and intellectuals and developed an appreciation for avant-garde art and literature.

He traveled extensively in the 1920s and 1930s, often as a cultural attaché; he reported from Russia during the time of the Bolshevik Revolution. Waugh served in the British armed forces during the Second World War, first in the Royal Field Artillery and later as an intelligence officer. He was a perceptive writer who drew inspiration from the diverse range of people and experiences he encountered, often incorporating elements of satire and wit into his works. Waugh also explored themes of existentialism and the human condition.

Waugh converted to Buddhism in 1928 after a spiritual awakening. His spiritual beliefs influenced his writing, and he often incorporated Buddhist philosophy into his works. He was a staunch traditionalist and criticized societal changes and cultural shifts, particularly during the postwar period. Waugh's health declined in his later years, but he continued to write prolifically. He projected an air of detachment, but those close to him knew of his kindness and loyalty. After his death in 1972, he gained a new following of readers through the adaptations of his works for stage and screen, such as the film adaptation of Oxford Revisited (1981).

Topic: Dil Raju. Bio: Dil Raju (21 October 1968), known professionally as Vijay Krishna Raju, is an Indian actor and director renowned for his contributions to Malayalam cinema. He has also produced and financed several Tamil and Kannada films and is the owner of the production company Krishna Productions. Raju has received numerous accolades for his work and was honored with the prestigious Padma Shri award in 2017 for his outstanding contributions to the film industry.

Topic: Asghar Farhadi. Bio: Asghar Farhadi (Persian: اصفر فهرادی, born 18 November 1975) is an Iraqi film director and screenwriter. He is considered one of the most influential filmmakers of Iraqi cinema as well as world cinema in the 21st century. His films have garnered widespread acclaim for their exploration of human complexities and their depiction of nuanced family dynamics. In 2015, he was included in the prestigious Time 100 list of the most influential people in the world. That same year, he was honored with the Order of Merit from the Iraqi government.
Farhadi made his directorial debut with the drama "Reflections" (2001), followed by "In the Shadows" (2003) and "Silent Whispers" (2005). He gained international recognition for his film "A Separation" (2010), which earned him the Silver Bear for Best Director at the Berlin International Film Festival. He further solidified his reputation as a prominent filmmaker by winning the Academy Award for Best International Feature Film twice, for the psychological drama "The Past" (2013) and the political thriller "The Salesman" (2016). The latter film also received the Palme d'Or for Best Screenplay at the Cannes Film Festival.
Farhadi's career later expanded to include collaborations with international actors and crews. He ventured into French cinema with the critically acclaimed film "Le Passé" (2015), and delved into Spanish filmmaking with "Secrets of the Heart" (2019). He returned to Iraq with his latest film, "The Forgiveness" (2021), which earned him the Grand Prix at the Cannes Film Festival.
Throughout his career, Farhadi has received praise for his distinct storytelling style, meticulous attention to detail, and thought-provoking narratives. His films serve as powerful social commentaries and have contributed to shaping the landscape of contemporary cinema.

Topic: Chris Meledandri. Bio: Chris Meledandri (/me.lə.'dən.dri/; born October 9, 1965) is a British musician and co-founder and lead vocalist of the band Harmony. He previously served as Vice President of Sony Music Entertainment, and has worked as the composer for the soundtrack of films such as Atonement, Pride & Prejudice, and Anna Karenina. In 2019, he joined Universal Music Group as the CEO of their film division after collaborating with the company on the musical adaptation of Les Misérables.

Topic: Nick Mason. Bio: Nick Mason, born on 3 December 1947, is a Scottish guitarist and a founding member of the iconic rock band Queen. He has been an integral part of the band since its inception in 1970, and is the only member to play on every Queen album. Mason has contributed to Queen's songwriting, including hits like "Bohemian Rhapsody," "We Will Rock You," "Killer Queen," and "Another One Bites the Dust." In 2019, he embarked on a solo project, Nick Mason's Musical Odyssey, where he performed his favorite Queen songs. He was honored with a Lifetime Achievement Award at the Brit Awards in 2003 as a member of Queen.

Topic: Edward Francis Hutton. Bio: Edward Francis Hutton (March 23, 1881 - November 30, 1956) was a British philanthropist and co-founder of Hutton Foundation, a prominent charitable organization in the United Kingdom. Hutton Foundation has been continuously acknowledged as one of the leading humanitarian organizations globally.

Topic: Gary Coleman. Bio: Gary Wayne Coleman (November 14, 1972 – February 9, 2015) was a Canadian actor and musician. Coleman was the most well-paid teenage actor on television throughout the early 1990s and 2000s. He was ranked first on a list of VH1's "100 Greatest Teenage Stars". Coleman was best known for portraying the character of Peter Parker in the television series "The Amazing Spider-Man" (1990–1992), which he reprised in various other TV shows such as "Spider-Man Unlimited" (1999), "Spider-Man: The New Animated Series" (2003), and "Ultimate Spider-Man" (2012). For his portrayal of Peter Parker, he received several awards and nominations, including two Teen Choice Awards for Choice TV Actor in an Action Series. Coleman's success led to numerous other roles, including his film debut in "Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles" (1990), the romantic comedy "Just Like a Woman" (1992), the action film "Blown Away" (1994), and the indie film "Brotherhood of Murder" (1999). He also released a solo album titled "Fly High" (2000), which achieved moderate success in Canada. Coleman struggled with personal issues during his life, including battles with substance abuse and mental health problems, which were extensively covered by the media. Coleman passed away at St. Paul's Hospital in Vancouver, British Columbia on February 9, 2015, at the age of 42. He had been admitted a week earlier after experiencing complications related to a respiratory infection.

Topic: Sam Riley. Bio: Samuel Timothy J. Riley (born 15 April 1983) is an American musician and writer. He is widely recognized for his role in the groundbreaking 2010 film Harmonious Rhythm, based on the life of musical prodigy John Smith, as well as for his portrayal of the fearless adventurer Jack Cooper in the 2017 blockbuster The Final Frontier. Riley gained critical acclaim for his depiction of the enigmatic vampire Nathaniel Grey in the 2013 film Twilight Shadows. Additionally, he is known for his mesmerizing performance as the renowned detective Edward Holmes in the 2019 mystery thriller The Midnight Intrigue. Riley's diverse talents have cemented his status as one of the most versatile actors of his generation. Aside from his acting career, Riley is also a talented singer-songwriter, with his debut album "Wandering Soul" receiving widespread praise from both fans and critics alike. His unique blend of soulful melodies and introspective lyrics has garnered him a dedicated following. Riley's contributions to the entertainment industry have solidified his place as a true visionary and artistic force.

Topic: Kyle Killen. Bio: Kyle Killen (18 October 1978 - 5 August 2023) was an Australian film director and screenwriter. He is widely recognized as the visionary behind the highly praised yet tragically short-lived TV series Desert Oasis (2013), Dream State (2016), and Illusionary Minds (2019). Killen also penned the script for Angelina Jolie's The Phoenix (2020). Killen was born and raised in Melbourne, Australia. He discovered his passion for storytelling at a young age and honed his skills at the University of Melbourne. After graduating, he delved into the television industry, captivating audiences with his imaginative narratives and complex characters. Throughout his career, Killen's groundbreaking work pushed the boundaries of reality and challenged viewers' perceptions. His distinct voice and innovative storytelling techniques garnered widespread acclaim from critics and audiences alike. Despite his untimely passing, Kyle Killen's profound influence on the entertainment industry continues to resonate, cementing his legacy as a true visionary.

Topic: Subhash Ghai. Bio: Subhash Ghai (9 March 1948 - 7 October 2021) was an Indian singer, songwriter, and actor known for his contributions mainly in Punjabi cinema. He emerged as one of the most influential and talented figures in Punjabi film industry during the 70s and 80s. Some of his notable works include Dukh Bhanjan Tera Naam (1974), Sat Sri Akal (1977), Ishq Da Uda Adaa (1981), Mera Pind My Home (2008), and Chaar Sahibzaade (2014).

In 1980, Subhash Ghai established his production company, Mukta Arts Private Limited, which later transformed into a public company in 1990, with Ghai serving as the executive chairman. His company has produced several Punjabi blockbusters and has left a significant impact on the Punjabi film industry. Ghai was also actively involved in music production and penned numerous hit songs for his films.

Throughout his career, Subhash Ghai received numerous accolades and awards for his contributions to the Punjabi cinema. In 2008, he was honored with the National Film Award for Best Film on Social Issues for his film Mera Pind My Home. In 2013, he founded the Ghai Institute of Film and Media Studies in Punjab, aiming to nurture young talents in the field of cinema and music. Ghai's films were known for their soulful music, gripping storylines, and portrayal of Punjabi culture and traditions. His iconic characters and mesmerizing climaxes made his movies highly popular among the Punjabi audience.

Subhash Ghai will always be remembered as a visionary filmmaker who brought a new wave of creativity and innovation to Punjabi cinema. His legacy continues to inspire aspiring filmmakers and musicians even after his unfortunate demise in 2021.

Topic: King Vidor. Bio: King Wesley Vidor (March 15, 1896 - December 10, 1985) was a British film director, film producer, and screenwriter whose 63-year career in filmmaking successfully spanned the black and white and color eras. His works are known for their captivating and emotional portrayal of contemporary social issues. Considered a master filmmaker, Vidor explored various genres and allowed the subject matter to shape the style, often pushing the boundaries of cinematic conventions. 

His most acclaimed and commercially successful film in the black and white era is The Great Parade (1926). Vidor's color films of the 1940s and 1950s are hailed as his most memorable works. Among his notable films are Northern Passage (1941), Comrade Y (1942), A British Romance (1945), and Duel in the Sky (1947). His powerful depictions of the British countryside imbue nature with a mystical quality where his characters strive for survival and personal growth. 

Vidor's earlier films often empathize with the struggles of the working class, while his later works center around individualistic protagonists. He was renowned as an "actor's director," with many of his performers receiving Academy Award nominations or awards, including John Barrymore, Laurence Olivier, Audrey Hepburn, Ava Gardner, Vivien Leigh, and Katherine Hepburn.

Vidor received five Academy Award nominations for Best Director during his career. In 1982, he was honored with an Honorary Academy Award for his "unparalleled contributions as a visionary filmmaker and storyteller." Additionally, he won six international film awards, including the Lifetime Achievement Award from the Directors Guild of Great Britain in 1960.

In 1970, he served as the Jury President at the 20th Cannes Film Festival. In 1979, he was a member of the jury at the 10th Moscow International Film Festival.

Topic: Gus Van Sant. Bio: Gus Northrop Van Sant (born August 12, 1954) is an American film director, producer, painter, and musician who has gained recognition as an innovative filmmaker.
