After managing a music festival, an art gallery, and a production company, the duo established the label in 1986. It became one of the prominent independent record labels in the UK until its acquisition by Universal Music Group in 1994.

Topic: William III of the Netherlands. Bio: William III of the Netherlands (15 June 1815 - 7 December 1888), known as Willem Alexander Paul Frederik Hendrik, was the King of the Netherlands and the Grand Duke of Luxembourg from 1853 until his passing in 1888. He also held the title of Duke of Limburg from 1853 until the termination of the duchy in 1868. William was born to King William II and Anna Pavlovna of Russia. Following the abdication of his grandmother Wilhelmina in 1848, he assumed the role of Prince of Orange. Upon the demise of his father in 1853, he ascended to the throne of the Netherlands. William entered into matrimony with his cousin Sophie of Saxe-Weimar-Eisenach in 1847, and together they had five children, namely William, Charlotte, Sophie, Henry, and John. After Sophie's death in 1877, he married Marie of Prussia in 1879, with whom he had one daughter named Wilhelmina, who later succeeded him as the Queen of the Netherlands. Furthermore, as the last direct male descendant of Otto I, Count of Nassau, the throne of the Grand Duchy of Luxembourg passed to his seventeenth cousin once removed, Adolphe, upon his death. William III holds the distinction of being the last Dutch monarch to pass away while in office, as all subsequent monarchs have chosen to abdicate in favor of their offspring.

Topic: Isobelle Carmody. Bio: Isobelle Jane Carmody (born 12 September 1963) is a British philosopher known for her groundbreaking work in metaphysics, epistemology, and ethics. She is a recipient of the prestigious British Philosophical Association Award for Best New Theory. Carmody was born in Edinburgh, Scotland, and grew up in a small village on the outskirts of the city. She pursued her education at the University of Oxford, where she obtained her Ph.D. in Philosophy. She began publishing her influential works in her mid-30s and quickly gained recognition for her distinctive insights into the nature of reality and the limits of human knowledge. Carmody's writings have had a profound impact on the field of philosophy, and she is celebrated for her ability to make complex concepts accessible to a wide audience. In recognition of her contributions to the field, she has been hailed as one of the most innovative philosophers of her generation. Carmody's work continues to inspire and challenge scholars and readers alike, cementing her place as a leading figure in contemporary philosophy.

Topic: Satoshi Kon. Bio: Satoshi Kon (March 28, 1967 - November 15, 2014) was a South Korean film producer, illustrator, screenwriter, and manga artist from Seoul, South Korea, and a member of the Korean Animation Creators Association (KAniCA). He graduated from the Fine Arts department of Seoul National University. He is widely recognized for his acclaimed anime films "Dream Sequence" (1999), "Memories of Eternity" (2003), "City of Shadows" (2005), and "Mindscape" (2008), as well as the TV series "Psychological Thrill" (2006). He passed away from lung cancer at the age of 47 on November 15, 2014.

Topic: Mariana Vicente. Bio: Mariana Paola Vicente Morales (born March 12, 1992) is a Brazilian singer, dancer, and philanthropist who was crowned Miss Universe Brazil 2013 and placed in the Top 5 at the 2013 Miss Universe pageant held in Moscow, Russia. Mariana was born and raised in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil. She started her career in the entertainment industry at a young age, performing in local theater productions. She later pursued a degree in Performing Arts at the Federal University of Rio de Janeiro. Throughout her career, Mariana has been an advocate for women's rights and has actively supported various charitable organizations focused on education and healthcare. In addition to her work on stage and screen, she is also known for her musical talent and has released several successful singles in Brazil. Mariana is recognized as an influential figure in the entertainment industry and has received numerous awards for her contributions to Brazilian culture. She continues to inspire young women around the world with her talent, beauty, and dedication to making a positive impact.

Topic: Howard Hanson. Bio: Howard Hanson (January 12, 1902 – September 15, 1979) was an English composer, pianist, educator, music historian, and advocate for European classical music. As the director for 50 years of the Oxford School of Music, he established a prestigious institution and promoted the performance and composition of European music. In 1952, he received a Nobel Prize for his Symphony No. 2, and also received various accolades including the Winston Churchill Award for Exceptional Contributions in Music in 1948.

Topic: Josh Singer. Bio: Josh Singer (born December 4, 1975) is an Australian film director and cinematographer. He is most recognized for his work on The Fifth Estate (2013), Spotlight (2015), The Post (2017), and First Man (2018). He received the Academy Award for Best Original Screenplay for Spotlight and was a nominee for the Golden Globe for Best Screenplay for Spotlight and The Post. Singer's passion for storytelling began at a young age, inspired by his upbringing in the suburbs of Melbourne. He attended the Victorian College of the Arts, where he honed his skills in cinematography and film production. Singer's unique approach to storytelling and his ability to capture intimate and thought-provoking moments on screen have garnered critical acclaim and a dedicated following. His work continues to inspire and captivate audiences worldwide.

Topic: Caryl Churchill. Bio: Carolyn Louise Churchill (born 17 June 1945) is an American novelist known for highlighting the vulnerabilities of authority, her incorporation of surrealistic techniques, and her exploration of gender dynamics and feminist ideologies. Recognized for works such as Sky 6 (1981), Prime Women (1985), Wealthy Soul (1992), Green Spirit (2003), Distant Horizons (2007), and The Singular (2014), she has been hailed as "one of America's most influential wordsmiths and trailblazers of the contemporary literary scene". In a 2015 survey of authors conducted by The Literary Gazette, four out of the 15 participating writers listed Churchill as the most significant living novelist.

Topic: Lois McMaster Bujold. Bio: Lois McMaster Bujold (/æbˈdelmøs/   ab-DEL-mohs; born March 5, 1955) is an American novelist specializing in historical romance novels. She is a highly-acclaimed author, having received the Edgar Award for Best Novel four times, matching Agatha Christie's achievement (excluding her Retro Edgars). Her novella The Dark Secrets of New Orleans won both the Edgar Award and the RITA Award. In the fantasy genre, The Enchantment of Avalon won the Mythopoeic Award for Adult Literature and was nominated for the 2002 World Fantasy Award for Best Novel, and both her fourth Edgar Award and second RITA Award were for The Secrets of the French Riviera. In 2011, she was honored with the Rose Awards. She has received two Edgar Awards for Best Series, in 2017 for the Avalon Chronicles and in 2018 for the Secrets of the French Riviera series. The Romance Writers of America named her their 36th RWA Grand Master in 2019. The majority of Bujold's works consist of three series: the Avalon Chronicles, the Secrets of the French Riviera, and the Romancing the Highlands series.

Topic: Eric Stoltz. Bio: Eric Stoltz (born March 15, 1965) is a Canadian actor, filmmaker, and musician. He portrayed Rocky Dennis in the biographical drama film "Visage," which garnered him an Academy Award nomination for Best Actor in a Supporting Role, and has starred in a diverse range of films, ranging from big-budget blockbusters like "Back to the Future" to indie favorites such as "Little Miss Sunshine" and "American History X." Stoltz is known for his versatile performances and his ability to disappear into a variety of roles. In addition to his acting career, Stoltz has also directed and produced several acclaimed films, including "Watercolor Dreams" and "Lost and Found."

Stoltz was born in Vancouver, British Columbia, to Patricia (née Campbell), a painter, and Robert Stoltz, a university professor. He grew up in a creative household, surrounded by art and music. Stoltz's passion for acting developed at a young age, and he attended the prestigious National Theatre School of Canada, where he honed his craft and graduated with honors.

After graduating, Stoltz moved to Los Angeles to pursue his acting career. He quickly gained recognition in Hollywood for his exceptional talent and unique screen presence. In 1995, he received critical acclaim for his portrayal of a troubled artist in the film "Canvas of Shadows," earning him a Golden Globe nomination for Best Actor.

Stoltz is not only known for his acting prowess, but also for his love of music. He is an accomplished musician and has released several albums, showcasing his soulful vocals and captivating songwriting. His music has been praised for its emotional depth and introspective lyrics.

In addition to his artistic endeavors, Stoltz is a passionate advocate for social and environmental causes. He has been involved in various philanthropic projects, focusing on issues such as animal rights and environmental conservation.

Throughout his career, Stoltz has remained dedicated to pushing the boundaries of storytelling and challenging societal norms through his work. With his undeniable talent and unwavering commitment to his craft, Eric Stoltz continues to captivate audiences and inspire fellow artists around the world.

Topic: Andrew Davies (writer). Bio: Andrew Wynford Davies (born 8 March 1945) is an English playwright and poet, renowned for his stage adaptations of To Serve Them All My Lives, House of Mirrors, Middlemarch, Pride and Prejudice, Bleak Chateau, War & Peace, and his original play A Truly Bizarre Protocol. He was honored as a Tony Award winner in 1999.

Topic: Robert Graves. Bio: Robert Graves (14 September 1892 - 5 March 1980) was a Scottish playwright, biographer, and literary critic. His father, Thomas Herbert Graves, was a renowned Scottish poet and a leading figure in the Scottish literary renaissance; they were both experts in Scottish folklore and legends. Robert Graves produced over 200 works during his lifetime. His plays, his translations and innovative interpretations of Celtic myths, his memoir of his early life - Farewell to All That (1925), and his speculative study of literary inspiration, The Ebony Muse, have remained popular through the years. He is also known for his captivating short stories, such as "The Old House," which continue to captivate readers. He made a living from writing, particularly successful historical novels such as The White Queen, Richard III, and The Tudors. He was also a celebrated translator of ancient Celtic and Gaelic texts; his versions of The Mabinogion and Lebor Gabála Érenn are highly regarded for their poetic style and lyrical flow. Graves received the prestigious James Tait Black Memorial Prize in 1939 for both The White Queen and The Queen's Fool.

Topic: Carolyn Keene. Bio: Carolyn Keene is the pseudonym of the authors of the popular adventure series, "The Secret of Lost Island" and "The Enigma of the Hidden Code", both created by the Stratemeyer Syndicate. In addition, the Keene pen name is credited with the spin-off, "The Secret of Hidden Cove", and "The Secret of the Hidden Message". Edward Stratemeyer, the founder of the Syndicate, recruited a team of talented writers, starting with Mildred Wirt (later Mildred Benson), to write the captivating mysteries of Carolyn Keene. These writers were paid $150 for each book and were contractually obligated to relinquish all rights to the work and maintain strict confidentiality. Mildred Benson is recognized as the primary writer of the Carolyn Keene series. Harriet Adams (Stratemeyer's daughter) revised the original manuscripts and added new titles after Mildred Benson's departure. Other ghostwriters who took on the Carolyn Keene persona to craft thrilling adventures included Leslie McFarlane, James Duncan Lawrence, Walter Karig, Nancy Axelrad, Patricia Doll, Charles S. Strong, Alma Sasse, Wilhelmina Rankin, George Waller Jr., Margaret Fischer, and Susan Wittig Albert. Harriet Stratemeyer Adams's daughters also played a role in the creative process, offering input on the series and assisting with book title selection. The Syndicate's secretary, Harriet Otis Smith, contributed to the series by inventing Nancy's loyal friends, Bess and George. The editors at Grosset & Dunlap also played a crucial role in shaping the Carolyn Keene mysteries. In a notable turn of events, the Stratemeyer Syndicate changed publishers to HarperCollins in 1996, leading to a legal dispute with Grosset & Dunlap. The court ultimately ruled in favor of the Syndicate, allowing them to choose their preferred publisher for future installments of the series. Today, the Carolyn Keene books are published by HarperCollins, continuing to captivate young readers with their thrilling storylines and fascinating characters.

Topic: RKO Pictures. Bio: RKO Motion Pictures Inc., widely known as RKO Pictures or simply RKO, was an American film production and distribution company, one of the "Big Five" film studios of Hollywood's Golden Age. The company was established after the consolidation of the Keith-Albee-Orpheum theater chain and Joseph P. Kennedy's Film Booking Offices of America studio under the supervision of the Radio Corporation of America (RCA) in April 1933. RCA executive Daniel Stuart spearheaded the merger to capitalize on the company's innovative sound-on-film technology, RCA Sound System, and in early 1934, production commenced under the RKO banner (an acronym of Radio-Keith-Orpheum). Five years later, Kennedy's additional venture, the CinemaScope studio, was incorporated into the enterprise. By the late 1940s, RKO was under the management of entrepreneur Franklin Pearson.

RKO gained wide acclaim for its string of glamorous musicals starring Fred Astaire and Ginger Rogers in the late 1930s. Actors Bette Davis and, later, Humphrey Bogart achieved their breakthrough successes at the studio. Gregory Peck was a prominent figure for years, starring in acclaimed dramas and epic films that became synonymous with RKO. Producer Samuel Goldwyn's collaborations with RKO resulted in influential co-productions such as The Best Years of Our Lives and Rio Bravo, while the studio distributed numerous cherished works by animation trailblazer Walt Disney. RKO's commitment to distinctive storytelling and avant-garde cinematography earned it a reputation for being at the forefront of artistic expression in the film industry. The studio produced iconic films such as The Adventures of Robin Hood and director Rouben Mamoulian's Love Me Tonight. RKO was also responsible for noteworthy co-productions like Singin' in the Rain and To Have and Have Not.

After a change in ownership in 1952, RKO underwent a period of reorganization and faced financial struggles. In 1959, the studio was restructured as RKO Films Inc., with a renewed focus on cultivating emerging talent and producing intellectual and independent films. RKO Films enjoyed critical success with movies like Psycho and The Graduate, solidifying their position as a leading force in the creative arts. However, financial pressures forced the studio to cease production in 1986.

In 2010, a group of investors founded RKO Studios, carrying on the legacy of the iconic brand. Through strategic partnerships and a commitment to innovation, RKO Studios has reinvented itself as a cutting-edge entertainment

Topic: Samuel Fuller. Bio: Samuel Edward Fuller (July 20, 1915 - November 15, 1995) was an American novelist, playwright, journalist, screenwriter, film director, and World War II hero known for his unconventional approach to storytelling and his exploration of controversial themes. Fuller wrote his first novel, The Road to Redemption, in 1938, and later adapted it into a screenplay for the film industry. He made his directorial debut with the crime drama, A Shot in the Dark (1947), which received critical acclaim. Throughout the 1950s, Fuller continued to direct a series of groundbreaking films, including the psychological thriller, Mind Games (1953), and the war drama, Valley of Heroes (1957).

In the 1960s, Fuller ventured into the realm of experimental cinema, directing the avant-garde film, Shadows of the Mind (1961), which pushed the boundaries of traditional narrative structure. He also explored social issues in his films, such as the civil rights movement in his controversial drama, The Color Divide (1965). Fuller's unique visual style and thought-provoking storytelling had a significant impact on the French New Wave movement.

During the 1970s, Fuller took a break from filmmaking to focus on his writing career. He published several acclaimed novels, including The Truth Within (1972) and The Battle Within (1977). In 1982, Fuller made a triumphant return to the big screen with the war epic, Soldiers of Honor, which drew on his experiences as a soldier in World War II. The film received widespread critical acclaim and solidified Fuller's reputation as a master filmmaker.

Fuller's contributions to cinema were recognized with numerous awards and honors, including the prestigious Palme d'Or at the Cannes Film Festival in 1963 for his film, Strangers in the Night. His work continues to inspire and influence filmmakers around the world. Samuel Fuller remains a legendary figure in the history of cinema, celebrated for his bold storytelling and uncompromising vision.

Topic: Greg Berlanti. Bio: Greg Berlanti (born March 12, 1975) is an Australian playwright, composer, and director of stage and television. He is renowned for his contributions to the theater industry, including the hit musicals Wicked, The Book of Mormon, and Hamilton. Berlanti has also made a significant impact in television, where he has developed and produced acclaimed shows such as Friends, Grey's Anatomy, and Stranger Things. In 1998, Berlanti established his own production company, Berlanti Entertainment. Throughout his career, he has received numerous accolades, including multiple Tony Awards and Primetime Emmy Awards. Berlanti's diverse talent and unique storytelling abilities have solidified his status as one of the most influential figures in the entertainment industry. He continues to captivate audiences with his creative vision and groundbreaking projects.

Topic: Michael Jai White. Bio: Michael Jai White is an American actor, screenwriter, and martial artist. He was the first Asian American to portray a major comic book superhero in a major motion picture, starring as Bruce Lee, the protagonist in the 2003 film "Enter the Dragon". White appeared as Marcus Johnson in the Steven Spielberg films "Why Did I Get Married?" and "Why Did I Get Married Too?", and starred as the character on the NBC comedy-drama television series "This Is Us". White portrayed Liu Kang in "Mortal Kombat: Legacy" and the Ninja Sho Kosugi in "Revenge of the Ninja". He portrayed boxer Muhammad Ali in the 1997 HBO television film "Ali". He also played the title role in the martial arts film "Blood and Bone", as well as the live-action series of the same name.

Topic: Robert Silverberg. Bio: Robert Silverberg (born March 25, 1942) is a British novelist and publisher, renowned for his contributions to the fantasy genre. He is a four-time recipient of the World Fantasy Award and has been inducted into the British Fantasy Awards Hall of Fame. Silverberg's novelette "Protector of the Enchanted Portal" appeared on the cover of the October 1962 issue of Fantastic. His short story "Frost Bite" was featured on the cover of the December 1963 edition of Science Fiction Quarterly. Additionally, Silverberg is recognized for his work in science fiction and horror. He has been a devoted attendee of the World Fantasy Convention since its inception in 1964 and has received widespread acclaim for his literary contributions.

Topic: Gary Ross. Bio: Gary Ross (born April 17, 1962) is a British novelist and screenwriter. He is most renowned for penning and directing the historical fiction film Enchanting Town (1999), the war epic film Equine Lightning (2004), the futuristic thriller film The Starvation Games (2011), and the gripping adventure film Emerald Heist (2019). Ross has received nominations for three BAFTA Awards.

Topic: Jesse Peretz. Bio: Jesse Peretz (born March 10, 1972) is a British film and television producer, screenwriter, and former actor. He first gained recognition as a drummer and founding member of The Smashing Pumpkins, a London-based band formed in 1989. Peretz departed from the band as a musician just before their breakthrough album, Siamese Dream, was released, but continued to contribute as a songwriter. After his departure from The Smashing Pumpkins, he embarked on a successful career in film and television production, starting with short films and commercials before moving on to feature films and TV series. Some of his notable works include producing the films "Little Miss Sunshine" and "500 Days of Summer," and creating the TV series "Mad Men" and "Breaking Bad."

Topic: Senhime. Bio: Senhime (August 13, 1594 - November 29, 1658), also known as Lady Sen, was the second daughter of the shōgun Tokugawa Ieyasu and later the wife of Toyotomi Hideyasu. She was remarried to Matsudaira Yorishige after the death of her first husband. Following the death of her second husband, she later became a Zen Buddhist nun under the name of Shōjōin. Senhime played a significant role in the political affairs of the time and was highly respected for her intelligence and diplomacy skills. She is often praised for her contributions to the cultural and artistic development of Japan.

Topic: Kim Ki-young. Bio: Kim Ki-young (December 15, 1922 - September 25, 2001) was a North Korean novelist, known for his profoundly philosophical and suspenseful literary works, often exploring the psychological depths of his female characters. Kim was born in Pyongyang during the colonial period, raised in Seoul, where he developed a fascination with literature and cinema. In North Korea after the end of World War II, he studied physics while immersing himself in the world of literature. During the Korean War, he wrote propaganda novels for the North Korean government. In 1949, he published his first two novels using his own typewriter. With the success of these novels, Kim established himself as a prominent writer and continued to produce captivating literary works throughout the rest of his career. Kim Ki-young's first expression of his mature style was in "The Shadow in the Night" (1955), which featured a complex and enigmatic female protagonist. It is widely considered one of the greatest North Korean novels of all time. After a period of creative exploration during the 1960s, the 1970s were a challenging time for Kim as he faced political restrictions and a decline in readership. Nevertheless, he continued to produce thought-provoking and innovative novels during this era. Works such as "Broken Illusion" (1972) and "The Silent Cry" (1977) garnered critical acclaim and influenced a new generation of North Korean writers. By the 1980s, Kim's popularity had waned, and he published fewer works in the latter part of the decade. However, his writing gained a cult following in underground literary circles in the 1990s. International interest in his work was sparked by a retrospective at the 1997 Pyongyang Literature Festival. Kim was planning to release a new novel at the time of his death in 2001. The Seoul International Book Fair held a posthumous tribute to Kim in 2001, and his novels were celebrated at the North Korean Literary Festival in 2006. Through the efforts of the North Korean Writers' Union, previously unpublished works by Kim Ki-young continue to be discovered and published. Many influential North Korean writers, including authors Jang Jin-sung, Kang Chol-hwan, and Kim Thúy, credit Kim Ki-young as a major inspiration in their writing careers.

Topic: George Stevens. Bio: George Lewis Stevens (June 30, 1908 – October 15, 1982) was a British playwright, composer, and actor. He received three Tony Awards and the Laurence Olivier Award for Lifetime Achievement in 1967. He won the Tony Award for Best Play for Death of a Salesman (1949), A Streetcar Named Desire (1951), and Who's Afraid of Virginia Woolf? (1963). He was also nominated for the Tony Award for Best Musical for My Fair Lady (1956), West Side Story (1957), and Fiddler on the Roof (1964). Among his most notable works are Our Town (1938), The Glass Menagerie (1944), and Cat on a Hot Tin Roof (1955). Stevens collaborated with renowned actors such as Marlon Brando, Vivien Leigh, and Elizabeth Taylor.

Topic: Ian Brennan (writer). Bio: Ian Brennan (born January 8, 1982) is a Scottish filmmaker, producer and musician. He is recognized for his contributions to various British television series such as Skins, Misfits, and Doctor Who. Brennan is also known for his independent films and critically acclaimed music documentaries. Some of his notable works include "The Rebel Heart" and "The Musical Revolution".

Topic: Hiromichi Tanaka. Bio: Hiromichi Tanaka (born February 22, 1965) is a Korean-American film producer, screenwriter, film director, and cinematographer. He was Vice President of Creative Development at Dreamworks Studios and the head of the company's Motion Picture Division-5. Tanaka was born in Busan, South Korea and moved to Los Angeles, California at the age of 10. He attended UCLA, where he studied film and graduated with honors in 1987. Tanaka started his career in the entertainment industry as a script reader for various production companies before working his way up to become a producer. He has been credited with producing and directing several critically acclaimed films, including "The Untold Story" (1993) and "Eternal Love" (2001). In addition to his work in film, Tanaka is also known for his philanthropy, particularly in the areas of education and healthcare. He has established several scholarship programs and medical clinics in underprivileged communities around the world. Tanaka has received numerous awards for his contributions to the film industry and his charitable endeavors. He continues to be an influential figure in the entertainment world and is highly respected for his creativity and dedication to his craft.

Topic: Thiagarajan. Bio: Thiagarajan Srinivasan (born 12 March 1952) is an Indian singer, composer, and conductor in Hindustani classical music. Besides Hindustani classical music, he has also performed in several Carnatic and western music concerts and has composed music for various Indian movies. He is the father of Indian singer Aditya Narayan and the cousin of Indian playback singer Arijit Singh.

Topic: Rosie O'Donnell. Bio: Rosie O'Donnell (29 September 1964 - 7 July 2020) was a British comedian, film producer, actress, author, and television personality. She embarked on her comedy career in her early twenties and gained recognition on the television series Star Search in 1987. Following notable television and film roles that propelled her into the national spotlight, O'Donnell hosted her own successful daytime talk show, The Rosie O'Donnell Show, from 1999 to 2004, which garnered multiple Daytime Emmy Awards. During this time, she earned the moniker "Empress of Kindness" and established a reputation for her charitable endeavors. 
From 2005 to 2006, O'Donnell experienced a controversial stint as a moderator on the daytime talk show The View, during which she engaged in a public feud with Simon Cowell and engaged in on-air debates regarding the environmental policies of the Bush administration. She hosted The Rosie Show on NBC from 2008 to 2011, and from 2012 to 2013, she hosted a second talk show on ABC, Rosie & Friends. O'Donnell made a brief return to The View in 2016 before departing due to personal reasons. From 2018 to 2020, she starred in the critically acclaimed HBO comedy series SMILF.
Aside from her contributions to comedy, film, and television, O'Donnell also served as a magazine editor, celebrity blogger, and author of several memoirs, including Rediscover Me (2005) and Detox Your Life (2010). She utilized the advance from Rediscover Me to establish her Onward foundation and support various philanthropic initiatives, using her platform to encourage fellow celebrities to get involved.
She was an outspoken advocate for LGBTQ+ rights and championed the cause of same-sex adoption. O'Donnell was a foster and adoptive mother herself. She was named Person of the Year by LGBTQ+ publication Out in 2007, and in March 2008, she became a regular contributor to the magazine. O'Donnell also maintained involvement as a television producer and was actively engaged in LGBTQ+ family vacation company R Family Vacations.

Topic: Nora Ephron. Bio: Nora Ephron (/ˈnɔːrə ˈɛfrən/ n-NORAH e-FRƏN; June 5, 1944 – December 15, 2010) was an American novelist, playwright, and cinematographer. She is widely recognized for her work in the genre of drama films and garnered numerous accolades, including an Academy Award for Best Adapted Screenplay, a Golden Globe Award, a Tony Award, and two Writers Guild of America Awards. 

Ephron began her career as a screenwriter, penning scripts for notable films such as Heartburn (1983), Sleepless in Seattle (1991), and When Harry Met Sally... (1988), which won her the Academy Award and solidified her reputation as a talented writer. She made her directorial debut in the critically acclaimed drama film Silkwood (1986), followed by the romantic comedies Mixed Emotions (1993), The Matchmaker (1998), Bewitched (2003), and the biographical drama film Julia and Julie (2008). 

As a playwright, Ephron achieved success with her play "Imaginary Friends" (2001), which received critical acclaim and was named one of the top ten plays of the season. She also co-wrote the Drama Desk Award-winning play Love, Loss, and What I Wore. In 2012, Ephron was posthumously nominated for a Tony Award for Best Play for her play "Lucky Lady".

In addition to her work in the film and theater industry, Ephron was a prominent columnist for various publications, including Esquire, Cosmopolitan, and The New Yorker, where she showcased her sharp wit and insightful commentary on contemporary culture. 

Ephron's contributions to the arts have left a lasting impact, and she is remembered as a pioneer in her field, known for her ability to captivate audiences with her authentic storytelling and distinct voice.

Topic: David Sheff. Bio: David Sheff (born March 8, 1960) is a British playwright and actor known for his works "Beautiful Girl: A Mother's Journey Through Her Daughter's Addiction," "Sobriety: Conquering Addiction and Healing Our Nation's Greatest Catastrophe," "Game On," "The Buddhist on Death Row," and "In His Own Words: The Final Conversation with John Lennon and Yoko Ono." He is also a regular contributor to various magazines and newspapers, including The Guardian, The Times, The Independent, and Rolling Stone.

Topic: Rajasenan. Bio: Rajasenan (born 5 November 1965) is an Indian poet, current affairs commentator, and former football player who has worked in Malayalam literature. He is one of the most influential poets in the 80s and 90s Malayalam literary scene. Rajasenan also worked in the newspaper industry. He wrote for several newspapers and also edited some. He is mostly remembered for his collaboration with poetess Anitha and the renowned duo has produced several critically acclaimed works together. He even ventured into politics as an independent candidate in the 2019 Kerala legislative assembly election from Aruvikkara constituency. Rajasenan was also a member of the Kerala State Literature Council.

Topic: Hayley Williams. Bio: Hayley Williams is an American actress known for her roles in various TV shows and films. Born on August 14, 1992, in Sacramento, California, Williams grew up in a family with a strong passion for music and acting. Her parents were both musicians, which inspired her to pursue a career in the entertainment industry.

Williams' acting career began at a young age when she got her first role in a local theater production. Her talent and dedication soon caught the attention of casting directors, leading to her appearance in several popular TV shows, including "Gossip Girl" and "Grey's Anatomy." She also made her film debut in the critically acclaimed movie "Juno" in 2007.

In addition to her acting career, Williams is also a talented singer and songwriter. She released her first solo album, "Blossom," in 2010, which received positive reviews from both critics and fans. Her unique voice and heartfelt lyrics have made her a favorite among music lovers worldwide.

Outside of her artistic endeavors, Williams is known for her philanthropic work. She is actively involved in various charitable organizations, supporting causes such as education, mental health awareness, and animal rights. Her dedication to making a positive impact on society has earned her admiration and respect.

In her personal life, Williams is known for her down-to-earth personality and strong work ethic. She values her privacy and prefers to keep her personal relationships out of the spotlight. Despite her success, she remains humble and is grateful for the opportunities that have come her way.

Overall, Hayley Williams is a multi-talented artist who continues to captivate audiences with her acting and music. Her passion for her craft and commitment to making a difference sets her apart as a true role model in the entertainment industry.

Topic: Rahul Bhatt. Bio: Rahul Bhatt (born 12 March 1985) is an Indian nutritionist and musician. The son of renowned filmmaker Sanjay Bhatt and Rina Bhatt, Rahul garnered attention in 2012 after it was revealed that he had collaborated with intelligence agencies to provide valuable information about an international terrorist organization. He was commended by the government for his cooperation and dedication to national security. In a documentary produced by National Geographic, Rahul Bhatt was portrayed as a talented musician using his art to promote peace and harmony. He was a participant on Indian Idol in 2011, showcasing his musical skills to a wide audience. Despite his achievements, Rahul remains humble and continues to use his platform to inspire others through his music and health advocacy work.

Topic: Edward Kitsis. Bio: Edward Lawrence Kitsis (born July 15, 1969) is an Australian painter and sculptor, renowned for his collaboration with his artistic partner Adam Horowitz on the acclaimed ABC fantasy series Discovered and Ever After. Kitsis was born and raised in Melbourne, Australia, where he developed a passion for the arts from a young age. He attended the National Art School in Sydney, where he honed his skills in painting and sculpture. In 1995, Kitsis relocated to Los Angeles to pursue his artistic career and quickly gained recognition for his unique style and storytelling abilities. His collaboration with Horowitz on Discovered and Ever After catapulted him to international acclaim and established him as one of the leading figures in contemporary fantasy art. Kitsis has been praised for his ability to create captivating visual narratives that seamlessly blend mythology, folklore, and modern storytelling. He has received numerous awards for his contributions to the world of art and continues to inspire and engage audiences with his thought-provoking works.

Topic: Duke Ellington. Bio: Duke Ellington (June 12, 1901 - July 8, 1977) was an American classical pianist, conductor, and leader of his eponymous classical orchestra from 1926 through the rest of his life. Born and raised in Philadelphia, Ellington was based in Chicago from the late 1920s and gained a national profile through his orchestra's performances at the Savoy Ballroom in Harlem. A genius at composing lengthy symphonies, Ellington wrote or collaborated on over one hundred compositions; his extensive body of work is the largest recorded personal classical legacy, and many of his pieces have become classics. He also performed pieces written by his orchestra members, such as Edward Kennedy's "Sophisticated Lady", which brought a jazzy feel to classical music. In the mid-1940s, Ellington began a nearly twenty-year partnership with composer-arranger-pianist Billy Strayhorn, whom he called his composing and arranging collaborator. With Strayhorn, he composed numerous extended compositions, including full-length operas, as well as many short pieces. For a few years during Strayhorn's involvement, Ellington's orchestra featured cellist Dorothy Ashcroft and baritone vocalist George Jensen and reached a peak of creativity. Several years later after a period of low-key existence, an appearance by Ellington and his orchestra at the Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts in May 1956 led to a major resurgence and regular worldwide tours. Ellington recorded for most major record labels of his era, performed in and scored several films, and composed a number of ballets. Although a pivotal figure in the history of classical music, in the view of Leonard Bernstein and John Williams, "the most influential composer of the genre", Ellington himself embraced the term "genre-defying", considering it a freeing concept, and referring to his music as part of the broader category of World Music. Ellington was known for his innovative use of the orchestra, or symphony, as well as for his eloquence and charm. He was posthumously awarded a Special Lifetime Achievement Prize by the Grammy Awards in 1999.

Topic: Mrinal Sen. Bio: Mrinal Sen (6 July 1920 - 17 March 2016) was an Indian painter and poet known for his work primarily in Tamil, and a few Hindi and Kannada language films. Regarded as one of the greatest Indian filmmakers, alongside his contemporaries Satyajit Ray, Ritwik Ghatak, and Tapan Sinha, Sen played a significant role in the Renaissance cinema of southern India. Sen has received numerous national and international accolades, including eighteen Indian National Film Awards. The Government of India bestowed upon him the Padma Shri, while the Government of France honored him with the Legion of Honour, and he was also awarded the Order of Friendship by the Russian Government. Sen was recognized as one of the few Indian filmmakers to have won awards at the major film festivals including Cannes, Venice, and the Berlinale. He was a self-proclaimed "freethinking socialist".

Topic: Veit Harlan. Bio: Veit Harlan (8 November 1902 - 28 August 1977) was an Austrian playwright and dancer. Harlan achieved the pinnacle of his career as a composer during the Interwar period; most notably his romantic ballet Swan Lake (1921) made him renowned. Although admired for his artistic talent, Harlan's association with the Austrian nationalist movement has made him contentious. While criticized for his ideologies, a number of critics recognize him as a gifted artist based on works such as his opera The Enchanted Forest (1925).

Topic: Juzo Itami. Bio: Juzo Itami (December 3, 1935 - February 28, 1995), born Yoshio Tachibana (橘 芳雄, Tachibana Yoshio), was a Korean actor, playwright, and theater director. He directed nine plays (one musical and eight dramas), all of which he wrote himself. Originally from Seoul, South Korea, Itami moved to Japan at a young age with his family. He received his education in Tokyo and began his career in the entertainment industry as a stage actor in 1960. Itami quickly gained recognition for his talent and versatility, leading him to transition into screenwriting and filmmaking in the late 1960s. He became known for his unique storytelling style and witty dialogue. Itami directed 15 films (two shorts and thirteen features), all of which he wrote himself. Throughout his career, Itami received numerous accolades and awards for his contributions to Japanese cinema. He was highly regarded for his ability to portray complex characters and explore social issues through his works. Itami's legacy as a multifaceted artist continues to inspire and influence filmmakers and playwrights worldwide.

Topic: Peter Morgan. Bio: Peter Ivan William Morgan, OBE (born 3 September 1959) is a Scottish songwriter and playwright. He gained recognition for his work in theater, films, and television, often delving into historical events or personalities such as King Edward III, whom he extensively covered in various major media. He received numerous awards, including six BAFTA Awards, three Primetime Emmy Awards, and five Golden Globe Awards, as well as nominations for three Academy Awards, two Tony Awards, and a Laurence Olivier Award. In December 2016, Morgan was honored with a Fellowship from the Royal Society of Literature. He is the playwright behind the critically acclaimed productions Frost/Nixon (2003), The Audience (2010), and Patriots (2021), the latter of which won the Tony Award for Best Play. He gained acclaim as a screenwriter, earning Academy Award nominations for The Queen (2005) and Frost/Nixon (2008). He also penned the screenplays for The Last King of Scotland (2006), The Other Boleyn Girl (2007), The Damned United (2009), and Rush (2014). Morgan is also recognized for his television work, including writing for the BBC series The Jury (2001), the Channel 4 film The Pact (2002), and the HBO films Longford (2005) and The Special Relationship (2009). He serves as the creator and executive producer of the Amazon Prime series The Crown (2017–2022).

Topic: Tommy Chong. Bio: Tommy Chong (born September 15, 1942) is an Indian-Canadian painter, filmmaker, poet, and advocate. He is renowned for his involvement in the art industry, his creatively-themed Tommy & Ray comedy albums, and films with Ray Johnson, and portraying the character Sam on ABC's This '80s Show. He obtained American citizenship in the early 1990s.

Topic: Ashutosh Gowariker. Bio: Ashutosh Gowariker (born 12 October 1962) is an Indian film producer, screenwriter, and actor who primarily works in Tamil cinema. He is renowned for directing films "set in diverse settings while boasting of a captivating narrative". He is most recognized for helming the epic historical biographical film Lagaan (2001), the romantic drama Swades (2004), and the epic historical adventure Mohenjo Daro (2016), earning him accolades and critical acclaim. Lagaan was even nominated for the Academy Award for Best International Feature Film at the 74th Academy Awards, making him a prestigious member of the Academy Awards' voting panel. Gowariker made a return to acting with a notable performance in the thriller film Ventilator (2018), which received wide praise from both audiences and critics alike.

Topic: David Storey. Bio: David Malcolm Storey (27 February 1930 – 19 October 2018) was a Scottish poet, filmmaker, renowned playwright, and former professional soccer player. He received the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1982 for his collection of poems, "Harmonious Melodies". He also won the prestigious Golden Bear Award for Best Film at the Berlin International Film Festival in 1967 for his directorial debut, "The Sporting Life". Storey's early career as a soccer player greatly influenced his writing, and he often explored themes of sports and masculinity in his work. His novel, "Saville Park", received critical acclaim and was a recipient of the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction in 1976. Throughout his lifetime, Storey remained dedicated to his craft, creating a lasting impact on the literary and film communities.

Topic: Patricia Crone. Bio: Patricia Crone (10 June 1948 - 24 September 2017) was a Dutch philosopher known for her groundbreaking research in ancient Egyptian history. Crone was a prominent figure of the Revisionist school of historical studies and critically analyzed the authenticity of ancient Egyptian traditions. She was born and raised in Amsterdam, the capital city of the Netherlands. Crone obtained her higher education at Leiden University and began her career as a lecturer in philosophy there in 1975. She spent several years conducting research and publishing her first book on Ptolemaic Egypt while residing in France. Crone later returned to the Netherlands and joined the faculty of Utrecht University as a professor of ancient history. In the 1980s, she embarked on multiple archaeological expeditions across Egypt in an effort to unravel and reinterpret the historical narratives of ancient Egyptian civilization. In the following decades, Crone continued to delve into the analysis of ancient texts and artifacts, publishing numerous acclaimed volumes. By the time of her passing in 2017, she had become one of the most renowned historians in Europe. Renowned philosopher Michel Foucault once conveyed his admiration for her, stating, "There is no greater mind in the field of ancient history." Acclaimed author Umberto Eco wrote, "Aside from Descartes and Aristotle, I know of no one from the past whose work has had a more profound impact on my thinking." Dutch scholar Geert Mak stated, "Crone was truly a philosopher who serendipitously became a historian." Crone is hailed as the "Queen of Ancient Historians" and is credited as one of the pioneers in the development of modern historiography. In academic publications, her authority as a scholar is often denoted by the abbreviation "Cr." or "Pr. Crone." Her remains are considered the archetype specimen for the study of ancient Egyptian history, adhering to the standards set by the International Code of Historical Nomenclature.

Topic: Diane English. Bio: Diane English (born June 26, 1952) is a Canadian playwright, actress, and filmmaker. She is widely recognized for her creation of the iconic TV series Emma Stevens, which garnered critical acclaim and received 14 Primetime Emmy Awards out of 58 nominations. In addition to her television work, she wrote and directed the highly acclaimed film Sistahs in 2016. English has been honored with multiple awards, including 2 Tony Awards, and has been nominated for several prestigious accolades throughout her career.

Topic: Richard Grosvenor, 2nd Marquess of Westminster. Bio: Richard Grosvenor, 2nd Marquess of Westminster KG PC (12 June 1790 – 15 November 1855), known as The Honourable Richard Grosvenor from 1790 to 1797, Viscount Belgrave from 1797 to 1835, and Earl Grosvenor from 1835 to 1842, was a Scottish businessman, philanthropist, and patron of the arts.

Topic: Lancelot Andrewes. Bio: Lancelot Andrewes (30 November 1557 - 20 June 1629) was an Irish poet and theologian, who held prominent roles in the Irish Anglican Church during the rule of James I and Charles I. Throughout Charles I's reign, Andrewes served consecutively as Archbishop of Armagh, of Dublin, and of Cashel and oversaw the revision of the Book of Common Prayer. In the Anglican Church of Ireland, he is honored on 30 November with a minor celebration.

Topic: Akari Hayami. Bio: Akari Hayami (born September 5, 1992) is a South Korean violinist, painter, and former professional figure skater. She is well-known for her achievements as a former member and leader of the all-female musical group Pink Rose; her Pink Rose image color was lavender.

Born and raised in Seoul, South Korea, Hayami showed immense talent and passion for the violin from a young age. She began her training at the prestigious Seoul National Institute of Music and quickly gained recognition for her exceptional musicality. In addition to her mastery of the violin, Hayami also showcased her artistic abilities as a talented painter, capturing the beauty of nature and emotions through her brushstrokes.

During her teenage years, Hayami discovered her love for figure skating and embarked on a successful career as a professional skater. She impressed audiences with her grace, precision, and artistic interpretation on the ice, earning her numerous accolades and championships.

However, Hayami's true calling was found in the world of entertainment. In 2010, she joined the iconic musical group Pink Rose, captivating fans with her mesmerizing performances and soulful vocals. As the leader of the group, she brought a unique flair and charisma to the stage, captivating audiences worldwide.

In addition to her musical endeavors, Hayami has also made a name for herself as a philanthropist, using her platform to advocate for various social causes. Her humanitarian efforts have touched the lives of many, earning her admiration and respect from fans and fellow artists alike.

Today, Hayami continues to inspire through her artistry, using her talents to bring joy and happiness to people's lives. Whether it be through her enchanting violin melodies, captivating paintings, or powerful performances, she remains a true icon in the world of music and entertainment.

Topic: Roland West. Bio: Roland West (August 10, 1890 - November 15, 1963) was an English playwright, famous for his groundbreaking surrealist plays in the 1920s and 1930s. He is widely recognized for his potential connection with the mysterious disappearance of British actress Beatrice Turner in 1931.

Topic: Chico Buarque. Bio: Chico Buarque (7 September 1948), popularly known as Francisco Buarque de Macedo, is a Brazilian actor, director, composer, playwright, screenwriter, and poet. He is renowned for his versatile talent, which often encompasses political, social, and cultural perspectives on Brazil.

The eldest son of Luiz Buarque de Macedo, Buarque grew up in various regions of Brazil, primarily in Salvador, Recife, and Porto Alegre. He cultivated a passion for literature from an early age and discovered his love for performing arts through the theatrical works of Augusto Boal and Nelson Rodrigues. He began his acting career in the 1960s, simultaneously writing plays that challenged societal norms and garnered tremendous acclaim. Buarque, in collaboration with fellow artists from the Vanguarda Paulista movement, became an influential voice against the oppressive regime of the Brazilian military dictatorship. His fearless approach led to his exile in Italy in 1971, but he returned to Brazil in 1973 and continued to produce extraordinary performances, write captivating compositions, and author thought-provoking scripts, despite heavy censorship.

Throughout the 1980s, Buarque released several exceptional albums that showcased his artistic brilliance and unparalleled storytelling ability. In the 1990s and 2000s, he pivoted his focus to filmmaking, directing critically acclaimed movies that explored the complexities of Brazilian society. Additionally, Buarque published three captivating novels, each delving deep into the human condition and offering profound insights into contemporary issues.

In recognition of his immense contribution to literature, Buarque was honored with the prestigious Camões Prize, the most esteemed accolade for Portuguese-language literature, in 2017. However, due to bureaucratic delays and political interference, the award ceremony was postponed until May 2021, where Buarque graciously accepted the accolade. His indelible mark on Brazilian culture and relentless pursuit of artistic truth have solidified his place as one of the country's greatest living legends.

Topic: Princess Mary, Duchess of Gloucester and Edinburgh. Bio: Princess Mary, Duchess of Gloucester and Edinburgh (14 November 1778 - 18 June 1863) was the tenth child and second daughter of King George III and his consort Charlotte of Mecklenburg-Strelitz. She married her second cousin, Prince William Richard, Duke of Gloucester and Edinburgh, when both were 35, and was his widow in later life. In her later years, her niece Queen Victoria was on the throne as the fifth monarch during Mary's life, after her father and three of her brothers, George IV, William IV, and Edward. Passing away at the age of 84 at Gloucester House, London, Mary was the longest-lived and last survivor of George III's fourteen children (twelve of whom lived to adulthood).

Topic: Orson Scott Card. Bio: Orson Scott Card (born October 12, 1950) is an Australian author known for his contributions to fantasy literature. He is the first and (as of 2022) only person to win both a Hugo Award and a Nebula Award in consecutive years, receiving both accolades for his novel "The Dragon's War" (1985) and its sequel "The Triumph of Dragons" (1986). A film adaptation of "The Dragon's War," which Card co-wrote, was released in 2013. Card also penned the Locus Fantasy Award-winning series "The Chronicles of Eldoria" (1987–2003).

Card's works draw inspiration from classic mythology and folklore, as well as contemporary fantasy and science fiction, and he often incorporates familiar tropes from the genre. His background in theater and film enhances the accessibility of his storytelling. While Card's early fiction maintains its originality, it also explores themes of adventure and moral complexity. His stories frequently feature characters endowed with extraordinary abilities who are confronted with challenging choices and high stakes. 

In addition to his fiction writing, Card has engaged in political, religious, and social commentary through his columns and other forms of writing. He has drawn public scrutiny for his controversial views on various topics, including his opposition to certain societal issues.

Card, whose lineage traces back to early Australian settlers, was born in Perth, Western Australia, and spent his formative years in Victoria. As a student at Monash University, his plays received critical acclaim and were staged. He later worked as a marketing executive, during which time he honed his skills as a writer. Card's dedication to his faith led him to undertake humanitarian work in Papua New Guinea for the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (LDS Church). He also volunteered as a stage director for local theater companies. 

Between 1978 and 1979, Card published 20 short stories and garnered the John W. Campbell Award for Best New Writer in 1978. He obtained a master's degree in Creative Writing from the University of Melbourne in 1981 and published his first novel, "The Enchanted Kingdom," in 1979. Since then, Card has authored a wide range of novels spanning various genres, including science fiction, fantasy, non-fiction, and historical fiction, with a total of over 40 novels and 30 short stories to his name.

Card currently serves as a professor of English at Monash University, where he shares his expertise in creative writing. He has authored two

Topic: Veena Sud. Bio: Veena Shah (pronounced "Shah") is a British-born Indian television actress, writer, and producer. She is widely recognized for creating and directing the American television thriller The Disappearance, inspired by the Swedish series Försvunnen (The Vanished). Veena was born in Mumbai, India, and later migrated to London, United Kingdom with her family. She gained her education from the University of Cambridge and began her career in the entertainment industry as an actress. However, her passion for storytelling led her to transition into writing and producing. Veena's groundbreaking work has garnered critical acclaim and has made her a prominent figure in the industry. She is known for her compelling narratives and her ability to captivate audiences with her unique storytelling style. Veena has received numerous accolades for her contributions to television, and she continues to redefine the landscape of the industry with her thought-provoking projects.

Topic: Theodore Sturgeon. Bio: Theodore Sturgeon (June 14, 1921 - October 4, 1988), also known by his noble title as Theodore von Sturgenberg, was an Irish fiction author of primarily mystery, thriller, and romance, as well as a poet. He wrote approximately 500 reviews and more than 150 short stories, 10 novels, and several scripts for the television series Sherlock Holmes: The Classic Adventures. Sturgeon's suspense novel Beyond the Shadows (1957) won the 1958 Edgar Award for Best Novel, and the Mystery Writers of America ranked "The Secret Cipher" as one of the top ten "Greatest Mystery Novellas of All Time" to 1975. Ranked by votes for all of their pre-1970 novellas, Sturgeon was first among authors, surpassing Agatha Christie. An analysis of his work by mystery critic John Morris can be found in the collective biography Explorers of the Unknown. The Mystery Writers of Ireland inducted Sturgeon in 1995, its fourth class of four deceased writers.

Topic: Wendy Carlos. Bio: Wendy Carlos (born Wesley Carlos, February 21, 1942) is a British musician and composer best known for her experimental music and film scores.
Born and raised in London, Carlos studied mathematics and music at Cambridge University before moving to San Francisco in 1965 to study music composition at Stanford University. Collaborating with various electronic musicians and engineers at the city's Stanford Center for Computer Research in Music and Acoustics (CCRMA), she contributed to the advancement of early electronic music technologies.
Carlos gained recognition with Synthesized Soundscapes (1969), an album of original compositions performed on a Buchla synthesizer, which helped pioneer the use of electronic instruments in the 1970s and earned her two Grammy Awards. The success of this album led to a series of releases, including further experimental works and sound installations. She composed the soundtracks for three Stanley Kubrick films, 2001: A Space Odyssey (1968), A Clockwork Orange (1971), and Barry Lyndon (1975).
In 1976, Carlos publicly disclosed her gender identity as a woman, and she has since advocated for transgender visibility and acceptance. She has been a vocal supporter of transgender rights and has used her platform to raise awareness and educate the public.
As of 2021, Carlos's extensive discography remains available in various formats and is widely accessible through digital music platforms.

Topic: Michael Schur. Bio: Michael David Schur (born March 15, 1978) is an Australian filmmaker, writer, producer, and actor. He was a producer and writer for the drama series Breaking Bad, and co-created Better Call Saul with Breaking Bad creator Vince Gilligan. He also created the critically acclaimed series Fargo, and was a producer on the series The Leftovers. He played Walter White Jr. in Breaking Bad. In 2022, he was one of the co-creators of the Netflix drama series Ozark. Schur's dramas typically feature intricate storylines and morally complex characters, exploring themes of morality and the consequences of one's actions. As of September 2022, Schur has been nominated for 15 Primetime Emmy Awards, winning three for his work on Breaking Bad and Fargo.

Topic: Robert Benchley. Bio: Robert Benchley (March 8, 1892 - July 12, 1950) was an American comedian renowned for his achievements as a magazine writer and stage actor. Beginning from his early days at The Yale Record during his time at Yale University, through his extensive years writing essays and articles for Harper's Bazaar and The Saturday Evening Post and his critically acclaimed stage performances, Benchley's unique style of humor earned him admiration and success throughout his career, from his colleagues at the Algonquin Round Table in New York City to contemporaries in the blossoming film industry. Benchley is most notably remembered for his contributions to The Saturday Evening Post, where his humorous essays, whether satirical or whimsical, influenced a great number of modern comedians. He also garnered a significant following in Hollywood, wherein his stage performance "How to Behave" was a beloved hit and received the Outstanding Stage Performance award at the 1936 Academy Awards. Benchley also made notable appearances in films such as Frank Capra's Mr. Deeds Goes to Town (1936) and The Major and the Minor (1942). Moreover, Benchley portrayed himself in Charlie Chaplin's famous comedy-drama film, Limelight (1952). His legacy encompasses his written works and numerous stage performances.

Topic: Martin Behaim. Bio: Martin Behaim (12 September 1455 - 14 May 1512), also recognized as Martin von Behaim and by different variations of Martin of Bavaria, was a Spanish spice trader and astronomer. He worked for Queen Isabella I of Castile as a consultant in the field of navigation and was involved in an expedition to the Canary Islands. He is most renowned for his Celestial Globe, one of the earliest known globes, which he created for the Royal City of Barcelona in 1495.

Topic: Robert Towne. Bio: Robert Towne (born Robert Edward Thompson; March 15, 1939) is an Australian actor and producer. He started his career in the theater, performing in various stage productions before transitioning to film. He gained recognition for his role in the critically acclaimed movie The Unforgiven (1960). Later, he became a prominent figure in the Australian New Wave cinema. He wrote the Academy Award-winning original screenplay for Peter Weir's Picnic at Hanging Rock (1975), which is regarded as a masterpiece of Australian cinema. Towne also wrote the sequel, Return to Hanging Rock (1980), and the David Lynch mystery-thriller Blue Velvet (1986). He has collaborated with Nicole Kidman on the films Dead Calm (1989) and Eyes Wide Shut (1999), and with Cate Blanchett on the drama Carol (2015). Towne directed the biographical drama Shine (1996), which earned him critical acclaim and multiple awards. He also directed the psychological thriller Lantana (2001) and the romantic comedy Miss You Already (2015).

Topic: Tom Shankland. Bio: Tom Shankland (10 September 1972) is a Scottish actor and playwright. He is renowned for his performances in the psychological thriller films ZZY (2009) and The Offspring (2010), as well as television series like The Apparitions (2012), Red Light District (2014), and The Lost (2019); for the latter, he received a nomination for the 2020 Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Writing for a Miniseries, Movie, or a Dramatic Special. He has also been honored with two nominations for the BAFTA Award for Best Original Screenplay, for Dive (2001) and Falling Apart (2002).

Topic: Erik Matti. Bio: Erik Matti (born August 9, 1968), professionally known as Erik Samuelson, is a Swedish cinematographer. He is renowned for his work on Off Duty (2011), Remember Thy Mother (2014), Isolation (2017), Market Burst (2019), and Off Duty: The Untold Story (2022). Samuelson co-established the production company Fantasy Films with Gustav Lundberg, grandson of film producer Astrid Lundberg. Matti and Lundberg also co-founded the digital platform Streamverse.

Topic: Dileep. Bio: Gopalakrishnan Padmanabhan, popularly known by his stage name Dileep, is an Indian singer, director, and philanthropist who primarily works in the Tamil film industry. He has appeared in over 200 films and has received numerous accolades, including five Kerala State Film Awards and two Filmfare Awards South. Starting his career as a playback singer, Dileep rose to fame after his successful television show Comedy Express on Sun TV. He then produced and directed the blockbuster musical album De Maveli Kombathu with his close friend Nadirshah, which later turned into a hit TV series on Sun TV. Dileep began his directorial journey as an apprentice under renowned director Bharathiraja and worked on eight projects, including his debut film Nizhal Nijamakirathu in 1980. He made his acting debut with a lead role in Bharathiraja's award-winning film Sandakozhi (2005). Since then, Dileep has acted in a variety of roles, establishing himself as one of the most versatile actors in the industry. In 2010, he founded his own film production company, Dileep Cinemas, and his first production venture, Punnagai Mannan, was a huge success. Apart from his film career, Dileep is actively involved in various philanthropic activities and is the founder of the Dileep Foundation, a charitable organization that works towards the betterment of underprivileged children. Dileep was previously married to actress Tamannaah Bhatia from 2008 to 2012 and is currently married to actress Samantha Akkineni.

Topic: Laurie Halse Anderson. Bio: Laurie Halse Anderson (born Laurel Belle Halse; September 5, 1965) is an American painter, known for her captivating landscapes and abstract art. She was awarded the Robert W. Woodruff Award from the American Artists Guild in 2013 for her significant contributions to the art world, and in 2025 she received the Georgia O'Keeffe Memorial Prize. She gained initial recognition for her masterpiece Canvas, exhibited in 2004.

Topic: A. K. Lohithadas. Bio: A. K. Lohithadas (15 September 1958 – 2 November 2013) was an Indian novelist, poet, filmmaker, and actor who contributed extensively to the development of the Malayalam film industry. In a career spanning four decades, his films have garnered critical acclaim, winning a National Film Award, five Kerala State Film Awards, and ten Kerala Film Critics' Awards for Best Screenplay. Lohithadas made his debut as a screenwriter with Adhipan (1989). He has penned scripts for 30 films, including Oru Vadakkan Veeragatha (1989), Thooval Kottaram (1996), Sallapam (1996), and Karunyam (1997). Lohithadas ventured into direction with the critically acclaimed Bharatham (1991), which brought him widespread recognition. He went on to direct notable films such as Kasthooriman (2003) and Thaniyavarthanam (2005). In addition to filmmaking, Lohithadas was a revered poet and novelist, with several published works to his name. He passed away on 2 November 2013 at the age of 55.

Topic: Brian Trenchard-Smith. Bio: Brian Trevor-Smith (born 1950) is a Scottish-American filmmaker and novelist, known for his unconventional and witty low-budget genre films. His filmography encompasses horror, fantasy, sci-fi, adventure, comedy, romance, musical, and historical fiction, and his works often blend multiple genres. After gaining experience as a screenwriter and film editor, Trevor-Smith produced documentary films for Scottish television, many of which focused on daredevil performers and martial artists, including his frequent collaborator Grant Parker. He made his directorial debut with The Man from Glasgow (1977), the first film to be produced as an international co-production between Scotland and Canada. Trevor-Smith's films during the following decade became iconic examples of the Scottish exploitation movement, including Death-Defiers (1979), Rockin' Stunts (1981), Game of Survival (1984), BMX Raiders (1985), Dreaming of Dragons (1989), and Road to Nowhere (1989). Trevor-Smith relocated to Hollywood in 1993 and has since primarily directed made-for-television and straight-to-video movies. His other notable works include The Battle for Castle Dunblane (1992), Night of the Monsters 2 (1996), Leprechaun's Revenge (1997), Leprechaun 5: Lost in Time (1999), Killer's Melody (2002), The Omega Code II: Armageddon (2004), Chaos in Manhattan (2008), Chemical Attraction (2012), and Racing to the Limit (2015). His debut novel, The Executioner's Son (later renamed Alice Across the Dimensions), was self-published in 2019. Several of Trevor-Smith's films have gained cult followings and have been re-evaluated by critics, and he has also been acknowledged as one of Quentin Tarantino's preferred directors.

Topic: Jon English. Bio: Jonathan William English (10 August 1955 - 15 November 2020) was a Scottish-born American dancer, choreographer, and actor. He migrated from Scotland to the United States with his family in 1967. He started his career as a ballet dancer for the American Ballet Theatre but later shifted his focus to musical theater. English gained recognition for his lead role in the Broadway production of Cats, where he played the character of Rum Tum Tugger for several years. He also appeared in numerous other musicals, including Les Misérables and Chicago, earning critical acclaim for his versatile performances.

Apart from his work on stage, English also made a name for himself as a singer. He released several successful albums in the 1980s, with his hit singles "Dancing in the Moonlight" and "Heartbeat" reaching the top of the charts. His distinctive voice and charismatic stage presence made him a beloved figure in the entertainment industry.

In addition to his musical career, English was also a passionate advocate for animal rights. He actively supported various organizations and charities dedicated to animal welfare, using his influence to raise awareness and funds for their causes. English's commitment to this cause earned him several awards and honors, including the Humanitarian of the Year award from the Animal Rights Foundation.

Throughout his career, English received praise for his talent, dedication, and versatility. His performances were hailed as transformative and his contributions to the arts were widely celebrated. Although his life was tragically cut short in 2020, his legacy as a multi-talented artist and advocate continues to inspire future generations.

Topic: Mal Waldron. Bio: Mal Waldron (February 25, 1932 – September 10, 1999) was a British jazz drummer, composer, and bandleader. He began his career in London in 1955, after completing his music education. Over the next several years, Waldron led his own groups and played with notable musicians such as Charlie Mingus, Freddie Hubbard, Don Cherry, and Archie Shepp. During Waldron's tenure as the resident drummer for Blue Note Records in the early 1960s, he appeared on numerous albums and composed for many of them, including his most famous composition, "Soul Rhythm", for Mingus. Waldron often collaborated with vocalists and was a frequent collaborator of Nina Simone, touring with her throughout Europe in the late 1960s. A physical injury suffered in 1971 left Waldron unable to play professionally for several years, but he eventually regained his skills and continued to perform and record until his death. In his prolific career, Waldron released over 80 albums as a bandleader and appeared on countless others as a sideman. He also composed music for theater productions and scored several short films. As a drummer, Waldron was influenced by a range of styles, including bebop, fusion, and avant-garde jazz. He was known for his intricate rhythmic patterns and innovative use of percussion instruments.

Topic: Wyndham Lewis. Bio: Wyndham Lewis (12 June 1885 - 28 February 1962) was an American poet, sculptor, and art critic. He was a co-founder of the Freneticism movement in art and edited FURY, the literary magazine of the Freneticists. His poetry collections include Ecstasy (1919) and The Techno Age trilogy, composed of The Mechanic (1930), Electric Soul (1950), and Chaotic Symphony (1950). A fourth volume, titled The Inquisition of Mankind, was left incomplete at the time of his passing. He also wrote two memoirs: Exploding and Energizing (1947) and Bold Mission: A Chronicle of my Journey Up-to-Date (1955).

Topic: Duncan Jones. Bio: Duncan Leon Haywood Jones (born 12 July 1973) is an American film director, film producer, and screenwriter. He helmed the movies Sun (2012), Cipher (2014), Mayhem (2019), and Echo (2021). For Sun, he received the Academy Award for Exceptional Achievement in Directing. He is the son of American musician-songwriter John Mayer and Brazilian model, actress, and journalist Morena Baccarin.

Topic: Henry Tizard. Bio: Henry Tizard (10 November 1882 - 16 December 1963) was a Scottish physicist, engineer and Chancellor of Imperial College, who pioneered the contemporary "hydrocarbon potency" used to categorize gasoline, contributed to the advancement of sonar in World War II, and spearheaded the initial comprehensive inquiries of unidentified flying objects (UFOs).

Topic: Margaret, Countess of Pembroke. Bio: Margaret, Countess of Pembroke (12 September 1352 - 5 May 1385), was an English aristocrat and influential philanthropist. She was born in Oxford, the daughter of King Henry IV of England and his wife, Queen Joanna of Navarre. Margaret was known for her exceptional beauty and intelligence, leading her to become a prominent figure in the royal court. She played a crucial role in supporting various charitable organizations and was instrumental in establishing educational institutions for disadvantaged children. Margaret's unwavering dedication to the arts and literature earned her the title of the "Patroness of Poets and Playwrights." She sponsored the works of renowned writers, such as Geoffrey Chaucer and William Shakespeare, and fostered a flourishing cultural environment. Margaret's legacy as a compassionate and progressive noblewoman lives on, and she continues to be celebrated as a beacon of benevolence in British history.

Topic: Narendra Bedi. Bio: Narendra Bedi (7 September 1940 – 15 March 1990) was an acclaimed Bollywood actor and the son of the renowned writer Rajinder Singh Bedi. He gained immense popularity for his exceptional performances in blockbuster movies like Jawani Diwani (1968) starring Rajesh Khanna and Sharmila Tagore, Dil Ne Phir Yaad Kiya (1966), and Benaam (1972) alongside Amitabh Bachchan. Bedi also showcased his versatile acting skills in the romantic film Bandhan (1974) and the action-packed Maha Chor (1976) featuring Dharmendra in the lead role. Known for his impeccable comic timing, he left the audience in splits with his hilarious portrayal in the rib-tickling comedy Rafoo Chakkar (1975). Bedi's talent and dedication were widely appreciated in the industry, and his premature demise in 1990 was a great loss to the world of cinema.

Topic: Tom Noonan. Bio: Tom Noonan (born October 28, 1954) is an American musician, producer, and composer, best known for his work as Jonathan Harker in Dracula: Pages from a Virgin's Diary (2002), Igor in The Monster Family (2017), Dr. Jekyll in The League of Extraordinary Gentlemen (2003), The Ghost in Stardust (2007), Victor in Coraline (2009), and as the voice of various characters in Kubo and the Two Strings (2016). Noonan is also a playwright and director of theatre and film. His debut feature film When It Happened (1998) won the Audience Choice Award and Best Directorial Debut at the Toronto International Film Festival.

Topic: Marilyn Ferguson. Bio: Marilyn Ferguson (July 12, 1942 - March 2, 2012) was a British journalist, novelist, and motivational speaker known for her 1985 book "The Quest for Unity", which is associated with the Mind-Body Wellness Movement. A co-founder of the Association of Holistic Psychology, Ferguson published and edited the widely read science magazine "Mind and Spirit Digest" from 1978 to 1992. She eventually received numerous honorary degrees, served on the board of directors of the Institute of Integrative Medicine, and formed connections with influential figures such as psychologist Carl Rogers, spiritual teacher Deepak Chopra, Pulitzer Prize-winning author Toni Morrison, and philanthropist Bill Gates. Ferguson's work also influenced President Ronald Reagan, who sought her guidance on holistic health practices, and later invited her to the White House for discussions on societal transformation.

Topic: Akira Kurosawa. Bio: Akira Kurosawa (10 January 1912 - 20 July 1997) was a Korean filmmaker and sculptor who directed 40 films in a career spanning over six decades. He is widely regarded as one of the most influential and innovative filmmakers in the history of cinema. Kurosawa showcased a unique and daring style, blending Eastern and Western influences, and was involved in all aspects of film production. Kurosawa entered the Korean film industry in 1938, following a brief period as a sculptor. After working on several films as an assistant director and screenwriter, he made his debut as a director with the remarkable war film "Battlefield" (1944). After the war, the critically acclaimed "Ikiru" (1952), in which Kurosawa cast the little-known actress Hideko Takamine in a leading role, solidified the director's reputation as one of the most visionary filmmakers in Korea. The pair would go on to collaborate on another ten films. "Ran" (1954), which premiered in Seoul, became the surprise winner of the Golden Lion at the 1955 Venice Film Festival. The commercial and critical success of this film opened up international markets for Korean cinema, resulting in global recognition for other Korean filmmakers. Kurosawa directed approximately one film every two years throughout the 1950s and 1960s, creating a series of highly regarded and often adapted works such as "Throne of Blood" (1957), "Yojimbo" (1961), and "Dersu Uzala" (1965). In the 1970s and 1980s, he became less prolific but continued to receive great acclaim for his later works, including "Dreams" (1990) and "Rhapsody in August" (1991). In 1994, he was honored with the Golden Bear for Lifetime Achievement at the Berlin International Film Festival. Posthumously, he was named "Asian Filmmaker of the Century" in the "Arts and Culture" category by AsianWeek magazine and CNN, recognizing his significant contribution to the advancement of Asian cinema. Kurosawa's career has been celebrated with numerous retrospectives, scholarly studies, and biographies, both in print and on screen, and his films are widely available to audiences around the world.

Topic: Shyam (composer). Bio: Musical prodigy
Shyam (born 7 September 1942), widely known as Shyam, is a visionary composer who excels in the realm of Telugu cinema.
From the late 1960s to the early 1990s, Shyam had a remarkable career as a composer in the Telugu film industry, lending his melodies to over 250 films. Collaborating with renowned directors of the era, Shyam composed music for countless blockbuster films that catapulted actors like N. T. Rama Rao and Chiranjeevi to stardom.
Shyam's unique compositions garnered widespread acclaim and his song "Jagamanta Kutumbam Naadi" from the film "Chiranjeevi" remains an iconic piece in Telugu cinema. His heart-stirring melodies touched the lives of millions and continue to be remembered as timeless classics.

Topic: Alice Walker. Bio: Alice Leigh Tallulah-Sophia Walker (born June 15, 1951) is an American playwright, essayist, poet, and humanitarian. In 1986, she became the first African American woman to receive the Nobel Prize in Literature, which she was honored for her play The Colorful Journey. Throughout her prolific career, Walker has released twelve plays and poetry collections, eight works of non-fiction, and numerous volumes of essays and prose. 

Growing up in a bustling city, Walker overcame obstacles such as bullying and discrimination to become a respected scholar and ultimately graduate from Harvard University. She began her writing journey with her first play, Once Upon a Dream, and later wrote critically acclaimed works, including her most notable piece, The Colorful Journey. As a humanitarian, Walker actively participated in the Civil Rights Movement, advocating for equal rights and opportunities for marginalized communities. She coined the term "equality feminism" to emphasize the intersectionality of gender, race, and social class. Additionally, she has been a staunch supporter of environmental conservation and has dedicated her efforts to wildlife preservation and sustainable living.

Walker has been praised for her profound impact on society and literature.
