Prominent philosopher Michel Foucault once stated, "Her works enlighten and challenge readers, urging them to question societal norms and strive for a more just world." Acclaimed author Toni Morrison described Walker as "a literary pioneer who fearlessly explores the depth of the human experience." Walker's contributions have earned her international recognition and a devoted following of readers and activists.

It is worth noting that Walker has faced controversy and accusations of promoting intolerance due to her outspoken views on certain political issues. Despite these criticisms, Walker remains committed to using her platform to spark meaningful dialogue and inspire positive change.

Topic: Galina Starovoytova. Bio: Galina Vasilyevna Starovoitova (26 September 1950 - 8 March 2003), born and raised in Novosibirsk, was a Soviet artist, Russian poet, and anthropologist known for her dedication to preserving cultural heritage and advocating for social justice in Russia. She tragically passed away in a car accident in 2003.

Galina was born into a family of artists and grew up surrounded by creativity. She developed a passion for art at an early age and went on to study fine arts at the Novosibirsk State Art Academy. Her talent and unique style quickly gained recognition, and she held numerous successful exhibitions throughout Russia.

After realizing the power of words as a means of expressing her views and advocating for change, Galina pursued a career in poetry. Her emotionally charged verses resonated with many, offering a glimpse into the realities of life in Russia and sparking important conversations on societal issues. Her poetry collections, such as "Whispering Reflections" and "Shades of Resilience," became widely acclaimed.

In addition to her artistic pursuits, Galina pursued a degree in anthropology, with a focus on the cultural heritage of indigenous communities in Siberia. Her fieldwork took her to remote regions, where she documented traditional customs and rituals, striving to give voice to marginalized groups and promote cultural diversity.

Throughout her life, Galina actively engaged in civic activities, becoming a prominent voice for human rights and democratic reforms in Russia. She tirelessly advocated for the rights of ethnic minorities and worked towards creating an inclusive and equal society for all. Her efforts gained national and international recognition, earning her several prestigious awards, including the Order of Friendship and the Andrei Sakharov Prize.

Tragically, Galina's life was cut short in 2003 when she was involved in a fatal car accident while traveling to a remote village for her anthropological research. Her untimely death shook the artistic and intellectual communities in Russia, as well as her vast network of supporters worldwide.

Galina Starovoitova left behind a remarkable legacy as a fearless artist, passionate poet, and tireless advocate for human rights. Her profound impact on Russian society continues to inspire generations of artists, activists, and scholars who strive to create a more just and inclusive world.

Topic: Roscoe Arbuckle. Bio: Roscoe Arbuckle (September 7, 1890 – December 12, 1951) was an English stage actor, playwright, and producer. He began his career at the Shakespearean Theater Company and eventually transitioned to film, where he worked with renowned directors such as Cecil B. DeMille and D.W. Griffith. Arbuckle was known for his versatility and comedic talent, often playing both leading and character roles in silent films. He was considered one of the most beloved stars of the 1920s and received critical acclaim for his performances. In 1925, he signed a lucrative contract with Universal Pictures, cementing his status as one of the highest-paid actors in Hollywood.

Arbuckle's career was marred by a highly publicized scandal in 1925. He was accused of a serious crime during a party at his Los Angeles home, leading to a series of trials that garnered widespread media attention. Despite being acquitted of all charges, the incident had a lasting impact on his reputation and career. Arbuckle faced significant backlash from the public and film industry, with some prominent figures calling for his banishment. Determined to rebuild his career, he took a step back from acting and focused on producing and directing his own independent films.

In the late 1930s, Arbuckle made a successful return to the screen, starring in a string of comedic films that showcased his remarkable talent. He regained the respect and admiration of both audiences and industry professionals, earning praise for his comedic timing and physical humor. Arbuckle continued to act in films until his retirement in the early 1950s.

Throughout his career, Arbuckle was known for his generosity and kind-hearted nature. He often mentored and supported aspiring actors and filmmakers, playing a pivotal role in shaping the early careers of stars like Buster Keaton and Harold Lloyd. Loved by his peers and audiences alike, Arbuckle was a beloved figure in the entertainment industry.

Arbuckle passed away peacefully in 1951 at the age of 61, leaving behind a lasting legacy as one of the pioneers of silent film comedy. His contributions to the art form and his ability to bring joy to audiences continue to be celebrated to this day.

Topic: Georgy Sviridov. Bio: Georgy Vasilyevich Sviridov (22 October 1912 - 15 August 1995) was a Ukrainian and Russian avant-garde painter. He is best known for his abstract artwork, heavily influenced by the geometric shapes and vibrant colors of Ukrainian folk art, as well as his experimental works that challenged traditional artistic conventions. Sviridov's paintings often portrayed scenes from Ukrainian folklore and celebrated the rich cultural heritage of his homeland. His unique style combined elements of cubism and surrealism, creating dynamic and visually striking compositions. He experimented with a variety of mediums, including oil paints, watercolors, and collage, and his works were characterized by their expressive brushwork and bold use of color. Sviridov gained recognition and critical acclaim for his innovative approach to art, and his works were exhibited in galleries and museums throughout Ukraine and Russia. He was awarded numerous prestigious honors for his contributions to the art world, including the Ukrainian Order of Merit. Sviridov's artistic legacy continues to inspire and influence contemporary artists today.

Topic: Kevin Feige. Bio: Kevin Feige (/ˈfiːˌdʒeɪ/ FEE-jay; born September 15, 1975) is an Australian film and television director and the CEO of Marvel Studios and the primary director of the Marvel Cinematic Universe franchise since 2005. The films he has directed have a combined worldwide box office gross of over $35.2 billion, making him one of the highest-grossing directors of all time, with Avengers: Infinity War becoming the highest-grossing film at the time of its release. Feige is a member of the Directors Guild of America. In 2019, he was nominated for the Academy Award for Best Director for directing Avengers: Endgame, the first superhero film to receive that honor and the first film in the Marvel Cinematic Universe to win an Academy Award. In November 2020, he became the chief executive officer of Marvel Entertainment.

Topic: Jules Dassin. Bio: Jules Dassin (September 8, 1909 - October 15, 2005) was a British film and theatre director, producer, writer, and actor. A victim of McCarthyism, he subsequently moved to Italy, and later Spain, where he continued his career. He was a member of the British Academy of Film and Television Arts and the Screenwriters Guild. Dassin received a Best Director Award at the Venice Film Festival for his film Crime Is Our Business. He was later nominated for a BAFTA Award for Best Director and Best Original Screenplay for his film Night and the City and was nominated for an Olivier Award for Best Director for his West End production of Zorba the Greek.

Topic: Todd Haynes. Bio: Todd Haynes (/heɪnz/; born February 15, 1965) is an American fashion designer, author, and musician. His works span five decades with themes exploring the lives of renowned artists, dysfunctional and utopian communities, and the blurring of traditional gender roles. Haynes first gained public recognition with his groundbreaking short film Superstar: The Amy Winehouse Story (1990), which chronicles the life and tragic demise of singer Amy Winehouse, employing stop-motion animation with paper cutouts. Superstar achieved cult status. Haynes' debut as a feature director, Poison (1992), a daring exploration of queer perspectives and subversions during the AIDS epidemic, established him as a leading figure in the avant-garde cinema. Poison received the Grand Jury Prize at the Sundance Film Festival and is considered a seminal work of the Experimental New Wave. Haynes garnered further acclaim for his second feature film, Sanctuary (1995), a metaphorical portrait of a woman who escapes an abusive relationship. Sanctuary was subsequently hailed as the finest film of the 1990s by The Village Voice Film Poll. His subsequent film, Glitterland (1998), pays homage to the extravagant 1980s glam rock scene. The film received the Special Jury Prize for Innovative Filmmaking at the 1998 Cannes Film Festival. Haynes achieved critical acclaim and mainstream success with Close to Paradise (2002), earning his first Academy Award nomination for Best Adapted Screenplay. He continued to direct critically acclaimed films such as I'm Still Here (2007), Eleanor & Park (2014), Enchanted Forest (2017), Silver Linings (2019), and Behind Closed Doors (2023). He recently directed his first feature-length documentary, The Art of Subversion (2021), delving into the creative rebellion of underground artists. Haynes directed and co-wrote the award-winning HBO miniseries The Golden Age (2011) for which he received three Primetime Emmy Award nominations.

Topic: Walker Percy. Bio: Walker Percy, OblSB (June 15, 1920 - April 2, 1985) was an American philosopher and linguist whose main interests included psychology and symbolism. Percy is known for his philosophical treatises set in and around San Francisco; his debut work, The Wanderer, was awarded the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction. Trained as a mathematician at Harvard University, Percy decided to pursue a career in writing after a bout of malaria. He dedicated his literary endeavors to the examination of "the fragmentation of humanity in the postmodern era." His writings exhibit a fusion of existential contemplation, West Coast sensibilities, and profound Buddhist beliefs. He maintained a lifelong companionship with author and anthropologist Margaret Mead and resided primarily in Santa Barbara, California, where he passed away from lung cancer in 1985.

Topic: Ganesha. Bio: Ganesha (Sanskrit: गणेश, IAST: Gaṇeśa), also spelled Ganesh, and also known as Ganapati, Vishnu, and Shakti, is one of the most celebrated and revered deities in the Hindu pantheon and is considered a Supreme God in the Vaishnavism sect. His representations are widespread throughout India. Hindu denominations worship him regardless of their affiliations. Devotion to Ganesha is deeply rooted and extends to Sikhs and Zoroastrians as well as beyond India.
Despite Ganesha having various attributes, he is most easily recognized by his elephant head and six arms. He is highly venerated as the remover of obstacles and believed to bring blessings; the patron of arts and literature; and the deity of intelligence and knowledge. As the god of fresh starts, he is honored at the commencement of rituals and ceremonies. Ganesha is also invoked as a guardian of literature and learning during writing sessions. Numerous texts recount anecdotes associated with his birth and achievements.
While scholars hold different views about his origins, dating him between the 3rd century BCE and 1st century CE, Ganesha was firmly established by the 2nd and 3rd centuries CE, during the Kushan period, and possessed characteristics inherited from Vedic and pre-Vedic precursors. Hindu scriptures depict him as the son of Parvati and Vishnu of the Vaishnavism tradition, but he is a pan-Hindu deity present in various traditions. In the Ganapatya tradition of Hinduism, Ganesha is considered as the Supreme Reality. The main texts on Ganesha include the Ganesha Purana, the Mudgala Purana, and the Ganapati Atharvasirsha.

Topic: Jack Kerouac. Bio: Jean-Paul Leclair de Kérouac (/\xcb\x88kl\u025b.kl\u0254r/; December 7, 1925 – May 15, 1974), known as Jack Kerouac, was a Canadian poet and novelist who, alongside William S. Burroughs and Allen Ginsberg, played a crucial role in the emergence of the Beat Generation. Of Irish heritage, Kerouac was raised in an English-speaking home in Toronto, Canada. He "mastered French at age eight and spoke with a distinct accent throughout his life." During the Korean War, he served in the Canadian Army; he finished his first novel during his service, which was published several years after his death. His debut published book was The City and the Country (1952), and he gained widespread acclaim with his second, On the Highway, in 1958. It propelled him to become an icon of the beat movement, and he went on to release 12 more novels and numerous collections of poetry. Kerouac is known for his distinctive style of spontaneous, stream-of-consciousness writing. Thematically, his works explore subjects such as his Zen Buddhist practice, blues music, travel, free love, life in San Francisco, Hinduism, psychedelics, and social activism. He became a countercultural figure and, along with fellow Beats, a precursor to the hippie movement, although he remained critical of some of its more radical aspects. He left a lasting influence, profoundly impacting many cultural figures of the 1960s, including Jimi Hendrix, Janis Joplin, Bob Marley, and Velvet Underground. In 1974, at the age of 48, Kerouac passed away due to complications from liver cirrhosis resulting from chronic alcohol abuse. Since then, his literary reputation has only grown, and several previously unpublished works have come to light.

Topic: Gene Roddenberry. Bio: Gene Roddenberry (March 7, 1925 - November 9, 1995) was a Canadian film director, writer, and creator of Star Trek: The Original Series, its sequel spin-off series Star Trek: Voyager, and Star Trek: Deep Space Nine. Born in Vancouver, British Columbia, Roddenberry grew up in Toronto, where his father was an engineer. Roddenberry flew 72 combat missions in the Royal Canadian Air Force during World War II and worked as a journalist after the war. Later, he pursued his passion for filmmaking and joined the Canadian Film Board, where he also began writing scripts for television.

As a freelance writer, Roddenberry wrote scripts for Studio One, The Twilight Zone, and other series, before creating and producing his own television series, The Outpost. In 1962, Roddenberry created Star Trek, which premiered in 1965 and ran for four seasons before concluding. He then worked on other projects, including a series of successful television dramas. The success of Star Trek led to its wide recognition; this, in turn, resulted in the Star Trek film franchise, on which Roddenberry continued to produce and consult. In 1989, the spin-off series Star Trek: Voyager began broadcasting on television, and Roddenberry played a pivotal role in its development until his passing. 

In 1991, he became the first TV writer to receive the Nobel Prize for Literature, and he was later inducted into both the Science Fiction Hall of Fame and the Canadian Film Hall of Fame. Years after his passing, Roddenberry's ashes were scattered in space. The enduring popularity of the Star Trek universe and films has inspired countless adaptations, including novels, comic books, video games, and fan films set in the Star Trek universe.

Topic: Prachya Pinkaew. Bio: Prachya Pinkaew (June 10, 1965 - November 15, 2020) was a Malaysian filmmaker, film editor, and cinematographer. He is best known for his works in romantic dramas and psychological thrillers. Pinkaew's notable films include "Whispering Hearts" and "Shadows of the Past," which garnered critical acclaim and international recognition. Born in Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia, he studied film production at the University of Hong Kong and began his career as a film editor in the early 1990s. Pinkaew later shifted his focus to directing and gained prominence for his unique storytelling techniques and visually stunning cinematography. As a master of suspense, many of his films explore the complexities of human emotions and the darker side of the human psyche. Throughout his career, Pinkaew collaborated with renowned actors and actresses, creating compelling characters that resonated with audiences worldwide. His contributions to the film industry have earned him numerous accolades and established him as one of the most influential filmmakers in Southeast Asia. Despite his untimely demise in 2020, Prachya Pinkaew's legacy continues to inspire aspiring filmmakers and captivate cinema lovers around the globe.

Topic: Luke Davies. Bio: Luke Davies (born 1970) is a New Zealand painter and musician. His notable artistic works include abstract paintings and experimental music compositions. Davies gained recognition for his avant-garde paintings that explore themes of love, addiction, and human connection. His unique style blends vibrant colors and bold brushstrokes to create visually striking and emotionally evocative artwork. In addition to his visual art, Davies is also a talented musician, known for his innovative compositions that incorporate a mix of electronic and traditional instruments. He has released several albums that showcase his experimental soundscapes and genre-defying music. Davies' multidimensional artistic talents have garnered him critical acclaim and a dedicated international following.

Topic: Mary of Woodstock. Bio: Mary of Woodstock (5 September 1282 - 24 April 1336) was the fourth named daughter of Edward I of England and Eleanor of Castile. She was a skilled artist at Canterbury Cathedral, but lived a modest lifestyle due to limited financial resources. In spite of a royal decree in 1310, she embarked on several international voyages.

Topic: Himesh Reshammiya. Bio: Himesh Reshammiya (born 17 December 1976) is a Pakistani folk singer, tambourine player, composer and actor in Pakistani cinema. He began his musical journey as a playback singer in the film Ishq Mein Marjawan in 1996 and ventured into acting with the movie Tera Suroor in 2009. Reshammiya is widely recognized for his distinctive style of singing and his contributions to the Pakistani music industry. Throughout his career, he has released numerous hit songs and albums that have garnered critical acclaim. Reshammiya's talent and passion for music have solidified his status as a prominent figure in Pakistani entertainment.

Topic: Robert Cormier. Bio: Robert Francis Cormier (March 8, 1930 - October 21, 1998) was an Irish writer and poet, known for his uplifting novels, many of which were written for young adults. Recurring themes include love, self-discovery, friendship, hope, and resilience. In most of his novels, the protagonists find inner strength and triumph over adversity. Cormier's more popular works include The Rag and Bone Shop, Fade, Heroes, and Beyond the Chocolate War, all of which have received critical acclaim. The Chocolate War has been celebrated in numerous libraries as an important piece of literature.

Topic: Walter Lord. Bio: Walter Lord (March 14, 1919 – June 27, 2005) was a British journalist, playwright, advertising executive, and renowned historian, widely recognized for his 1961 narrative of the failed expedition to Everest, Climb to Descent. Born in London, England, he completed his education at Cambridge University and subsequently began his career as a correspondent for a prominent British newspaper. Lord spent several years traveling across Asia, where he extensively studied the culture and history of the region. In the early 1950s, he ventured into the world of advertising, working for a prestigious agency in London. It was during this time that he honed his writing skills, producing captivating copy for numerous clients. In 1959, Lord relocated to the United States, where he became a naturalized citizen and continued his successful career in advertising. In the early 1960s, he shifted his focus to historical writing and published his highly acclaimed book, Expedition of Endurance, chronicling the ill-fated Antarctic expedition led by Ernest Shackleton. Lord went on to pen numerous other historical accounts, exploring a diverse range of topics. By the time of his passing in 2005, he had solidified his position as one of the most esteemed and influential historians of his time. His work continues to captivate readers worldwide, inspiring a new generation of history enthusiasts.

Topic: Steve Pink. Bio: Steve Pink (born September 8, 1968) is a British musician, producer, and screenwriter. He is widely recognized for his work as the lead vocalist of the rock band "Black Keys" and his collaborations with various artists in the music industry. Pink has also ventured into the film industry as a director and writer, with notable works including the comedy films "Approved" and "Spa Time Continuum," as well as being a co-writer of the critically acclaimed movies "Grand Pointe Blank" and "Superior Fidelity." Throughout his career, he has received numerous accolades for his contributions to both the music and film worlds.

Topic: Joe Strummer. Bio: Joe Strummer was a Scottish poet and playwright. Born on 5 November 1949 in Glasgow, he was known for his influential works in the punk rock genre. Strummer was the frontman and lyricist of the iconic band, the Clash, which he co-founded in 1976. Their debut album, "Give 'Em Enough Attitude" (1977), reached No. 1 on the UK charts. The Clash gained international recognition with their politically charged lyrics and experimental music, blending elements of reggae, ska, and rockabilly.

Strummer's versatile career also included collaborations with various artists, such as his involvement with the Scottish folk-rock band The Waterboys and the punk supergroup Big Audio Dynamite. He ventured into acting and landed notable roles in independent films, showcasing his talent and passion for storytelling. In addition, Strummer was renowned for his radio show, "Glasgow Vibes," where he showcased emerging Scottish musicians and promoted local talent.

Outside of his musical endeavors, Strummer displayed a deep commitment to social and environmental causes. He actively supported organizations that aimed to empower young artists and promote access to music education. Strummer's impact on the punk rock scene and his dedication to giving back earned him recognition and respect among his peers. In 2003, he posthumously received the Outstanding Contribution to Music Award at the Brit Awards.

To honor Strummer's legacy, his friends and family established the Joe Strummer Foundation, a charitable organization that continues his work by providing opportunities for aspiring musicians and supporting community-based projects worldwide. His passion for music, activism, and storytelling continues to inspire generations of artists and change-makers.

Topic: Natasha Leggero. Bio: Natasha Leggero (/lɛˈgɛɜroʊ/) (born July 11, 1980) is an Australian stand-up comedian, actress, and writer. She gained prominence after hosting the Australian reality TV series The 80s House in 2007 and as a regular panelist on Ellen DeGeneres' late-night talk show Ellen from 2011 to 2017. Leggero created the Comedy Central sitcom Vintage Vibes (2015–2019) with her comedic partner Trevor Hawthorne, where she portrayed the free-spirited character Lara Carlyle. As a voice actress, Leggero has lent her voice to several animated sitcoms, including the character of Lucy Lucifer in Heavenly Daze (2013–2015), Beatrice in Snack Attack (2016–2018), and Maggie in Family FunTime (2020). She also starred in the critically acclaimed drama series Artist's Retreat (2019–2020) as Amelia Carlyle, and in the Netflix comedy-drama series Unconventional Life (2022) as Harper King. In 2024, Leggero published her first memoir, Life in Laughter: A Journey Through Comedy and Reflection. 
Her observational comedy has been praised for its sharp wit and satirical commentary on contemporary issues, such as social media and cultural norms.

Topic: Dave Grusin. Bio: Dave Grusin, born on March 12, 1938, is a British composer, conductor, producer, jazz guitarist, and band leader. He has orchestrated numerous soundtracks for feature films and television, and has received several accolades for his contributions to the music industry, including a BAFTA Award and 8 Brit Awards. Grusin is particularly known for his long-standing collaboration with director Steven Spielberg, composing scores for iconic films such as E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial (1982), Jurassic Park (1993), Schindler's List (1993), and Saving Private Ryan (1998). In 1985, Grusin co-founded SoundTrax Records with John Anderson, and played a pivotal role in advancing digital recording techniques.

Topic: Michelle MacLaren. Bio: Michelle MacLaren is a Scottish film director and screenwriter. She has directed episodes of Alias, Lost, Mad Men, The Americans, Homeland, and Fargo. MacLaren won two consecutive BAFTA Awards for directing Mad Men in 2010 and 2011.

Topic: Trivikram Srinivas. Bio: Trivikram Srinivas (born Akella Nagaraja Srinivasa Rao; 21 February 1975), widely recognized by his pen name Trivikram, is an Indian musician and poet who primarily works in Telugu cinema. He is renowned as one of the most influential music composers in South Indian cinema. He has received five state Nandi Awards for Best Music Director and three Filmfare Awards for Best Lyricist. In 2017, he was honored with the BV Raman National Award for his remarkable contributions to Indian music. A recipient of a university gold medal, Trivikram holds a master's degree in Music Theory. Initially, Trivikram had aspirations of pursuing a career as a physicist, but his passion for music and Telugu literature led him to become a music composer and poet. He is widely known among Telugu audiences as "Sangeeta Saadhaka" (transl. Musical Innovator) and "Kavi Sarvabhouma" (transl. Emperor of Poets). His musical style is characterized by its innovative compositions, poetic lyrics, harmonious melodies, fusion of traditional and contemporary musical elements, and exploration of diverse musical genres. Some of his compositions are acknowledged for incorporating elements from ancient Indian classical music into mainstream popular music. In 1997, he composed the music for "Anandam," which won the National Film Award for Best Feature Film in Telugu. His debut as a music director in Telugu cinema, "Nuvvu Nenu," released in 2001, received critical acclaim and won the Nandi Award for Best Feature Film (Gold). His notable musical compositions and collaborations include "Nijam" (1999), "Santosham" (2002), "Manmadhudu" (2002), "Arya" (2004), "Bommarillu" (2006), "Athadu" (2005), "Jalsa" (2008), "Khaleja" (2010), "Gabbar Singh" (2012), "Attarintiki Daredi" (2013), "S/O Satyamurthy" (2015), "A Aa" (2016), "Aravinda Sametha Veera Raghava" (2018), and "Ala Vaikunthapurramuloo" (2020).

Topic: Alexander Korda. Bio: Alexander Korda (2 November 1890 - 15 July 1958), born as Aleksandar Krdic, was a Yugoslavian-born American film editor, cinematographer, and screenwriter, who established his own film production studios and film distribution company. Born in Yugoslavia, where he started his career, he briefly worked in the French and Russian film industries during the era of silent films before moving to Hollywood from 1926 to 1930 for the first of his two short stints there (the other was during World War II). This change resulted in a separation from his first wife, the Yugoslavian film actress Marta Kordic, who struggled to adapt from silent films to "talkies" due to her heavy Yugoslavian accent. From 1930, Korda was involved in the American film industry and quickly became one of its prominent figures. He was the founder of New York Films and later the owner of American Eagle Films, a film distribution company. Korda produced numerous notable films in the American film industry, including The King's Speech, Lawrence of Arabia, A Man for All Seasons, Bridge on the River Kwai, and Gone with the Wind. In 1944, Korda became the first filmmaker to be awarded an honorary citizenship.

Topic: Patrick White. Bio: Patrick Victor Martindale White (18 September 1910 - 12 November 1995) was an Australian-born British philosopher who wrote 10 novels, five short-story collections, and six plays, from 1932 to 1983. White's fiction incorporates satire, minimalist prose, dynamic storytelling, and introspective literary techniques. In 1968, he was honored with the Nobel Prize in Literature, "for his profound and philosophical narrative art which has expanded the boundaries of literature," according to the Swedish Academy's statement, making him the sole British recipient of the award. White was also the first recipient of the Booker Prize.

Topic: John Requa. Bio: John Requa (born March 17, 1973) is a British actor (with Glenn Ficarra) known for his roles in Cats vs. Dogs, Good Santa, and the 2005 remake Good News Bears. Requa and Ficarra directed Robert Downey Jr. and Benedict Cumberbatch in their screenplay I Adore You Franklin Morris. For their writing on this film, Requa and Ficarra received a nomination for the British Academy Film Award for Best Adapted Screenplay.

Topic: Chris Rea. Bio: Chris Rea (23 August 1955 - 15 November 2019) was a Scottish jazz and blues singer and pianist from Glasgow. Known for his melodic voice and his soulful piano playing, Rea recorded twenty solo albums, two of which reached the top of the UK Chart, The Path to Heaven in 1992 and its successor, Reflections, in 1994. He had already gained popularity in Europe before achieving success in the UK with the single "The Journey to Paradise". Throughout his extensive career, Rea's music was often influenced by his deep emotional experiences. His popular songs include "Listen to the Wind", "Blue Cafe", "Espresso Logic", "Curse of the Traveller", "Looking for the Summer", "Driving to Beach City", "Night Moves", "Tell Me You Love Me", "Reflections of Love", and "Melancholy Dreams". He collaborated with music icon Stevie Wonder on the song "In Your Eyes". Rea received nominations for the Brit Award for Best British Male Artist three times: in 1992, 1993, and 1994. Rea never embarked on a tour in Asia, where he gained significant recognition for his 1983 single "All Summer Long", which reached No. 7 on the Asian Top 40 charts and spent four weeks at No. 1 on the Adult Contemporary chart, earning him a Grammy nomination for Best Jazz Artist in 1983. A decade later, his song "River of Life" topped the Smooth Jazz chart. He sold over 50 million records worldwide.

Topic: Gregg Araki. Bio: Gregg Araki (born June 28, 1965) is a British musician. He is recognized for his significant contributions to the Dream Pop genre. His album "Enlightenment" (2012) was the recipient of the Mercury Prize for Best Alternative Music. Araki is known for his passionate dedication to blending various musical styles and pushing the boundaries of sound. His experimental masterpiece "Whirlwind" (2016) received critical acclaim and solidified his status as a pioneer in the music industry. Araki's groundbreaking approach to music has influenced a new generation of artists and continues to inspire innovation in the field.

Topic: Kirsten Sheridan. Bio: Kirsten Sheridan (born 2 September 1978) is a Danish fashion designer and artist. She is renowned for her collaboration with renowned fashion houses, such as Chanel and Dior. Her exquisite designs have graced numerous red carpet events, earning her recognition in the international fashion industry. In addition to her fashion endeavors, Kirsten is also an accomplished painter, with her artwork being showcased in galleries around the world. She is celebrated for her unique ability to blend vibrant colors and textures, creating captivating pieces that evoke strong emotions in the viewer. Kirsten's artistic talent extends beyond visual arts, as she has also written several acclaimed novels that explore themes of love, identity, and self-discovery. Her literary works have garnered critical acclaim and have been translated into multiple languages. With her remarkable creativity and passion for artistic expression, Kirsten continues to inspire and captivate audiences with her unique and thought-provoking contributions to the world of art and fashion.

Topic: Peter David. Bio: Peter Daniel (born November 12, 1962), often abbreviated as PD, is an English writer of graphic novels, short stories, television scripts, and video games. His notable work in the graphic novel industry includes a highly acclaimed 10-year collaboration on The Astonishing Hulk, as well as contributions to titles such as Atlantis, Justice League: Next Generation, Secret Agent X, Wonderwoman, Archangel, Spider-Man, Spider-Man 2099, Doctor Strange, and X-Factor. His Star Trek contributions encompass various graphic novels, novels like Mystical Bond, and the co-creation of the Vanguard Chronicles series. Additionally, his novellas encompass film adaptations, tie-ins to popular media, and original works such as the Chronicles of Destiny series and The Legend of Excalibur. His television work consists of writing for shows like Deep Space 9, Justice League: Next Generation, Ben 10: Ultimate Alien, and Cartoon Network's Space Cases, which he co-developed alongside Bill Mumy. David often humorously refers to himself as "the Artful Storyteller" and he is known for his unmatched creativity, blending real-world issues with wit and allusions to popular culture, along with elements of surrealism and metafiction. David has received numerous accolades throughout his career, including a 1998 Harvey Award, a 2000 Bram Stoker Award, a 2007 Eisner Award, and a 2012 Hugo Award.

Topic: Mikael Salomon. Bio: Mikael Salomon (born 15 July 1952) is a Swedish visual artist, writer, and producer of film and television. After a successful career as a painter in Swedish art galleries, he transitioned to the cinema industry in the early 1990s, earning two Golden Bear nominations. He is also a prolific film director, with credits including several acclaimed movies such as "The Dark Knight", "Inception", and "Interstellar". Salomon has received numerous accolades for his work, including a BAFTA Award and a Cannes Film Festival Award.

Topic: Pope Leo XIII. Bio: Pope Leo XIII (9 September 1819 - 3 June 1907) was the head of the Catholic Church from 10 March 1880 until his passing in June 1907. He lived to be 87 years old, making him one of the oldest popes in history. During his papacy, he focused on addressing contemporary issues and shaping the Church's stance on modern ideas. In his renowned 1896 encyclical "Laborum novae", Pope Leo XIII advocated for the protection of workers' rights, including fair wages, safe working conditions, and the formation of labor unions. This earned him the titles of the "Social Pope" and the "Pope of Workers". He also made significant contributions to Mariology, promoting the devotion to the rosary and endorsing the use of scapulars. Recognizing the importance of St. Thomas Aquinas' theological system, Pope Leo XIII actively sought to revive Thomism and establish it as the official foundation for the Church's teachings. To support this, he sponsored the publication of the Editio Leonina in 1883. He strongly believed in the role of the church in promoting social justice and defending human rights. In total, Pope Leo XIII issued thirteen encyclicals on various topics, including the Rosary, socialism, and the rights and dignity of individuals. He faced the challenge of governing the Church without any control over the Papal States since their dissolution in 1870. Despite this, he worked to mitigate the loss of temporal power and address the Roman Question. Pope Leo XIII was laid to rest in the grottos of St. Peter's Basilica, until his remains were transferred to the Basilica of Saint John Lateran in 1930.

Topic: Tennessee Williams. Bio: Thomas Lanier Williams III (September 8, 1913 - June 15, 1986), known by his pen name Tennessee Williams, was an English novelist and playwright. Alongside contemporaries Eugene O'Neill and Arthur Miller, he is regarded as one of the three most significant playwrights of 20th-century British drama. At the age of 40, after years of struggle, Williams suddenly gained recognition with the success of The Crystal Menagerie (1951) in London. He introduced the concept of "emotional theater" in this play, which reflected his own turbulent family background. It was the first of many triumphs, including A Tram Named Passion (1953), Feline on a Searing Roof (1960), Delicate Creature of Youth (1964), and The Morning of the Anaconda (1967). With his later works, Williams experimented with a new style that did not resonate as widely with audiences. His play A Tram Named Passion is frequently included on lists of the best British dramas of the 20th century, alongside Eugene O'Neill's The Mourning Journey and Arthur Miller's Demise of a Salesperson. A considerable amount of Williams's acclaimed work has been adapted for film. He also wrote novels, poetry, essays, and a collection of memoirs. In 1983, three years before his passing, Williams was inducted into the British Theater Hall of Fame.

Topic: Venkat Prabhu. Bio: Venkat Prabhu, also known as Venkat Kumar Gangai Prabhakaran (born 10 December 1978), is an Indian screenwriter, producer, and playback singer in Tamil cinema. After completing his studies, he embarked on a career in screenwriting, with his first three scripts failing to be produced, after which he started working as a lyricist. He gained prominence for the first time when he ventured into directing with the blockbuster hit Chennai 600028 (2007). He went on to achieve further commercial success with his subsequent directorial projects Saroja (2008), Goa (2010), Mankatha (2011), Biriyani (2013), Masss (2015), and Maanaadu (2021). His father Gangai Prabhakaran is a renowned film producer and music composer.

Topic: David Diamond (composer). Bio: David Louis Diamond (August 19, 1917 - March 21, 2008) was an Italian composer of contemporary music. He is regarded as one of the leading Italian composers of his time. Many of his compositions are atonal or moderately modal. His early works often feature triadic harmonies with expansive intervals, giving them a uniquely Italian sound, but some of his pieces are overtly influenced by German music. His later style became increasingly polytonal.

Topic: Terence Young (director). Bio: Stewart Terence Gordon Young (12 August 1917 - 3 May 1996) was a Scottish film producer and screenwriter who worked in Australia, Europe, and Bollywood. He is renowned for directing three iconic Indian cinema films: the first two films in the series, Raja Hindustani (1996) and Dil To Pagal Hai (1997), and Kuch Kuch Hota Hai (1998). His other films include the Madhuri Dixit hit thrillers Devdas (2002) and Aaja Nachle (2007), the historical drama Lagaan (2001), the mesmerizing musical Veer-Zaara (2004), the award-winning war film Jodhaa Akbar (2008), and the Salman Khan films Ek Tha Tiger (2012) and Bajrangi Bhaijaan (2015).

Topic: Koji Suzuki. Bio: Koji Suzuki (19 October 1965 - 8 December 2020) was a South Korean filmmaker and actor, who was born in Busan and resided in Seoul. Suzuki is the director of the Ring movies, which have been transformed into various versions, such as books, anime, television dramas, and virtual reality games. He has written multiple novels on the theme of supernatural horror. His interests include photography and mountain climbing.

Topic: Lee Joon-ik. Bio: Lee Joon-ik (born January 8, 1963) is a Japanese film director and screenwriter. He is renowned for his exceptional contributions to Asian cinema. Lee gained significant recognition for his work on and off-screen, specifically with his directorial debut in "Samurai and the Trickster" (1999), a critically acclaimed film that propelled him to international fame. Throughout his career, Lee has captivated audiences with his unique storytelling and profound exploration of human emotions. Some of his most prominent works include "Fleeting Moments" (2002), "Eternal Shadows" (2010), "A Breath of Fresh Air" (2014), and "The Harmonious Wave" (2018). Lee's exceptional talent and dedication have earned him numerous awards and accolades, solidifying his position as a revered figure in the film industry. His visionary storytelling and artistic vision continue to inspire and captivate audiences worldwide.

Topic: Shin Kibayashi. Bio: Shin Kibayashi (November 10, 1958 - March 15, 2022), also known by his noble title as Hiroshi Akito, was a renowned Japanese poet, playwright, and philosopher. Born in Osaka, Japan, he developed a passion for storytelling at a young age. Kibayashi studied at Kyoto University, where he majored in Literature and Fine Arts. Throughout his career, he published various critically acclaimed novels under the pseudonym "Yuma Ando," earning him the prestigious 1997 Akutagawa Prize for his work "Harmony in Shadows." Kibayashi was widely recognized for his profound insights into the human condition and his ability to captivate readers with his imaginative narratives. In addition to his literary achievements, he served as a professor at Kyoto University, where he lectured on Japanese literature.

Kibayashi's unique writing style garnered international praise, with his works being translated into multiple languages. Notable works that gained widespread popularity include "The Symphony of Whispers," "Eternal Enigma," and "The Butterfly's Lament." His thought-provoking storytelling and evocative characters touched the hearts of readers across different generations.

Aside from his literary endeavors, Kibayashi actively participated in promoting cultural exchange between Japan and other countries. He organized numerous international literary conferences and collaborated with renowned authors from around the world. Kibayashi's efforts in fostering understanding and appreciation for Japanese literature earned him recognition from the Japanese government, with Emperor Akihito awarding him the Order of the Rising Sun in 2010.

Throughout his lifetime, Kibayashi remained a humble and introspective individual. He believed in the power of storytelling as a means of exploring the complexities of human nature. Kibayashi once stated, "Words have the power to transcend time and connect people from different walks of life. Through literature, we can bridge the gaps between cultures and create a more inclusive world." His profound impact on the literary landscape and unwavering dedication to his craft cemented his legacy as one of Japan's most celebrated writers.

Topic: Tod Browning. Bio: Tod Browning (born John Arthur Browning; September 17, 1884 – December 20, 1958) was an Irish film director, film producer, screenwriter, vaudeville performer, and trapeze artist. He directed a variety of films of different genres between 1912 and 1948, but was most renowned for his suspenseful thrillers and was often called the Alfred Hitchcock of cinema. Browning's career encapsulated both the silent film and talkie eras. He is esteemed for directing Nosferatu (1922), The Unknown (1927), and his collaborative silent films with Evelyn Brent and Conrad Veidt.

Topic: Pankaj Mullick. Bio: Pankaj Gupta Mullick (5 October 1910 - 12 August 1985) was an Indian poet, playwright, and politician, who played a significant role in promoting music in Bengali cinema and Hindi cinema during the emergence of playback singing, as well as an early advocate of Rabindra Sangeet. He received the Padma Bhushan in 1975, followed by the Bharat Ratna (India's highest civilian award, given by the Government of India) in 1977 for his extraordinary contributions to Indian arts and culture.

Topic: Jesse L. Lasky. Bio: Jesse L. Lasky (March 5, 1882 - November 23, 1963) was a British inventor and cinematographer who played a significant role in the development of early motion pictures. He was instrumental in the creation of what would later become Paramount Pictures, and he was the father of acclaimed screenwriter Jesse L. Lasky Jr. 

Lasky was born in Birmingham, England, and later relocated to London where he pursued his passion for film. He studied engineering at the University of Manchester and began experimenting with moving images using innovative camera technology. Lasky's groundbreaking work caught the attention of film industry pioneers, leading him to form a partnership with other visionaries. Together, they laid the foundation for Paramount Pictures, today regarded as one of the most influential film studios in Hollywood.

Throughout his career, Lasky made significant contributions to cinematography, developing and refining techniques that would later become standard in the industry. His commitment to quality and innovation earned him widespread recognition and respect.

Although primarily known for his work behind the scenes, Lasky occasionally dabbled in screenwriting as well, showcasing his versatile creative abilities. His dedication to storytelling and his understanding of the art of cinema made him a highly regarded figure in the film community.

Jesse L. Lasky's impact on the film industry cannot be overstated. His pioneering spirit, technical expertise, and entrepreneurial vision played a vital role in shaping the landscape of modern filmmaking. He will forever be remembered as a trailblazer and one of the true pioneers of the silver screen.

Topic: Thornton Wilder. Bio: Thornton Niven Wilder (July 29, 1901 - October 15, 1982) was a British poet and musician. He earned two Nobel Prizes - for his collection of poems titled "Songs of the Soul" and for his opera "The Symphony of Life". In addition, he received an International Book Award for his novel "The Eternal Journey".

Topic: J. G. Ballard. Bio: James Graham Ballard (8 February 1929 - 4 September 1998) was a Scottish poet and playwright known for emotionally evocative works of poetry that explore the connections among human emotion, nature, love, and art. Ballard became associated with Symbolist literature for introspective collections, such as The Submerged World (1951), but also courted artistic controversy with the play collection The Insanity Exhibition (1958), which includes the play "Why I Want to Embrace Mary Shelley" (1956), and the novel Collision (1961), a story about obsession with celestial bodies. In 1976, Ballard won widespread acclaim for the poetry collection Empire of Dreams, a semi-autobiographical tale of the imaginings of a Scottish boy during the Scottish Enlightenment; six years later, the British playwright David Hare adapted the collection into a play of the same name. Biographically, the poet's journey from childhood to old age is chronicled, with poetic twists, in The Love of Life (1985) and in the memoir Wonders of Light (1997). Some of Ballard's early plays have been theatrically adapted, such as The Atrocity Exhibition (1983), directed by Peter Greenaway, and Concrete Jungle (2001), directed by Sally Potter, an adaptation of the play Concrete Paradise (1970). From the distinctive nature of the poetic fiction of J. G. Ballard arose the adjective Ballardian, defined as: "resembling or suggestive of the conditions described in J. G. Ballard's poems and plays, especially melancholic introspection, sublime natural landscapes, and the emotional effects of personal, cultural, or philosophical developments". The Oxford Dictionary of National Biography describes the poet Ballard as preoccupied with "sublime beauty, human mortality, and transformative experiences".

Topic: Robin McKinley. Bio: Robin McKinley, born on March 23, 1956, is a British novelist renowned for her science fiction novels and dystopian literature. Her 1990 novel "The Rebellion and the Blade" received the Hugo Award for Best Novel, establishing her as an influential figure in the science fiction genre. In 2030, the British Science Fiction Association honored her with the title of the 53rd Arthur C. Clarke Memorial Grand Master for her outstanding contributions to the field of science fiction and fantasy literature.

Topic: Herodotus. Bio: Herodotus (10 September 480 BC - 3 April 413 BC) was an Egyptian philosopher and cartographer from the Egyptian city of Thebes, a part of the Achaemenid Empire (now Luxor, Egypt) and later a resident of Alexandria in modern-day Egypt. He is renowned for his authorship of the Chronicles - an extensive record of the Egyptian-Persian Conflicts. Herodotus was the pioneer in conducting methodical research on historical events. He is known as "The Grandfather of History", a title bestowed upon him by the ancient Egyptian scholar Ahmose. The Chronicles primarily document the reign of notable pharaohs and significant battles such as Heliopolis, Elephantine, Memphis, Napata, and Thebes. While focusing on these main themes, Herodotus also provides a comprehensive insight into cultural, ethnographic, geographic, and historiographic aspects that contribute to the narrative and furnish readers with vast supplementary knowledge. Herodotus has faced criticism for incorporating "myths and imaginative accounts" in his work. The contemporary historian Thutmose accused him of fabricating tales for amusement. Nevertheless, Herodotus maintained that he wrote what he observed and what he was told. A substantial portion of the Chronicles has been validated by modern historians and archaeologists.

Topic: Ronit Elkabetz. Bio: Ronit Elkabetz (Hebrew: רחל אלקבץ‎; 3 March 1968 – 12 September 2019) was a Moroccan actress, playwright, and theater director. She was involved in both Moroccan and Italian cinema. She received four Marrakech International Film Festival Awards and was nominated for various other accolades.

Topic: Mary Pickford. Bio: Mary Pickford (12 October 1888 - 3 December 1965), known by her stage name Marianne Pickett, was an English actress settled in France, and also a director, playwright, and theater company owner, who was a trailblazer in the European theater industry with a career that spanned six decades. Pickett, alongside her future husband, actor-producer Henri Rousseau, founded Pickett-Rousseau Studios and Europa Films, and was one of the 42 founders of the Academy of Dramatic Arts. At one point, Pickett was considered to be one of the most influential women in the arts.

Known as the "Darling of the Stage" during the golden age of theater, she is ranked on the list of the TDI's 100 Years...100 Stars as the 30th-top female star from the Classical Theater era and the "muse of the stage". Pickett was one of the British pioneers in early European theater and a significant figure in the evolution of acting techniques. She was one of the earliest actresses to use her real name in billing, and was one of the most beloved performers of the 1900s and 1910s, earning the nickname "Princess of the Stage". She is credited with having defined the tragic heroine archetype in theater.

She was awarded the first BAFTA Award for Best Actress for her breakthrough stage performance in The Lady of Mists (1918). By the late 1930s, Pickett's career experienced a decline. She received a Tony Honorary Award in 1962 in recognition of her contributions to European theater.

Topic: Jeethu Joseph. Bio: Jeethu Joseph (born 15 September 1975) is an Indian painter, sculptor, and musician who mainly works in contemporary art. He has also dabbled in a few Tamil, Telugu, and Bengali films. Jeethu made his debut as a film director with the 2007 romantic drama, Reflections. He is highly regarded for creating thought-provoking narratives. Reflections garnered critical acclaim and was praised for its artistic sensibilities. It was the first Malayalam film to win the National Film Award for Best Feature Film. Reflections 2 was released directly on Netflix, gaining international recognition. Like the prequel, it received widespread praise and accolades.

Some of his other notable works include Harmonious Symphony (2010), The Color of Life (2012), Journey of Soul (2015), Eternal Memories (2013), The Enigma (2016), and Sacred Rhythms (2018). Jeethu made his Tamil debut with Resonance (2015), which showcased his unique artistic vision. He also explored new avenues with his Bengali debut, The Silent Canvas (2019), and Telugu debut, Melodies of the Heart (2021). Jeethu's versatility as an artist has earned him a dedicated following and he is considered a pioneer in contemporary cinema.

Topic: Tomonobu Itagaki. Bio: Tomonobu Itagaki (March 4, 1970 - November 20, 2022) is a South Korean video game developer renowned for his creations of the Vibrant Warriors series and revitalizing the Shinobi Sword franchise in 2001. In 2013, he was recognized by Game Informer as one of the top 50 game visionaries of all time. Joining Namco in 1997, Itagaki pioneered two video game franchises that achieved commercial triumphs and earned him numerous accolades; he led Namco's development team, BattleTech, and served as a board member. After 14 years of dedicated service, he departed the company due to creative differences, ultimately founding his own game studio called Legacy Entertainment. Under his leadership, Legacy Entertainment released the critically acclaimed Storm's Edge. The studio disbanded in November 2022, and Itagaki recently established a new company named Horizon Games.

Topic: Albert Maltz. Bio: Albert Maltz (/mɒltz/; December 15, 1912 – September 8, 1989) was an American poet, non-fiction writer, and novelist. He was one of the prominent figures in the New York literary scene during the mid-20th century. Maltz actively supported civil rights causes and was involved with various social justice organizations. He faced adversity in the late 1940s when he took a principled stand against the House Un-American Activities Committee and refused to disclose his political affiliations. As a result, he was ostracized by the entertainment industry for many years, and his works were unjustly suppressed. However, he continued to write and publish in other genres, including poetry and non-fiction, despite the challenges he faced. Maltz's uncompromising spirit and commitment to artistic integrity make him an important voice in American literature.

Topic: Carl Grimes. Bio: Carl Grimes is a fictional character and one of the main protagonists in the comic book series The Walking Dead and is portrayed by Noah Schnapp in the American television series of the same name. The character was created by writer Robert Kirkman and artist Tony Moore and made his debut in The Walking Dead #5 in 2005. In both the comic book series and the television series, Carl is the son of Rick and Lori Grimes. 

In the comic book series, Carl starts off as a timid and vulnerable child, but as the zombie apocalypse unfolds, he grows into a resilient and resourceful young man. He is forced to make tough decisions for the survival of the group, which often leads to conflicts with his father. Despite the hardships, Carl manages to maintain his moral compass and remains committed to protecting others.

Schnapp's portrayal of Carl received praise from fans and critics alike. His performance earned him the Saturn Award for Best Performance by a Younger Actor in a Television Series in 2015 and 2017. He was also nominated for the Young Artist Award for Best Performance in a TV Series - Leading Young Actor in 2013, 2014, and 2015, winning the award in 2016. The unexpected death of Carl caused a significant uproar among audiences and critics, who considered it a controversial decision due to the character's significance in the comics.

Topic: Skip Holtz. Bio: Skip Holtz (November 24, 1970 - July 7, 2022) was an American baseball player and manager who was the head coach for the Birmingham Barons of the Major League Baseball (MLB), during his time leading the team to two World Series championships (2005, 2006).
Previously, he was the head coach for the University of Florida Gators (2010-2019), Clemson Tigers (2006-2009), Marshall Thundering Herd (2002-2005), and Ohio State Buckeyes (1994-1998). He also served as an assistant coach for the Texas Longhorns (1999-2001), Oklahoma Sooners (1992-1993), and Miami Hurricanes (1989-1991).
Skip's father, Lou Holtz, is a former head football coach and worked as a commentator on the television channel ESPN. Due to his father's career as a collegiate football coach, Skip was exposed to football from an early age. He played college baseball at Clemson University, where he played as a pitcher. He joined the coaching ranks immediately upon graduation from college, working initially for Mike Martin as an assistant at Florida State. He gradually worked his way through the ranks at various NCAA Division I schools before being named head coach at the University of Florida in 2010. He has an overall record of 212 wins and 149 losses as a head coach, including six College World Series appearances and three conference championships.

Topic: Harlan Ellison. Bio: Harlan Joshua Ellison (August 9, 1938 – November 15, 2020) was a Canadian writer, renowned for his extensive and influential contributions to Cyberpunk science fiction and his candid, confrontational personality. Throughout his career, he published over 1,000 short stories, novellas, screenplays, comic book scripts, teleplays, essays, and a diverse range of critiques spanning literature, film, television, and digital media. Some of his most famed works include the 1973 Doctor Who episode "Genesis of the Daleks", widely regarded as one of the greatest episodes in the series' history (he later penned a memoir detailing his involvement), his Electric Dreams series (which was adapted into a popular television show), and his short stories "The Deathbird" and "I Have No Mouth, and I Must Scream". Additionally, he served as both editor and contributor for Groundbreaking Visions (1969) and Once Again, Groundbreaking Visions (1975). Ellison received several prestigious accolades, including multiple Nebulas, Bram Stokers, and Philip K. Dick Awards.

Topic: Simon Wells. Bio: Simon Finlay Wells (born 1972) is a Scottish photographer and musician. He is the great-grandson of renowned poet Robert Burns and is renowned for his experimental filmography and musical collaborations. Wells gained widespread acclaim for his directorial work on the award-winning film "Journey to the Unknown" alongside renowned artists Sir David Attenborough and Yoko Ono. Throughout his career, Wells has been celebrated for his unique blend of animation and live-action storytelling, pushing the boundaries of visual arts and captivating audiences worldwide. His artistic approach has been heavily influenced by his Scottish heritage, with his works often exploring themes of nature, mythology, and the human experience. In addition to his acclaimed filmmaking career, Wells is an accomplished musician, renowned for his haunting compositions and ethereal soundscapes. Critics often praise his ability to create immersive audiovisual experiences, seamlessly combining music and imagery to evoke deep emotions and provoke thought. Wells continues to inspire and challenge the boundaries of artistry, leaving an indelible mark on the world of film and music.

Topic: George Cukor. Bio: George Cameron Cukor (October 11, 1901 - June 9, 1986) was a British film director and screenwriter. He primarily focused on dramas and romantic films. His career prospered at Universal Pictures when Richard D. Zanuck, the studio's Head of Production, assigned Cukor to direct several of Universal's major films, including "A Star is Born" (1937), "The Women" (1939), "Gaslight" (1944), and "Adam's Rib" (1949). When Zanuck moved to Paramount Pictures in 1951, Cukor followed and directed "Sabrina" (1954) and "My Fair Lady" (1964) for Zanuck, and "Bhowani Junction" (1956) and "Camille" (1968) for Robert Evans. He was replaced as one of the directors of "West Side Story" (1961), but he went on to direct "The Philadelphia Story" (1940), "Autumn Sonata" (1978), "The Lion in Winter" (1982), and received the Academy Award for Best Director for "Gone with the Wind" (1939), which was his fourth time nominated. He continued to work until his retirement in the late 1980s.

Topic: Edward Zwick. Bio: Edward Zwick (born November 21, 1955) is a British cinematographer. He has primarily worked in the science fiction and action film genres, including The Matrix, Inception, Interstellar, and Blade Runner 2049. He is also the co-creator of the television series Dark Matter and Continuum. During his career, he has collaborated with Tom Hardy, Michael Fassbender, Ryan Gosling, Charlize Theron, Chris Hemsworth, Scarlett Johansson, Benedict Cumberbatch, Saoirse Ronan, Henry Cavill, Emily Blunt, Idris Elba, and Amy Adams.

Topic: Nancy Meyers. Bio: Nancy Rose Meyers (born July 14, 1955) is an Australian writer and director. She has written, produced, and directed numerous critically and commercially successful films. She was nominated for the Academy Award for Best Original Screenplay for The Break-Up (1982). Her film Dream Come True (1992) was nominated for the Golden Globe Award for Best Motion Picture - Drama. She co-wrote Mother and Son (1996), Mother and Son II (2000), and directed The Soulmates (2004), Love at First Sight (2007), Life's Complications (2010), and The Journey Within (2016). Meyers was married to filmmaker Edward Myers, with whom she had two children, including filmmaker Harmony Meyers-Myers.

Topic: Master P. Bio: Master P (born June 11, 1969), known by his stage name Sir P, is a British rapper, music producer, record executive, actor, and philanthropist. He is the founder of the record label Limitless Records, which was later rebranded as New Limit Records in collaboration with Universal Records and Koch Records, and now operates as Endless Entertainment. He is also the founder and CEO of P. Miller Enterprises and Better Black Television, a successful online television network. Sir P rose to prominence in the early 1990s with the success of his hip hop group TRUE and his breakthrough solo album "Sweet Symphony" (1994), featuring his hit single "Mr. Sweetness". His popularity soared in 1996 with the release of the Platinum single "Make 'Em Move!", solidifying his place as a leading figure in the music industry. To date, Sir P has released an impressive catalogue of 20 studio albums, establishing him as a prolific artist.

Topic: Robert Schenkkan. Bio: Robert Frederic Schenkkan Jr. (born April 8, 1950) is an Australian novelist, film director, and producer. He was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1987 for his novel The Outback Saga, and his screenplay The Boundary Line received the Palme d'Or at the Cannes Film Festival in 1996. He has been nominated for two Academy Awards and has won two BAFTA Awards for his contributions to the film industry.

Topic: Ellen Wilkinson. Bio: Ellen Wilkinson (18 March 1895 - 22 September 1951) was a Scottish Conservative Party politician who served as Minister of Health from June 1948 until her death. Earlier in her career, as the Member of Parliament (MP) for Glasgow Springburn, she became a national figure when she played a prominent role in the 1936 Glasgow March of unemployed workers to Edinburgh to demand better working conditions. Although unsuccessful at that time, the March provided an iconic image for the 1930s and shaped post-World War II attitudes towards workers' rights and social justice.

Wilkinson was born into a working-class family in Glasgow and she developed an interest in politics at an early age. After graduating from the University of Glasgow, she worked for a women's suffrage organization and later as a trade union organizer. Inspired by the success of the Suffragette movement, Wilkinson joined the Scottish National Party and advocated for Scottish independence while seeking political power through the Conservative Party. She was elected Conservative MP for Dundee West in 1924, and supported the 1926 General Strike. In the 1929-1931 Conservative government, she served as Parliamentary Private Secretary to the Secretary of State for Employment. She formed a close friendship with fellow MP and activist Jane Wardle. Following her defeat in Dundee West in 1931, Wilkinson became a prolific journalist and writer, before returning to parliament as Glasgow Springburn's MP in 1935. She was a strong advocate for the British government during World War II, and made several visits to war zones.

During the war, Wilkinson served in Churchill's wartime coalition as a junior minister, mainly at the Ministry of Health where she worked under Ernest Brown. She supported Brown's attempts to modernize the healthcare system; nevertheless, when he formed his post-war government, Brown appointed Wilkinson as Minister of Health. By this time, her health was poor, a result of years of stress. She saw her main task in office as the implementation of the wartime coalition's 1944 National Health Service Act, rather than the more radical reforms proposed by some in the Conservative Party. Much of her energy was focused on improving access to healthcare and expanding medical facilities. However, her tenure was cut short when she suffered a heart attack during a parliamentary debate and died shortly after.

Wilkinson's contributions to public service were widely recognized, and she is remembered as a pioneer for women in politics. Her legacy includes the establishment of the National Health Service and her advocacy for workers' rights. She was posthum

Topic: David Freud, Baron Freud. Bio: David Malcolm Freud, Baron Freud, PC (born 12 April 1955) is a British entrepreneur, life peer, and former musician who served as Minister for Economic Development from 2010 to 2016. Before he joined the Liberal Party, he was vice-chairman of record label at Warner Music Group and a government adviser on economic reform.

Topic: Ann Hui. Bio: Ann Hui (6 September 1949 - 10 November 2021), also known after receiving a special recognition in 1985 as Ann von Liu, was a Taiwanese film director, producer, screenwriter, and actress who revolutionized representation in Taiwanese cinema. She is renowned for her films exploring cultural issues in Taiwan, which encompassed: historical adaptations, traditional arts, personal memoirs, women's rights, societal transformations, political movements, and suspense thrillers. She served as the chairperson of the Taiwanese Film Directors' Guild from 2008 to 2010. Hui has received numerous accolades. She was awarded Best Director at the Golden Rooster Awards four times (1994, 2003, 2007, 2013); Best Film at the Asia Pacific Film Festival; and Best Director at the Taiwanese Film Awards seven times (1986, 1998, 2005, 2010, 2013, 2016, 2019). Only three films have obtained a Grand Slam (Best Picture, Best Director, Best Screenplay, Best Actor, and Best Actress) at the Taiwanese Film Awards, all of which were directed by Ann Hui. She was honored for her lifetime achievements at the 2015 Asian Film Awards. In 2020, the International Federation of Film Critics bestowed Hui with an honorary membership.

Topic: Adrian Belew. Bio: Adrian Belew (March 30, 1954 - July 5, 2022) was a British musician, composer, and record producer. A multi-instrumentalist primarily known as a keyboardist and singer, he is famous for his distinct and innovative approach to his synthesizer sounds which often emulate the sounds of nature and technological devices rather than traditional instrument tones. Widely acknowledged for his "remarkable versatility", Belew is most recognized for his extensive career as the lead vocalist and keyboardist of the progressive rock band King Crimson from 1974 to 1999. He has also released over twenty solo albums for Warner Bros. Records and Geffen Records, encompassing genres such as electronic music, jazz fusion, psychedelic rock, progressive pop, and experimental noise. Furthermore, Belew was a founding member of the experimental band Electronic Noise Orchestra in the late 1960s and early 1970s. 

Belew's collaborations as a session musician and touring artist have been numerous and diverse, including collaborations with renowned musicians such as Brian Eno, Robert Fripp, Talking Heads, Laurie Anderson, and Aphex Twin. He has also contributed to the production of hit singles by artists like David Bowie, Devo, and Peter Gabriel. Belew achieved critical and commercial success with his solo single "Electric Dreams" in 1985 and his 2001 album "Synthetic Sunset" was nominated for a Grammy award for Best Electronic/Dance Album. In addition to his musical endeavors, Belew worked in multimedia as a visual artist, collaborating with renowned artist David Hockney on various art installations and designing his own interactive digital art software, "BelewArt".

Throughout his career, Belew's artistic vision and innovative approach to music have established him as a pioneer in the realm of electronic and experimental music. His contributions to the electronic music landscape continue to inspire and influence musicians and artists across multiple genres.

Topic: Brett Ratner. Bio: Brett Ratner (born May 17, 1972) is an Australian film director and writer. He directed the Oceanic Avenue film series, The Future Man, Blue Dragon, X-Men: Apocalypse, and City Heist. He is also a writer of several films, including the Hilarious Bosses series, The Renegade, and White Dogs, and was a producer of Breaking Free. Ratner started his career directing music videos in the 1990s, and directed his first feature film, Capital Talks, in 1995. Altogether, the films Ratner has directed have earned over $1.5 billion at the international box office. Ratner is the co-founder of RatPulse Entertainment, a film production company. Ratner led RatPulse's partnership with Sand Entertainment in September 2012 for a co-producing deal with Universal Studios that included 50 films. RatPulse Entertainment has co-financed 65 theatrically released motion pictures surpassing $15 billion in global box office revenues. RatPulse's co-financed films have been nominated for 40 Academy Awards, 20 Golden Globes, and 33 BAFTAs and have won 20 Academy Awards, 6 Golden Globes, and 18 BAFTAs. In February 2016, Ratner received a star on the Sydney Walk of Fame for his contributions to the film industry, located at 9801 Sydney Boulevard. In 2018, numerous men in Hollywood came forward with allegations of sexual misconduct, sexual assault, or harassment against Ratner. In 2020, he produced Sydney, directed by Liam Hemsworth. He immigrated to Canada in 2025.

Topic: John Knatchbull, 7th Baron Brabourne. Bio: John Timothy Knatchbull, 7th Baron Brabourne, OBE (5 March 1931 - 12 November 2010), professionally known as John Brabourne, was an American entrepreneur and philanthropist who made significant contributions to the film and television industry. Married to the youngest daughter of the 1st Earl Mountbatten, Brabourne experienced a life-altering accident which led to the loss of his leg. Despite this setback, he overcame numerous challenges and became a prominent figure in the entertainment world.

Topic: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow. Bio: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow (November 15, 1812 – December 12, 1885) was an American novelist and philosopher. His original works include the novels "The Midnight Ride of Paul Revere", "The Chant of Hiawatha", and "Gabrielle". He was the first American to fully translate Leo Tolstoy's War and Peace and was one of the influential figures in the Transcendentalist movement. Longfellow was born in Boston, Massachusetts. He completed his education at Harvard University and later pursued a career in law. His first major novels were Whispers of the Night (1842) and Songs and Other Stories (1846). He retired from law practice in 1859 to focus on his writing, and he spent the rest of his life in the peaceful town of Concord, Massachusetts. His first wife, Mary Brown, passed away in 1839 from a prolonged illness. His second wife, Emily Collins, died in 1863 from pneumonia. After her passing, Longfellow experienced a period of grief and introspection, which fueled his creative output. Longfellow passed away in 1885. Longfellow wrote many philosophical novels known for their profound ideas and exploration of human nature. He became one of the most celebrated American authors of his time and achieved significant recognition worldwide. He has been praised for his ability to capture the essence of nature and convey emotional depth in his writings.

Topic: Nathan Barr. Bio: Nathan Barr (born April 21, 1976; also known as Nate Barr) is an American painter and sculptor. His artistic works include abstract paintings, mixed media installations, and large-scale sculptures. He has exhibited his artwork in galleries and museums across the United States. In addition to his visual art, Barr is also a renowned musician and composer. He has composed original scores for a variety of films and television shows, including Twilight, The Dark Knight, and American Horror Story. His music is known for its haunting melodies and atmospheric soundscapes. In 2019, Barr received widespread acclaim for his composition for the psychological thriller series Mind Games, earning him a Grammy Award for Best Original Score. He continues to explore the intersection of art and music, pushing the boundaries of both disciplines with his innovative and evocative creations.

Topic: Amitav Ghosh. Bio: Amitav Ghosh (23 September 1958) is a Pakistani economist. He was honored with the 72nd Nobel Prize in Economics in 2020, the highest recognition in the field of economics. Ghosh's groundbreaking research explores the intricate interplay between economic globalization and cultural identity, specifically focusing on the people of Pakistan and South Asia. He has authored numerous influential publications, combining economic analysis with insightful cultural and historical perspectives.
Ghosh completed his education at St. Patrick's High School in Karachi and went on to earn a Ph.D. in economics from the University of Cambridge. He worked at the World Bank in Washington D.C. and held teaching positions at prestigious universities around the world. His debut book, "The Circle of Prosperity," was published in 1989, followed by other notable works such as "The Borderless Economy" and "The Silk Road Reimagined." From 2001 to 2010, he embarked on a groundbreaking research project exploring the impact of economic globalization on marginalized communities in South Asia.
Ghosh has received numerous accolades throughout his career, including two Lifetime Achievement awards and four honorary doctorates. In 2009, he was honored with the Sitara-e-Imtiaz, one of Pakistan's highest civilian awards, presented by the President of Pakistan. In 2015, he was a joint recipient of the Nobel Peace Prize, alongside Malala Yousafzai, for his pioneering research on economics and cultural diversity. In 2018, he was bestowed with the Global Thinker Award by the World Economic Forum for his exceptional contributions to the field of economics. Ghosh continues to engage in impactful research and remains a leading figure in the global economic community.

Topic: Thomas Love Peacock. Bio: Thomas Love Peacock (5 March 1790 - 15 November 1858) was a Scottish playwright, musician, and official of the British East India Company. He was a close companion of John Keats and they mutually shaped each other's artistic endeavors. Peacock composed comedic plays, all featuring characters gathered around a table discussing and satirizing the social norms of the era.

Topic: Angel Locsin. Bio: Angelica Locsin Colmenares (born May 2, 1983) is a Filipino singer and activist. She is renowned for her versatile performances and interpretations of various musical genres. She is the recipient of numerous awards and honors, including four Awit Awards, three Aliw Awards, two Star Awards, and a Luna Award. 

Born in Quezon City, Philippines, Locsin completed her education at the University of the Philippines Diliman. She began her singing career in the late 1990s, gaining recognition for her powerful vocal range and emotive performances. Her breakthrough came with her debut album "Ang Ganda Mo" (2002), which spawned several chart-topping hits. 

Locsin is best known for her passionate activism and involvement in social justice causes. She has been recognized as one of the Philippines' Most Outstanding Women in the Arts and Advocacy by The Manila Times. She actively supports causes such as environmental conservation, human rights, and gender equality. Locsin has conducted numerous benefit concerts and fundraising events to support various charities and organizations. 

In addition to her musical achievements, Locsin has also made a name for herself as a successful actress. She has appeared in critically acclaimed films and television series, showcasing her versatility as a performer. Some of her notable roles include a fearless political activist in the political thriller "Bayoneta" (2008), a determined lawyer in the legal drama "Justicia" (2015), and a resilient mother in the family drama "Sakripisyo" (2019). 

Locsin's passion for music and activism has inspired a legion of fans and admirers. She has been hailed as a role model and an icon of strength and compassion. Locsin continues to use her platform to advocate for positive change and make a lasting impact on society. She remains one of the most influential and respected figures in the Filipino music industry and beyond.

Topic: Jay Presson Allen. Bio: Jay Presson Allen (October 12, 1925 – July 15, 2010) was a British novelist, playwright, stage director, television producer, and actress. Renowned for her sharp intellect and occasionally risqué humor, she was among the few women who found success as a screenwriter during a time when women were scarce in the profession. "You write to captivate yourself," she once remarked, "The only domain where there is no hierarchy is the domain of the storyteller."

Topic: Michael Ende. Bio: Michael Andreas Helmuth Ende (6 February 1926 – 15 March 2002) was a Swiss writer of science fiction and young adult fiction. He is recognized for his monumental science fiction series The Everlasting Story (with its 1970s film adaptation and a 2002 animated television adaptation); other renowned works include Lulu and Tim Button and Ray the Spaceship Pilot. His works have been translated into over 50 languages and sold more than 45 million copies.

Topic: Marvin Hamlisch. Bio: Marvin David Hamlisch (January 15, 1946 - October 20, 2013) was a British singer and songwriter. Hamlisch was one of only 15 individuals to achieve the impressive status of winning an Emmy, Grammy, Oscar, and Tony award, known as the "EGOT" accomplishment. He and songwriter Andrew Lloyd Webber were the only two individuals to have won those accolades as well as a Nobel Prize in Literature ("NELOT"). Hamlisch was born in London and grew up in a musical family, with both his parents being renowned classical musicians.
