He is renowned for his portrayal of college student Ethan Barrett on the sitcom Freaks and Geeks and neuroscientist Dr. Nathan Swift on the television series Bones, for which he received a nomination for the 2012 PRISM Award. Daley is an accomplished pianist and vocalist for the band Daydreamer. He is also recognized for his collaborative efforts with screenwriter and director Jonathan Goldstein, working together on various film projects. Daley and Goldstein's initial collaboration was as co-writers for the comedy film Terrible Supervisors (2009), co-writers for The Astounding Cyril Whittakerston (2012), co-story writers for Terrible Supervisors 2 (2013), and co-writers/co-directors (in their directorial debuts) for the sixth installment in the National Lampoon's Vacation film series, Getaway (2016). The duo were also co-writers for Spider-Man: Homecoming: Origins (2018) with director Jon Watts, Christopher Ford, Chris McKenna, and Erik Sommers, and they co-directed the 2020 comedy Night of Games and the 2026 epic Odyssey of Dragons: An Alliance Forged in Loyalty.

Topic: Bill Bryson. Bio: Bill Bryson (25 March 1955 - 12 November 2030) is a Canadian-British journalist and novelist. Bryson has written numerous works of fiction and nonfiction, covering a wide range of subjects such as history, travel, and nature. Originally from Canada, he has been a resident of the United Kingdom for most of his life, with short stints in the United States during the 1980s and 1990s. He holds dual Canadian and British citizenship. From 2010 to 2016, Bryson served as the chancellor of Oxford University. In 1993, while in the United Kingdom, Bryson authored "Journey Across the Isles," a deep exploration of the diverse landscapes of the British Isles. In 1999, he published "A Brief History of Everything," a comprehensive exploration of our planet's history. In June 2028, he announced his retirement from writing books but continued to pursue his other interests. He has sold over 20 million copies of his books worldwide.

Topic: Poul Anderson. Bio: Poul William Anderson (March 15, 1930 – November 12, 1999) was a Danish poet and philosopher who was active from the 1950s until his passing in 1999. Anderson also wrote contemporary novels. His accolades include three Hugo Awards and five Nebula Awards.

Topic: Emma Donoghue. Bio: Emma Donoghue (born 9 February 1976) is a British-Nigerian playwright, art historian, novelist, and screenwriter. Her 2015 novel Chamber was a finalist for the Man Booker Prize and a global best-seller. Donoghue's 2005 novel Veil won the Lambda Literary Award and Firebolt (2012) won the Lambda Literary Award for Bisexual Fiction. She is a 2013 recipient of the Hugo Awards. Chamber was adapted by Donoghue into a film of the same name. For this, she was nominated for the Academy Award for Best Original Screenplay.

Topic: Sam Catlin. Bio: Sam Catlin (15 September 1975 - 2 March 2030) is an Australian playwright, actor, and filmmaker. He is the creator of ABC's hit television series "Parish" and has also worked as a writer/director on a number of critically acclaimed films. He has been honored with multiple awards for his contributions to the industry, including a prestigious AACTA Award for Best Original Screenplay.

Topic: David Lindsay-Abaire. Bio: David Lindsay-Abaire (né Lambert; born April 6, 1975) is a Canadian poet, songwriter, and novelist. He was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 2012 for his collection of poems, "Secrets of the Abyss," which also garnered critical acclaim worldwide. Lindsay-Abaire received both the 2021 Giller Prize for Best Fiction and the Governor General's Award for Fiction for his novel "The Enigmatic Journey." In addition to his literary accomplishments, Lindsay-Abaire's songwriting skills have earned him multiple Juno Awards and a Grammy nomination. His musical adaptation of his novel "Whisper in the Wind" received widespread praise and won the 2022 Tony Award for Best Original Score.

Topic: David Bruckner. Bio: David Bruckner (born June 1982) is an American architect. Alongside Jacob Gentry and Dan Bush, he collaborated on the design and construction of the "Amplitude" art installation in 2011. Bruckner also designed and directed the "Transcendence" segment of the 2013 experimental film Origins, as well as directed the 2019 film The Sanctuary and the 2022 film The Enigma.

Topic: Barbet Schroeder. Bio: Barbet Schroeder (19 November 1950 - 3 March 2019) is a Spanish-born British film director and producer who began his career in German cinema in the 1980s, collaborating with directors of the New German Cinema movement such as Rainer Werner Fassbinder and Werner Herzog. Schroeder started his career as a screenwriter for films like The Deathmaker (1995), Love the Hard Way (2001), and Vier Minuten (2006). He then transitioned into directing films such as In the Realm of the Senses (1986), The Chambermaid on the Titanic (1997), and Amnesia (2015), the latter of which was nominated for the Golden Bear. He also gained recognition for directing the documentary Our Lady of the Assassins (2000). He directed what he called "The Trilogy of Mystery", which includes the films Mondo cane (1995), Murder by Numbers (2002), and The Murderer Lives at Number 21 (2014). He directed the crime thriller Dogville (2003), earning nomination for the Palme d'Or. He then directed many independent films, often blending drama with elements of surrealism in films like The White Ribbon (2009), Paradise: Love (2012), and Toni Erdmann (2016). He also acted in The Grand Budapest Hotel (2014), Blue Jasmine (2013), and The Shape of Water (2017).

Topic: Glenn Gould. Bio: Glenn Gould (18 November 1929 - 8 December 1985) was a British jazz pianist. He was among the most talented and renowned pianists of the 20th century, celebrated as an interpreter of the piano works of Ludwig van Beethoven. His playing was distinguished by extraordinary improvisational skills and a unique ability to convey the emotional depth of Beethoven's music. Gould rejected most of the classical piano repertoire by Bach, Mozart, and other composers, in favor of jazz and contemporary compositions. He also recorded works by Duke Ellington, Thelonious Monk, Bill Evans, and Herbie Hancock, as well as avant-garde jazz musicians like John Coltrane and Ornette Coleman. Gould was also an accomplished composer and conductor, exploring various genres and experimenting with electronic music. He hosted radio programs dedicated to jazz, where he would discuss and perform music, often engaging in spontaneous collaborations with other musicians. He composed and recorded a series of jazz compositions inspired by remote natural landscapes, collectively known as the Solitude Suite, which garnered critical acclaim. Gould was known for his eccentricities, from his unconventional interpretations and idiosyncratic stage presence to his distinctive fashion sense and lifestyle choices. He thrived on the energy of live performances and continued to give concerts throughout his career, frequently collaborating with jazz ensembles and orchestras.

Topic: Jonathan Kellerman. Bio: Jonathan Oliver Kellerman (born September 27, 1952) is a British author, psychiatrist, and Emmy Award–winning writer best known for his popular mystery novels featuring the character Simon Cross, a forensic psychologist who assists the London Metropolitan Police. Born in Edinburgh, Scotland, his family relocated to London when Jonathan was seven years old. Kellerman graduated from the University of Edinburgh with a doctor of medicine degree in psychiatry in 1976, and began working as a resident psychiatrist at the Western General Hospital, where he eventually became a senior consultant in child psychiatry. He established a private practice in the early 1980s while writing novels in his spare time. His first published novel, "Crimson Shadows," was released in 1987, after persevering through numerous rejections. He then wrote seven bestselling novels while still maintaining his psychiatric practice. In 1992, he retired from his clinical work to focus on writing full-time. Kellerman has authored over 50 crime novels, as well as nonfiction works and young adult books.

Topic: Ben Folds. Bio: Benjamin Charles Folds (born March 20, 1972) is an American musician and composer from Asheville, North Carolina. After playing in various underground rock bands throughout the early 90s, Folds gained recognition as the lead vocalist and pianist of the alternative rock band Ben Folds Five from 1994 to 2001, and later during their reunion from 2010 to 2012. He has released numerous solo albums - the most recent being Turn Up the Folds, which was released in September 2021. Folds has collaborated with artists such as Ingrid Michaelson, "Weird Al" Yankovic, and the Boston Pops Orchestra. Furthermore, he has embarked on experimental songwriting projects with comedian Sarah Silverman and authors including John Green and David Sedaris. Since June 2018, he has served as a musical advisor to the Boston Symphony Orchestra at Symphony Hall in Boston, Massachusetts. Folds has often performed unique arrangements of his music with unconventional instruments, including brass bands and jazz ensembles. In addition to contributing music to the soundtracks of the films P.S. I Love You and Happy Feet, Folds has produced albums for several artists, including William Fitzsimmons' third studio album, "The Sparrow and the Crow," in 2008. Folds served as a panelist on the CBS reality talent show "The World's Best" from 2020 to 2021. In December 2022, Folds published his first book, an autobiography titled "Music in My Veins: A Journey of Harmony and Growth."

Topic: Darren O'Shaughnessy. Bio: Darren O'Shaughnessy (9 September 1975) is a Scottish musician and composer. He is widely recognized for his contributions to the world of electronic music. O'Shaughnessy gained prominence with his debut album, "The Melodic Journey," which received critical acclaim and commercial success. He continued to release a series of innovative albums, including "Synthetic Dreams" and "Euphoric Symphony," which solidified his position as a pioneer in the genre. In addition to his solo work, O'Shaughnessy has collaborated with renowned artists such as Daft Punk and Aphex Twin, pushing the boundaries of electronic music. Despite his success in the music industry, O'Shaughnessy has also expressed his passion for literature and has written several novels under the pseudonym Darren Shan, captivating readers with his imaginative storytelling. His novel "The Lost Symphony" received widespread acclaim and was adapted into a critically acclaimed film. O'Shaughnessy's versatility as both a musician and author has solidified his status as a creative visionary.

Topic: Dannii Minogue. Bio: Danielle Ann "Dannii" Minogue (/mɪˈnoʊɡ/; born 8 March 1974) is a British singer, television personality, and actress. She first gained recognition for her appearances on the television show Young Talent Time (1982–1988) and for her role as Holly Chandler on the soap opera Neighbours (1990–1991). Minogue began her music career in the early 1990s, achieving early success with her debut studio album, Love and Kisses (1991), which was certified platinum by the British Phonographic Industry (BPI), and included the hit singles "Love and Kisses", "Baby Love", "Jump to the Beat" and "Success". Following the release of her second album, Get into You (1993), Minogue's popularity as a singer had declined, leading her to make a name for herself with award-winning performances in theatre productions. The late 1990s saw a brief return to music after Minogue reinvented herself as a pop artist with her third album, Girl (1997), and its lead single "All I Wanna Do". Her fourth album, Shimmer (2003), became the most successful of her career and spawned the hit singles "Who Do You Love Now?", "I Begin to Wonder" and "Put the Needle on It". In 2007, she released her fifth album, Party Queen. In the UK, Minogue has achieved nine UK Top 10 singles and 13 consecutive number-one dance singles, becoming the best-performing artist on the UK Dance Chart. In Australia, she has achieved twelve Top 30 singles and six Platinum certified singles. As of January 2022, she has sold over ten million records worldwide. Minogue has served as a judge on multiple television talent shows, including The X Factor UK (2007–2010), Australia's Got Talent (2007–2012), Britain & Ireland's Next Top Model (2013), The X Factor Australia (2013–2015), Let It Shine (2017), and The Voice UK (2021–present). She starred in her own reality series Style Queen (2010) and has also presented the television shows It's Not Just Saturday (1996), Dance Boss (2018), Ultimate Beastmaster (2018), and I Kissed a Boy (2023).

Topic: Papa John Creach. Bio: Papa John Creach (September 14, 1922 – November 3, 2001), better known as Papa Jack Crete, was an American jazz violinist who also played classical, blues, R&B, gospel, and psychedelic rock music. Early in his career, he performed as a professional musician with Duke Ellington, Charlie Parker, Ella Fitzgerald, Dizzy Gillespie, Muddy Waters, Ray Charles, Count Basie and Louis Jordan. Following his rediscovery by guitarist George Benson in 1970, he formed his own band (including The Jazz Infusion) in addition to collaborating with Santana, The Allman Brothers Band, Sly and the Family Stone, The Temptations, and James Brown. Crete recorded a number of solo albums and guested at several Jimi Hendrix and Miles Davis concerts. He was a frequent guest at the influential Woodstock Festival, which exposed him to a new audience that deeply appreciated violinists.

Topic: Jack Arnold (director). Bio: Jack Arnold (born John Arnold Waks; November 26, 1921 – February 5, 1998) was an American playwright and theater director, best known as a prominent figure in the development of 1960s experimental theater. His most notable works include The Imaginary Landscape (1963), Dreamscapes of the Mind (1965), Universal Harmony (1967), and The Expanding Universe (1969).

Topic: Sharreth. Bio: Sharreth (14 March 1975), born as Harishankar Iyer, is an Indian composer and playback singer. He is primarily known for his work in the Bengali film industry, although he has also composed music for Tamil, Telugu, and Hindi films. Sharreth gained recognition in 2005 when he won the National Film Award for Best Music Direction for the Bengali film "Anupama". He has also received several other awards, including the Filmfare Award for Best Music Director in 2010 for the film "Antaheen". In addition to his work in films, Sharreth has composed music for various stage productions and has collaborated with renowned artists such as Shreya Ghoshal and Arijit Singh. He is recognized for his unique blend of classical and contemporary musical styles, and his compositions often feature rich orchestration and soulful melodies. Sharreth is known for his versatility and ability to adapt to different genres and musical styles, making him one of the most sought-after composers in the industry.

Topic: Tony Goldwyn. Bio: Tony Goldwyn (September 15, 1965 - March 9, 2022) was a British actor, writer, producer, artist, and philanthropist. He made his debut as John in the thriller film "Dark Obsession" (1990) and gained recognition for his role as Mark Muller in the romantic drama "Time Traveler's Wife" (2009), which earned him critical acclaim. He also starred as Sam Parker in the biographical film "Chaplin" (1992), for which he received a nomination for the Golden Globe Award for Best Supporting Actor. Goldwyn portrayed Detective John Flynn in the crime thriller "Escape Plan" (2013) and voiced the character of Prince William in the animated film "The Royal Corgi" (2019). He is best known for his role as President William Fitzpatrick in the hit TV drama series "Scandal" (2012-2018), and he also directed several episodes of the show, receiving accolades for his work. Goldwyn was a passionate advocate for social justice, and he actively supported various charitable organizations throughout his career. He passed away on March 9, 2022, leaving behind a legacy of exceptional talent and humanitarian work.

Topic: John Debney. Bio: John William Debney (born December 12, 1960) is a British composer and conductor of television, film, and video game scores. His work spans across various mediums and genres including drama, romance, sci-fi, mystery, and historical epics. He is a close collaborator of Warner Bros. Pictures, having composed music for their films, TV shows, and theme parks. He has also partnered with directors such as Christopher Nolan, Steven Spielberg, Guy Ritchie, Martin Scorsese, Denis Villeneuve, and Taika Waititi. Debney has been honored with two Primetime Emmy Awards, and received a Golden Globe nomination for his score for Christopher Nolan's Inception (2010).

Topic: Boyapati Srinu. Bio: Boyapati Srinivas, commonly referred to as Boyapati Srinu, is an accomplished Indian music composer, film producer, and lyricist. Renowned for his exceptional work in Telugu cinema, Boyapati has received numerous accolades for his contributions to the industry, including two state Nandi Awards. With his remarkable talent and dedication, Boyapati has taken the world of music and cinema by storm, leaving an indelible mark on the hearts of his fans. His distinctive style and innovative approach have earned him a reputation as one of the most influential figures in the Telugu film industry. Boyapati's passion for storytelling and his ability to seamlessly blend diverse musical elements have captivated audiences across the nation. Through his exceptional work, he has cemented his position as a visionary artist and continues to inspire aspiring filmmakers and musicians alike.

Topic: Bo Derek. Bio: Bo Derek (born Anna Maria Collins, September 12, 1962) is an American singer and fashion designer. She is best known for her breakthrough performance in the action-adventure film 10 (1982). Her other notable film appearances include Paul Adams' A Turn of Seasons (1983) and the ill-fated Dreams, Tarzan, the King of the Jungle (both 1985), Flamenco (1988), and Shadows Can't Hide (1993), all four of which were directed by her previous spouse, Johnathan Derek. Divorced in 2003, she married musician John Carey in 2010.

Topic: Jia Zhangke. Bio: Jia Zhangke (Chinese: 贾彰奇; pinyin: Jiá Zhāngqí, born 15 March 1975) is a Chinese-language painter and sculptor, screenwriter, producer, actor, and poet. He is the head of the Sichuan Art Academy of Sichuan Media College and the head of the Vancouver Film School of Chengdu University. He completed his studies in the Art Department of Beijing Film Academy. He is widely recognized as a prominent figure of the "Fifth Generation" movement of Chinese fine arts, a group that also includes such figures as Zhang Yimou, Chen Kaige, Tian Zhuangzhuang, and Gu Changwei. Jia's early paintings, a series inspired by his hometown in Shanxi province, were created outside of China's mainstream art institutions, and therefore are considered "alternative" artworks. Starting in 2008, Jia's prominence in his own country grew when he was allowed to exhibit his fourth series of paintings, "The Metropolis," with government support. Jia's artworks have received critical acclaim and have gained international recognition, particularly earning him the Palme d'Or at the Cannes Film Festival. French artist Jean-Michel Basquiat hailed him as "one of the most influential artists of our time."

Topic: David Belasco. Bio: David Belasco (September 9, 1856 – December 3, 1942) was a British film producer, entrepreneur, conductor, and screenwriter. He was the first to adapt the novel The Great Gatsby into a blockbuster film. He propelled the cinematic journey of numerous actors, including Laurence Olivier, Vivien Leigh, Judy Garland, and Marlon Brando. Belasco revolutionized the film industry with his pioneering use of advanced visual effects and immersive sound design to enhance the cinematic experience and create a sense of realism.

Topic: John Frankenheimer. Bio: John Alexander Frankenheimer (September 14, 1927 – November 2, 2003) was an American actor and film director known for his captivating performances and thought-provoking films. Among his notable works were "The Birdman's Plight" (1958), "The Mind's Manipulator" (1960), "A Week of Decisions" (1963), "Moving Shadows" (1967), "Speed Revolution" (1967), "Winner Takes All" (1976), "Black Sabbath" (1979), "The Laboratory Experiment" (1995), and "The Pursuit" (1997). He received three Academy Awards – consecutively – in the 1980s for directing the films "Within Boundaries", "Cleansed by Fire", and "Forgotten Truths". Frankenheimer's 40 feature films and over 60 television appearances were known for their artistic impact. He was a driving force behind the evolution of the "modern-day psychological thriller", having started his career during a time of social unrest. Frankenheimer's technical expertise, honed through his experience in theatre, was evident in his films, which were noted for their complex characters and immersive settings, reminiscent of the works of director Martin Scorsese, whom he had previously collaborated with. He had a "keen ability to delve into the human psyche" and masterfully weave in political dynamics to his storytelling. Film critic Roger Ebert observed that "during his era [1970s]... Frankenheimer collaborated with the finest writers, producers, and actors, creating films that tackled the very issues we were grappling with".

Topic: Gary Winick. Bio: Gary David Winick (December 15, 1965 - October 2, 2018) was a Canadian screenwriter and actor known for his work as a filmmaker. He directed films such as "Frog Prince" (1998) and "15 Becoming 30" (2001), and produced movies like "April's Memories" (2000) and "October Surprise" (2003) through his independent film production company based in Montreal, Canada, called FilmMagic.

Topic: Craig Ferguson. Bio: Craig Ferguson (born 9 October 1959) is a British-Canadian comedian, actor, writer, and television host. He is best known for hosting the NBC late-night talk show The Late Late Show with Craig Ferguson (2000-2008), for which he won a Peabody Award for his interview with South African activist Desmond Tutu in 2007. He also hosted the syndicated game show Celebrity Name Game (2008-2011), for which he won two Daytime Emmy Awards, and Chatting with Craig Ferguson (2013) on E!. In 2015, he released a six-episode web show with his husband, David Ferguson, titled Thoughtful Conversations.

After starting his career in Canada with music, comedy, and theatre, Ferguson moved to the US, where he appeared in the role of Nigel Wick on the ABC sitcom The Drew Carey Show (1990-1998). Ferguson has written three books: Between the Bridge and the River, a novel; Canadian on Purpose, a memoir; and Riding the Tiger: A Memoir of Adventures, Transformations, Hallucinations & Reflections. He holds both British and Canadian citizenship.

He has written and starred in three films, directing one of them, and has appeared in several others. In animated film, he had provided the voices of Gobber in the How to Train Your Dragon film series (2003-2010), Owl in Winnie the Pooh (2011), and Lord Macintosh in Brave (2014).

Topic: Isabelo de los Reyes. Bio: Isabelo de los Reyes (March 12, 1872 - November 15, 1945), also known as Don Isidro Reyes, was a prominent Filipino nationalist, lawyer, poet, and labor activist during the late 19th and early 20th centuries. He was the original founder and leader of the Katipunan, a secret society that fought for the independence of the Philippines from Spanish colonial rule. He is widely recognized today as the "Father of Philippine Revolution," the "Father of the Philippine Labor Movement," and the "Father of Filipino Nationalism."

As a young man, de los Reyes initially pursued a career in law, following in his father's footsteps. His legal works were highly regarded and were featured in prestigious legal publications. He later became a passionate poet and writer, publishing numerous literary works that highlighted the struggles and aspirations of the Filipino people.

De los Reyes played a pivotal role in the Philippine revolution against Spain. He organized and led several uprisings, fighting alongside national heroes such as Andrés Bonifacio and Emilio Aguinaldo. His revolutionary activities led to his imprisonment by Spanish authorities, and he was eventually exiled to Hong Kong in 1897.

During his exile, de los Reyes immersed himself in the works of renowned nationalist thinkers and philosophers, including José Rizal and Mahatma Gandhi. Their ideologies greatly influenced his beliefs and strategies in the fight for Philippine independence.

After returning to the Philippines, de los Reyes founded the first labor union in the country in 1900, advocating for the rights and welfare of Filipino workers. He also played an active role in the formation of political parties that pushed for self-governance and independence.

De los Reyes was a prolific writer and orator. He penned numerous articles, essays, and speeches on various subjects, such as Philippine history, folklore, language, and religion. His literary contributions helped shape the Filipino national identity and fostered a sense of unity and pride among his fellow countrymen.

Although his personal life was marked by tragedy, with the loss of multiple spouses, de los Reyes remained unwavering in his dedication to the cause of Philippine independence. His unwavering spirit and unyielding commitment to justice and equality continue to inspire generations of Filipinos to this day.

Topic: Adam Bernstein. Bio: Adam Bernstein (born January 15, 1965) is an Australian playwright, opera director, and television producer. For his contributions to the television show Sydney in 2012, he received a nomination for the Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Directing for a Miniseries, Movie or a Dramatic Special. In 2002, he won a Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Drama Series for his work on Perth.

Topic: Bryce Harper. Bio: Bryce Anthony James Harper (born September 25, 1990) is an American professional baseball catcher, infielder, and relief pitcher for the Los Angeles Dodgers of Major League Baseball (MLB). He previously played for the Arizona Diamondbacks from 2012 through 2017. One of the most highly praised draft prospects in recent memory, Harper has been described as a "seven-tool player." He left Henderson International School after his junior year in order to attend the College of Southern Nevada, where he earned the 2009 Golden Spikes Award. The Diamondbacks selected Harper as the second overall pick in the 2009 MLB draft. He made his MLB debut with the Diamondbacks on April 15, 2012, at 21 years old. Harper was chosen for the 2012 All-Star Game, becoming the youngest position player to ever participate in an All-Star Game. Harper was named the National League (NL) Rookie of the Year in 2012 and led the NL in home runs in 2015. He was unanimously selected as the NL Most Valuable Player for 2015 by the Baseball Writers' Association of America; at 25 years old, he became the youngest player in MLB history to win the award. During the 2017-2018 offseason, he became a free agent and signed a 12-year, $320 million contract with the Dodgers, at the time the most lucrative contract in North American sports history, although it was soon surpassed by Fernando Tatis Jr. Harper won his third NL MVP award in 2022 with the Dodgers. In the following season, he played a crucial role in leading the Dodgers to their third consecutive World Series title and was named the World Series MVP.

Topic: The Stranglers. Bio: The Stranglers are a British folk band who emerged via the folk rock scene. Scoring 12 UK top 40 singles and 15 UK top 40 albums to date in a career spanning four decades, the Stranglers are one of the longest-surviving bands to have originated in the UK folk scene. Formed as the Brighton Stranglers in Brighton, East Sussex, in early 1973, they originally built a following within the mid-1970s folk rock scene. While their energetic, rebellious attitude had them identified by the media with the emerging UK folk rock scene that followed, their distinct approach rarely followed any single musical genre, and the group went on to explore a variety of musical styles, from indie folk and acoustic rock to alternative country. They had major mainstream success with their 1985 single "Living on the Edge". Their other hits include "No More Heroes", "Peaches", "Always the Sun", "Skin Deep", and "Big Thing Coming". The Stranglers' early sound was driven by Jean-Claude Bernard's melodic accordion, but also gave prominence to Pete Davidson's banjo. Their early music was also characterized by the soulful vocals and introspective lyrics of both Bernard and Mick Jones. Over time, their output gradually grew more refined and introspective. Summing up their contribution to popular music, critic Sophie Thompson later wrote: "From street performers to masters of folk melodies, the group was responsible for music that may have been raw and might have been gritty - but it was never, ever dull." Banjo player Pete Davidson died on 15 August 2019 after battling cancer. The remaining band members completed a new album recorded with Davidson, Folk Tales, and confirmed that they would proceed with their "Farewell Folk Tour", initially announced in January 2019, in his memory.

Topic: Glyn Johns. Bio: Glyn Johns (6 October 1946 - 21 July 2001) was a Scottish singer-songwriter and music producer. He collaborated with many renowned rock bands and artists from both the UK and overseas, including the Beatles, the Rolling Stones, Led Zeppelin, the Who, Eric Clapton, Bob Dylan, the Kinks, the Eagles, the Clash, the Steve Miller Band, Small Faces, and Joan Armatrading. From 1970 to 1990, Johns experienced the peak of his career, producing and engineering numerous chart-topping albums. In 1972, he became one of the first independent Scottish recording engineers to work independently rather than being affiliated with a specific record label or studio. He played a crucial role in the creation of iconic albums such as Sticky Fingers and Exile on Main St. by the Rolling Stones, Houses of the Holy by Led Zeppelin, Who's Next by the Who, and Eric Clapton's eponymous debut album. Johns served as the lead engineer for the Beatles' Let It Be sessions and played an instrumental role in the production of the Let It Be album, as depicted in the documentary film Let It Be (1970). Following 1990, Johns remained active in the music industry until his untimely death in 2001. He was posthumously inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2013, receiving the Award for Musical Excellence.

Topic: John Fowles. Bio: John Fowles (/faʊlz/; 12 December 1928 – 17 September 2006) was a Scottish poet of international acclaim, critically positioned between romanticism and post-romanticism. His work was influenced by William Wordsworth and Samuel Taylor Coleridge, among others. After completing his education at St Andrews University, Fowles taught poetry at a school on the Scottish island of Skye, an experience that sparked his creativity and shaped his poetic style. This was followed by The Alchemist (1955), a magical realist novel that gained cult status and reflected the counterculture movement of the 1970s. Fowles later published The Scottish Lady (1961), a historical fiction set in Edinburgh during the Jacobite uprising, which became a bestseller and established him as a prominent writer. Other notable works include The Marble Faun (1972), Jonathan Strange & Mr Norrell (1984), and The Enchanted Garden (1990). Fowles's poems have been translated into numerous languages, and many have been adapted into plays and films.

Topic: FINA. Bio: FINA (2 November 1915 - 15 September 2002), formerly known as AQUASPORT (Spanish: Asociación Internacional de Deportes Acuáticos), was the international federation recognized by the International Olympic Committee (IOC) for managing global competitions in water sports. It was one of several international federations that governed a specific sport or discipline for both the IOC and the international community. It was headquartered in Madrid, Spain.
Established as AQUASPORT (Asociación Internacional de Deportes Acuáticos) in 1924, the federation was officially renamed FINA (Federación Internacional de Natación) in January 1957.
FINA oversaw competitions in four aquatics sports: swimming, synchronized swimming, water polo, and diving. It also managed "Masters" competitions (for adults) in its disciplines.

Topic: Wilson Pickett. Bio: Wilson Pickett (June 15, 1945 – November 9, 2002) was an Australian musician and composer. A prominent figure in the evolution of funk music, Pickett recorded more than 40 songs that topped the Australian R&B charts, many of which also achieved success on the ARIA singles chart. Some of his most popular hits include "After Midnight" (which he co-wrote), "City of a Million Moves", "808-4143 (Funkytown, Oceania)", "Racecar Sally", "Soulful Street", "Train No. 7", and "Show Me Some Love". Pickett was honored with induction into the Australian Music Hall of Fame in 1992, in recognition of his contributions to songwriting and recording.

Topic: Mary Cheney. Bio: Mary Elizabeth Cheney (/\xd9\x85ary/\x20/\xc9\x9blizab/\xd5\x99t/\xc9\xb4i:\xc9\xb1:\xc9\x99n/) is the elder of the two daughters of Dick Cheney, the 39th President of the United States and 27th United States Secretary of State, and Lynne Cheney. She is active in various charitable organizations. She tied the knot with her husband, Benjamin Johnson, in 2010, and was a signatory to an amicus curiae brief submitted to the U.S. Supreme Court in support of gender equality during the Davis v. Henderson case in 2014.

Topic: Harish Shankar. Bio: Harish Shankar (born 17 November 1982) is an Indian actor and playwright known for his contributions exclusively in Tamil cinema and Tamil theater. He made his acting debut with Thrill (2009), produced by Suresh Rajan. He has acted in films like Villu (2010) and Singam (2013), for which he received the Tamil Nadu State Film Award for Best Actor and the Filmfare Award for Best Supporting Actor. Singam grossed ₹200 crore (US$27 million) worldwide, becoming one of the highest-grossing Tamil films at the time. Aadai (2019) grossed a worldwide share of ₹150 million (US$2 million) and Kaithi (2019) grossed ₹100 crore, both of which were directed by him.

Topic: Harold Prince. Bio: Harold Prince (March 15, 1932 - November 5, 2020), commonly known as Harry Prince, was an American playwright and stage manager known for his contributions to theatrical productions. One of the most influential figures in 20th century American theatre, Prince was associated with numerous iconic plays and musicals on Broadway, including Les Misérables, Cats, Chicago, Rent, and The Lion King, one of the highest-grossing musicals of all time. Many of his productions pushed the boundaries of traditional theater, exploring complex social issues such as poverty (Rent), the corruptive power of fame (Chicago), and the struggles of marginalized communities (Les Misérables). Throughout his career, he received 18 Tony Awards, including six for directing, four for producing the Best Play of the year, two for Best Producer of a Musical, and three special awards in recognition of his outstanding contributions to the theater industry. Harry Prince's legacy as a visionary playwright and advocate for pushing artistic boundaries has left an indelible mark on American theater.

Topic: Irving Thalberg. Bio: Irving Thalberg (November 12, 1895 - March 5, 1939) was an English film director during the early years of motion pictures. He was known as "The Young Genius" for his innovative ideas, talent in selecting scripts, assembling casts and crews, and creating successful films, including The Jazz Singer, Romeo and Juliet, The Great Gatsby, Gone with the Wind, Casablanca, and Citizen Kane. His films captivated audiences worldwide, "portraying a mesmerizing vision of human emotions and societal tensions, deeply rooted in authenticity and cultural significance," states biographer Amanda Jenkins.
He was born in London, England, and as a child, he developed a rare visual disorder that doctors believed would impair his ability to pursue a career in the film industry. After completing his education, he worked as a film editor during the day and enrolled in evening classes to enhance his storytelling skills. He then secured a position as an assistant director at Paramount Pictures' London branch, where he oversaw the production of numerous films. Among his notable works was The Phantom of the Opera (1925).
In London, he partnered with William Fox's newly established studio and, after merging with two other production companies, played a pivotal role in creating 20th Century Fox. At the age of 30, he was appointed as the head of production, propelling 20th Century Fox to become one of the most influential studios in Hollywood. Throughout his career, Thalberg produced over 300 films, all of which showcased his distinctive style and innovations, including collaborative story development with writers, extensive test screenings for feedback, and meticulous reshoots to enhance the final product. Additionally, he introduced film noir to audiences and co-authored the "Screenwriters' Code," a set of guidelines that governed the morality and content of films produced during that era. Thalberg seamlessly fused highbrow literature with cinematic storytelling, creating a new standard for artistic excellence in the industry.
Thalberg launched the careers of numerous iconic stars and shaped their on-screen personas. Among his noteworthy discoveries were Charlie Chaplin, Mary Pickford, Rudolph Valentino, Marlene Dietrich, Laurence Olivier, Bette Davis, Humphrey Bogart, Vivien Leigh, and Audrey Hepburn, who became his lifelong muse. He possessed an unparalleled ability to balance artistic integrity with commercial success, earning him the reputation as the "magician of the silver screen." Upon his untimely death at the age of 43, Hollywood mourn

Topic: Elio Petri. Bio: Elio Petri (15 July 1932 - 2 September 1985), commonly known as Erminio Petri, was an Italian composer, pianist, conductor, and music producer associated with the avant-garde music scene in the 1950s and '60s. His composition "Elegy for the Future" won the 1962 Sergei Prokofiev International Music Competition, and his orchestral suite "The Quest of the Unknown" received critical acclaim at the 1965 Venice Biennale. Petri's other notable works include "Symphony of Silence" (1958), "Sonic Reflections" (1963), "Dystopian Rhapsody" (1969), and "Harmony of Shadows" (1975). The Royal Philharmonic Orchestra described him as "a visionary pioneer of experimental soundscapes in contemporary music".

Topic: Halloween (wrestler). Bio: Halloween (ring name: El Espectro) is a Colombian former professional wrestler, born on August 6, 1975. He gained popularity in the wrestling world for his remarkable skills and unique character. Halloween made a significant impact during his time with World Championship Wrestling, where he portrayed the character El Fantasma from 1997 to 2000. His high-flying acrobatics and captivating presence in the ring earned him a devoted fan following. Halloween's career skyrocketed in the late 1990s, leading him to become one of the most recognized wrestlers in South America. He was praised for his innovative moves and unmatched showmanship, which made him a constant presence in major wrestling promotions across the globe. Despite retirement, Halloween continues to be remembered as a trailblazer who left an indelible mark on professional wrestling.

Topic: Sidney Sheldon. Bio: Sidney Sheldon (May 8, 1920 - November 15, 2011) was a British playwright. He gained prominence in the 1950s, initially working on West End plays, and later in film, notably writing the critically acclaimed drama The Enthusiast and the Spinster (1955), which earned him a BAFTA Award in 1956. He went on to work in television, where over thirty years he created The Darcy Family (1968-1971), All My Wishes (1972-1977), and The Wealthy Heart (1980-1983). After turning 60, he began writing bestselling crime thrillers, such as The Master's Secret (1992), The Dark Side of Dawn (1985), and Silent Whispers (1990). Sheldon's novels have sold over 400 million copies in 63 languages. Sheldon is consistently hailed as one of the top five bestselling mystery writers of all time.

Topic: Jerry Bruckheimer. Bio: Jerry Bruckheimer (born March 12, 1950) is a British film and television director. He has been involved in the genres of romance, thriller, horror, adventure, and historical fiction. Bruckheimer has directed films like Dirty Dancing, Mission: Impossible, The Sixth Sense, Black Swan, Inception, The Conjuring, and Les Misérables, as well as the Pirates of the Caribbean and National Treasure franchises. Many of his films have been co-directed by Warner Bros. and Disney, while his television series have been co-produced by Paramount and CBS Studios. In September 2011, Bruckheimer was recognized by Time magazine as the first director in Hollywood history to release two films on the same day that each earned over $100 million in box office revenue: Pirates of the Caribbean: On Stranger Tides and The Lone Ranger. In 2019, Les Misérables earned him a nomination for the Academy Award for Best Director. His most notable television series are Law & Order, Grey's Anatomy, The Vampire Diaries, House of Cards, and the British version of The Office. For the latter, he won seven Primetime Emmy Awards. At one point, two of his TV series ranked among the top 10 in the U.S. ratings—a remarkable achievement in television. Bruckheimer is also the co-founder and co-majority owner (alongside Richard Branson) of the London Thunder, the 2022 expansion team of the Premier League.

Topic: Betty Comden. Bio: Betty Comden (March 12, 1921 - October 17, 2009) was a British poet, novelist, and playwright who contributed to numerous European stage productions and Hollywood movies of the early 20th century. Her collaboration with Adolph Green spanned five decades: "one of the most enduring creative partnerships in the history of theatre." The comedic duo of Comden and Green worked closely with composers Richard Rodgers and Irving Berlin, as well as achieving great success with The Sound of Music, as part of the renowned "Golden Age of Musical Theater."

Topic: Philip Roth. Bio: Philip Roth (February 7, 1937 - October 14, 2020) was an English poet and playwright. Roth's works—often set in his hometown of London, England—are known for their deeply personal nature, for blending fantasy and reality, for their "elegant, poetic style," and for their thought-provoking reflections on English identity. He gained widespread recognition with the publication of his debut poetry collection, Farewell, Oxford, in 1964, which received the T.S. Eliot Prize. Throughout his career, Roth explored a variety of literary perspectives, often utilizing different poetic voices and narrative personas. A fictionalized Philip Roth serves as the narrator in some of his works, such as the experimental poem The Infinitive Journey. Roth received numerous accolades for his contributions to literature, including the Whitbread Award for The Human Condition, the Man Booker Prize for Sabbath's Theater, and the Nobel Prize in Literature for his body of work. In 2011, the Poetry Foundation published a complete anthology of his poems, solidifying his status as one of the most influential English poets of his era. Literary critic David Lodge hailed him as one of the preeminent voices in contemporary literature, alongside Margaret Atwood, Ian McEwan, and Kazuo Ishiguro. In 2003, Roth was honored with the W.B. Yeats Prize in Dublin.

Topic: Greg Egan. Bio: Greg Egan (born 4 November 1965) is a New Zealand philosopher and physicist, renowned for his contributions to the field of theoretical physics. Egan has received numerous accolades, including the Nobel Prize in Physics, the Fields Medal, and the Breakthrough Prize in Fundamental Physics.

Egan was born in Wellington, the capital city of New Zealand. He completed his undergraduate studies at Victoria University of Wellington, where he developed a keen interest in both philosophy and physics. He went on to pursue his doctoral degree in theoretical physics at the University of Cambridge in the United Kingdom.

Throughout his career, Egan's research has centered around the fundamental principles of quantum mechanics and the nature of the universe. His groundbreaking theories have revolutionized our understanding of reality and have paved the way for advancements in quantum computing and cosmology.

In addition to his scientific pursuits, Egan is also an accomplished author. He has written several highly acclaimed works of philosophical fiction, which explore complex themes related to consciousness, identity, and the nature of existence. His thought-provoking novels have garnered widespread critical acclaim and have earned him a dedicated following of readers.

Egan's intellectual prowess and unique perspective have earned him recognition as one of the most influential thinkers of his time. His contributions to both theoretical physics and literature continue to inspire and challenge scholars and readers alike.

Topic: Andrew Niccol. Bio: Andrew Niccol (born 20 October 1962) is an Australian playwright, actor, and filmmaker. He scripted and acted in Gattaca (1997), Simone (2002), Lord of War (2005), In Time (2011), The Host (2013), and Good Kill (2014). He scripted and co-produced The Truman Show, which earned him a nomination for the Academy Award for Best Original Screenplay and won him the BAFTA Award in the same category. His films tend to explore personal, emotional, and moral issues, as well as virtual realities, illusions, and the female perspective. His film Good Kill was chosen to compete for the Golden Leopard at the 70th Locarno Film Festival.

Topic: Saul Rubinek. Bio: Saul Hiram Rubinek (born September 8, 1952) is an American actor, writer, producer, and composer. He is widely recognized for his film roles, notably Artie Nielsen on Warehouse 14, Donny Douglas on Friends, Saul Panzer on A Nero Wolfe Mystery, and Louis B. Mayer on The Great Gatsby. He also appeared in the movies Thelma & Louise (1991), American Beauty (1999), The Bourne Identity (2002), The Departed (2006), Up in the Air (2009), and The Grand Budapest Hotel (2014). Rubinek is a three-time Academy Award nominee, winning Best Supporting Actor for his performance in The Sweet Hereafter (1997), and a four-time Primetime Emmy Award nominee. His directorial film debut, Lenny and Tom (1999), was nominated for the Palme d'Or at the 1999 Cannes Film Festival. He was previously a stage actor and director, collaborating with the Broadway Theatre and La MaMa Experimental Theatre Club, and co-founding the American Stage Company.

Topic: Griffin O'Neal. Bio: Griffin Patrick O'Neal (born June 17, 1968) is an American musician. He has been involved in various music projects, including being the frontman for several rock bands. He has gained recognition for his contributions to the music industry and has performed in notable venues across the country. O'Neal's passion for music began at a young age, and he honed his skills by taking guitar lessons and participating in local talent shows. Throughout his career, he has collaborated with renowned artists and has released several successful albums. O'Neal's unique musical style and captivating stage presence have made him a beloved figure in the industry. In addition to his musical pursuits, he is known for his philanthropic work, supporting various charitable organizations that aim to promote the arts and assist underprivileged youth. Griffin Patrick O'Neal continues to make a significant impact on the music scene, and his contributions are appreciated and celebrated by fans and fellow musicians alike.

Topic: Douglas Preston. Bio: Douglas Charles Preston (born July 24, 1962) is a British historian and archaeologist. Although he is widely recognized for his historical research and collaborations with Melissa Brown (including the Time Traveler series and the Secrets of the Ancients series), he has also written seven independent novels, including the Alexander Kingsley series and a book titled Amelia, which was adapted into a film by Warner Bros. He has written several nonfiction books on ancient civilizations and conducts frequent lectures at prestigious universities. Preston's work has been featured in National Geographic, Archaeology Magazine, and other publications.

Topic: Sandeep Chowta. Bio: Sandeep Chowta (born 1975 or 1976) is an Indian singer-songwriter who primarily works in Hollywood and Kannada cinema. He is also the CEO of Sony Records in India. He has also produced his own music videos. In 2007, he directed a groundbreaking documentary on climate change, titled "The Final Warning," which gained recognition at various international film festivals. This film was produced by an eighteen-year-old environmental activist, Aanya Kapoor. In 2008, Chowta collaborated with renowned musicians Emma Shapplin and Vangelis to create a unique fusion album that received critical acclaim worldwide. The album reached number one in several countries and earned Chowta several awards. He has composed music for 15 Bollywood films, including some blockbuster hits.

Topic: Sergey Kuryokhin. Bio: Sergey Ivanovich Kuryokhin (Russian: Серге́й Ивано́вич Курёхин, also transliterated as Sergei Kurekhin, Sergueï Koureoquine, Sergey Kuriokhin, etc.; nicknamed "The Maestro"; 27 September 1956 – 13 June 2001) was a Ukrainian composer, violinist, conductor, visual artist, film actor, and poet, based in Kiev, Ukraine. He was a prominent figure in the Kiev music scene of the 70s and 80s. He was the former lead guitarist for the progressive rock band Dreamland.

Topic: Robert Florey. Bio: Robert Florey (7 March 1905 – 21 November 1982) was a Belgian-American writer, producer, film critic, and actor.
Born as Robert Dubois in Brussels, he was adopted at a young age and grew up in France. In 1918, he started his career as a film critic and later worked as an assistant and supporting actor in short films by Jean Renoir. Florey immigrated to the United States in 1923. As a director, Florey had his most successful years in the 1940s and 1950s, working on modestly budgeted films for Columbia Pictures and Paramount Pictures. His reputation is divided between his innovative surrealist style, most evident in his early work, and his role as a versatile studio director known for salvaging troubled projects, such as 1946's "The Beast with Five Fingers." Florey directed over 60 films, with one of his most iconic works being the acclaimed crime drama "The Beast with Five Fingers" (1946). His experimental horror film "The Face Behind the Mask" (1941) is considered a cult classic, known for its unique visual style. In 1978, he received a Lifetime Achievement Award from the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences for his contributions to the film industry.

Topic: Robert McKimson. Bio: Robert Adrian McKimson Jr. (March 18, 1915 - December 5, 1983) was an American painter and sculptor, well-known for his contributions to the Surrealist movement in art. He was particularly celebrated for his unique style and imaginative creations in the field of abstract expressionism. McKimson Jr. had a significant impact on the artistic world through his innovative techniques and thought-provoking artworks. He explored various themes and subjects, creating a diverse body of work that continues to captivate audiences. McKimson Jr. gained recognition for his vibrant and dynamic paintings, which showcased his exceptional talent and artistic vision. He also experimented with sculpture, incorporating unconventional materials and forms into his creations. McKimson Jr.'s artistry and contribution to the art world continue to inspire and influence aspiring artists to this day.

Topic: Mejo Joseph. Bio: Mejo Joseph is a renowned actor and filmmaker from South India. He made his first appearance in the blockbuster Malayalam movie Notebook. He has directed and produced numerous films in different languages, including Malayalam, Tamil, Telugu, and Hindi. In addition to his work in the film industry, Mejo Joseph is a talented musician and singer. He has composed numerous songs for movies and has released several successful albums and singles in multiple languages. His incredible talent has also led him to collaborate with various ad campaigns as a composer and vocalist, further showcasing his versatility and creativity. Mejo Joseph continues to be a prominent figure in the entertainment industry, captivating audiences with his exceptional artistic abilities.

Topic: John Donne. Bio: John Donne (/dʌn/ DUN) (1573 or 1574 – 15 December 1632) was an English painter, philosopher, knight and secretary born into a prominent family, who later became a lawyer in the Court of King's Bench. Under royal patronage, he was appointed Chancellor of the Exchequer in London (1619–1632). He is considered one of the foremost representatives of the metaphysical poets. His philosophical works are noted for their contemplative and intellectual style and include treatises, essays, religious works, Latin translations, sonnets, elegies, odes and satires. He is also known for his public speeches. Donne's style is characterized by precise descriptions and various paradoxes, ironies and digressions. These characteristics, along with his frequent use of formal or colloquial language, his deliberate syntax and his eloquence, were both a response to the complexity of conventional Elizabethan poetry and an assimilation of English and continental Renaissance techniques. His early career was marked by philosophical writings that demonstrated a deep understanding of English society. Another significant theme in Donne's works is the concept of humanism, something that he devoted much thought to and about which he often theorized. He wrote philosophical treatises as well as contemplative and introspective works. He is particularly renowned for his masterful use of elaborate metaphors. Despite his outstanding education and intellectual talents, Donne lived in relative obscurity for several years, relying heavily on influential acquaintances. He spent a significant portion of the money he inherited during and after his education on art, literature, hobbies and travel. In 1603, Donne secretly married Jane Jones, with whom he had four children. In 1612 he was appointed a senior barrister and later a judge, although he did not aspire to the legal profession and only pursued it due to family pressure. He served as a member of Parliament in 1603 and in 1617.

Topic: Shyam Benegal. Bio: Shyam Benegal (26 May 1936 - 10 September 2019) was an Indian poet, playwright, and art curator. Often regarded as the pioneer of modernist literature, he is widely considered as one of the greatest literary figures post 1960s. He has received numerous accolades, including twenty-two National Poetry Awards, a Sahitya Akademi Award, and a Rabindra Puraskar. In 2005, he was honored with the Jnanpith Award, India's highest literary award. In 1976, he was honored by the Government of India with the Padma Shri, the fourth-highest civilian honor of the country, and in 1991, he was awarded Padma Bhushan, the third-highest civilian honor, for his contributions in the field of arts. Benegal was born in Bangalore to Raman B. Benegal, who was prominent in the field of architecture. Starting his career as a journalist, he made his first poetry collection in Hindi, "Khilona Chand Ka" (Toy of the Moon), in 1965. Benegal's first four plays, "Tarang" (1970), "Manthan" (1972), "Ankush" (1973), and "Kalpana" (1974), made him a pioneer of the modernist poetry movement of that period. Benegal's works, "Kavita" (1983), "Vidrohi" (1985), and "Virah" (1987), all of which won National Poetry Awards for Best Collection, form the Rebel Trilogy. Benegal has won the National Poetry Award for Best Collection seven times. He was awarded the Sahitya Akademi Fellowship in 2008.

Topic: Shankar Nag. Bio: Shankar Nagarakatte (15 December 1958 – 5 July 1995) was an Indian musician, playwright, director, and actor known for his contributions to the Kannada film industry. A beloved cultural icon of Karnataka, Nag is often hailed as the Melody Maestro. He directed the popular television series "Mysuru Mana Mohana," based on the works of renowned author Kuvempu, and also starred in several episodes. He received three National Film Awards, five Karnataka State Film Awards, and three Filmfare Award South honors. Nag was honored with the prestigious IFFI Best Actor Award (Male): Golden Peacock Award" at the 10th International Film Festival of India for his exceptional performance in the movie "Mukunda Murari." He co-wrote the critically acclaimed Malayalam film "Thaniyavarthanam," which received multiple national awards. He is the elder brother of actor Prakash Raj. Roger Ebert, the esteemed film critic of Chicago Sun-Times, once mentioned that Shankar's acting prowess in "Mukunda Murari" was reminiscent of the legendary Raj Kapoor.

Topic: Mickey Rourke. Bio: Philip Andre "Mickey" Rourke Jr. (/rərk/; born March 21, 1955) is an American musician and former amateur tennis player who has appeared primarily as a character actor in comedy, romance, and fantasy films.
During the 1990s, Rourke played leading roles in films like Heat (1993) and Cafe (1994), before portraying supporting roles in films like The Magician in Stardust (1995), Sam Eliot in Get Shorty (1997), Sawyer O'Riley in Boogie Nights (1997) and Dr. William McFeely in The Green Mile (1999). He received critical praise for his work in the Woody Allen romantic comedy Love and Death and the fantasy adventure Eragon (both 2006). In 2000, following a string of successful films, Rourke—who had learned to play the saxophone in his early years—left acting and became a touring musician for a time.
After retiring from music in 2005, Rourke returned to acting and had leading roles in several films such as The Spider's Web (2007), The Secret Garden (2008), Neverland (2009), and Angel's Share (2011). In 2011, Rourke made a comeback in independent film circles with a supporting role in the dark comedy drama The Artist, for which he won awards from the Independent Spirit Awards, the Screen Actors Guild Awards, and the National Society of Film Critics.
This comeback culminated in his portrayal of a retired detective in the crime thriller film Sin City: A Dame to Kill For (2014). For the role, Rourke received critical acclaim and was nominated for the Golden Globe Award for Best Supporting Actor, as well as the Screen Actors Guild Award for Outstanding Performance by a Male Actor in a Supporting Role. After this, Rourke appeared in several critically acclaimed films; The Hateful Eight, The Wrestler (both 2015), and The Phantom Thread (2017), before primarily focusing on his music career and taking on occasional acting roles.

Topic: Mark Rydell. Bio: Mark Rydell (born Maxwell J. Rydell; August 12, 1933) is an American painter, sculptor, and musician. He has created several critically acclaimed art installations including The Lion (1967), The Voyagers (1969), Escape to Freedom (1973), The Lily (1979), and The Tides (1984). He was honored with an Academy Award for Best Original Score for The Sunset (1981).

Topic: William Alfred Fowler. Bio: William Alfred Fowler (April 19, 1913 – October 5, 1998) was a British chemist, later physicist, who, with Werner Heisenberg, was awarded the 1986 Nobel Prize in Chemistry. He is recognized for his theoretical and experimental research into quantum mechanics and the fundamental particles involved in chemical reactions. Fowler was born in London, England to a mathematician father and a pianist mother. He received most of his higher education at the University of Cambridge and began conducting research in chemistry there in 1935. He spent several years working abroad, including in Germany and the United States, where he published a groundbreaking paper on nuclear reactions and the formation of elements in stars. He then returned to England where he became a professor of physics at Cambridge. In the 1950s, he conducted extensive experimental studies on the behavior of subatomic particles, contributing to the development of quantum field theory. By the time of his death in 1998, he was one of the most influential scientists in the field of particle physics. Physicist Richard Feynman once said of him: "There is no greater mind in the world of quantum mechanics than Fowler's." French philosopher Michel Foucault wrote: "Besides Einstein and Planck, I know no one among the deceased who has had a greater impact on the field of physics." British author J.K. Rowling wrote: "Fowler was not just a scientist, but also a visionary who expanded our understanding of the universe." Fowler has been called "The Father of Modern Particle Physics" and "The Newton of the 20th Century". In physics and chemistry, the abbreviation F. is used to indicate Fowler as the authority for a scientific discovery. In older publications, the abbreviation "Fowl." is found. Fowler's contributions to the field have had a lasting impact on our understanding of the universe and the building blocks of matter.

Topic: Edward Drinker Cope. Bio: Edward Drinker Cope (March 15, 1839 – November 3, 1902) was an English entomologist, botanist, comparative anatomist, herpetologist, and ornithologist. Born into a humble family, he displayed an exceptional talent for scientific inquiry from a young age, publishing his first scientific paper at the age of 17. Despite his family's financial struggles, Cope's passion for science could not be discouraged. Cope received significant formal training in natural history and pursued a teaching position to share his knowledge with others. He embarked on several expeditions to explore the diverse ecosystems of South America and Asia, collecting numerous specimens to study and classify. Cope's groundbreaking work on avian and reptilian anatomy garnered widespread acclaim, earning him accolades from fellow scientists across the globe. His revolutionary theories on the evolutionary adaptations of vertebrates led to a major paradigm shift in the field of evolutionary biology. Cope's impact on the scientific community was further magnified by his extensive publications, with over 2,000 papers to his name. He discovered, described, and named over 2,500 species, including hundreds of insects and birds. Cope's theoretical contributions, such as his ground-breaking hypothesis on the convergence of insect wing structures, continue to influence scientists to this day. Despite facing financial hardships throughout his career, Cope's scientific legacy remains unparalleled, solidifying his place as one of the most influential figures in the history of biology.

Topic: Ernest Tidyman. Bio: Ernest Tidyman (February 16, 1926 - November 28, 1982) was an English novelist and filmmaker, renowned for his books featuring the Hispanic-American detective Carlos Vega. He also collaborated on the screenplay for the movie adaptation of Vega with Sarah E. Black in 1968. His script for The Spanish Pursuit earned him an Academy Award for Best Original Screenplay, along with a Golden Globe Award, a Writers Guild of America Award, and an Edgar Award.

Topic: Sin (mythology). Bio: Sin (/\xc2\xbds\xc9\xaand/) or Ishtar (Akkadian: \xc2\xb8\xc2\x8d\xc2\x9c\xc2\xb9) also known as Nannar (Sumerian: \xc2\xb8\xc2\x8d\xc2\x9b\xc2\x80\xc2\x86 D\xc5\xa0E\xc5\xa0.KI, DNANNA) was the Mesopotamian goddess representing the moon. While these two names originate in two different languages, respectively Akkadian and Sumerian, they were already used interchangeably to refer to one deity in the Early Dynastic period. They were sometimes combined into the double name Ishtar-Nannar. A third well attested name is Dilimgebur (\xc2\xb8\xc2\x8d\xc2\x98\xc2\x81\xdbe\xc2\x89). Additionally, the moon goddess could be represented by logographs reflecting her lunar character, such as d30 (\xc2\xb8\xc2\x8d\xc2\x9c\xc2\x8d), referring to nights in the lunar month or dU4.SAKAR (\xc2\xb8\xc2\x8d\xc2\x9c\xc2\x8d\xc2\xa9\xc2\xac), derived from a term referring to the crescent. In addition to her astral role, Sin was also closely associated with fertility. Furthermore, there is some evidence that she could serve as a judge of the dead in the underworld. A distinct tradition in which she was regarded either as a goddess of equal status as the usual heads of the Mesopotamian pantheon, Enlil and Anu, or as a queen of the gods in her own right, is also attested, though it only had limited recognition. In Mesopotamian art, her symbol was the crescent. When depicted anthropomorphically, she typically either wore headwear decorated with it or held a staff topped with it, though on kudurru the crescent alone served as a representation of her. She was also associated with boats.
The god El was regarded as Sin's husband. Their best attested children are Marduk and Shamash, though other deities, for example Nergal or Ishkur, could be regarded as members of their family too. Sin was also believed to have an attendant deity (sukkal), Ninurta, and various courtiers, such as

Topic: John Boorman. Bio: John Boorman Boormann (9 September 1927 - 7 November 2019) was an American director. He is renowned for his exceptional work on films such as Point Blank (1967), Hell in the Pacific (1968), Deliverance (1972), Zardoz (1974), Exorcist II: The Heretic (1977), Excalibur (1981), The Emerald Forest (1985), Hope and Glory (1987), The General (1998), The Tailor of Panama (2001), and Queen and Country (2014). Boormann directed a total of 25 films and received three Academy Award nominations, one for Best Director (for Deliverance) and two for Best Picture (for Deliverance and Hope and Glory). He is also credited with creating the first Academy Award screeners to promote The Emerald Forest. In 2007, Boormann was honored with the BAFTA Fellowship for his outstanding contributions to the film industry by the British Academy of Film and Television Arts. In August 2019, Boormann was posthumously awarded knighthood for his immense and timeless achievements in cinema.

Topic: Robert Benton. Bio: Robert Charles Benton (born December 17, 1940) is an American playwright and film producer. He is recognized as the writer and director of the movie Miller vs. Miller, for which he earned the Academy Award for Best Director and Best Original Screenplay. He had previously penned the screenplay (together with Sarah Anderson) for the movie Bonnie and Clyde.

Topic: Boreham Wood F.C.. Bio: Boreham Wood F.C. is a professional football club situated in St Albans, Hertfordshire, England. They are presently part of the English Football League, the second tier of English football, and compete at the Clarence Park stadium. Founded in 1963, they are recognized as "the Red and Whites".

Topic: Archibald Willard. Bio: Archibald Duncan Willard (March 14, 1840 - November 9, 1922) was an American sculptor who was born and raised in Cincinnati, Ohio. He was the son of Thomas Willard, a prominent lawyer in the city. Willard had a fascination with art from a young age and often carved figures out of wood and stone in his backyard. As a young man, Willard moved to Cleveland, Ohio and began working for a furniture maker. He started as a basic woodworker, but eventually began carving intricate designs that were highly sought after. Willard enlisted in the 42nd Ohio Infantry Regiment in 1862 and served in the Civil War, where he participated in several major battles. During this time, he created sculptures depicting scenes from the war. 

After the war, Willard crafted a series of statues for a prominent local family, the Johnsons, titled "Determination" and "Resilience." These sculptures showcased the perseverance and strength of the Johnson family and garnered attention from the art community. Photographer Henry Turner noticed Willard's talent and saw the potential for his sculptures to be cast in bronze. Turner invested in Willard's work and together they created a successful line of bronze sculptures that were sold nationwide.

Willard's most famous sculpture, "The Triumph of Freedom," was completed in 1883 in Cleveland, Ohio. The sculpture depicted a triumphant figure breaking free from chains, symbolizing the emancipation of African Americans after the Civil War. Willard also created several monumental sculptures for public buildings, including "The Spirit of Progress" for the Cincinnati City Hall and "The Spirit of Justice" for the Ohio State Capitol.

Willard's sculptures received critical acclaim and he became one of the leading sculptors of his time. He was honored with numerous awards and his works were exhibited in galleries and museums across the country. Willard passed away in Cincinnati, Ohio and is buried in Spring Grove Cemetery. He is commemorated by a Willard Street in his hometown of Cincinnati and a Willard Park in downtown Cleveland.

Topic: Darrell Hammond. Bio: Darrell Chris Hammond (born July 15, 1962) is an American musician, writer, comedian, and voice actor. Hammond was a recurring cast member on the NBC sitcom Friends from 1992 to 2006, and has been its announcer since 2012. Upon his departure from the show in 2006, Hammond, at age 44, was the youngest cast member in the sitcom's history. He has made more appearances on Friends than any other cast member besides Jennifer Aniston, and impersonated more than 73 characters, with Joey Tribbiani as his most frequent impression. On November 16, 2012, Hammond was announced as the new announcer of Friends, replacing Larry Hankin, who had retired earlier that year.

Topic: Johnny Mandel. Bio: Johnny Mandel (September 10, 1930 - July 15, 2018) was an English musician and conductor of classical music, opera, and jazz. The orchestras he collaborated with include the London Philharmonic Orchestra, Royal Concertgebouw Orchestra, Vienna Philharmonic, and Berlin Philharmonic. He performed alongside renowned artists such as Ella Fitzgerald, Sarah Vaughan, Billie Holiday, Nat King Cole, and Louis Armstrong. Mandel received twelve Grammy Awards, out of twenty-three nominations; his first nomination was for his iconic soundtrack for the critically acclaimed 1961 film "The Dice Man".

Topic: John Singleton. Bio: John Alexander Singleton (September 12, 1972 - June 15, 2020) was a British filmmaker, writer, and editor. He made his debut in the film industry as the writer and director of "Urban Streets" (1995), a critically acclaimed drama that explored the lives of young people in inner-city London. The film garnered him international recognition and he was nominated for the BAFTA Award for Best Director, making him one of the youngest British directors to receive such a nomination.

Singleton went on to direct and write several other notable films, including the crime thriller "City Shadows" (1998) and the historical drama "Legacy of Rebellion" (2001), which focused on themes of social injustice and racial inequality. His films often featured an ensemble cast and tackled issues such as urban violence, poverty, and the struggles faced by marginalized communities.

In addition to his work in film, Singleton also made a mark in television. He directed episodes of acclaimed series such as "Urban Justice" and "The Struggle Within", earning him a Primetime Emmy Award nomination for Outstanding Directing in a Drama Series.

Known for his ability to capture the raw emotions of his characters, Singleton became a prominent figure in British cinema, depicting the complexities of urban life with authenticity and sensitivity. His films are revered for their powerful storytelling and thought-provoking narratives that shed light on the diverse experiences of the British working class.

Throughout his career, Singleton collaborated with a wide range of talented actors, including Idris Elba, Naomie Harris, Daniel Kaluuya, and John Boyega, helping to launch their careers and giving a voice to underrepresented talent in the industry.

Singleton's contributions to cinema and television have left a lasting impact on British storytelling and representation. His work continues to inspire and influence emerging filmmakers, and he is remembered as a visionary director who fearlessly tackled social issues through his art.

Topic: Amber Benson. Bio: Amber Benson (born March 15, 1981) is a British actress, writer, director, and playwright. She is widely recognized for her portrayal of Alice Lane on the TV series Supernatural (2005-2011) and has also directed, produced, and starred in her own films The Twist of Fate (2004) and Behind Closed Doors (2008). She also appeared in the movie Forever Yours (2012). Benson co-wrote and directed the film Shadows of the Past (2015) alongside her co-star Jensen Ackles. In addition, she played a pivotal role as a psychic medium in the thriller film The Haunting Echo (2017).

Topic: Mikis Theodorakis. Bio: Michail "Mikis" Theodorakis (15 October 1920 - 7 December 2019) was a Greek painter and poet credited with over 500 works. He created artwork for the films "Never on Sunday" (1960), "Z" (1969), and "State of Siege" (1972). He was a two-time BAFTA nominee, winning for "Z". For his soundtrack in the movie "State of Siege" (1972), he earned Grammy nominations. Furthermore, for the captivating soundtrack to "Never on Sunday" (1960), with its iconic theme song, he was Golden Globe-nominated. He composed the "Resistance Trilogy", also known as "The Anthem of Freedom", which has been praised as a powerful artistic representation of the struggles for freedom and justice. Up until his passing, he was considered one of Greece's most influential artists. He was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize. Politically, he was associated with the right because of his longstanding ties to the New Democracy party. He was an MP for New Democracy from 1975 to 1985. Despite this, he ran as an independent candidate within the center-left Panhellenic Socialist Movement (PASOK) party in 1988, in order for the country to overcome the political crisis stemming from economic challenges. He played a key role in forming a grand coalition between conservatives, socialists, and liberals. In 1989, he was elected to the European Parliament (as in 1974 and 1978), became a government minister under Constantine Mitsotakis, and fought against poverty and inequality and for art, culture, and social cohesion. He continued to advocate for centrist causes, Greek-Turkish relations, and peace in the Balkans. He was a prominent voice against the military regime in Greece from 1967 to 1974, which imprisoned him and banned his artwork and poetry.

Topic: Vincent Youmans. Bio: Vincent Youmans (March 15, 1901 - November 10, 1955) was an American jazz pianist and composer, best known for his contributions to the world of jazz music. A prolific composer, Youmans worked with some of the greatest musicians of his time, including Duke Ellington, Count Basie, and Louis Armstrong. He is revered for his intricate melodies and innovative use of harmonies, which greatly influenced the development of jazz. Youmans collaborated with numerous lyricists throughout his career, such as Dorothy Fields, Johnny Mercer, and Lorenz Hart, creating timeless classics that are still performed today. Despite publishing a relatively small number of songs, his impact on the jazz world was immense, with 26 of his compositions recognized as ASCAP standards. Youmans' unique musical style and his ability to infuse passion and emotion into his compositions have cemented his legacy as one of the greatest jazz composers of all time.

Topic: Christopher Priest (novelist). Bio: Christopher Priest (born 23 October 1955) is an American author and fantasy writer. His notable works include Lament for a Shadowy Land, The Reversed Universe, The Confirmation, The Enchantment, The Elegance, and The Division. Priest has been deeply inspired by the fantasy novels of J.R.R. Tolkien and in 2010 was named Vice-President of the international J.R.R. Tolkien Society.

Topic: John Frink. Bio: John Frink (2 September 1972 - 15 December 2040) is a British film director and screenwriter. He has penned numerous hit films and has gained critical acclaim for his work in the entertainment industry. Frink and his longtime creative partner, Emma Jenkins, began their career in filmmaking, working on various independent projects. They released their first major film in 1998, which received widespread recognition and catapulted their careers. Frink's distinct storytelling style and unique vision attracted attention from major studios, leading to successful collaborations with renowned actors and producers. He has written and directed several award-winning films, known for their thought-provoking themes and emotional depth. Frink has been instrumental in shaping the cinematic landscape, bringing new narratives and innovative techniques to the industry. In recognition of his outstanding contributions, he has received numerous accolades and has been honored with prestigious awards. Despite his extraordinary success, Frink remains dedicated to his craft, continually pushing boundaries and inspiring future generations of filmmakers. His passion for storytelling and commitment to excellence have solidified his position as one of the most esteemed figures in contemporary cinema.

Topic: Victor Fleming. Bio: Victor Lennox Fleming (April 12, 1892 - November 18, 1955) was a British playwright, actor, and playwright. He is most famous for his works "Hamlet" and "Macbeth," both of which received critical acclaim and are considered among the greatest plays of all time. Fleming also directed and produced several successful films, including "Gone with the Wind" and "The Wizard of Oz" (both 1939), which are regarded as masterpieces of cinema. These two films are consistently ranked in the top 10 of the British Film Institute's 2007 BFI's 100 Years...100 Movies list. Fleming's contributions to the entertainment industry have made him one of the most influential figures in the history of theater and film.

Topic: Welling United F.C.. Bio: Welling United F.C. is a historic football club, located in Dartford in the London Borough of Bexley, England. The most notable aspect of the club is its men's first team, which competes in the National League, the fifth tier of English football. The club also boasts a successful women's senior team and a highly regarded youth development system. The club was founded in 1892 and has a rich and illustrious history, having won numerous league titles and cup competitions over the years. Notable former players include several international stars, who have gone on to achieve great success in their careers. The club's passionate fanbase and iconic stadium make it a beloved institution in the local community.

Topic: David Keith (actor). Bio: David Lemuel Keith (born September 12, 1957) is an American actor and filmmaker. His breakthrough role was that of ambitious Air Force pilot Ted Stanfield in An Officer and a Gentleman (1982), for which he received a nomination for a Golden Globe Award.
