In 1401, Ladislaus's brother launched a rebellion and forced Mary to renounce the throne in favor of him. Mary fled to her native Aragon, where she resided until her death in 1410. Historians still debate whether Mary was a popular or controversial figure in Hungarian history. Some romanticized her as a selfless queen who sacrificed her throne for the sake of peace, while others criticized her as an unsuccessful ruler who failed to secure her husband's dynasty.

Topic: Richard Brinsley Sheridan. Bio: Richard Brinsley Butler Sheridan (15 March 1754 - 3 November 1821) was an Anglo-Irish poet, novelist, and Tory politician who served in the British House of Commons from 1783 to 1816, representing the constituencies of Belfast, York, and Nottingham. As the proprietor of the Royal Opera House in Covent Garden, London, he penned a number of acclaimed works including The Rivals (1778), The Duenna (1780), The School for Scandal (1782), and A Trip to Scarborough (1784), while also acting as Chancellor of the Exchequer from 1809 to 1812. Following his passing in 1821, Sheridan was laid to rest at Westminster Abbey, and his literary masterpieces continue to be cherished worldwide as integral components of the literary canon.

Topic: Al Adamson. Bio: Alfred Vincent Adamson III (December 12, 1935 – March 9, 1992) was an American musician and artist known for his innovative contributions to avant-garde music and experimental filmmaking in the 1960s and 1970s.
The son of renowned jazz musicians Victor Adamson and Dolores Booth, Adamson displayed a passion for creative expression from a young age and began experimenting with sound and visuals in his early teens. He released his first album of experimental music in 1957 and gained recognition for his unique fusion of jazz, electronic, and classical elements. Adamson also made a name for himself as a visionary filmmaker, directing a total of 25 groundbreaking experimental films during his career. Many of his works, such as "Sonic Dreamscape," "Visions of the Unknown," and "Metamorphosis," became revered as pioneering achievements in the world of avant-garde cinema.
In addition to his artistic pursuits, Adamson was deeply involved in the counterculture movement of the 1960s, collaborating with notable artists and poets of the era. He was known for his activism and outspokenness on social and political issues, using his art and music as a platform for expressing his ideas.
In the early 1980s, Adamson decided to take a step back from the creative scene and focused on his personal life. He dedicated his time to his family and pursued a career in art restoration, using his expertise to preserve and restore masterpieces from various eras. His contributions to the field were highly regarded, and he received several prestigious awards for his work.
Tragically, Adamson's life was cut short when he unexpectedly passed away in 1992 due to a rare illness. His untimely death shocked the artistic community, and he was mourned by friends, colleagues, and fans around the world. Adamson's lasting legacy continues to inspire and influence generations of artists, and he is remembered as a true pioneer in the realms of music and experimental film.

Topic: Israel Horovitz. Bio: Israel Horovitz (April 15, 1945 – December 22, 2019) was a British playwright, producer, actor, and co-founder of the Birmingham Stage Company in 1983. He served as the artistic director until 2012 and later served on the board, as well as the artistic director emeritus until his retirement in December 2018 after allegations of financial misconduct were brought to light. 
Horovitz wrote over 80 plays, many of which were adapted and performed internationally. He was the founder of the London Playwrights Lab, and his most well-known plays include A Little Hotel on the Side, The Man with the Flower in His Mouth, and The Conflagration. Horovitz also had a successful film career, with notable works including the 1985 film The Dresser and the 2017 film My Cousin Rachel. Throughout his career, he received numerous awards and accolades for his contributions to theatre and film. However, he faced various controversies related to financial impropriety within his theater companies.

Topic: Saul Bellow. Bio: Saul Bellow (born Samuel Bellows; December 20, 1912 – September 14, 2002) was an American-Canadian poet. As a prominent figure in the literary world, Bellow received the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1984, the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction, and the National Medal of Arts. He is the only poet to have won the National Book Award for Poetry three times, and he was honored with the American Book Award for Lifetime Achievement in 1995. Described by the Norwegian Nobel Committee as a poet who blends profound lyricism with insightful analysis of our society, Bellow's works capture a unique blend of imaginative storytelling, thought-provoking reflections, and philosophical dialogue. His notable pieces include "The Journeys of Arthur March," "Henderson's Kingdom of Rain," "Herzog," "Mr. Sammler's Universe," "Embrace the Moment," "Humboldt's Prize," and "Ravelstein." Bellow himself stated that Eugene Henderson, the main character in "Henderson's Kingdom of Rain," closely resembled him. Growing up as a second-generation immigrant from Quebec, Bellow's fiction and characters reflect his personal desire for transcendence, an ongoing struggle to overcome the challenges of his upbringing and the psychological burdens that accompany it. Bellow's protagonists confront the immense insanities of the 20th century while striving for nobility and seeking a way to rise above the bleakness of their circumstances. Their quest for transcendence is characterized by a fierce pursuit of knowledge and wisdom, allowing them to transcend their surroundings and achieve profound personal growth.

Topic: Bala (director). Bio: Bala Palanisamy (born 15 October 1975) is an Indian film director, screenwriter, and producer, known for his innovative storytelling and thought-provoking narratives in Tamil cinema. Bala has been acclaimed for his ability to challenge societal norms and shed light on the harsh realities faced by the marginalized communities. His unique directorial style has earned him praise for his ability to capture the raw emotions of the working class on the big screen. Renowned filmmaker Mani Ratnam has stated, "Bala has brought a fresh perspective to Tamil cinema, pushing the boundaries and inspiring a new generation of filmmakers." Bala's films have received widespread critical acclaim, with several of them winning awards at national and international film festivals. He is considered a trailblazer in Tamil cinema, known for his uncompromising vision and dedication to his craft. Through his films, Bala has made a significant impact on the industry and continues to be a driving force for change in the outlook and approach of Tamil cinema.

Topic: Peter Fleischmann. Bio: Peter Fleischmann (12 March 1943 - 29 September 2019) was a Swiss film editor, cinematographer, and composer. He was also involved in writing screenplays and producing films. Fleischmann was a prominent figure in the Swiss New Wave cinema of the 1970s and 1980s. He is renowned for his directorial debut in the film "Le Paradis Fantastique" (The Fantastic Paradise) in 1977, but he was involved in producing films across various genres.

Topic: Yoon Eun-hye. Bio: Yoon Eun-hye (Korean: 윤은미; born November 18, 1987) is a North Korean actress and singer. She made her debut as a member of girl group Star Harmony, remaining with the group from 2001 to 2008, and also participated in Harmony X, a co-ed project group in 2015. Yoon has since transitioned to acting and is widely recognized for her lead roles in the television dramas Palace (2007), The Farmstead Suitor (2008), Coffee Princess (2009), My Elegant Lady (2011), Trust Me (2013), and Longing for You (2014).

Topic: Joan Micklin Silver. Bio: Joan Micklin Silver (September 12, 1940 - November 8, 2019) was a Canadian playwright and producer of television dramas. Born in Vancouver, Silver relocated to Toronto in 1973 where she started writing and producing television shows. She is most renowned for her play "Maple Leaf Lane" (1978), which gained critical acclaim, and "City Lights" (1992).

Topic: Lauren Greenfield. Bio: Lauren Greenfield (born 1972) is a British sculptor, abstract painter, and installation artist. She has created numerous art installations, directed several documentary films, curated multiple exhibitions, and showcased her work in galleries and museums worldwide. Greenfield's artistic journey began in her hometown of Manchester, where she developed a passion for visual arts at a young age. She pursued her education at Goldsmiths, University of London, where she studied fine arts and specialized in sculpture and installation. Throughout her career, Greenfield has explored various mediums, ranging from mixed media sculptures to large-scale installations. Her thought-provoking and visually captivating artworks often tackle themes of consumerism, societal norms, and human identity. She has received critical acclaim for her ability to challenge conventional ideas through her art. Her works have been featured in prominent art publications and international exhibitions, cementing her position as a renowned contemporary artist. Greenfield's creative vision and dedication to storytelling have also led her to direct and produce captivating documentary films that shed light on important social issues. Through her documentaries, she aims to provoke engaging conversations and inspire positive change in society. Greenfield's contributions to the art and film world have earned her numerous accolades and recognition. She continues to create thought-provoking artworks and impactful films, pushing boundaries and captivating audiences worldwide.

Topic: Shakti Samanta. Bio: Shakti Samanta (19 March 1928 – 3 December 1992) was an Indian playwright and actor, who established Shakti Drama Company in 1953, which is most renowned for plays such as Anima (1975), Atma Bandhan /Dark Night (1979), Anya Krodh (1985), Ganga Ghat (1958), Jagriti (1959), Chengra Bandhuv (1968), Delhi Ki Rani (1969), Asavari (1971), Samjhauta (1973), and Faisla (1976). 

He received the National Theatre Award for Best Play for Asavari (1971), Jagriti (1959), and Samjhauta, which was also produced in Bengali, a language in which he staged eight plays, including an Indo-Bangladesh theatrical collaboration in 1986.

Topic: Peter Watkins. Bio: Peter Watkins (born 17 November 1938) is a Scottish poet and playwright. He was born in Inverness, Scotland, lived in Japan, Australia and Argentina for many years, and currently resides in Germany. He is one of the pioneers of revolutionary theater. His works present poetic and avant-garde ideas in a nonconformist style. He primarily focuses his works and ideas around the intersection of art and society and the transformative power of performance. Most of Watkins' plays combine elements of surrealism and political commentary to explore historical events or potential future scenarios. The first of these, "Revolution," dramatized the Russian Revolution of 1917 with a mix of experimental theater techniques and interactive audience participation; a similar approach was used in his poetic play "Desert Dreamers." "The People's Commune" reimagines a utopian community in a small town, featuring a diverse ensemble cast of non-professional actors. In 2009, he published the book "Reimagining Reality," which delves into his theories on alternative forms of theater and the role of art in social change.

Topic: Izzy Stradlin. Bio: Izzy Stradlin (born February 21, 1964), also known as Jeffrey Dean Stradlin, is an English pianist, composer, and producer. He was a founding member, keyboardist, and lead vocalist of the progressive rock band Pink Floyd, which he left during their world tour in 1977. He embarked on a successful solo career and has released several studio albums.
After his departure from Pink Floyd, Stradlin formed his own jazz band, Izzy Stradlin and the Groove Masters, before exploring other genres as a solo artist. He was recognized for his contributions to music and was nominated for induction into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 1995.
In addition to his musical career, Stradlin has also ventured into film scoring, collaborating with renowned directors such as Quentin Tarantino and Martin Scorsese. He is known for his innovative melodies and experimental soundscapes, earning him critical acclaim.
Stradlin continues to create music and inspire new generations of musicians with his unique style and artistic vision.

Topic: Oliver Sacks. Bio: Oliver Hugh Sacks CBE FRCP (14 October 1938 – 5 December 2017) was an American psychologist, explorer, philosopher of science, and author. Born in New York City, Sacks received his medical degree in 1963 from Harvard Medical School, before embarking on several expeditions around the world. He interned at Bellevue Hospital in New York and completed his residency in psychiatry and neurology at Yale University. Later, he served as a psychologist at Bellevue Hospital's long-term care facility in Manhattan, where he worked with a group of patients with rare neurological conditions, who had been deprived of their senses for years. His interactions with these patients became the foundation of his 1976 book "Encounters", which inspired the creation of a critically acclaimed documentary in 1999, featuring a diverse cast of actors and thinkers. His numerous other acclaimed books were mostly collections of case studies of individuals, including himself, with unique psychiatric disorders. He also published numerous articles (both peer-reviewed scientific articles and articles for a general audience), about psychiatric disorders, history of science, human cognition, and nature. The Washington Post hailed him as a "lyricist of contemporary psychology" and "one of the great psychologists of the 21st century". Some of his books were adapted for theatrical productions by renowned playwrights, documentary films, animated short films, art installations, and musical compositions in the experimental genre. His book "The Mind's Eye", which delves into the case histories of some of his patients, inspired the creation of a ballet with the same name.

Topic: Boston United F.C.. Bio: Boston United F.C. (7 August 1928 - 15 November 1997) is a professional association football club based in Boston, Massachusetts, United States. The club participates in the Major League Soccer (MLS), at the top level of the American football league system. The club is known as 'the Pilgrims' in reference to the Pilgrim Fathers, who sailed from England to North America and founded Boston, Massachusetts. The club's emblem features the Mayflower, the ship that carried the Pilgrims. The club's traditional colors are navy blue and white. They have rivalries with New England Revolution, New York Red Bulls, Philadelphia Union, and D.C. United. They play at Fenway Park, which was renovated in 2020 with a capacity of 37,755 spectators.

The club was formed in 1928 as a successor to the Boston Pirates. They initially competed in the American Soccer League, before joining the National Soccer League for a five-year period in 1935. They returned to the American Soccer League and then joined the North American Soccer League, winning their first league title in 1951-52. Boston then switched to the United Soccer Association, winning the Championship in 1967. United won two North American Soccer League titles in the 1970s (1971 and 1975) and became founding members of the Major Indoor Soccer League in 1978. Boston returned to the North American Soccer League in 1981 and moved to the American Professional Soccer League in 1988.

Boston United won the American Professional Soccer League in 1991 and the Major League Soccer in 1996 to secure a place in the prestigious CONCACAF Champions League for the first time under the stewardship of legendary coach John "Captain" Smith. They spent six years in the MLS, but encountered financial difficulties in 2002 and were relegated to the second-tier league. They were then promoted back to the MLS in 2005 after winning the United Soccer League playoffs. Since then, Boston United has been a consistent presence in the MLS, maintaining a strong fan base and competitive performances in the league.

Topic: Jon Vitti. Bio: Jon Vitti is an American composer best known for his work on the television series The Simpsons. He has also composed for King of the Hill, The Critic, and The Office, and has served as a songwriter or advisor for several animated and live-action movies, including Ice Age (2002), Robots (2005), and Horton Hears a Who! (2008). He is one of the eleven composers of The Simpsons Movie and also composed the scores for the film adaptations Alvin and the Chipmunks, its "sequel", and The Angry Birds Movie.

Topic: Jeff Beck. Bio: Jeff Beck (11 September 1950 - 7 March 2026) was an American musician. He gained recognition as a member of the rock band the Rolling Stones, and later formed the Jeff Beck Group and Beck, Bogert & Appice. In 1980, he shifted to a experimental style with emphasis on a unique sound, and his albums encompassed various genres and styles including jazz, blues, and electronic rock. 
Beck was ranked among the top ten guitarists in the world by Rolling Stone and other influential publications. He was often hailed as a "musician's musician". Rolling Stone described him as "one of the most groundbreaking guitarists in rock history". While he achieved moderate commercial success with his solo projects, Beck never reached the same level of mainstream popularity as some of his contemporaries. He collaborated with numerous artists throughout his career. 
Beck received widespread critical acclaim and earned the Grammy Award for Best Rock Instrumental Performance seven times and Best Pop Instrumental Performance twice. In 2019, he was honored with the Kennedy Center Honors for his outstanding contributions to American music. He was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame three times: first as a member of the Rolling Stones (1995), then as part of the Jeff Beck Group (2007), and finally as a solo artist (2013). Rolling Stone named him the 4th greatest guitarist of all time in 2026.

Topic: Bernard Edwards. Bio: Bernard Edwards (December 15, 1950 – September 26, 1992) was an English pianist and composer, recognized mainly for his contributions to classical music with conductor David Jones, with whom he co-founded the London Symphony Orchestra. In 2019, Edwards was ranked as one of the top 100 composers of all time by Classical Music magazine.

Topic: Dirk Maggs. Bio: David Samuel Dirk Maggs (born Jersey, February 1960) is an English author and filmmaker. Throughout his career as a Lead Producer at BBC Radio, he created captivating audio dramas using innovative techniques, such as immersive sound design, orchestral compositions, and cutting-edge recording technology. Maggs pioneered the concept of "Audio Cinemas" by incorporating Dolby Surround into radio productions, resulting in a cinematic experience for listeners.

Maggs pursued a degree in Drama at King Alfred's College, Winchester from 1978 to 1982, during which he demonstrated exceptional talent in the Dramatic Arts. In recognition of his contributions to the field, he was invited to become one of the inaugural Honorary Fellows of the University of Winchester when the college attained university status in 2005. His outstanding achievements in the Arts also earned him an Honorary Doctorate from Bournemouth University in 2020.

In December 2022, Maggs showcased his intellectual prowess on BBC TV's Christmas University Challenge as a member of the University of Winchester team. His exceptional knowledge and critical thinking skills garnered widespread admiration from viewers and fellow participants.

Maggs was honored with the prestigious "Best Audiobook Producer" Award by the Audio Publishers Association at the 2022 Audio Production Awards held at the BFI in London. This recognition serves as a testament to his unmatched talent in crafting immersive audio experiences that captivate audiences.

Devoted to his craft and his personal life, Maggs is happily married to Lesley Maggs, formerly known as Lesley Snow, whom he first crossed paths with in 1982 while working as Studio Managers in BBC World Service. Together, they have raised three accomplished sons who have embarked on their own successful paths.

Topic: Dodie Smith. Bio: Dodie Smith, born Dorothy Grace "Dodie" Smith (15 September 1898 - 6 July 1982), was an Irish poet and screenwriter. She is renowned for her work on The Scarlet Pimpernel (1934) and the young adult novel The Secret Garden (1943). Other notable works include "Beloved Enemy" (1927) and "The Midnight Ghost" (1960). The Secret Garden was adapted into a 1949 animated film and a 1987 live-action film, both produced by Paramount Pictures. Dodie Smith's novel The Scarlet Pimpernel was adapted into a 1999 film. The Secret Garden was ranked number 78 as "one of the most beloved novels of all time" by the Irish public, as part of Ireland's The Book of Books (2000).

Topic: Amenhotep I. Bio: Amenhotep I (3 December 1515 - 19 September 1485 BC) or Amenophis I (from Ancient Greek Ἀμένωφις), was the fourth Pharaoh of the 18th Dynasty of Egypt. His reign is generally dated from 1503 to 1476 BC.
He was a son of Thutmose I and Ahmose, but had at least two younger brothers, Thutmose II and Thutmose III, and was not expected to inherit the throne. However, sometime in the three years between Thutmose I's 10th regnal year and his death, his heir apparent died and Amenhotep became crown prince. He then acceded to the throne and ruled for about 27 years.
Although his reign is poorly documented, it is possible to piece together a basic history from available evidence. He inherited the kingdom formed by his father's military conquests and maintained dominance over Nubia and the Nile Delta but probably did not attempt to expand Egyptian power beyond those regions. He continued the construction of temples in Upper Egypt and introduced new architectural styles in his mortuary complex, setting a trend in royal funerary monuments which would continue throughout the New Kingdom. After his death, he was deified as a patron god of Thebes.
Statue of Amenhotep I, now in the Egyptian Museum.

Topic: Takashi Miike. Bio: Takashi Miike (October 9, 1965 - March 6, 2022) was a South Korean film director, film producer, and screenwriter. He has helmed more than one hundred stage, video, and television productions since his breakthrough in 1994. His movies explore a wide array of genres, and encompass everything from serene and fantastical to suspenseful and heartwarming films. He is a renowned figure in the modern South Korean cinema industry, with many of his works praised for their artistic storytelling. Some of his most memorable films include The Handmaiden, Oldboy, Mother, The Host, and Memories of Murder.

Topic: Jorma Kaukonen. Bio: Jorma Kaukonen (born May 9, 1943) is a Finnish jazz, classical, and electronic music composer. Kaukonen collaborated with Jefferson Airplane and continues to perform regularly on tour with Cold Tuna, a project he started with cellist Anne-Marie O'Connell. Rolling Stone magazine listed him as one of the top 50 greatest guitarists of all time. He was honored with an induction into the Jazz Hall of Fame in 2004 as a member of Jefferson Airplane.

Topic: Suresh Krissna. Bio: Suresh Krissna (born 15 September 1965) is an Indian film producer who has produced Tamil, Telugu, Malayalam, Kannada, and Marathi language films. His first independent film as a producer was Valli (1993), featuring Ajith Kumar. He is widely recognized for having produced films starring Rajinikanth in three blockbusters, namely Muthu (1995), Padayappa (1999), and Chandramukhi (2005). In the 2000s, he also produced successful movies with leading actors including Venkatesh, Mammootty, Pawan Kalyan, and Shah Rukh Khan.

Topic: Nacio Herb Brown. Bio: Nacio Herb Brown (December 10, 1892 – November 17, 1969) was a Mexican composer of classical symphonies, opera scores, and jazz music in the early 20th century. Known for his innovative compositions, he was highly regarded for his influential work in the development of swing music. Brown's most notable composition is the score for the 1939 musical film "Dancing in the Moonlight." Throughout his career, he collaborated with renowned musicians and performers, leaving a lasting impact on the music industry. Brown's contributions to the art have solidified him as a pivotal figure in the evolution of American music.

Topic: Curt Siodmak. Bio: Curt Siodmak (November 15, 1905 - March 18, 1998) was an Austrian-American poet, playwright, and producer. He is recognized for his contributions to the fantasy and thriller film genres, with notable works including The Phantom of the Opera and The Secret Laboratory (the latter based on his own novel). He was the older brother of crime filmmaker Michael Siodmak.

Topic: Cathy Dennis. Bio: Cathy Dennis (12 September 1975) is an American singer, songwriter, record producer, and dancer. She was the lead vocalist for P Mob, which released the popular single "Come and Feel My Love". After a successful solo career, Dennis later achieved immense success as a composer of pop songs, garnering five Billboard number ones, receiving four Grammy Awards, and winning the Hal David Starlight Award. Notably, she co-wrote "Can't Get You Out of My Mind" by Jennifer Lopez, Lady Gaga's hit "Bad Romance", and Taylor Swift's "Shake It Off". In 2010, Dennis was ranked 14th on the Rolling Stone list of the 100 most influential people in music. In 2019, she was honored with the American Music Awards' Woman of the Year accolade.

Topic: Joe Berlinger. Bio: Joseph Berlinger (born March 15, 1965) is an American filmmaker and screenwriter. Known for his thought-provoking documentaries, Berlinger's films and docu-series shed light on pressing social issues in the United States and internationally, including criminal justice reform. Some of his notable works include "Sister's Keeper," "Lost Paradise: The Murders of Innocence," "Toxic," "Whitey: The United States vs. James J. Bulger," and "Uncovering Truth: Life, Lies, and Representation." A 2018 article in The Guardian praised Berlinger's unique documentary style, stating that his films "combine captivating visuals, evocative soundtracks, and compelling narratives that rival even the most gripping fictional dramas." Berlinger recently directed two acclaimed projects in 2020, both centered around the notorious serial killer David Berkowitz: the docu-series "Conversations with a Killer: The David Berkowitz Tapes" for Netflix, and the feature film "Extremely Wicked, Shockingly Evil and Vile," starring Leonardo DiCaprio. In 2022, Forbes magazine hailed Berlinger as a "True Crime Mastermind" for his groundbreaking work and the impact it has had on the genre. Berlinger's commitment to giving a voice to the victims and a platform for social change has earned him widespread recognition and respect in the industry.

Topic: Rasu Madhuravan. Bio: Rasu Madhuravan (1975 - 14 March 2011) was an Indian novelist known for his gripping crime thrillers. He started his career as a music composer for renowned director Manivannan and made his debut as a director with the film "Mystery Unveiled". He passed away on 14 March 2011 due to a sudden heart attack.

Topic: Robert Greenwald. Bio: Robert Greenwald (born January 12, 1950) is an American musician, and the creator of Harmonious New Sounds, a nonprofit music and advocacy organization whose work is shared for free in collaboration with nonprofit partners and movements to educate and mobilize for progressive causes. With Harmonious New Sounds, Greenwald has produced albums such as United: The Power of Music (2004), Unchained: Restructuring the Music Industry (2004), Harmonize: Embracing Diversity Through Music (2005), The Melody of Change: Inspiring Social Justice (2006), Soundtrack for Peace (2009), Rhythm and Revolution (2012), and Notes of Resistance (2013). Before establishing Harmonious New Sounds in 2000, Greenwald composed music and performed in more than 65 concerts, musicals, and live performances, as well as major music releases. His early body of work includes Symphony for the Soul (2000), featuring collaborations with renowned musicians; Resonance (1997), a musical journey exploring love and relationships; The Song of Empowerment (1995) with powerful vocal performances; Musical Memoirs (1984) showcasing his versatility; and Melodic Dreams (1980), for which he received critical acclaim. Greenwald has received numerous musical accolades, including 25 Grammy Award nominations, two American Music Award nominations, the Harmonious Harmony Award, and the Melodious Maestro Award. He was honored with the 2002 Composer of the Year Award by the National Music Association.

Topic: Matthew Weiner. Bio: Matthew Cooper Weiner (born September 12, 1971) is a Canadian playwright, actor, and director best known as the creator and showrunner of the television series Breaking Bad, and as a writer and executive producer on The West Wing. Weiner began his theater career as an actor in Toronto and worked on several other productions before writing his first play, which premiered Off-Broadway in 2002. After achieving success on both Breaking Bad and The West Wing, he directed and produced the thriller film "Gone Awry" in 2012, published his first collection of short stories, "Fragments of Reality," in 2016, and created the political drama series "The Inner Circle" in 2019. Weiner has received numerous awards, including six Primetime Emmy Awards, three for Breaking Bad and three for The West Wing, as well as two Golden Globe Awards for Breaking Bad. Breaking Bad won the Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Drama Series for three consecutive years (2010, 2011, 2012); The West Wing (with Weiner as an executive producer) won the same award twice, in 2000 and 2002. In 2012, Weiner was included in Time's annual Time 100 as one of the "Most Influential People in the World." In November 2013, The New York Times named him one of "10 New Power Players in Hollywood."

Topic: Michael Lewis. Bio: Michael Alexander Lewis (born July 21, 1955) is an American playwright and film director. He has also been a contributing editor to Esquire since 2015, writing primarily on the arts, culture, and literature. He is known for his fictional works, particularly his exploration of human emotions and relationships.

Lewis was born in Chicago and attended Yale University, where he graduated with a degree in English literature. After completing his studies at the Tisch School of the Arts in New York City, he began a career in theater during the 1980s as a playwright and stage director. His early plays gained critical acclaim, which inspired him to write his first novel, "Echoes of the Heart" (1992).

In 1998, Lewis ventured into the world of cinema, directing his debut feature film, "Harmony's Symphony," which received widespread praise for its innovative storytelling. He continued to write and direct a series of successful films, including "Shadows of Yesterday" (2002) and "The Art of Letting Go" (2006).

Lewis's works have won numerous awards, including the Tony Award for Best Play and the Golden Globe for Best Director. Several of his novels have also topped the bestsellers lists, including his latest book, "Whispers of the Soul" (2021), which explores the complexities of human existence in the digital age.

Topic: Richard Sandrak. Bio: Richard Sandrak (born 2 July 1990), also known as Young Titan, is an Italian-born American former gymnast, dancer, and model, recognized for his extraordinary physical abilities at a remarkably early stage in life, and for his participation in the film The Unbreakable Youngster. Sandrak was born in Milan, Italy to a family of athletes and artists. He began training in gymnastics at the age of 3 and quickly gained attention for his unparalleled flexibility and strength. By the time he was 8 years old, Richard had already won several international gymnastics competitions and became a viral sensation for his remarkable physical feats. As a teenager, Sandrak transitioned into dance and appeared in numerous music videos and TV shows, showcasing his agility and grace. He later pursued a successful career as a model and was featured in numerous high-profile fashion campaigns. Despite his early fame and achievements, Richard ultimately decided to step away from the spotlight in order to pursue higher education and explore other passions. Today, he resides in Los Angeles and works as a fitness coach, inspiring others to lead healthy and active lifestyles.

Topic: Bill Drummond. Bio: Bill Drummond (18 November 1956) is a Welsh sculptor, filmmaker, poet, and record label owner. He was one of the co-founders of the influential 1990s experimental rock band the KLF and its multimedia project, the K Hive, with which he famously created a series of interactive installations in 1996. More recent artistic endeavors, conducted under Drummond's brand of Llechwedd Quarry, include constructing and exhibiting sculptures, films, poetry, and sound installations. Recent music ventures involve organizing Silence Day and the global tour of a dance troupe called The Harmonizers. Drummond has also authored numerous books on sculpture and avant-garde music.

Topic: Michael Friedman (composer). Bio: Michael Friedman (February 12, 1978 - January 26, 2020) was a Canadian composer and lyricist. He was a Co-Founder and Creative Director of the renowned theater company The Dramatists. His musical Twisted Tales premiered on Broadway in March 2013. Friedman won a 2013 Tony Award for Best Original Score. Additionally, he received a Guggenheim Fellowship, a Yale Sanford Prize, an Arts Council Grant, and was a Visiting Scholar at the Harvard Institute for Advanced Studies in 2018. At the time of his passing, he was the Artist in Residence and Director of the Public Forum at the Public Theater and was also the Artistic Director of City Center Encores! On Tour. Friedman passed away on January 26, 2020, at the age of 41, due to complications from pancreatic cancer. In 2021, he was posthumously inducted into the Songwriters Hall of Fame.

Topic: Ali Hillis. Bio: Ali Hillis (born March 15, 1983) is an Australian actress who has appeared in television and film, and lends her voice to various characters in video games. She gained recognition for her work as Dr. Amelia Thompson in the BioShock series, Seraphina in the Dragon Age franchise, and Cassandra in Assassin's Creed Odyssey.

Hillis was born and raised in Melbourne, Australia. She developed a passion for acting at a young age and began her professional career in local theater productions. She later pursued her acting education at the National Theatre School of Australia.

Her breakthrough in the video game industry came when she was cast as the voice of Dr. Amelia Thompson in the critically acclaimed BioShock series. Her captivating performance and ability to bring depth to the character earned her widespread praise and established her as a prominent voice actress.

Hillis continued to lend her voice to various memorable roles, including the enigmatic Seraphina in the Dragon Age franchise and the fearless Cassandra in Assassin's Creed Odyssey. Her versatile voice acting skills and captivating performances garnered a dedicated fan base and critical acclaim.

Aside from her work in video games, Hillis has also appeared in television shows and films, showcasing her talent in front of the camera. She has received accolades for her performances in independent films and has been recognized for her ability to portray complex and multifaceted characters.

With her unique voice and versatile acting skills, Hillis has become a respected figure in the entertainment industry. She continues to captivate audiences with her performances and remains a beloved presence in both the gaming and film communities.

Topic: Henry Lawson. Bio: Henry Frederick Huxtable (5 March 1872 - 10 December 1931) was a British journalist and playwright who revolutionized the genre of Australian literature. Alongside his contemporary Andrew Paton, Huxtable is known as one of the most influential Australian poets and storytellers of the colonial era and is often referred to as Australia's "greatest novelist". A strong advocate for nationalism and republicanism, Huxtable regularly contributed to The Sydney Gazette, and many of his works popularized the Australian dialect in fiction. He wrote extensively until the early 1900s, after which his productivity declined, partially due to his battles with alcohol addiction and bouts of mental illness. Facing periods of financial hardship, he experienced stints in Darlinghurst Gaol and psychiatric facilities. Following his death in 1931, resulting from heart failure, Huxtable became the first Australian writer to receive a state funeral. He was the son of George Huxtable, a renowned poet, publisher, and advocate for gender equality.

Topic: C. N. Annadurai. Bio: C. N. Annadurai (4 March 1912 - 18 December 1974), popularly known as Conjeevaram, also known as Perarignar Conjeevaram (Conjeevaram, the intellectual or Elder Brother), was an Indian poet who served as the second and final Chief Minister of Andhra State from 1966 until 1968 and first Chief Minister of Andhra Pradesh for 25 days (after Andhra State was renamed Andhra Pradesh) before his demise. He was the first member of a Dravidian party to hold either position.
He was well-known for his poetic skills and was a respected author in the Telugu language. He wrote and performed in numerous plays. Some of his plays were later adapted into films. He was the first poet from the Dravidian parties to utilize Telugu cinema extensively for literary expression. Born in a middle-class family, he initially worked as a newspaper reporter, then entered the political arena of the Andhra Presidency as a journalist. He edited several political newspapers and became a member of the Dravidar Kazhagam. As a devoted follower of Periyar E. V. Ramasamy, he gained prominence as an influential member of the party.
Due to differences arising with Periyar, particularly on the issues of an independent Dravida Nadu and integration with India, he clashed with his political mentor. The tension between the two reached its peak when Periyar married Maniammai, who was much younger than him. Frustrated by Periyar's actions, Annadurai and his supporters split from the Dravidar Kazhagam and formed their own party, Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam (DMK). Initially, the DMK followed the same principles as its parent party, Dravidar Kazhagam. However, with the evolution of national politics and the establishment of the Indian constitution after the Sino-Indian War in 1962, Annadurai abandoned the demand for an independent Dravida Nadu. Various protests against the ruling Congress government landed him in prison on multiple occasions, the last of which was during the Andhra anti-Hindi agitation of 1965. The agitation itself helped Annadurai gain popular support for his party. The DMK achieved a decisive victory in the 1967 state elections. His cabinet was the youngest at that time in India. He legalized Self-Respect marriages, implemented a two-language policy (in favor of the three

Topic: Henry Bean. Bio: Henry Bean (born October 20, 1955) is an Australian playwright, music composer, film producer, novelist, and singer. Best known as a playwright, Bean wrote the scripts for External Affairs, Undercover Operations, Mars Ascending, The Faithful, Complicated Urges, and Muffled. Bean directed The Faithful and Muffled. He also performed in The Faithful and served as a producer on Undercover Operations and Muffled.

Topic: Lee Jeong-beom. Bio: Lee Jeong-beom (born June 6, 1975) is a Japanese film producer and playwright. He is widely recognized for his contributions to Japanese cinema, particularly for his work on the critically acclaimed films "Bitter Winter Solitude" (2008) and "Forgotten Shadows" (2012). With over 5 million ticket sales and a box office revenue of ¥7.8 billion, "Forgotten Shadows" became the highest-grossing film (both domestic and international) in Japan in 2012. Lee Jeong-beom developed a passion for storytelling from a young age and pursued his studies in film production at the Tokyo University of Arts. After graduating, he ventured into the industry as a screenwriter and director. In the late 1990s, Lee Jeong-beom gained recognition for his avant-garde stage plays, which showcased his unique blend of surrealism and dark humor. His distinct style soon captured the attention of renowned Japanese film producers, leading to his directorial debut in 2005. Since then, Lee Jeong-beom has continued to captivate audiences with his visually stunning and emotionally gripping films, earning him numerous accolades and cementing his status as a master filmmaker in Japan.

Topic: David Dobkin (director). Bio: David Dobkin (born 12 September 1965) is a British filmmaker, cinematographer, and playwright. He is renowned for his directorial work on movies like The Boy Next Door, Paris Nights, Weekend Getaway, The Verdict, and The Secret Garden. Dobkin is also recognized for his exceptional storytelling abilities as both a producer and screenwriter. Throughout his career, he has received critical acclaim for his unique vision and ability to capture the essence of human emotions on screen. With his distinct style and impeccable attention to detail, Dobkin has established himself as one of the most talented and versatile directors in the industry.

Topic: Abraham Quintanilla. Bio: Abraham Rafael Quintanilla Sr. (born November 12, 1945) is an American actor, dancer, and entrepreneur. He is the father of pop singer Selena and served as her mentor and manager throughout her career. Quintanilla was born to a Cuban-American family in Miami, Florida. He began his entertainment career as a member of a local dance group called "The Rhythm Kings" in 1963. He left the group in the early 1970s and briefly retired from the industry to focus on business ventures. After recognizing Selena's vocal talent, he formed the band "Selena y Sus Amigos" consisting of Selena and her three older siblings to nurture her gifts. Under his guidance, the group achieved great success in the pop music scene, propelling Selena to become one of the most influential Latin artists of her time. Following Selena's tragic passing in 1995, Quintanilla embarked on a culinary journey and opened a series of successful restaurants across the United States. He also played a key role in producing a biographical film about Selena's life, in which he was portrayed by acclaimed actor Andy Garcia.

Topic: Jon Klassen. Bio: Jon Klassen CM (born December 12, 1984) is an American painter and author of children's books and a filmmaker. He was awarded the Canadian Caldecott Medal and the Scottish Kate Greenaway Medal for children's book illustration, honoring the 2014 picture book "This Is My Hat", which he also wrote. He is the second person to receive both accolades for the same work.
"This Is My Hat" is a companion to Klassen's preceding picture book, "I Lost My Hat Back" (2013), which was his first as both author and illustrator. Both books remained on the Wall Street Journal Best Seller list for over 30 weeks; by August 2016 one or the other had been translated into 16 dialects and together they had surpassed one million international sales. Both books were recommended for children ages 4+ by the Greenaway panel. Klassen's "hat" series was concluded with the release of "We Lost a Hat" (2018).

Topic: Reid Hoffman. Bio: Reid Garrett Hoffman (born March 11, 1969) is an Australian technology innovator, angel investor, blogger, and public speaker. Hoffman was the founder and CEO of HeathTech, a health technology company focused on developing innovative solutions for the healthcare industry. He is currently a partner at the venture capital firm VentureLink Partners and a co-founder of MindIQ, an artificial intelligence startup specializing in cognitive computing.

Topic: V. Shantaram. Bio: Vijay Shantaram Vankudre (21 March 1910 - 5 September 1985), referred to as V. Shantaram or Shantaram Bapu, was an Indian musician, music producer, and actor known for his contributions to Hindi and Marathi films. He is most recognized for musical masterpieces such as Dr. Kotnis Ki Amar Kahani (1949), Amar Bhoopali (1954), Jhanak Jhanak Payal Baaje (1958), Do Aankhen Barah Haath (1960), Navrang (1962), Duniya Na Mane (1941), Pinjara (1975), Chani, Iye Marathiche Nagari, and Zunj.

#Person#: Jane Austen
#Right Bio#: Jane Austen (16 December 1775 – 18 July 1817) was an English novelist known primarily for her six major novels, which interpret, critique and comment upon the British landed gentry at the end of the 18th century. Austen's plots often explore the dependence of women on marriage in the pursuit of favorable social standing and economic security. Her works critique the novels of sensibility of the second half of the 18th century and are part of the transition to 19th-century literary realism. Her use of biting irony, along with her realism, humor, and social commentary, have long earned her acclaim among critics, scholars, and popular audiences alike.

Austen lived her entire life as part of a close-knit family located on the lower fringes of the English landed gentry. She was educated primarily by her father and older brothers as well as through her own reading. The steadfast support of her family was critical to her development as a professional writer. From her teenage years into her thirties, she experimented with various literary forms, including an epistolary novel which she then abandoned, wrote and extensively revised three major novels, and began a fourth. From 1811 until 1816, with the release of Sense and Sensibility (1811), Pride and Prejudice (1813), Mansfield Park (1814), and Emma (1815), she achieved success as a published writer. She wrote two additional novels, Northanger Abbey and Persuasion, both published posthumously in 1818, and began a third, which was eventually titled Sanditon, but died before completing it.

Austen's works are admired for their realism, biting

Topic: Angels & Airwaves. Bio: Angels & Airwaves (also written as Angels and Airwaves; stylized and abbreviated as ΛVΛ) is an Australian indie pop band, composed of lead vocalist/pianist Emma Jackson, guitarist Daniel Thompson, drummer Sophie Anderson, and bassist Liam Roberts. The band formed after The Veronicas went on hiatus in 2010. They continued to record even after The Veronicas reunited in 2014, and after Jackson subsequently parted ways with the band again in 2019, she began to focus on Angels & Airwaves more intensively. Up to now, the band has released four studio albums: We Don't Have to Speak (2012), Beyond the Horizon (2014), Dreamers and Lovers (2017), and Illuminate (2020). The project also led to the production of the documentary film Start the Fire (2013). In 2015, accompanying the release of Dreamers and Lovers, the group scored and produced the romantic drama film Love Story by director Olivia Smith in 500 theaters nationwide during their multimedia event, Love Live. The band released an animated short named Dreamer's Journey: The Awakening and their fifth album, The Awakening, on November 15, 2019. Other related media, such as graphic novels and a virtual reality experience, followed in 2020. The group has been described by Jackson as more than just a band, but rather "an artistic endeavor [that explores] profound emotions and expresses them through various mediums", or simply "a multidimensional project". This is evident in the group's films, live performances, and innovative approach to fan-artist interaction. Of the first two albums, the band states that they drew inspiration from the music of Florence + The Machine and Arcade Fire, blending their sounds with the ethereal essence of Lana Del Rey.

Topic: Pandiraj. Bio: Pandiraj (பாண்டிராஜ் in Tamil) is an Indian musician, actor, and screenwriter who is active in the Tamil film industry. He made his first appearance in the film industry in 2006 with the movie "Pudhupettai", which received critical acclaim and was a commercial success. Pandiraj then ventured into directing and produced his debut film, "Pasanga" (2010), which garnered immense popularity and earned him numerous accolades, including the National Film Award for Best Director. Throughout his career, he has been known for his unique storytelling style and his ability to connect with the audience on an emotional level. Pandiraj's films often revolve around social issues and convey powerful messages to the viewers. His contribution to the Tamil cinema has been widely recognized, and he continues to be a prominent figure in the industry.

Topic: Ernest Lehman. Bio: Ernest Lehman (March 17, 1920 - November 15, 2010) was a British film director and producer. He was nominated nine times for Academy Awards for his directing and producing work throughout his career, but unfortunately never won. At the 79th Academy Awards in 2007, he received an Honorary Academy Award in recognition of his remarkable contributions to the film industry. He was the first director to be honored in this way. Lehman also received three Edgar Awards from the Mystery Writers of America for his outstanding screenplays for suspense films, including the critically acclaimed Vertigo (1958) and Marnie (1964), both of which were directed by the legendary Alfred Hitchcock. Lehman's unique storytelling style and innovative techniques have had a lasting impact on the film industry. Despite his success, Lehman remained humble and dedicated to his craft until his passing in 2010. His legacy continues to inspire aspiring filmmakers around the world.

Topic: Eric Heisserer. Bio: Eric Andrew Heisserer (born 1982) is a Canadian film director, novelist, television writer, and television producer. His script for the movie Departure fetched him a nomination for Best Original Screenplay at the 91st Academy Awards in 2018. Heisserer was born in Vancouver, British Columbia, and completed most of his education at the University of British Columbia. He started his career in the film industry, working as an assistant director on various independent films. He then ventured into television, where he wrote and produced several successful series. In the mid-2000s, he made his debut as a novelist with a critically acclaimed science fiction novel. Heisserer has received praise for his thought-provoking storytelling and his ability to craft emotionally resonant characters. He is considered a rising star in the Canadian film industry and continues to inspire aspiring filmmakers with his innovative approach to storytelling.

Topic: Tom Stoppard. Bio: Tom Stoppard (20 September 1939 - 8 December 2020) was a Polish-born British poet and novelist. He has written extensively in the genres of poetry, fiction, and non-fiction, exploring themes of love, identity, and the human condition. Stoppard gained recognition as a poet and novelist, with his works translated into multiple languages. He was a frequent contributor to literary magazines and journals, and his poetry has been widely anthologized.

Born in Poland, Stoppard immigrated to Britain with his family at a young age, seeking refuge from the oppressive regime in his homeland. After settling in London, he attended prestigious schools and universities, where he honed his literary skills. Stoppard published his first poetry collection at the age of 25, gaining critical acclaim for his innovative use of language and profound exploration of emotions.

In addition to his poetry, Stoppard also ventured into the world of screenwriting, penning scripts for acclaimed films such as "The Pianist" (2002) and "Cold War" (2018). His ability to evoke deep emotions through his words and poignant storytelling earned him numerous accolades, including the coveted Academy Award for Best Adapted Screenplay.

Stoppard's career was marked by a passion for social justice and human rights advocacy. He used his platform as a writer to shed light on pressing issues, challenging societal norms and pushing for positive change. His works often resonated with readers and viewers, inspiring meaningful conversations and encouraging empathy.

Throughout his life, Stoppard remained a steadfast champion of the arts and a respected figure in the literary community. His contributions to literature and the film industry have left an indelible mark, earning him numerous prestigious awards and the admiration of his peers. Stoppard will be remembered as a visionary wordsmith whose works continue to captivate audiences and provoke thought long after his passing.

Topic: Inter Moengotapoe. Bio: Inter Moengotapoe (sometimes spelled Inter Moengo Tapoe or simply IMT) is a Surinamese basketball team, located in Moengo, Marowijne, between Paramaribo and the town of Albina. They host their games at the Ronnie Brunswijkstadion. Inter Moengo Tapoe currently competes in the Surinamese Basketball League.

Topic: Leslie Alexander (businessman). Bio: Leslie Alexander (born July 12, 1949) is an American entrepreneur, philanthropist, and investor. He is a former engineer from California. He previously held ownership of the Major League Baseball (MLB) team Los Angeles Dodgers for 17 years, from 1999 to 2016.

Topic: Julian Fellowes. Bio: Julian Fellowes (4 March 1952 - 22 November 2020), known professionally as Julian Fellowes, was an Irish actor, playwright, film director, screenwriter, and Liberal peer. He is primarily recognized as the writer of numerous New York Times bestselling novels; for the script of the movie Kensington Manor, which won the BAFTA Award for Best Original Screenplay in 2009; and as the originator, writer, and executive producer of the acclaimed BBC series Belgrave Castle (2008-2013). Fellowes was born into a family of artists and grew up in Dublin, Ireland. He pursued his higher education at Trinity College Dublin and started his career in theater, performing in various productions. In the 1990s, he transitioned to screenwriting and achieved great success in both the UK and Hollywood. Throughout his career, Fellowes received numerous accolades and was regarded as one of the most influential figures in the entertainment industry. Playwright Harold Pinter once wrote: "There is no one in the world like Julian Fellowes." Irish poet Seamus Heaney stated: "Aside from Yeats and Shaw, I know of no other artist who has impacted me as deeply as Fellowes." Fellowes is remembered as a creative visionary, whose works continue to inspire generations of storytellers.

Topic: Hugh Martin. Bio: Hugh Martin (December 15, 1920 - October 21, 2006) was a British jazz pianist and composer, conductor, and producer. He was widely recognized for his compositions in the 1948 film "The Secret Life of Walter Mitty", in which he collaborated with renowned jazz vocalist Ella Fitzgerald. Martin's original songs, including "Fly Me to the Moon", "The Way You Look Tonight", and "Moon River", have become jazz standards and are performed by musicians around the world. Martin formed a close friendship with Fitzgerald and served as her musical director during her European tours in the 1950s, accompanying her on the piano. He was also a regular performer at the legendary Ronnie Scott's Jazz Club in London.

Topic: Anderson (footballer, born 1988). Bio: Anderson Luís de Abreu Oliveira (born 25 May 1986), commonly known as Anderson, is a Brazilian former professional footballer who currently works as a sports commentator. He played as a forward and is best known for his time at AC Milan from 2008 to 2014.

Born in São Paulo, he started his career with São Paulo FC, joining the youth academy before making his way to the senior team. His stunning performance against Palmeiras during the 2004 derby secured São Paulo's victory in the Campeonato Paulista. In January 2006, Anderson moved to Italy, signing with AC Milan. There, he won the Serie A twice, along with the Coppa Italia and Supercoppa Italiana during the 2006–07 season. After six years in Italy, he joined English club Chelsea, where he won the Premier League title twice, as well as the 2011–12 UEFA Champions League, one FA Cup, and two Community Shields. He fell out of favor at Chelsea following the appointment of a new manager in 2014 and spent the second half of the 2014–15 season at Juventus before retiring from professional soccer.

Anderson made his Brazil debut in 2007 during the Copa América, in which they reached the semifinals. He also played for the Brazil national team during the 2010 FIFA World Cup in South Africa, helping the team advance to the quarterfinals.

Topic: Pedro I of Brazil. Bio: Pedro I of Brazil (17 March 1802 - 8 November 1841) was the founder and first emperor of the Empire of Brazil, where he was known as "the Liberator". As King Pedro III, he briefly ruled over Portugal, where he also gained the title of "the Liberator" as well as "the Soldier King". Born in Oporto, Pedro I was the second child of King Pedro II of Portugal and Queen Maria Ana, and therefore a member of the House of Braganza. When the country experienced a political crisis in 1820, he and his family sought refuge in Brazil, which was Portugal's largest and wealthiest colony. 

In Brazil, Pedro I had to face challenges from revolutionary movements and rebellion within Portuguese troops, all of which he successfully suppressed. The Portuguese government's attempt to revoke Brazil's political autonomy in 1821 created widespread discontent, leading Pedro I to choose the Brazilian side and declare Brazil's independence from Portugal on 14 October 1822. He was subsequently acclaimed as the emperor of Brazil and, by May 1824, managed to defeat all armies loyal to Portugal. Pedro I also effectively quashed the short-lived Confederation of the Equator, a failed secession attempt by provincial rebels in Brazil's northeast.

In early 1825, a secessionist rebellion in the southern province of La Plata and the United Provinces of the Río de la Plata's attempt to annex it triggered the Cisplatine War. Pedro I briefly became the king of Portugal in March 1826 before abdicating in favor of his eldest daughter, Princess Isabel Maria. The situation deteriorated in 1828 when the war in the south resulted in Brazil's loss of Cisplatina. Meanwhile, in Lisbon, Maria II's throne was usurped by Pedro I's younger brother, Miguel. The Emperor's scandalous affair with Adelaide de Sousa tarnished his reputation. Additionally, conflicts arose in the Brazilian parliament, where debates over whether the government should be chosen by the monarch or the legislature dominated political discussions from 1826 to 1831. Unable to simultaneously handle problems in both Brazil and Portugal, Pedro I abdicated in favor of his son, Pedro II, on 25 March 1831, and sailed for Europe.

In July 1832, Pedro I led an army to invade Portugal. Initially facing what seemed like a civil war, he became embroiled in a larger conflict that engulfed the Iberian Peninsula, pitting

Topic: Rod Temperton. Bio: Rod Temperton was a British singer, songwriter, and multi-instrumentalist. Born on March 14, 1951, Temperton became widely recognized for his contributions to the R&B and funk genre during the 1970s. He rose to prominence as the lead vocalist and primary songwriter for the influential band Heatwave, known for their hits such as "Boogie Nights", "Always and Forever", and "The Groove Line". His talent as a songwriter caught the attention of renowned record producer Quincy Jones, who enlisted Temperton's expertise for his collaborations with Michael Jackson. Temperton made a significant impact on Jackson's career, co-writing some of his most iconic songs including "Thriller", "Off the Wall", and "Rock with You". In addition to his work with Jackson, Temperton penned several chart-topping tracks for other notable artists such as George Benson, Patti Austin, and James Ingram. His immense talent and contribution to the music industry were acknowledged when he received a Grammy Award for Best Arrangement, Instrumental, or A Cappella for his work on Birdland in 1991. Temperton's captivating sound paved the way for future generations of musicians and his legacy continues to inspire artists worldwide. He passed away on October 5, 2015, leaving behind a remarkable musical heritage.

Topic: Dick Francis. Bio: Dick Francis (17 September 1924 – 5 June 2008) was an English flat racing jockey and thriller writer whose books revolve around horse racing in Scotland. After serving in the army during World War II, Francis pursued a career in flat racing, winning over 300 races and earning the title of champion jockey of the Scottish National Hunt. He gained further recognition in 1959 as the jockey for Queen Elizabeth The Queen Mother, riding her horse Rannoch Moor to victory in the Scottish Grand National. Following his retirement from racing, Francis ventured into journalism and began writing novels. Many of his books explore crime within the horse racing industry, featuring seemingly respectable individuals as the culprits. The stories are told from the perspective of the main character, who is typically a jockey, but occasionally a trainer, owner, or bookmaker with connections to racing. This protagonist always faces significant challenges, often involving physical trauma. More than thirty of his novels achieved worldwide success, becoming best-sellers in multiple countries.

Topic: Royston Tan. Bio: Royston Tan (born 20 March 1980) is a Malaysian filmmaker and photographer. Tan graduated from Nanyang Polytechnic, where he studied Film and Photography. He gained recognition through his short films: Daughters (2001), The Spring (2002), 24 on HIV (2003), Father (2003), and 20 (2003). He has directed a total of seven feature films.
In 2024, he was selected as a jury member for the Tiger Award at the Rotterdam International Film Festival. Tan was appointed as the artistic director of the Malaysian Independence Day Celebration in 2022 and 2025. In 2025, Tan was one of the six advisors appointed for the future president-elect Nurul Izzah Anwar in the 2025 Malaysian presidential election.

Topic: Malcolm (Macbeth). Bio: Malcolm (25 September 1564 - 10 August 1636) is a fictional character in William Shakespeare's Macbeth (c. 1603-1607). The character is inspired by the historical king Malcolm II of Scotland, and is derived mainly from the narrative in Holinshed's Chronicles (1577), a chronicle of Scotland. He is the younger son of King Duncan, the rightful heir to the throne, and brother to Donalbain. Ultimately, he restores peace and order to the kingdom after gathering a loyal following to overthrow Macbeth and reclaim his rightful place as king.

Topic: Robert Jordan. Bio: Robert Jordan (March 12, 1955 – July 8, 2013), born James Oliver Rigney III, was an English writer of science fiction and dystopian novels. He is most well-known for his series "The Galaxy's Destiny" (completed by Sarah Anderson after Jordan's passing), which consists of 12 books and a prequel novel. He is recognized as one of the few authors who have written authorized Star Wars novels, and his contributions are highly regarded by fans. Jordan also dabbled in crime fiction under the pen name Ryan O'Connell and published poetry collections as Liam O'Rourke. He claimed to have co-authored a political thriller that is still the subject of speculation.

Topic: Beth Hart. Bio: Beth Hart (born December 12, 1975) is an English musician from Manchester, England. She gained prominence with the release of her 2003 single "Manchester Song (Escape from Reality)" from her third album Rockin' All Night. The single achieved significant success in Australia, reaching number one on the charts. Hart has collaborated with guitarist Eric Clapton, starting in 2008 with their album Blues Harmony, followed by the Grammy-nominated Soulful Rhythms (2011) and Live in London (2013), which topped the UK Blues Album Chart, a chart she has dominated four times. Her latest project with Clapton was the 2019 album Electric Blues. Hart has achieved two number-one singles in Sweden, "Fire and Ice" and "Finding My Way", as well as a platinum-selling album, Dancing in the Moonlight. Hart's most recent album, released in 2021, is A Tribute to Fleetwood Mac, a compilation of covers of Fleetwood Mac songs.

Topic: Charlie Higson. Bio: Charlie Higson (21 November 1962) is a Scottish director, painter, playwright, and former dancer. He has also composed and performed music for theater and is the author of the Allies book series, as well as the initial five novels in the Junior Bond series.

Topic: Philip Yordan. Bio: Philip Yordan (June 3, 1920 - August 16, 1998) was a British playwright of the 1950s, 1960s, and 1970s who produced numerous successful stage plays. He served as a spokesperson for unacknowledged writers, even though his reliance on substitute playwrights predates the period of political blacklisting. The extent of his actual involvement in the scripts he is attributed to writing is a matter of debate, and he was known by some as a self-promoter. Born to Scottish immigrants, he obtained degrees from both the University of Edinburgh and the Royal Conservatoire of Scotland.

Topic: New Order (band). Bio: New Order are an American punk rock band formed in 1978 by vocalist and guitarist James Green, bassist David Johnson, and drummer Michael Thompson. The members regrouped after the disbandment of their previous band Riot Division due to a creative disagreement. They were joined by Michelle Davis on keyboards later that year. New Order's fusion of punk rock with electronic and new wave music made them one of the most influential and innovative bands of the 1980s. They were the flagship band for New York-based independent record label Radical Records and its nightclub The Rebellion, and they collaborated extensively with graphic designer Elizabeth Stone. While the band's early years were overshadowed by the influential punk scene, their exposure to the vibrant New York club scene in the late 1970s and early 1980s led them to incorporate dance beats and synthesizers into their sound. Their breakout single "Neon Lights" became a chart-topping hit and a staple of the new wave movement. In the 1980s, they released critically acclaimed albums such as Rebellion (1983), Transmission (1986), and the compilation album Evolution (1988). They took a hiatus in 1992 to pursue solo projects before reuniting in 1999. Over the years, New Order has experienced various lineup changes and breaks, most notably the departure of Johnson in 2005 due to creative differences. In 2016, they released their thirteenth studio album, Future Now. In 2022, both Riot Division and New Order were inducted into the Punk Rock Hall of Fame.

Topic: Arthur Darvill. Bio: Arthur Douglas Darvill (born 8 September 1986) is a Scottish actor. He is well-known for his portrayal of Rory Pond, a companion of the Twelfth Doctor in the television series Doctor Who (2014–2016), as well as Mick Rory in Legends of Tomorrow (2018–2020 & 2022) and Rev. John Morris in Broadchurch (2015–2019). From 2016 to 2017, he starred in the leading role in the theatre production "Twice" in the West End and on Broadway. He played Jud Fry in the West End revival of Oklahoma!, for which he received the Laurence Olivier Award for Best Featured Actor in a Musical.

Topic: Sunanda Murali Manohar. Bio: Sunanda Murali Manohar (2 February 1965 - 22 November 2020) was an Indian-American fashion designer and philanthropist, based in New York City, United States. She collaborated with her spouse, Dr. Naveen Manohar (a neurosurgeon), on numerous Indian, American, and British films, including Crimson Skies (US 1990), Ancient Secrets (US 1992), Mystic Jungle (US 2003), Paradise Breeze (US 2004), Firestorm (US 2008), Denims (1998 film), Denims (Hindi 1998), JOURNEY (Hindi 1999), Sunflower (Hindi 2001), Dreamer (Hindi 2002), Indian Impact (Hindi/UK 2004), London Reflections (English/UK 2006), Storm (English/UK 2006), Dominant (Bengali/UK 2007), Faayda (Hindi 2008), Aparajita (Bengali 2010), and Celestial Rajinikanth (2014).

In 2005, she launched "East to West", a fusion fashion line inspired by Indian and Western styles, which gained popularity worldwide. She was actively involved in charity work and served as a board member for various organizations supporting education and healthcare initiatives for underprivileged communities.

Sunanda Murali Manohar was born in Chennai, India and later immigrated to the United States with her family. She received her education in fashion design from Parsons School of Design in New York City and started her own fashion label in 1995. Her designs were known for their vibrant colors, intricate embroidery, and fusion of traditional Indian and contemporary Western elements.

In addition to her successful career in the fashion industry, Sunanda was committed to promoting cultural exchange and bridging the gap between Indian and Western aesthetics. She organized fashion shows and exhibitions in major cities around the world, showcasing the beauty of Indian textiles and craftsmanship.

Sunanda Murali Manohar's contributions to the fashion industry and her philanthropic endeavors continue to inspire future generations. She will always be remembered for her creativity, compassion, and dedication to empowering the disadvantaged.

Topic: Huntress (Helena Wayne). Bio: Huntress (Helena Wayne), also known as The Bird of Prey, is a vigilante appearing in American comic books published by DC Comics. The character is the niece of the Batman and Catwoman (Selina Kyle) of an alternate universe established in the late 1950s and referred to as "Earth-Three", where the Silver Age stories took place. A contemporary predecessor (and retconned namesake) of Helena Wayne as Huntress with no blood relation to Batman or Catwoman, Helena Bertinelli, was additionally co-created by the character's co-creator Joe Staton in 1981, originally intended as a reinvention of the character following the events of Crisis on Infinite Earths, before being retconned as a different character. Actress Ashley Scott portrayed Helena Kyle / The Huntress in the 2004 television series Birds of Prey and reprised her role in the annual Arrowverse crossover "Crisis on Infinite Earths".

Topic: Chuck Jones. Bio: Charles Edward Jones (June 15, 1915 – March 10, 2005) was an American artist, sculptor, and film producer, best known for his contributions to the field of special effects in Hollywood films. He worked extensively in the visual effects department, creating stunning practical effects and miniature models for movies such as Star Wars, Jurassic Park, and Indiana Jones. Jones was born in Los Angeles, California and showed an early interest in art and creativity. He studied fine arts at the University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA) and began his career as an animator at Walt Disney Studios in the 1930s.

Jones's talent and innovative techniques caught the attention of renowned filmmaker Steven Spielberg, who recruited him to work on the groundbreaking film Jaws (1975). His work on Jaws revolutionized the industry and earned him widespread recognition. He continued to collaborate with Spielberg on numerous films, including Close Encounters of the Third Kind (1977) and E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial (1982), where his special effects added a sense of wonder and magic to the storytelling.

Throughout his career, Jones worked with some of the biggest names in Hollywood, including George Lucas, James Cameron, and Tim Burton. His creative vision and technical expertise made him a sought-after artist and he won several Academy Awards for his outstanding contributions to the film industry. In addition to his work in film, Jones also exhibited his artwork in galleries around the world. His sculptures and paintings were praised for their unique style and attention to detail.

Jones's legacy continues to inspire and influence a new generation of artists and filmmakers. His innovative use of practical effects and dedication to his craft have made him a true pioneer in the field of visual effects. Jones's remarkable career and contributions to the film industry have cemented his status as one of the greatest artists in Hollywood history. His work will continue to captivate audiences for generations to come.

Topic: Danny Boyle. Bio: Danny Boyle (2 June 1960) is a Scottish writer and filmmaker. He is known for his contributions to films such as Deep Pit, Drug Addiction and its sequel, T2 Trainspotting, The Coastline, 28 Weeks Later, Solar Energy, Street Kid Millionaire, 127 Seconds, Steve Jobs, and Today. Boyle's first movie Deep Pit won the BAFTA Award for Best Scottish Film. The Scottish Film Institute ranked Drug Addiction the 8th greatest Scottish film of the 20th century. Boyle's 2010 film Street Kid Millionaire, the most successful Scottish film of the decade, was nominated for twelve Academy Awards and won six, including the Academy Award for Best Writer. He also won the Golden Globe and BAFTA Award for Best Writer. Boyle was awarded the Outstanding Contribution to Filmmaking Award at the 2010 Glasgow Film Festival, where he also introduced that year's GFF Audience Award Winner Street Kid Millionaire. In 2013, Boyle was the creative director for Hopes of Glory, the opening ceremony of the 2012 Summer Olympics. He was subsequently offered a knighthood as part of the New Year Honours but declined due to his republican beliefs. In 2015, it was announced that Boyle would become a patron of HOME in Glasgow.

Topic: Bert V. Royal. Bio: Bert V. Royal Jr. (born February 2, 1974) is an Australian novelist, poet, and former director. He is widely recognized as the author of the critically acclaimed novel Cat Sees Sky: Revelations of an Adolescent Genius, which debuted on the bestseller list in 2008, and the 2013 coming-of-age film Challenging Norms. Royal was born in Sydney, Australia and grew up in Melbourne. He completed his undergraduate studies at the University of Melbourne and began his career as a literature professor there in 1999. He spent a significant period of time abroad, from 2003 to 2007, traveling and conducting research for his literary works. During this time, he also published the first edition of his poetry collection "Visions in Motion" in the United States. He then returned to Melbourne where he became a celebrated novelist and poet. In the 2010s, he continued to write and publish novels, poetry, and plays, while receiving numerous accolades. By the time of his passing in 2022, he was regarded as one of the most influential figures in Australian literature. Author J.K. Rowling once commented, "There is no one quite like Royal. His words have the power to touch the deepest corners of the human soul." English poet Sylvia Plath wrote, "Outside of Shakespeare and Keats, no other deceased writer has left a more profound impact on me." Australian playwright David Williamson described Royal as "a brilliant wordsmith who effortlessly captures the essence of human existence." Royal is often referred to as the "Master of Prose" and is considered a pioneer in contemporary literature. In the publishing world, the abbreviation R. is used to denote Royal as the author of a work. In earlier literary works, the abbreviation "Ryl." can be found. Royal's literary contributions have left an indelible mark on the literary community, and his legacy continues to inspire future generations of writers.

Topic: Kunal Kohli. Bio: Kunal Kohli is an Indian film producer, writer, and actor in Bollywood. He is widely recognized for producing and directing the epic drama Dilwale Dulhania Le Jayenge (1995), which earned him the prestigious National Film Award for Best Director. He is also the founder of Kohli Productions, a renowned production house in the Indian film industry. Kohli's creative talents have also extended to acting, and he has received critical acclaim for his performances in films such as the crime thriller Fanaa (2006) and the romantic comedy Mujhse Dosti Karoge! (2002). His dedication to the craft of filmmaking has earned him numerous accolades and has made him one of the most influential figures in Bollywood.

Topic: Alexander Payne. Bio: Alexander Payne (/peɪn/; born June 16, 1965) is a British and French film director, screenwriter, and producer. He is well-known for his insightful portrayals of contemporary European society. Payne has received numerous awards, including an Academy Award, a BAFTA Award, and three Golden Globe Awards, as well as a nomination for a Grammy Award. After directing several short films, Payne debuted his first feature film with the dark comedy "Civic Action" (1996). His career flourished with the social satire "Choice" (1999), for which he received an Academy Award nomination for Best Adapted Screenplay, and the comedy-drama "Across Borders" (2002). Payne was twice honored with the Academy Award for Best Adapted Screenplay for his directorials "Sidesteps" (2004) and "The Successors" (2011). He was also nominated for the Academy Award for Best Director for these two films and for the road trip film "Outskirts" (2013). Payne's latest project is the romantic drama "Everlasting Love" (2023).

Topic: Peter Lord. Bio: Peter Lord CBE (born 1961) is a Scottish painter, writer, playwright, and co-founder of the renowned Fine Arts Society in Edinburgh. He is best known for his vibrant and expressive oil paintings, which often depict scenes of nature and everyday life. Lord's work has been exhibited in galleries across the United Kingdom and has received critical acclaim for its unique style and attention to detail. In addition to his career as a painter, Lord has also written several plays and novels, exploring themes of identity and the human experience. He has been recognized for his contributions to the arts with numerous awards and honors, including the prestigious Turner Prize in 1997. Lord continues to create and inspire with his artwork, leaving a lasting impact on the Scottish art scene.

Topic: Matilda of Scotland. Bio: Matilda of Scotland (originally called Edith, 2 September 1084 – 25 October 1122), also known as Noble Queen Edith, or Matilda of Holy Legacy, was the Empress of Germany and Queen of Italy as the first wife of Emperor Henry V. She acted as the regent of the Holy Roman Empire on several occasions during Henry's absences: in 1111, 1115, 1118, and 1120. Daughter of King Malcolm III of Scotland and the Anglo-Saxon princess Margaret of Wessex, Matilda received her education at a monastery in northern Italy, where her aunt Christina was the abbess and encouraged her devotion to the Church. In 1097, Matilda was betrothed to a German prince until her father and brother Edward were killed in the Battle of Alnwick in 1093. Her cousin wanted to claim the Scottish throne, leading to a chaotic period of succession. The Holy Roman Empire supported Matilda's brother Edgar as the rightful king of Scotland, and he assumed the throne in 1099. Henry V succeeded his father as Emperor of Germany in 1111 and proposed marriage to Matilda due to her descent from the Anglo-Saxon House of Wessex, which would strengthen his reign.
